topics.title: Network Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Hardware** refers to the physical components, devices, and transmission media that are responsible for the **transmission, reception, processing, and storage of data** across a computer network. These are the tangible (touchable) elements that work together to enable communication between computing devices, governed by physical and electrical engineering principles.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITT402 - Module 1)**, network hardware is broadly classified into three primary categories:

1. **Transmission Media** – The physical path (wired) or atmospheric path (wireless) over which signals propagate.
2. **Network Devices (Intermediary Devices)** – Components that direct, amplify, convert, or filter the data signals (e.g., hubs, switches, routers, modems, repeaters).
3. **End Devices (Hosts / DTE)** – The source and destination computing devices (e.g., PCs, servers, smartphones, IoT sensors).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Human Postal System

Imagine the internet is like a giant **postal system**:

- **Your letter (data packet)** is the message you want to send.
- **The road/truck (transmission media)** is the physical path it travels on — trucks on highways, planes in the sky, or ships on water represent the different media.
- **The post office and sorting hubs (routers, switches, gateways)** decide where the letter must go next.
- **Your hand and the receiver's hand (NIC – Network Interface Card)** are what actually put the letter on the road and take it off the road.
- **A translator (modem)** converts your letter from one language (digital) to another (analog signals) so it can travel through the road infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Every network hardware component is mapped to a specific **OSI Layer**. For instance, a Hub operates at **Layer 1 (Physical)**, a Switch at **Layer 2 (Data Link)**, a Router at **Layer 3 (Network)**, and a Gateway at **Layer 7 (Application)**. This mapping is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical properties are central to all network hardware:

- **Speed of Light in Vacuum ($c$):** $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Speed of Light in Fiber Optic Core (typical):** $2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Standard Bandwidth Units:** **bps, Kbps, Mbps, Gbps, Tbps**
- **Standard Frequency Bands (Wireless):** 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 60 GHz
- **Attenuation Unit:** **dB/km (decibels per kilometer)**
- **Characteristic Impedance of Coaxial Cable:** **50 $\Omega$ (Ethernet) or 75 $\Omega$ (Cable TV)**

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Network Hardware

| Category | Wired (Guided) | Wireless (Unguided) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Transmission Media** | Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber Optic | Radio, Microwave, Satellite, Infrared |
| **Network Devices** | Hub, Switch, Router, Bridge, Repeater, Modem | Access Point, Wireless Router, Satellite Dish |
| **End Devices** | Desktop, Server, Printer | Laptop, Smartphone, Tablet, IoT Sensor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Guided Transmission Media (Wired)

### 2.1.1 Twisted Pair Cable
A **Twisted Pair** consists of two insulated copper wires twisted around each other to reduce **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** and **crosstalk** from neighbouring pairs.

- **Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP)** – Most common in LANs (Ethernet Cat5e, Cat6, Cat7). Cheaper, flexible.
- **Shielded Twisted Pair (STP)** – Has an additional metallic foil/braid shielding. Used in high-noise industrial environments.
- **Why twist?** The twists ensure that any external noise affects both wires equally, allowing the receiver to cancel out the common-mode noise (this is called **differential signalling**).

### 2.1.2 Coaxial Cable
A **Coaxial Cable** has a central copper conductor, an insulating dielectric, a metallic shield, and an outer plastic jacket. It offers higher bandwidth and better noise immunity than twisted pair.

- **Baseband Coaxial** (50 $\Omega$) – Used in legacy Ethernet (10Base2, 10Base5).
- **Broadband Coaxial** (75 $\Omega$) – Used for Cable TV and internet (DOCSIS standard).

### 2.1.3 Fiber Optic Cable
Uses **pulses of light** (generated by LEDs or laser diodes) traveling through a glass/plastic core. It is immune to EMI and supports extremely high bandwidth over long distances.

- **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** – Core diameter ~9 µm; uses laser; supports long distances (>100 km); more expensive.
- **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** – Core diameter ~50/62.5 µm; uses LED; shorter distances (<2 km); cheaper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** The principle of **Total Internal Reflection** governs light propagation in fiber optics. Light enters at an angle $\geq$ the critical angle, causing it to reflect entirely within the core, with virtually zero loss.

## 2.2 Unguided Transmission Media (Wireless)

### 2.2.1 Radio Waves
- **Frequency Range:** 3 kHz to 300 GHz.
- **Properties:** Omnidirectional, long range, can penetrate walls, used in Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular (4G/5G).
- **Limitation:** Highly susceptible to interference from other devices operating in the same unlicensed ISM bands.

### 2.2.2 Microwave Transmission
- **Frequency Range:** 1 GHz to 300 GHz.
- **Properties:** Unidirectional (line-of-sight), used in terrestrial point-to-point links and satellite communication.
- **Limitation:** Requires clear line-of-sight; affected by rain fade (rain attenuation).

### 2.2.3 Satellite Communication
A **satellite** acts as a repeater in the sky. Key frequency bands:
- **L-band (1–2 GHz)** – GPS, mobile satellite.
- **C-band (4–8 GHz)** – TV broadcasting.
- **Ku-band (12–18 GHz)** – VSAT, DTH TV.
- **Ka-band (26–40 GHz)** – Modern broadband satellites (e.g., Starlink).

### 2.2.4 Infrared
- **Frequency Range:** 300 GHz to 400 THz.
- **Properties:** Very short range, line-of-sight, cannot penetrate walls. Used in TV remotes, IrDA ports.

## 2.3 Network Devices — Layer-by-Layer Mapping

| Device | OSI Layer | Core Function | Key Characteristic |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Repeater** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Regenerates and amplifies weak signals | No MAC address awareness |
| **Hub** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Broadcasts data to all connected devices | Single collision domain |
| **Bridge** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Connects two LAN segments, filters using MAC | Reduces collision domain |
| **Switch** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Forwards frames based on MAC address table | Each port is a separate collision domain |
| **Router** | Layer 3 (Network) | Forwards packets between networks using IP | Each port is a separate broadcast domain |
| **Gateway** | Layer 7 (Application) | Protocol translation between dissimilar networks | Highest-level device |
| **Modem** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Modulates/Demodulates digital ↔ analog | Enables digital data over analog lines |
| **NIC** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Interface between computer and network | Has unique 48-bit MAC address |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are tested repeatedly in KTU ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the variable definitions and standard units.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\text{Signal Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Power loss over a medium | $P_{\text{in}}$ = input power (W), $P_{\text{out}}$ = output power (W) |
| 2 | $\text{Voltage Attenuation (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Voltage signal loss | $V_{\text{in}}$, $V_{\text{out}}$ in volts |
| 3 | $\text{Propagation Delay} (T_p) = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$ | Time for a bit to travel | $T_p$ in seconds, distance in meters, speed in m/s |
| 4 | $\text{Transmission Delay} (T_t) = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size (L)}}{\text{Bandwidth (R)}}$ | Time to push all bits onto the link | $L$ in bits, $R$ in bps |
| 5 | $\text{Total Delay} = T_p + T_t + T_q + T_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end delay | $T_q$ = queuing, $T_{\text{proc}}$ = processing |
| 6 | $\text{Nyquist Bit Rate} = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2(M)$ | Max bit rate of noiseless channel | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels |
| 7 | $\text{Shannon Capacity} (C) = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Max bit rate of noisy channel | $B$ in Hz, $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio |
| 8 | $\text{Noise (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | SNR in decibels | Dimensionless ratio expressed in dB |
| 9 | $\text{Speed of Light in Fiber} = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | Where $n$ is refractive index | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $n \approx 1.5$ for glass |
| 10 | $\text{Max nodes on Ethernet} = 2^{48}$ | From 48-bit MAC address | Practical limit enforced by address space |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Network hardware forms the **physical backbone** of every digital infrastructure:

- **Telecommunications:** Cellular towers (radio + microwave + fiber backhaul).
- **Data Centers:** Massive deployments of switches, fiber optics, and routers.
- **Smart Cities:** IoT gateways, wireless sensors, fiber-to-the-home (FTTH).
- **Healthcare:** Real-time telemedicine requires high-bandwidth, low-latency fiber and 5G links.
- **Industrial Automation:** Hubs and switches in harsh environments use shielded twisted pairs (STP) and fiber.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Attenuation in dB

**Problem Statement:** A signal enters a 10 km long fiber optic cable with power $P_{\text{in}} = 2$ mW, and exits with power $P_{\text{out}} = 0.05$ mW. Calculate the total attenuation and the attenuation per km.

**Step 1: Identify the formula.**

The attenuation in decibels is defined as the logarithmic ratio of input to output power.

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 \text{ mW}}{0.05 \text{ mW}}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the ratio inside the logarithm.**

$$
\frac{2}{0.05} = \frac{2 \times 100}{5} = \frac{200}{5} = 40
$$

**Step 4: Compute the logarithm base 10.**

$$
\log_{10}(40) = \log_{10}(4 \times 10) = \log_{10}(4) + \log_{10}(10) = 0.602 + 1 = 1.602
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by 10 to get dB.**

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 1.602 = 16.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6: Compute attenuation per km (dB/km).**

$$
A_{\text{per km}} = \frac{16.02 \text{ dB}}{10 \text{ km}} = 1.602 \text{ dB/km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Inference:** Typical single-mode fiber has an attenuation of ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm wavelength. The value of 1.602 dB/km indicates this is likely a higher-loss multi-mode fiber or operates at a different wavelength (e.g., 850 nm).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: End-to-End Delay

**Problem Statement:** A 10,000-bit frame is transmitted over a 2000 km fiber link with a bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Speed of light in fiber is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Find the total delay (assume processing and queuing delays are negligible).

**Step 1: Compute Propagation Delay ($T_p$).**

$$
T_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{2}{200} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Transmission Delay ($T_t$).**

$$
T_t = \frac{\text{Frame Size (L)}}{\text{Bandwidth (R)}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^9 \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
T_t = 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.01 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: Add the delays (Processing and Queuing assumed zero).**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_p + T_t = 10 \text{ ms} + 0.01 \text{ ms} = 10.01 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For long-haul fiber links, **propagation delay dominates** because the speed of light is finite. This is why even with a 1 Gbps link, latency on transcontinental fiber (e.g., USA to Europe, ~8000 km) is ~40 ms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shannon Channel Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A wireless channel has a bandwidth of 20 MHz. The signal power is 100 mW and noise power is 1 mW. Find the theoretical maximum data rate.

**Step 1: Compute the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{100 \text{ mW}}{1 \text{ mW}} = 100
$$

**Step 2: Express SNR in dB (cross-check).**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Shannon's Theorem.**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100)
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate $\log_2(101)$.**

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by bandwidth.**

$$
C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 6.658 \approx 133.16 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Match:** This is exactly why **4G LTE** and **Wi-Fi 5/6** channels in the 20 MHz band achieve real-world throughput in the 100–150 Mbps range — Shannon's bound is hard to exceed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Cable Performance)

| Parameter | UTP (Cat6) | Coaxial (RG-6) | Single-Mode Fiber |
|-----------|------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Max Bandwidth** | 250 MHz – 500 MHz | 1 GHz | > 10 THz |
| **Max Distance (at 1 Gbps)** | 100 m | 500 m | 100+ km |
| **Attenuation (dB/km)** | ~20 dB/km | ~10 dB/km | 0.2 dB/km |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | Moderate | Low | Immune |
| **Cost (per meter)** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Typical Use** | LAN (Ethernet) | Cable TV, Broadband | Backbone, Long-haul |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Network Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Source End Device - PC Server] --> B[NIC - Network Interface Card]
    B --> C[Modem - Digital to Analog Conversion]
    C --> D[Transmission Media - Wired or Wireless]
    D --> E[Repeater - Signal Regeneration]
    E --> F[Hub Switch Bridge - LAN Distribution]
    F --> G[Router - Inter Network Forwarding]
    G --> H[Gateway - Protocol Translation]
    H --> I[Destination End Device]
    J[End Devices Category] -.-> A
    K[Network Devices Category] -.-> C
    L[Transmission Media Category] -.-> D
```

## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Network Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NH[Network Hardware] --> A1[Transmission Media]
    NH --> A2[Network Devices]
    NH --> A3[End Devices]

    A1 --> B1[Guided Wired]
    A1 --> B2[Unguided Wireless]

    B1 --> C1[Twisted Pair UTP STP]
    B1 --> C2[Coaxial Cable]
    B1 --> C3[Fiber Optic SMF MMF]

    B2 --> D1[Radio Waves]
    B2 --> D2[Microwave]
    B2 --> D3[Satellite]
    B2 --> D4[Infrared]

    A2 --> E1[Layer 1 - Hub Repeater Modem]
    A2 --> E2[Layer 2 - Bridge Switch NIC]
    A2 --> E3[Layer 3 - Router]
    A2 --> E4[Layer 7 - Gateway]

    A3 --> F1[Workstations PCs]
    A3 --> F2[Servers]
    A3 --> F3[Mobile Devices]
    A3 --> F4[IoT Sensors]
```

## 4.3 OSI Layer-to-Device Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[OSI Layer 7 - Application] --> GW[Gateway]
    L6[OSI Layer 6 - Presentation] --> GW
    L5[OSI Layer 5 - Session] --> GW
    L4[OSI Layer 4 - Transport] --> RT[Router]
    L3[OSI Layer 3 - Network] --> RT
    L2[OSI Layer 2 - Data Link] --> SW[Switch Bridge NIC]
    L1[OSI Layer 1 - Physical] --> HB[Hub Repeater Modem Cable]
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Through a Typical LAN

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC1 as Source PC
    participant NIC1 as Source NIC
    participant SW as LAN Switch
    participant RT as Router
    participant NET as ISP Network
    participant PC2 as Destination PC

    PC1->>NIC1: Generate Data
    NIC1->>SW: Frame with MAC addresses
    SW->>RT: Forward to default gateway
    RT->>NET: Packet with IP addresses
    NET->>PC2: Route via routers and links
    PC2->>PC2: NIC receives and decodes frame
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Explain the difference between a Hub and a Switch. Mention the OSI layer at which each operates.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Hub | Switch |
> |---------|-----|--------|
> | OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
> | Intelligence | Dumb repeater; broadcasts to all ports | Intelligent; uses MAC address table |
> | Collision Domain | Single shared collision domain | Each port is a separate collision domain |
> | Address Awareness | No MAC address | Learns and uses 48-bit MAC addresses |
> | Bandwidth Sharing | All devices share total bandwidth | Each port gets dedicated bandwidth |
>
> A **Hub** simply repeats incoming electrical signals to all other ports, creating a shared medium with collisions. A **Switch** builds a **MAC address table** and forwards frames **only to the intended destination port**, drastically improving efficiency and reducing collisions.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – July 2023]`

**Compare Twisted Pair, Coaxial, and Fiber Optic cables in terms of bandwidth, cost, and EMI immunity.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Twisted Pair | Coaxial | Fiber Optic |
> |-----------|--------------|---------|-------------|
> | Bandwidth | Low–Medium (up to 500 MHz) | Medium (up to 1 GHz) | Very High (THz range) |
> | Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
> | EMI Immunity | Poor (UTP) to Moderate (STP) | Good | Complete Immunity |
> | Signal Type | Electrical | Electrical | Light (Optical) |
> | Max Distance | 100 m (Cat6) | 500 m | 100+ km |
>
> **Conclusion:** Fiber is best for long-distance and high-bandwidth backbones; Twisted Pair is best for cost-effective LANs; Coaxial is suitable for broadband and legacy TV.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Always **state the OSI layer** of any device you mention — failing to do this costs 1–2 marks.
> - When comparing devices, use a **comparison table**, not just a paragraph — tabular answers get better partial marks.
> - For numerical problems, always **write the formula, substitute values, show units, and box the final answer**.
> - Never confuse "collision domain" with "broadcast domain" — this is a top-1 silly mistake that KTU examiners catch.
> - For fiber optics, mentioning **Total Internal Reflection** is mandatory for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[Adapted from KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the different types of **transmission media** used in computer networks. Compare guided and unguided media with suitable examples.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A fiber optic link of length 50 km has an input power of 1 mW and output power of 0.1 mW.
(i) Calculate the total attenuation in dB.
(ii) Calculate the attenuation per km.
(iii) If the link is upgraded to a single-mode fiber with attenuation of 0.2 dB/km, what would be the output power for the same input?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Transmission media are the physical pathways that carry electromagnetic signals between sender and receiver. They are classified as **Guided (Wired)** and **Unguided (Wireless)**.

**Guided Media (3 marks):**

1. **Twisted Pair Cable** — Two insulated copper wires twisted together to reduce crosstalk. Variants: UTP and STP. Used in Ethernet LANs (Cat5e, Cat6). Max distance ~100 m.
2. **Coaxial Cable** — A central conductor surrounded by dielectric and metallic shield. Higher bandwidth than twisted pair. Used in broadband internet and cable TV.
3. **Fiber Optic Cable** — Uses light pulses through glass core. **Single-mode** for long distances, **multi-mode** for short distances. Immune to EMI, very high bandwidth.

**Unguided Media (2 marks):**

1. **Radio Waves** — Omnidirectional, 3 kHz–300 GHz. Used in Wi-Fi, cellular, Bluetooth.
2. **Microwave** — Unidirectional, line-of-sight, used in point-to-point terrestrial links.
3. **Satellite** — Microwave repeater in space; used for long-distance communication (VSAT, GPS).
4. **Infrared** — Very short range, line-of-sight, used in TV remotes and IrDA.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Guided | Unguided |
|---------|--------|----------|
| Path | Physical cable | Air/Vacuum |
| Security | Higher | Lower (eavesdropping easy) |
| Installation Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Mobility | Limited | High |

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Attenuation (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1 \text{ mW}}{0.1 \text{ mW}}\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \times 1 = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

**(ii) Attenuation per km (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{per km}} = \frac{10 \text{ dB}}{50 \text{ km}} = 0.2 \text{ dB/km}
$$

**(iii) Output Power for Single-Mode Fiber (2 marks):**

Total attenuation on 50 km = $0.2 \times 50 = 10$ dB.

Since 10 dB = ratio of 10, we have:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{10} = \frac{1 \text{ mW}}{10} = 0.1 \text{ mW}
$$

**Answer:** The output power remains **0.1 mW** (because both cables have the same total attenuation in this case).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[Adapted from KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the function of the following network devices and identify the OSI layer they operate at:
(i) Repeater
(ii) Bridge
(iii) Router
(iv) Gateway

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A 5000-bit message is sent over a channel of bandwidth 2 Mbps. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s and the link length is 4000 km.
(i) Calculate the transmission delay.
(ii) Calculate the propagation delay.
(iii) Which delay dominates, and why?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Repeater (Layer 1 – Physical) [1.5 marks]:**
   A repeater regenerates weak signals to extend the physical reach of a network. It does not interpret the data; it simply amplifies/reshapes the electrical signal. Operates at the **Physical Layer**.

2. **Bridge (Layer 2 – Data Link) [1.5 marks]:**
   A bridge connects two LAN segments and uses **MAC addresses** to filter traffic, reducing collisions. It builds a forwarding table and learns which devices are on which segment. Operates at the **Data Link Layer**.

3. **Router (Layer 3 – Network) [2 marks]:**
   A router forwards **packets** between different IP networks using routing tables and protocols (OSPF, BGP). It is the primary device that enables the **Internet**. Each port of a router is a separate **broadcast domain**. Operates at the **Network Layer**.

4. **Gateway (Layer 7 – Application) [2 marks]:**
   A gateway performs **protocol translation** between dissimilar networks (e.g., Ethernet to Frame Relay, IPv4 to IPv6, email to SMS). It is the most intelligent network device and operates at the **Application Layer**.

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Transmission Delay (3 marks):**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{5000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Propagation Delay (2 marks):**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Dominant Delay (2 marks):**

$$
T_p \; (20 \text{ ms}) \;\gg\; T_t \; (2.5 \text{ ms})
$$

**Propagation delay dominates** because the link is very long (4000 km) and the speed of light in fiber is finite. For long-distance fiber links, propagation delay is always the dominant component of end-to-end delay.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Network Hardware**

- **OSI Layer Mapping is KING:**
  - Layer 1 → Hub, Repeater, Modem, Cable
  - Layer 2 → Bridge, Switch, NIC
  - Layer 3 → Router
  - Layer 7 → Gateway

- **Collision vs Broadcast Domain:**
  - Hub = 1 collision domain
  - Switch = N collision domains (one per port), 1 broadcast domain
  - Router = N broadcast domains (one per port)

- **Cable Hierarchy (Low to High Bandwidth):** Twisted Pair < Coaxial < Fiber Optic

- **Formulas You Must Memorize:**
  - Attenuation (dB) = $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}} / P_{\text{out}})$
  - Propagation Delay = distance / speed
  - Transmission Delay = frame size / bandwidth
  - Shannon Capacity = $B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$

- **Twisted Pair Categories:** Cat5 (100 Mbps), Cat5e (1 Gbps), Cat6 (1–10 Gbps), Cat7 (10 Gbps), Cat8 (25–40 Gbps)

- **Fiber Variants:** SMF (long distance, laser), MMF (short distance, LED)

- **Wireless Frequency Bands:** Radio (3 kHz–300 GHz), Microwave (1–300 GHz), Infrared (300 GHz–400 THz)

- **Key Principle:** Light propagation in fiber = **Total Internal Reflection**.

- **Common Pitfall:** A **Hub** and a **Repeater** are NOT the same — a repeater connects exactly 2 segments; a hub connects multiple devices.

- **Real-World Insight:** Modern data centers use a **leaf-spine architecture** of high-speed switches and fiber optics to achieve microsecond-level latency.

- **KTU Past Pattern:** Questions on **device-vs-layer mapping**, **UTP categories**, and **attenuation numericals** appear almost every semester — practice these thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Network Software"?

**Network Software** refers to the set of conceptual frameworks, layered architectures, protocols, service primitives, and reference models that govern how data is exchanged between networked devices. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it encompasses the *logical architecture* of networks — i.e., the **Protocol Hierarchy**, the **Service Primitives**, the **OSI Reference Model**, and the **TCP/IP Reference Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCITT402 — Module 1):** *Network software is the collection of hierarchical protocols, reference models (OSI & TCP/IP), and service definitions that enable structured, reliable, and interoperable communication between heterogeneous computing systems.*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The International Postal System

Imagine you (in **Kerala, India**) want to send a registered letter to a friend in **Berlin, Germany**. You don't personally handle the aeroplane, the customs, the sorting, or the delivery van. You simply hand the letter to a **post office clerk**. The post office handles *everything else* through a chain of well-defined, independent departments:

| Real-World Step | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You write the letter | Application Layer (creates the *data/message*) |
| You put it in an envelope with addresses | Transport / Session layer (formats and synchronizes) |
| Post office sorts by city/country | Network Layer (logical addressing & routing) |
| Post office puts it in a mailbag | Data Link Layer (framing, error detection) |
| Mailbag travels on a truck/plane | Physical Layer (actual electrical/optical signals) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Layering:** *Each layer offers services to the layer **above** it and uses services from the layer **below** it — a fundamental concept that Tanenbaum emphasizes in every edition of "Computer Networks".* No layer has to know how the other layers do their jobs. This is **modularity**.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Layered Network Software?

1. **Modularity** — Complex problems are broken into smaller, manageable sub-problems.
2. **Interoperability** — Devices from different vendors can communicate if they obey the same protocol at each layer.
3. **Ease of Design & Debugging** — A failure in one layer can be isolated and fixed without redesigning the entire system.
4. **Technology Independence** — Layers can be upgraded independently (e.g., replacing copper with fiber at the physical layer).
5. **Standardization** — ISO, IEEE, and IETF publish standards that every manufacturer follows.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Network Software

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Protocol Hierarchy (Implementation View)** — *How* each layer does its job (rules, formats, algorithms).
> 2. **Service Architecture (Interface View)** — *What* each layer offers to the layer above (services & primitives).

A **service** is a set of operations a layer provides to the layer above. A **protocol** is the set of rules governing the format and meaning of the frames, packets, or messages exchanged between *peers* (i.e., instances of the same layer on different machines).

---

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics & Concepts (Bold for Emphasis)

- **OSI Model: 7 layers** (ISO Standard 7498)
- **TCP/IP Model: 4 layers** (RFC 1122, RFC 791)
- **Service Primitives: 5 types** — `LISTEN`, `CONNECT`, `ACCEPT`, `SEND`, `RECEIVE`, `DISCONNECT`
- **Two Service Types:** **Connection-oriented** and **Connectionless**
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** Named differently at each layer (Frame, Packet, Segment, Datagram, etc.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Protocol Hierarchies — The Heart of Network Software

A **protocol hierarchy** (also called a *protocol stack*) is an ordered stack of protocols, one per layer, working in cooperation. Each layer on a source machine communicates *logically* with its peer layer on the destination machine, but *physically* data flows down through the local stack, across the medium, and up the remote stack.

**Operational Flow (sender side):**
1. Application produces a **Message** (M).
2. M is wrapped with a Transport header → **Segment**.
3. Segment is wrapped with a Network header → **Packet (Datagram)**.
4. Packet is wrapped with a Data Link header/trailer → **Frame**.
5. Frame is converted to **Bits** and transmitted on the physical medium.

> [!NOTE]
> This process is called **Encapsulation** — each layer adds its own header (and sometimes a trailer) as the data moves down the stack. On the receiving end, this is reversed: each layer **decapsulates** (strips) its corresponding header.

### 2.2 Design Issues for the Layers

Every layer in a network must solve a set of recurring problems. Forouzan's textbook lists the following **canonical design issues**:

| # | Design Issue | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Addressing / Identification** | Each layer needs a unique ID for the sender/receiver (IP at Network, Port at Transport, MAC at Data Link). |
| 2 | **Error Control** | Bits/frames/packets may be corrupted in transit; layers use CRC, checksums, ARQ to recover. |
| 3 | **Flow Control** | Sender must not overwhelm a slower receiver (e.g., sliding-window protocols). |
| 4 | **Sequencing** | Packets may arrive out-of-order; layers must reorder them before delivery. |
| 5 | **Connection Management** | Establishing, maintaining, and tearing down sessions (3-way handshake in TCP). |
| 6 | **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** | Multiple application sessions share one physical link (upward/downward demux). |
| 7 | **Routing** | (Network Layer) Choosing the best path through intermediate routers. |
| 8 | **Congestion Control** | Preventing network collapse when too many packets flood routers. |
| 9 | **Fragmentation & Reassembly** | Large packets broken into smaller ones, then reassembled at the destination. |
| 10 | **Quality of Service (QoS)** | Guarantees on bandwidth, delay, jitter, and loss. |

### 2.3 Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Services

This is the **most important conceptual distinction** in Module 1 and a guaranteed KTU question.

**Connection-Oriented Service** (e.g., TCP, virtual circuit):
- A **connection is established** before data transfer (3-way handshake).
- All packets follow the **same logical path** (virtual circuit).
- Packets arrive **in order**.
- **Reliable**: acknowledgments, retransmission, flow control built in.
- Analogy: A **phone call** — you dial, the other side picks up, you talk, then hang up.

**Connectionless Service** (e.g., UDP, datagram):
- **No prior setup**; each packet is independent.
- Each packet may take a **different path** through the network.
- Packets may arrive **out of order** or be lost.
- **Unreliable** (no guarantees) but **faster** and **lighter**.
- Analogy: **Postal letters** — each letter is independent and self-contained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six combinations exist** (Reliable/Unreliable) × (Connection-oriented/Connectionless) — but not all are practical. The four important ones are:
> 1. Reliable connection-oriented (e.g., TCP)
> 2. Unreliable connectionless (e.g., UDP, IP)
> 3. Reliable connectionless (rare — e.g., TFTP, but typically implemented atop UDP)
> 4. Unreliable connection-oriented (rare — e.g., digitized voice over ATM AAL1)

### 2.4 Service Primitives

A **service primitive** is an elementary operation that a user of a service can request from the service provider. Tanenbaum defines **6 primitives** for a simple connection-oriented service:

| Primitive | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `LISTEN` | Server waits for an incoming connection (block). |
| `CONNECT` | Client requests connection establishment. |
| `ACCEPT` | Server accepts, a connection is created. |
| `SEND` | Send data over the established connection. |
| `RECEIVE` | Block until data arrives. |
| `DISCONNECT` | Terminate the connection. |

The **time-sequence diagram** (see Section 4) is the standard KTU way of illustrating this flow.

### 2.5 The OSI Reference Model (7 Layers)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is the ISO's standard (ISO/IEC 7498-1) for a 7-layer architecture. From top to bottom:

| # | Layer | Function | PDU | Example Protocols/Standards |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Application** | Network processes to applications; closest to end user. | Data | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Data representation, encryption, compression. | Data | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | **Session** | Establishes, manages, terminates sessions. | Data | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP |
| 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end connection, reliability, flow control. | Segment / Datagram | **TCP (reliable)**, **UDP (unreliable)** |
| 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing, routing across networks. | Packet / Datagram | **IP**, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, ARP |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Node-to-node framing, MAC addressing, error detection. | Frame | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, HDLC |
| 1 | **Physical** | Transmission of raw bits over a medium. | Bits | IEEE 802.3 (cable), fiber, radio, RS-232 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Top-Down): "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**
> **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up): "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**

### 2.6 The TCP/IP Reference Model (4 Layers)

The **TCP/IP** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) was developed by DARPA (US Department of Defense) in the 1970s. It predates the OSI model and is the *de facto* model of the Internet.

| # | Layer | Function | PDU | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | **Application** | Combines OSI's Session, Presentation, and Application layers. | Message / Data | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| 3 | **Transport** | Host-to-host communication. | Segment / Datagram | TCP, UDP |
| 2 | **Internet (Network)** | Logical addressing & routing across networks. | Datagram | IP, ICMP, ARP, RARP |
| 1 | **Network Access / Link** | Combines OSI's Data Link and Physical layers. | Frame / Bits | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ATM, Frame Relay |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OSI Model** is the **teaching standard** used in textbooks (Tanenbaum, Forouzan, Stallings) and in KTU question papers. Engineers use it as a *common vocabulary* when diagnosing network problems ("it's a Layer 2 issue" means a switch/MAC problem).
- **TCP/IP Model** is the **implementation standard** of the global Internet. Every device that connects to the Internet runs the TCP/IP stack.
- Understanding layering is essential for **cybersecurity** (e.g., a firewall operates at Layers 3, 4, and 7).

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Quick-Fire Formulas & Concepts

Although this module is largely conceptual, here are a few quantitative relations that *do* appear in KTU questions:

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total transmission time of a message | $T = L \div R + D_{prop}$ | $L$ = message length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps), $D_{prop}$ = propagation delay (s) |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) | $\text{BDP} = R \times D_{prop}$ | bits — the number of bits "in flight" on a link |
| Effective Throughput with $n$ headers | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{L + \sum h_i} \times 100\%$ | $h_i$ = header size at each layer $i$ |
| Number of layers in OSI | $7$ | — |
| Number of layers in TCP/IP | $4$ | — |
| TCP Header minimum size | $20$ bytes | Optional options may extend to $60$ bytes |
| IPv4 Header | $20$ to $60$ bytes | — |
| Ethernet Frame overhead | $38$ bytes (preamble + SFD + header + FCS) | Frame payload $\le 1500$ bytes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Encapsulation Overhead Analysis

Suppose a user at Host A sends an HTTP message of size $L = 1000$ bytes to Host B over an Ethernet+IP+TCP path.

We want to find the **total number of bytes transmitted on the wire** including all headers, and the **efficiency** (ratio of application data to total bits).

**Step 1:** Identify the headers added at each layer.

At the Transport Layer (TCP), a header of size $h_{tcp} = 20$ bytes is added.

$$
L_{segment} = L + h_{tcp} = 1000 + 20 = 1020 \text{ bytes}
$$

At the Network Layer (IPv4), a header of size $h_{ip} = 20$ bytes is added.

$$
L_{packet} = L_{segment} + h_{ip} = 1020 + 20 = 1040 \text{ bytes}
$$

At the Data Link Layer (Ethernet), a header of $h_{eth} = 14$ bytes and a trailer (FCS) of $t_{eth} = 4$ bytes are added.

$$
L_{frame} = L_{packet} + h_{eth} + t_{eth} = 1040 + 14 + 4 = 1058 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to bits for transmission over the physical medium (multiply by 8).

$$
L_{bits} = 1058 \times 8 = 8464 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **encapsulation efficiency** $ \eta $.

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{L_{frame}} = \frac{1000}{1058} \approx 0.9452 = 94.52\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "wasted" bytes are $\sum h_i + t_{eth} = 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 58$ bytes. This is exactly $5.48\%$ overhead. KTU questions often ask to compute this for a given L and protocol stack.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Transmission Time with Layered Encapsulation

Given:
- Message size: $L = 1000$ bytes
- Link rate: $R = 1$ Mbps
- Propagation delay: $D_{prop} = 50$ ms
- No processing / queueing delays

**Step 1:** Total bits on the wire (from Section 3.1).

$$
N_{bits} = 1058 \times 8 = 8464 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** Transmission delay (time to push all bits onto the medium).

$$
D_{trans} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R} = \frac{8464}{10^6} = 8.464 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Total time from "first bit sent" to "last bit received."

$$
T_{total} = D_{trans} + D_{prop} = 8.464 + 50 = 58.464 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** If the receiver must acknowledge (ACK) and the ACK is $L_{ack} = 40$ bytes (TCP ACK), the ACK frame size is:

$$
L_{ack\ frame} = 40 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 98 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
L_{ack\ bits} = 98 \times 8 = 784 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
D_{ack\ trans} = \frac{784}{10^6} = 0.784 \text{ ms}
$$

Total round-trip time (RTT):

$$
\text{RTT} = 2 \times (D_{prop} + D_{trans}) + D_{ack\ trans} = 2(50 + 8.464) + 0.784 = 117.712 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PDU Naming at Each Layer

The **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** is the formal name of a data unit at a given layer. KTU frequently asks for these.

| Layer (OSI) | Layer (TCP/IP) | PDU Name | Header Size (typical) | What It Contains |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application / Presentation / Session | Application | **Message / Data** | Variable | User information |
| Transport | Transport | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) | $20$ / $8$ bytes | Port numbers, sequence, ACK, checksum |
| Network | Internet | **Packet / Datagram** | $20$–$60$ bytes (IPv4) | Source/Dest IP, TTL, protocol |
| Data Link | Network Access | **Frame** | $14$ bytes (Ethernet) | Source/Dest MAC, type, FCS |
| Physical | Network Access | **Bits / Symbols** | N/A | Electrical/optical pulses |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: OSI vs TCP/IP Comparison

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | ISO (1984) | DARPA / IETF (1970s) |
| Number of Layers | $7$ | $4$ |
| Design Philosophy | Protocol-independent; "open" | Protocol-centric; "what works" |
| Adoption | Reference/teaching model | Practical Internet standard |
| Bottom Layer | Physical (Layer 1) | Network Access / Link |
| Transport Layer | Explicit (Layer 4) | Explicit (Layer 2 of TCP/IP) |
| Session & Presentation | Separate layers | Bundled into Application |
| Standardization | Strict, slow | Pragmatic, fast |
| Status | A **reference**, not an implementation | A **suite** of implemented protocols |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 7-Layer OSI Reference Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS"]
    L6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION\nSSL, TLS, JPEG, MPEG"]
    L5["LAYER 5 - SESSION\nNetBIOS, RPC, SIP"]
    L4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT\nTCP, UDP, SCTP"]
    L3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK\nIP, ICMP, OSPF, ARP"]
    L2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK\nEthernet, WiFi, PPP, HDLC"]
    L1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL\nCables, Fiber, Radio Waves"]

    L7 --- L6
    L6 --- L5
    L5 --- L4
    L4 --- L3
    L3 --- L2
    L2 --- L1
```

### 4.2 The 4-Layer TCP/IP Reference Model (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TCP4["LAYER 4 - APPLICATION\nHTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH"]
    TCP3["LAYER 3 - TRANSPORT\nTCP, UDP, DCCP"]
    TCP2["LAYER 2 - INTERNET\nIP, ICMP, IGMP, ARP"]
    TCP1["LAYER 1 - NETWORK ACCESS\nEthernet, WiFi, ATM, SONET"]

    TCP4 --- TCP3
    TCP3 --- TCP2
    TCP2 --- TCP1
```

### 4.3 OSI vs TCP/IP Side-by-Side Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSI7["OSI L7 - Application"]
    OSI6["OSI L6 - Presentation"]
    OSI5["OSI L5 - Session"]
    OSI4["OSI L4 - Transport"]
    OSI3["OSI L3 - Network"]
    OSI2["OSI L2 - Data Link"]
    OSI1["OSI L1 - Physical"]

    TCP4["TCP/IP L4 - Application"]
    TCP3["TCP/IP L3 - Transport"]
    TCP2["TCP/IP L2 - Internet"]
    TCP1["TCP/IP L1 - Network Access"]

    OSI7 --> TCP4
    OSI6 --> TCP4
    OSI5 --> TCP4
    OSI4 --> TCP3
    OSI3 --> TCP2
    OSI2 --> TCP1
    OSI1 --> TCP1
```

### 4.4 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["APP: HTTP GET Request - 1000 bytes"] --> A2["TRANSPORT: Add TCP Header 20 bytes - 1020 bytes"]
    A2 --> A3["NETWORK: Add IP Header 20 bytes - 1040 bytes"]
    A3 --> A4["DATA LINK: Add MAC Header 14 + FCS 4 bytes - 1058 bytes"]
    A4 --> A5["PHYSICAL: Transmit as Bits"]

    A5 --> A6["PHYSICAL: Receive Bits"]
    A6 --> A7["DATA LINK: Remove MAC Header and FCS"]
    A7 --> A8["NETWORK: Remove IP Header"]
    A8 --> A9["TRANSPORT: Remove TCP Header"]
    A9 --> A10["APP: Deliver 1000 bytes of HTTP Data"]
```

### 4.5 Connection-Oriented Service Primitive Flow (Mermaid Time-Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Application
    participant S as Server Application
    participant TS as Transport Service

    C->>TS: CONNECT.request
    TS->>S: CONNECT.indication
    S->>TS: CONNECT.response
    TS->>C: CONNECT.confirm
    Note over C,S: CONNECTION ESTABLISHED
    C->>TS: SEND.request (DATA)
    TS->>S: SEND.indication (DATA)
    S->>TS: RECEIVE.request
    TS->>C: RECEIVE.confirm
    Note over C,S: DATA TRANSFER COMPLETE
    C->>TS: DISCONNECT.request
    TS->>S: DISCONNECT.indication
    S->>TS: DISCONNECT.response
    TS->>C: DISCONNECT.confirm
```

### 4.6 Connectionless Datagram Service Primitives (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    participant TS as Transport Service

    Note over C,S: NO CONNECTION NEEDED
    C->>TS: SEND.request (DATA, DEST_ADDR)
    TS->>S: SEND.indication (DATA, SOURCE_ADDR)
    Note over S: Independent operation - no ACK

    C->>TS: SEND.request (DATA, DEST_ADDR)
    TS->>S: SEND.indication (DATA, SOURCE_ADDR)
    Note over S: Datagrams may arrive out of order
```

### 4.7 End-to-End Communication: Peers, Interfaces, and Services (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HostA["SOURCE HOST A"]
        HA7["L7 App"]
        HA4["L4 Transport"]
        HA3["L3 Network"]
        HA2["L2 Data Link"]
        HA1["L1 Physical"]
    end

    subgraph HostB["DESTINATION HOST B"]
        HB7["L7 App"]
        HB4["L4 Transport"]
        HB3["L3 Network"]
        HB2["L2 Data Link"]
        HB1["L1 Physical"]
    end

    HA1 ---|Physical Medium| HB1

    HA7 -.->|Logical Peer-to-Peer| HB7
    HA4 -.->|Logical Peer-to-Peer| HB4
    HA3 -.->|Logical Peer-to-Peer| HB3
    HA2 -.->|Logical Peer-to-Peer| HB2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a protocol hierarchy. Why is layering important in network software?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- A **protocol hierarchy** is a stack of protocols, one per layer, where each layer communicates with its peer on the remote machine using a defined protocol, while offering services to the layer above and using services from the layer below. *(1 mark)*
- Layering is important because it provides **modularity**, allowing complex communication to be broken into manageable sub-problems. *(1 mark)*
- It enables **interoperability** between heterogeneous devices from different manufacturers, simplifies **debugging** (faults localized to specific layers), and allows independent **technology upgrades** (e.g., swapping copper with fiber at Layer 1). *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between connection-oriented and connectionless services with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Setup | Three-way handshake required | No prior setup |
| Path | Same virtual path for all packets | Independent path per packet |
| Reliability | Reliable, ordered delivery | Unreliable, may be lost or reordered |
| Example | **TCP** | **UDP** |
| Analogy | Phone call | Postal letter |

*(1 mark for setup/path, 1 mark for reliability/order, 1 mark for example + analogy)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Module 1 Choice (Option 1)*

> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. List all **7 layers** with their primary functions and one example protocol/standard at each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of **service primitives** for a connection-oriented service. List the **4 types of primitives** and show their time-sequence between a client and a server. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Reference Model** was developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** as a generic, protocol-independent framework for network communication. It defines **7 layers**, each performing a distinct set of functions. *(1 mark)*

| Layer | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **7. Application** | Provides network services to end-user applications. | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| **6. Presentation** | Data translation, encryption, compression. | TLS/SSL, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII |
| **5. Session** | Establishes, maintains, and terminates sessions between applications. | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| **4. Transport** | End-to-end logical connection, segmentation, flow & error control. | TCP, UDP, SCTP |
| **3. Network** | Logical addressing, routing, fragmentation across networks. | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP |
| **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection on a single link. | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP |
| **1. Physical** | Transmits raw bits as electrical/optical/radio signals. | RS-232, 100BASE-TX, SONET |

*(6 marks — half mark per layer, including function + example)*

**Mnemonic (Top-Down): "All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** *(bonus recall aid)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

A **service primitive** is the elementary operation that a service user requests from the service provider. The four primitive types (according to ISO 8509 / Tanenbaum) are:

1. **REQUEST** — Issued by a service user to invoke a service.
2. **INDICATION** — Issued by the provider to inform the user of an event.
3. **RESPONSE** — Issued by the user to acknowledge an indication.
4. **CONFIRM** — Issued by the provider to confirm the completion of a request.

For a **connection-oriented transport service**, the typical sequence of primitives is: *(3 marks for the sequence)*

| # | Primitive | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `CONNECT.request` | Client initiates connection. |
| 2 | `CONNECT.indication` | Server is told of incoming request. |
| 3 | `CONNECT.response` | Server agrees. |
| 4 | `CONNECT.confirm` | Client is told the connection is established. |
| 5 | `DATA.request` / `DATA.indication` | Data exchange. |
| 6 | `DISCONNECT.request` / `DISCONNECT.indication` | Connection tear-down. |

**Time-Sequence Diagram (3 marks for diagram):**

```
CLIENT                          SERVER
  |                                |
  |--- CONNECT.request -------->  |
  |                                |
  |<-- CONNECT.indication ------- |
  |                                |
  |<-------- CONNECT.response --- |  (server accepts)
  |                                |
  |--- CONNECT.confirm ------->  |
  |                                |
  |--- DATA.request (data) -->  |
  |<-- DATA.indication (data) -  |
  |                                |
  |--- DISCONNECT.request ----> |
  |<-- DISCONNECT.indication --- |
  |                                |
  (Connection terminated)
```

The four-primitive naming (`.request / .indication / .response / .confirm`) provides a **vendor-neutral, technology-independent interface** between adjacent layers. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Module 1 Choice (Option 2)*

> [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Compare the **OSI and TCP/IP reference models** in detail. Why is the TCP/IP model considered the **de facto standard** for the Internet? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **encapsulation and decapsulation** process with a neat diagram. An application generates a message of $L = 1200$ bytes. It passes through a protocol stack with headers: TCP = 20 bytes, IP = 20 bytes, Ethernet = 14 bytes header + 4 bytes trailer. Calculate the **(i)** total frame size, **(ii)** total bits transmitted, **(iii)** encapsulation efficiency, and **(iv)** the wasted bytes. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Year of Origin | $1984$ (ISO 7498) | $1974$–$1983$ (DARPA) |
| Number of Layers | $7$ | $4$ |
| Layer Names (Top to Bottom) | Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical | Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access |
| Session and Presentation | Separate layers | Merged into the Application layer |
| Design Philosophy | **Generic reference** — protocol-independent | **Protocol-centric** — built around TCP and IP |
| Adoption | Teaching and reference; rarely fully implemented | **Practical** — powers the global Internet |
| Reliability Concern | Defined for both; but model is abstract | IP is "best-effort"; reliability is in TCP |

*(5 marks — well-labelled table)*

**Why TCP/IP dominates the Internet (2 marks):**
1. **Simplicity** — fewer layers → faster processing and easier troubleshooting.
2. **Proven protocols** — TCP and IP were battle-tested on the ARPANET before the OSI model was even finalized.
3. **Open standards** — freely available RFCs (e.g., RFC 791 for IPv4, RFC 793 for TCP).
4. **Vendor neutrality** — implemented by every OS (Linux, Windows, macOS, Android).
5. **Backward compatibility** — the stack has evolved gracefully (IPv4 → IPv6, HTTP/1.1 → HTTP/2 → HTTP/3) without breaking the model.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer adds its own **header** (and sometimes a **trailer**) to the data unit it receives from the layer above, as the data moves down the protocol stack. **Decapsulation** is the reverse process at the receiver.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
SENDER SIDE                       RECEIVER SIDE
+---------+                         +---------+
|  App    |  Message    Data        |  App    |  ← Strip App header
+---------+                         +---------+
|  TCP    |  Segment    20 + M      |  TCP    |  ← Strip TCP header
+---------+                         +---------+
|  IP     |  Packet     20 + S      |  IP     |  ← Strip IP header
+---------+                         +---------+
|  Eth    |  Frame      14 + P + 4  |  Eth    |  ← Strip Eth header + FCS
+---------+                         +---------+
|  Phys   |  Bits                   |  Phys   |  ← Receive signals
+---------+                         +---------+
      -------- physical medium --------
```

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $L = 1200$ bytes, $h_{tcp} = 20$, $h_{ip} = 20$, $h_{eth} = 14$, $t_{eth} = 4$.

**(i) Total frame size:**

$$
L_{frame} = L + h_{tcp} + h_{ip} + h_{eth} + t_{eth}
$$

$$
L_{frame} = 1200 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 1258 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) Total bits transmitted:**

$$
L_{bits} = 1258 \times 8 = 10064 \text{ bits}
$$

**(iii) Encapsulation efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{L}{L_{frame}} = \frac{1200}{1258} \approx 0.9539 = 95.39\%
$$

**(iv) Wasted bytes:**

$$
L_{waste} = L_{frame} - L = 1258 - 1200 = 58 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students often **forget the trailer** (FCS) in the Ethernet frame — that alone costs 1 mark. Always mention both header **and** trailer at the Data Link layer.
> 2. In efficiency calculations, the **denominator must be the total frame size**, not just the original message — many students incorrectly use $L$ in the denominator.
> 3. Do **not** confuse **bit** vs **byte** conversions. $1$ byte $= 8$ bits. A common slip is forgetting to multiply by $8$.
> 4. When listing PDU names, **Transport layer PDU is "Segment" only for TCP**; for UDP it is "Datagram". Examiners will deduct marks for generic "packet" answers at Layer 4.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Network Software: Key Takeaways**

- **Protocol Hierarchy:** A stack of layers, each with a defined protocol and clear service interface to adjacent layers.
- **Encapsulation vs Decapsulation:** Headers (and trailers) added as data moves down; stripped as data moves up.
- **5 Key Design Issues:** Addressing, Error Control, Flow Control, Sequencing, Connection Management (plus multiplexing, routing, congestion control).
- **Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless:** TCP-style (phone call) vs UDP-style (postal letter). Not all 4 combinations of (reliable, unreliable) × (connection-oriented, connectionless) are commonly used.
- **Service Primitives (4 types):** `.request`, `.indication`, `.response`, `.confirm`. Six primitives for a connection-oriented service: `LISTEN`, `CONNECT`, `ACCEPT`, `SEND`, `RECEIVE`, `DISCONNECT`.
- **OSI Model = 7 Layers:** Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical.
- **TCP/IP Model = 4 Layers:** Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access.
- **PDU Names:** Message (App) → Segment/Datagram (Transport) → Packet (Network) → Frame (Data Link) → Bits (Physical).
- **OSI vs TCP/IP:** OSI is the **reference** model; TCP/IP is the **implementation** model of the Internet. OSI separates Session & Presentation; TCP/IP merges them into the Application layer.
- **Header Sizes (typical):** TCP = 20 B, UDP = 8 B, IPv4 = 20–60 B, Ethernet = 14 B header + 4 B trailer.
- **Mnemonics:** "**A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing" (top-down) and "**P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way" (bottom-up).
- **Real-World Takeaway:** Whenever you "ping" a website, you trigger **all 7 OSI layers** in under a second — DNS at Layer 7, UDP/TCP at Layer 4, IP at Layer 3, Ethernet/Wi-Fi at Layers 1–2.
- **Critique of OSI:** Bad timing (competing with TCP/IP), poor technology choices (initially), complex session/presentation layers that were rarely implemented. **Critique of TCP/IP:** Not a true general model, omits session/presentation, but succeeded by **pragmatism, simplicity, and open RFCs**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** is a **seven-layered abstract conceptual framework** defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1** (originally published in 1984 as the X.200 recommendation from ITU-T). It standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into **abstraction layers**, where each layer serves the layer above it and is served by the layer below it. The model partitions the flow of data in a communication network into **seven distinct functional groups**, enabling heterogeneous systems to interoperate over diverse physical media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OSI model is *not* a protocol itself — it is a **reference model** that categorizes and standardizes the functions performed by a protocol suite. The actual implementation in modern networks is the **TCP/IP model** (4-layer), but the OSI 7-layer model remains the universal teaching and design reference for all KTU Board Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Service"

Imagine you want to send a **gift** from your home in **Kerala** to a friend in **Delhi**. You do not personally carry the parcel; instead, you go through a series of services:

| Real-World Actor | OSI Layer Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| You write a letter and pack the gift | Application + Presentation + Session | Prepare & format the content |
| Postal clerk adds a tracking slip | Transport | Adds delivery confirmation (port/segment ID) |
| Address with PIN code written on parcel | Network | Adds logical address (IP) |
| Local post office wraps a barcode pouch | Data Link | Adds MAC address, error check |
| Truck carries the parcel physically | Physical | Actual bit transmission on media |

Each layer does **one specific job** and **hands the parcel down** to the next. The friend on the receiving end does the **reverse process** (decapsulation). This is *exactly* how OSI works — **Sender Top-Down, Receiver Bottom-Up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **SAP (Service Access Point):** The logical interface between two adjacent layers.
> - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The formatted unit of data at any given layer.
> - **Primitive:** The four service operations — *Request, Indication, Response, Confirm*.

## 1.3 Why Layered Architecture?

1. **Modularity:** Each layer can be designed and updated independently.
2. **Abstraction:** A programmer writing an application need not worry about cable voltages.
3. **Interoperability:** Equipment from different vendors (Cisco, Juniper, Huawei) can communicate because they all implement the same layer functions.
4. **Easier Troubleshooting:** Network failures can be isolated to a specific layer.
5. **Standardization:** Acts as a common global vocabulary for engineers.

## 1.4 The Seven Layers — Mnemonic (Top-Down)

> **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing
> *(Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical)*

Reverse (Bottom-Up): **P**lease **D**o **N**ot **T**hrow **S**ausage **P**izza **A**way.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Functionality Breakdown

### Layer 7 — Application Layer
- **Closest to the end-user.** Provides network services to user applications.
- Protocols: **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, POP3, IMAP, Telnet, SSH**.
- Data Unit: **Data (Message)**.
- Function: Identification of communication partners, resource availability, synchronization.

### Layer 6 — Presentation Layer
- Concerned with **syntax and semantics** of the information exchanged.
- Performs **Data Translation** (e.g., EBCDIC ↔ ASCII), **Encryption/Decryption** (AES, DES), **Compression** (ZIP, JPEG), and **Formatting** (TIFF, GIF, MPEG).
- Data Unit: **Data**.

### Layer 5 — Session Layer
- Controls the **dialogues (sessions)** between two hosts. Establishes, manages, and terminates connections.
- Functions: **Dialog Control** (half-duplex/full-duplex), **Token Management**, **Synchronization** (checkpoints during long transfers).
- Protocols: **NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP**.
- Data Unit: **Data**.

### Layer 4 — Transport Layer
- Provides **end-to-end logical communication** between processes running on different hosts.
- Two main protocols: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol — connection-oriented, reliable)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol — connectionless, best-effort)**.
- Functions: **Segmentation & Reassembly, Port Addressing, Flow Control, Error Recovery, Multiplexing**.
- Data Unit: **Segment (TCP)** or **Datagram (UDP)**.

### Layer 3 — Network Layer
- Provides **end-to-end logical addressing and routing** across multiple networks.
- Protocols: **IP (IPv4, IPv6), ICMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP, ARP, IGMP, IPsec**.
- Devices: **Routers, Layer 3 Switches**.
- Data Unit: **Packet (Datagram)**.

### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer
- Provides **node-to-node reliable data transfer** on the same network segment.
- Sub-layers: **LLC (Logical Link Control — IEEE 802.2)** and **MAC (Media Access Control — IEEE 802.3/802.11)**.
- Functions: **Framing, MAC Addressing (48-bit), Error Detection/Correction (CRC), Flow Control**, **Media Access Control** (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA).
- Devices: **Switches, Bridges, NICs**.
- Data Unit: **Frame**.

### Layer 1 — Physical Layer
- Defines the **electrical, mechanical, procedural, and functional** specifications for activating/deactivating the physical link.
- Functions: **Bit Synchronization, Bit Rate Control, Physical Topology**, **Transmission Mode** (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex), **Signal Encoding** (NRZ, Manchester).
- Devices: **Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Connectors, Modems**.
- Data Unit: **Bits**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **PDU Naming Convention** | L7–L5 → *Data*; L4 → *Segment*; L3 → *Packet*; L2 → *Frame*; L1 → *Bits* | Names the data unit as it descends the stack |
| **Encapsulation Math** | $D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{orig}} + \sum_{i=1}^{7} H_i$ | Total bytes transmitted = original data + sum of all headers added at each layer |
| **Transmission Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{D_{\text{orig}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{D_{\text{orig}}}{D_{\text{orig}} + \sum H_i} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of bandwidth carrying **useful** payload |
| **Header Overhead Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Inverse of efficiency, expressed as % |
| **Best Case (No Upper Layer Data)** | $\eta \to 0$ as $D_{\text{orig}} \to 0$ | Tiny packets are dominated by header overhead (e.g., 40-byte TCP/IPv6 header vs. 1-byte payload) |
| **Worst Case (Tunneling)** | $\eta_{\text{compound}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \dots \times \eta_n$ | Multiplicative efficiency loss when multiple protocols encapsulate (e.g., IPsec + GRE + MPLS) |
| **IP Checksum (L3 header only)** | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i=1}^{N} 16\text{-bit words}}$ | One's complement of the one's complement sum of 16-bit header words |
| **CRC Polynomial Degree** | Generator of degree $n \Rightarrow$ frame of $D \cdot 2^n$ expanded data | Used in L2 to detect burst errors |
| **Throughput (Stop-and-Wait)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{frame}}}{T_{\text{frame}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{prop}}}$ | L4/L2 link utilization for ARQ protocols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Whenever a question mentions "explain the OSI model", examiners expect you to draw the **vertical 7-layer stack**, mention the **PDU name** at each layer, give **2 protocols per layer**, and identify **2 devices per layer**. Missing any one of these is the most common cause of 2–3 mark deductions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OSI model is the **diagnostic backbone** of the modern network industry:
- **Cisco CCNA/CCNP** certification uses the OSI model as the basis for every troubleshooting step (e.g., "ping fails at L3, trace route fails at L1").
- **Wireshark**, the world's most popular packet analyzer, organizes its display by OSI layer.
- **5G and IoT protocol stacks** (e.g., NB-IoT, LTE-M) explicitly map every protocol to an OSI layer.
- **Cybersecurity frameworks** (NIST, ISO 27001) classify attacks by OSI layer (e.g., DDoS = L3/L4, SQL injection = L7).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section converts the conceptual OSI flow into a **rigorous, step-by-step mathematical and logical exposition** suitable for KTU 14-mark analytical answers.

## 3.1 Encapsulation — Sender-Side Mathematical Walkthrough

**Problem Setup:** Suppose an application generates a **message of size $D$ bytes**. This message must traverse all 7 OSI layers to reach the physical medium. Each layer $i$ (where $i$ counts downward from 7 to 1) appends a **header $H_i$ bytes**, and the Data Link layer additionally appends a **trailer $T_2$ bytes** (e.g., CRC).

**Step 1 — Application Layer (L7):**
The user application prepares a message of size $D$ bytes. No header is added.
$$P_7 = D$$

**Step 2 — Presentation Layer (L6):**
Performs optional encryption/compression. If encryption expands the data by factor $k$ (with $k \geq 1$), then the new payload becomes:
$$P_6 = k \cdot P_7 + H_6$$
In the simplest case (no encryption), $k = 1$, so $P_6 = D + H_6$.

**Step 3 — Session Layer (L5):**
Adds session control information (e.g., session token):
$$P_5 = P_6 + H_5 = D + H_6 + H_5$$

**Step 4 — Transport Layer (L4):**
**Segments** the data. If $D$ exceeds the Maximum Segment Size ($MSS$), the transport layer splits it into $n$ segments. For simplicity, assume $D \leq MSS$ (one segment). Then:
$$P_4 = P_5 + H_4 = D + H_6 + H_5 + H_4$$
Typical values: $H_4 = 20$ bytes (TCP) or $H_4 = 8$ bytes (UDP).

**Step 5 — Network Layer (L3):**
Adds the IP header. For IPv4: $H_3 = 20$ bytes (min) to 60 bytes (max). For IPv6: $H_3 = 40$ bytes (fixed).
$$P_3 = P_4 + H_3 = D + H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3$$

**Step 6 — Data Link Layer (L2):**
Adds the MAC header **AND** the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) trailer:
$$P_2 = P_3 + H_2 + T_2 = D + H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2$$
Typical Ethernet values: $H_2 = 14$ bytes, $T_2 = 4$ bytes (CRC-32).

**Step 7 — Physical Layer (L1):**
Converts the frame into a stream of bits. **No header added** (no PDU at L1, only a bit stream):
$$P_1 = 8 \cdot P_2 = 8 \cdot (D + H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2) \text{ bits}$$

### 3.1.1 General Encapsulation Formula

$$\boxed{D_{\text{total}} = D + \sum_{i=2}^{6} H_i + T_2}$$

Equivalently, the **bit stream** transmitted by the physical layer is:
$$N_{\text{bits}} = 8 \left( D + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5 + H_6 + T_2 \right)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Highly Probable in KTU)

**Given:**
- Application data $D = 1200$ bytes
- HTTP header $H_7 = 0$ (application layer, no separate header)
- TLS record header $H_6 = 5$ bytes
- Session token $H_5 = 4$ bytes
- TCP header $H_4 = 20$ bytes (no options)
- IPv4 header $H_3 = 20$ bytes
- Ethernet header $H_2 = 14$ bytes
- Ethernet trailer (CRC) $T_2 = 4$ bytes

**Find:** (a) Total bytes transmitted, (b) Transmission efficiency.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute total header + trailer overhead:**
$$H_{\text{total}} = H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2$$
$$H_{\text{total}} = 5 + 4 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 67 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total bytes on the wire:**
$$D_{\text{total}} = D + H_{\text{total}} = 1200 + 67 = 1267 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{D}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{1200}{1267} \times 100\%$$
$$\eta \approx 94.71\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute header overhead ratio:**
$$\rho = \frac{67}{1267} \times 100\% \approx 5.29\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of bits on the wire:**
$$N_{\text{bits}} = 8 \times 1267 = 10136 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** For **large $D$**, efficiency $\eta \to 100\%$. For **small $D$** (e.g., a 1-byte ping), $\eta$ can drop below 50% due to fixed header overhead. This is why **Nagle's algorithm** and **header compression (ROHC, VJHC)** are used in real networks to coalesce small packets.

## 3.3 Decapsulation — Receiver-Side Reverse Process

The receiver executes the **inverse operations** at each layer:

$$\text{Step 1: L1} \rightarrow \text{Bits} \xrightarrow{\text{decode}} \text{Frame (L2)}$$
$$\text{Step 2: L2} \rightarrow \text{Verify CRC using } T_2; \text{ if fail, drop frame}$$
$$\text{Step 3: L3} \rightarrow \text{Inspect IP header; decrement TTL; route if needed}$$
$$\text{Step 4: L4} \rightarrow \text{Reassemble segments using sequence numbers; deliver to socket via port}$$
$$\text{Step 5: L5} \rightarrow \text{Validate session token; manage dialog state}$$
$$\text{Step 6: L6} \rightarrow \text{Decrypt / decompress / convert encoding}$$
$$\text{Step 7: L7} \rightarrow \text{Deliver plaintext data to the application}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PDU vs. Layer vs. Addressing Scheme

| Layer | PDU Name | Addressing Used | Header Size (Typical) | Example Device / Protocol |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 7 — Application | Data / Message | None (uses URL/URI) | 0 (per-layer) | HTTP, DNS, SMTP |
| 6 — Presentation | Data | None | 0 (per-layer) | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG |
| 5 — Session | Data | None | 0 (per-layer) | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| 4 — Transport | Segment / Datagram | **Port Number** (16-bit, 0–65535) | 20 B (TCP), 8 B (UDP) | TCP, UDP, SCTP |
| 3 — Network | Packet / Datagram | **IP Address** (32-bit IPv4, 128-bit IPv6) | 20–60 B (IPv4), 40 B (IPv6) | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP |
| 2 — Data Link | Frame | **MAC Address** (48-bit) | 14 B (Ethernet) + 4 B (CRC) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |
| 1 — Physical | Bits | None | 0 | Hub, Repeater, Cable |

## 3.5 The "Why" Behind Each Address Type

- **Physical address (MAC, L2):** Required because the signal must reach a specific NIC on the local segment (LAN). MAC is **burned into hardware** by the manufacturer (OUI + serial).
- **Logical address (IP, L3):** Required because the packet may traverse **many different physical networks**. IP is **assigned administratively** and supports **hierarchical routing**.
- **Port address (L4):** Required because a single host runs **many applications simultaneously**. Port distinguishes which application (web browser, email client) should receive the data.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Vertical 7-Layer OSI Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION\nHTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SNMP"]
    B["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION\nSSL, TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII"]
    C["LAYER 5 - SESSION\nNetBIOS, RPC, SIP, PPTP"]
    D["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT\nTCP, UDP - PORT BASED"]
    E["LAYER 3 - NETWORK\nIP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP - IP BASED"]
    F["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK\nEthernet, Wi-Fi, PPP - MAC BASED"]
    G["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL\nCables, Hubs, Bits, Voltages"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Data Encapsulation Flow (Sender → Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER
    direction TB
    S7["L7: HTTP GET Request\nData D"]
    S6["L6: TLS Encrypted\nData D + H6"]
    S5["L5: Session Token Added\nData D + H6 + H5"]
    S4["L4: TCP Header Added\nSegment D + H6 + H5 + H4"]
    S3["L3: IP Header Added\nPacket D + H6 + H5 + H4 + H3"]
    S2["L2: MAC Header + CRC Trailer\nFrame D + H6 + H5 + H4 + H3 + H2 + T2"]
    S1["L1: Encoded as Bits\nN bits on the wire"]
    end
    S7 --> S6 --> S5 --> S4 --> S3 --> S2 --> S1
    S1 -.BIT STREAM.-> R1
    subgraph RECEIVER
    direction TB
    R1["L1: Bits Received and Decoded"]
    R2["L2: CRC Check - MAC Stripped"]
    R3["L3: IP Decremented - IP Stripped"]
    R4["L4: TCP Reassembled - Segment Stripped"]
    R5["L5: Session Validated"]
    R6["L6: TLS Decrypted"]
    R7["L7: Data Delivered to Web Browser"]
    end
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

    end

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the OSI Reference Model. List all its seven layers in order from top to bottom.**

**Model Answer:**
The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model is a **seven-layer abstract framework** standardized by **ISO (ISO/IEC 7498-1)** to enable interoperability between heterogeneous computer systems. It defines communication functions in a hierarchical, modular manner.

The seven layers from top to bottom are:
1. **Application Layer**
2. **Presentation Layer**
3. **Session Layer**
4. **Transport Layer**
5. **Network Layer**
6. **Data Link Layer**
7. **Physical Layer**

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Award **1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for mention of ISO/IEC 7498-1, and **1 mark** for correctly listing all 7 layers in order. Even one transposition (e.g., Network before Transport) costs 1 mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is meant by "encapsulation" in the OSI model? Identify the Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the Transport, Network, and Data Link layers.**

**Model Answer:**
**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer in the sender's protocol stack adds its own control information (header, and sometimes trailer) to the data received from the layer above before passing it to the layer below.

| Layer | PDU Name |
|---|---|
| Transport (L4) | **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP) |
| Network (L3) | **Packet** (or Datagram) |
| Data Link (L2) | **Frame** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Definition = 1 mark. PDU table = 1.5 marks. Mention of "header at each layer" = 0.5 marks.

---

### Q3. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Distinguish between Connection-Oriented and Connectionless services in the OSI model. Give one example protocol for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Setup | Requires 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | No prior setup |
| Reliability | Acknowledged, retransmitted | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Ordering | Guaranteed sequence | No order guarantee |
| Overhead | High | Low |
| Example | **TCP** (Layer 4) | **UDP** (Layer 4), **IP** (Layer 3) |
| Use Case | File transfer, email, web | Live video, DNS, VoIP |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and **must include an internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **OSI Reference Model** with a neat labelled diagram. List the **functions** of each of the seven layers. **    (7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **data encapsulation and decapsulation** processes in the OSI model with a suitable example showing the headers added at each layer. **    (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a 7-layer conceptual framework defined by **ISO/IEC 7498-1** that standardizes how data moves through a network. The seven layers, their PDUs, functions, and example protocols are tabulated below.

| Layer | PDU | Function | Protocol Examples |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 7. Application | Data | Interface to user / network services | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP |
| 6. Presentation | Data | Encoding, encryption, compression | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG |
| 5. Session | Data | Establishes, manages, ends sessions | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| 4. Transport | Segment / Datagram | End-to-end reliability, port addressing, flow control | TCP, UDP |
| 3. Network | Packet | Logical addressing, routing across networks | IP, ICMP, OSPF |
| 2. Data Link | Frame | MAC addressing, error detection, framing | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |
| 1. Physical | Bits | Electrical/mechanical transmission of raw bits | Cables, Hubs, Repeaters |

*(Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.1 for the labelled vertical stack.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 7-Mark Subpart:** Students frequently lose **1.5 marks** by failing to mention **PDU name at each layer** and another **1 mark** by omitting **2 example protocols per layer**. Always include both in the answer.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Encapsulation** (Sender side, top-down):

| Step | Layer | Action | Data Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application | Original message $D$ generated | Data ($D$) |
| 2 | Presentation | Optional encryption/compression (adds $H_6$) | Encrypted Data |
| 3 | Session | Adds session control ($H_5$) | Session Data |
| 4 | Transport | Adds TCP/UDP header ($H_4$); segments if large | Segment |
| 5 | Network | Adds IP header ($H_3$) | Packet |
| 6 | Data Link | Adds MAC header ($H_2$) and CRC trailer ($T_2$) | Frame |
| 7 | Physical | Converts frame to bits; transmits on medium | Bits |

Total transmitted:
$$D_{\text{total}} = D + H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2$$

**Decapsulation** (Receiver side, bottom-up): The receiver reverses the process at each layer — Physical extracts bits, Data Link verifies CRC and strips MAC, Network decrements TTL and strips IP, Transport reassembles segments, Session validates tokens, Presentation decrypts, Application delivers the message to the user.

*(Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.2 for the visual end-to-end flow.)*

**Example (HTTP over TCP/IP over Ethernet):**
- Application data $D = 1000$ B
- TLS record header $H_6 = 5$ B
- TCP header $H_4 = 20$ B
- IPv4 header $H_3 = 20$ B
- Ethernet header $H_2 = 14$ B, CRC trailer $T_2 = 4$ B
- Total: $1000 + 5 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 1063$ bytes on the wire.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 7-Mark Subpart:** Many students describe encapsulation but **forget the trailer at L2 (CRC)**. This costs 1 mark. Also, decapsulation must mention **CRC verification at L2** as the first step.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
