$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Shannon's capacity (39.87 kbps) is the *theoretical upper bound* and is **always higher** than Nyquist's noiseless capacity (8 kbps) for the same bandwidth when SNR is large. Shannon's formula tells us the *theoretical maximum*; the actual link uses coding schemes that approach this bound.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Media

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Source: Digital Data / Bits] --> B{Physical Medium Selection}
    B -->|Short distance, low cost| C[Twisted Pair Cable]
    B -->|Medium distance, better shielding| D[Coaxial Cable]
    B -->|Long distance, high bandwidth| E[Fiber Optic Cable]
    C --> C1[UTP - Unshielded]
    C --> C2[STP - Shielded]
    D --> D1[Baseband 50 ohm]
    D --> D2[Broadband 75 ohm]
    E --> E1[Multimode Fiber]
    E --> E2[Single Mode Fiber]
    E1 --> E1a[Step Index]
    E1 --> E1b[Graded Index]
    C1 --> F[Destination: Receiver]
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1a --> F
    E1b --> F
    E2 --> F
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Anatomy Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TP[Twisted Pair Cable - Cross Section]
        TP1[Insulated Copper Conductor 1] --- TP2[Insulated Copper Conductor 2]
        TP2 --- TP3[Twist along length]
    end
    subgraph CX[Coaxial Cable - Cross Section]
        CX1[Inner Copper Core] --> CX2[Dielectric Insulator]
        CX2 --> CX3[Metallic Shield - Braid or Foil]
        CX3 --> CX4[Outer PVC Jacket]
    end
    subgraph FO[Fiber Optic Cable - Cross Section]
        FO1[Glass Core - 8 to 50 micrometer] --> FO2[Glass Cladding - Lower n]
        FO2 --> FO3[Buffer Coating]
        FO3 --> FO4[Strength Member - Kevlar]
        FO4 --> FO5[Outer Jacket]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Medium to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Need to connect two nodes] --> Q1{Distance greater than 100 m?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need shielding against EMI?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{More than 1 km?}
    Q2 -->|No| UTP[Use UTP Cat 5e or Cat 6]
    Q2 -->|Yes| STP[Use STP or Coaxial]
    Q3 -->|No| COAX[Use Coaxial Cable]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Bandwidth above 1 Gbps?}
    Q4 -->|No| MMF[Use Multimode Fiber]
    Q4 -->|Yes| SMF[Use Single Mode Fiber with LASER]
    UTP --> End[End of Selection]
    STP --> End
    COAX --> End
    MMF --> End
    SMF --> End
```

### 4.4 Total Internal Reflection — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source: LED or LASER] --> B[Couples into Fiber Core]
    B --> C{Angle of Incidence at Core-Cladding Boundary}
    C -->|Greater than Critical Angle| D[Total Internal Reflection - Light Guided]
    C -->|Less than Critical Angle| E[Light Refracts Out - Lost in Cladding]
    D --> F[Light Propagates Down Core]
    F --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between guided and unguided transmission media. State one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Guided Media | Unguided Media |
|---|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | Signal travels along a *bounded, physical pathway* (cable). | Signal propagates *freely through space* (no physical path). |
| 2 | Provides high security and is less affected by weather. | Susceptible to atmospheric interference and eavesdropping. |
| 3 | Installation cost is higher due to cabling infrastructure. | Lower deployment cost; only antennas needed. |
| 4 | **Example:** Twisted Pair Cable. | **Example:** Microwave link. |

*(3 marks – one mark for each meaningful difference + example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Total Internal Reflection. State the two conditions required for TIR to occur.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Total Internal Reflection (TIR) is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray, travelling from a denser medium to a rarer medium, is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the interface, with **zero transmission** into the rarer medium.

**Conditions for TIR:**
1. Light must travel from a **denser medium to a rarer medium** (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The **angle of incidence must exceed the critical angle** $\theta_c$ of the pair of media.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and working of an **optical fiber cable**. Discuss the principle of **total internal reflection** and derive the expression for the **numerical aperture**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fiber has core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate:
   (i) the **critical angle** at the core-cladding boundary,
   (ii) the **numerical aperture**, and
   (iii) the **acceptance angle** in air. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working:**
Refer to the cross-section described in Section 2.3. An optical fiber has three concentric layers — core, cladding, and protective buffer/jacket. Light pulses from an **LED (for multimode)** or **LASER (for single-mode)** are coupled into the core. Because the core has a *higher* refractive index than the cladding, light striking the boundary at an angle greater than $\theta_c$ undergoes **Total Internal Reflection** and is repeatedly reflected down the length of the fiber with negligible loss.

**Derivation of Numerical Aperture** (write the full derivation as in Section 3.3, ending with):

$$
\boxed{\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**(i) Critical angle:**

$$
\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} = 0.9667
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9667) \approx 75.16°
$$

**(ii) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}
$$

$$
\text{NA} = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.384
$$

**(iii) Acceptance angle in air:**

The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ is related to NA by $\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a$ (since air has $n_0 \approx 1$):

$$
\sin\theta_a = 0.384
$$

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.384) \approx 22.6°
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the construction of **UTP** and **STP** twisted pair cables. Why are the wires twisted? Discuss the categories (Cat 3, Cat 5, Cat 6) and their typical bandwidth applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A communication channel has a bandwidth of **8 kHz** and a signal-to-noise ratio of **40 dB**.
   (i) Calculate the **maximum theoretical data rate** using the **Shannon-Hartley theorem**.
   (ii) If the channel uses **4 signal levels**, what is the **data rate** using **Nyquist's criterion**?
   (iii) Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Provide the construction details from Section 2.1 — UTP has no shield; STP has a metallic foil/braid around each pair. The twist serves to cancel **common-mode noise** (EMI) and reduce **crosstalk** between adjacent pairs. For category table, refer to the table in Section 2.1. Cat 3 → 16 MHz; Cat 5 → 100 MHz; Cat 6 → 250 MHz.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Shannon Capacity:**
Convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = 8000 \times \log_2(1 + 10{,}000)
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} = 8000 \times \log_2(10{,}001)
$$

$$
\log_2(10{,}001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10{,}001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{4.00004}{0.3010} \approx 13.288
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 8000 \times 13.288 \approx 106{,}304 \text{ bps} \approx 106.3 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist Capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 V = 2 \times 8000 \times \log_2 4
$$

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 16{,}000 \times 2 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iii) Comment:** The Shannon limit (106.3 kbps) is the absolute theoretical maximum governed by noise; Nyquist gives the *achievable* rate when we constrain the number of discrete signal levels to 4. The Shannon capacity is **higher** because it assumes an *optimal* coding scheme that exploits the channel's noise profile, whereas Nyquist assumes an *ideal noiseless* channel with V levels. **In practice, no real system achieves Shannon capacity** — it is an asymptotic upper bound.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong unit in Attenuation formula:** Students frequently write $\log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$ — the formula is **$\log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$**. Getting this inverted costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Missing the condition $n_1 > n_2$:** When asked to "state the conditions for TIR", students often give only the angle condition and forget to mention the *refractive index inequality*. The examiner **always** checks this.
> 3. **Forgetting the cosine identity in NA derivation:** Jumping from $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ directly to NA without showing $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c}$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Unit confusion in SNR:** Writing SNR as a *linear ratio* when the problem gives *dB* (or vice versa). Always convert dB → linear first: $S/N = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
> 5. **Conflating multimode and single-mode fiber distances:** Students say "fiber works for 100 km" without distinguishing single-mode (100+ km) from multimode (≤ 2 km). The examiner expects this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Section Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Physical Layer** is the *bit-transport* layer; it does not understand frames or packets.
- [ ] **Guided media** = Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber Optic.
- [ ] **Twisting** in TPC cancels EMI and crosstalk — never describe it as cosmetic.
- [ ] **UTP vs STP:** UTP = no shield (cheaper, LANs); STP = foil/braid (better EMI, expensive).
- [ ] **Categories of UTP:** Cat 3 → 16 MHz, Cat 5 → 100 MHz, Cat 5e → 100 MHz (1 Gbps), Cat 6 → 250 MHz, Cat 7/8 → 600+ MHz.
- [ ] **Coaxial cable** has a central conductor + dielectric + outer shield — used for cable TV (75 Ω) and legacy Ethernet (50 Ω).
- [ ] **Fiber optic cable** uses **light pulses** guided by **Total Internal Reflection**.
- [ ] **TIR conditions:** (1) $n_1 > n_2$ and (2) angle of incidence $> \theta_c$.
- [ ] **Critical angle formula:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$.
- [ ] **Numerical Aperture (NA)** = $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ — measures light-gathering ability.
- [ ] **Multimode fiber:** larger core (50/62.5 µm), LED source, short distance (≤ 2 km).
- [ ] **Single-mode fiber:** small core (8–10 µm), LASER source, long distance (≥ 100 km).
- [ ] **Attenuation (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$.
- [ ] **Attenuation coefficient** = Total dB loss ÷ distance in km.
- [ ] **SNR (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. To convert dB to linear: $S/N = 10^{\text{dB}/10}$.
- [ ] **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 V$.
- [ ] **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the *true* theoretical maximum.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity > Nyquist capacity** when $S/N$ is large.
- [ ] **Speed of light in medium:** $v = c/n$.
- [ ] **Connectors to remember:** RJ-45 (UTP), BNC (coax), SC/LC/ST (fiber).
- [ ] **Fiber is immune to EMI** — repeat this in every comparative question.
- [ ] **Attenuation ranking (lowest to highest loss):** Fiber < Coax < Twisted Pair.
- [ ] **Bandwidth ranking (lowest to highest):** Twisted Pair < Coax < Fiber.
- [ ] **Always show Snell's Law** in any TIR or NA derivation — examiners expect it.
- [ ] **Practical Twisted Pair max distance:** 100 m (Ethernet limit).
- [ ] **Coaxial still used for:** Cable TV, broadband internet, CCTV, undersea telecom (legacy).
- [ ] **Fiber used for:** Backbone networks, undersea cables (99% of international internet traffic), FTTH (Fibre-to-the-Home), data centers.

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Physical Layer: Guided Transmission Media.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Transmission (Radio Transmission, Microwave Transmission, Infrared Transmission)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Transmission (Radio Transmission, Microwave Transmission, Infrared Transmission)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wireless Transmission — Formal Definition

**Wireless Transmission** is the method of conveying information (voice, data, video) from a source to a destination through the air or free space using **electromagnetic (EM) waves** as the carrier medium, without requiring a physical, wired conductor (such as copper or optical fiber).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Wireless transmission utilizes the **electromagnetic spectrum**, where signals are modulated onto carrier waves whose frequency and wavelength are governed by the universal relation $c = f\lambda$. The three principal wireless modalities studied under Module 1 — **Radio**, **Microwave**, and **Infrared** — differ in their operating frequency bands, propagation behaviour, and application domains.

The governing physical constant is the **speed of light in free space**:

$$c \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistle, Flashlight, and Laser Pointer" Model

Imagine three ways to attract a friend's attention across a noisy playground:

| Analogy | Real Counterpart | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Whistle** — sound spreads in **all directions** (omnidirectional), reaches a wide area, but anyone can hear it | **Radio Transmission** | Omnidirectional, long range, penetrates walls |
| **Flashlight** — focused beam aimed at a specific point, requires **line-of-sight** | **Microwave Transmission** | Directional, line-of-sight, used for point-to-point links |
| **Laser Pointer** — extremely short-range, blocked by walls, confined to a single room | **Infrared Transmission** | Very short range, does not penetrate solid objects, secure by default |

> [!NOTE]
> The **higher the frequency**, the **shorter the wavelength**, the **more directional** the wave becomes, and the **less it can penetrate obstacles**. This single rule unifies the behaviour of all three transmission types.

## 1.3 The Electromagnetic Spectrum — Context for Wireless Transmission

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Transmission Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| VLF (Very Low Frequency) | 3 kHz – 30 kHz | 100 km – 10 km | Submarine Radio |
| LF / MF (AM Broadcast) | 30 kHz – 3 MHz | 10 km – 100 m | AM Radio |
| HF (Shortwave) | 3 MHz – 30 MHz | 100 m – 10 m | Shortwave Radio |
| VHF (TV, FM) | 30 MHz – 300 MHz | 10 m – 1 m | VHF Radio, FM |
| UHF (TV, Mobile, Wi-Fi) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 m – 10 cm | **Radio / Microwave boundary** |
| SHF (Satellite, Radar, Wi-Fi) | 3 GHz – 30 GHz | 10 cm – 1 cm | **Microwave** |
| EHF (mmWave, 5G) | 30 GHz – 300 GHz | 10 mm – 1 mm | Microwave / mmWave |
| Infrared | 300 GHz – 400 THz | 1 mm – 750 nm | **Infrared Transmission** |
| Visible Light | 400 THz – 750 THz | 750 nm – 400 nm | Optical (Li-Fi) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The boundaries between *Radio*, *Microwave*, and *Infrared* are not absolute physical laws but engineering conventions. In KTU examinations, treat **Radio** as the **< 1 GHz** band, **Microwave** as the **1 GHz – 300 GHz** band, and **Infrared** as the **300 GHz – 400 THz** band.

## 1.4 Why Three Different Wireless Modalities?

Different applications demand different trade-offs among:

- **Range** (how far the signal travels)
- **Bandwidth** (how much data can be carried)
- **Penetration** (ability to pass through walls/obstacles)
- **Directionality** (point-to-point vs. broadcast)
- **Security** (ease of eavesdropping)
- **Licensing** (spectrum regulation)

The choice of Radio, Microwave, or Infrared is determined by optimizing these trade-offs for a given engineering use case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics — Wave Parameters

Every electromagnetic wave is characterized by:

- **Frequency ($f$)** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, indicates oscillations per second.
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** — measured in **metres (m)**, the spatial distance of one cycle.
- **Amplitude ($A$)** — the peak strength of the electric/magnetic field.
- **Phase ($\phi$)** — the position within the wave cycle.
- **Speed ($c$)** — for EM waves in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

The **master relation** that ties frequency and wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

This single equation explains *everything*: as frequency **increases**, wavelength **decreases**, and shorter wavelengths behave more like particles (rays), explaining why Infrared behaves like light.

## 2.2 Radio Transmission

### Operational Characteristics

- **Frequency Band:** typically **3 kHz – 1 GHz** (VLF to UHF).
- **Propagation:** **Omnidirectional** — waves radiate in all directions from the antenna.
- **Antenna Size:** physically larger antennas required at lower frequencies (e.g., a quarter-wave antenna at 1 MHz is **75 m long**).
- **Penetration:** excellent — can pass through walls, buildings, and even the human body to some extent.
- **Range:** can span continents (via ionospheric reflection) or remain confined to a few metres (Bluetooth at 2.4 GHz is *technically* still a radio frequency).

### Operational Logic (How a Radio Link Works)

1. The transmitter applies the baseband signal (data) onto a carrier wave via **modulation** (AM, FM, PM, QAM, etc.).
2. The antenna, sized to $\lambda/4$ (quarter-wavelength), radiates the modulated EM wave.
3. The wave propagates through three primary mechanisms: **ground wave**, **sky wave (ionospheric)**, and **line-of-sight**.
4. The receiver antenna intercepts the wave; the demodulator extracts the original baseband signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ground Wave** — hugs the Earth's surface, used for AM broadcasting (up to ~2 MHz).
> **Sky Wave** — reflects off the ionosphere, used for shortwave / international broadcast (3–30 MHz).
> **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** — direct propagation, used at VHF/UHF (> 30 MHz).

### Path Loss (Free-Space Model)

For radio waves, signal strength degrades with the **square of the distance** in free space. The **Friis Free-Space Path Loss** is given by:

$$FSPL = \left(\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4 \pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}$$

Expressed in decibels:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

where $d$ is in **kilometres (km)** and $f$ is in **megahertz (MHz)**.

### Applications of Radio Transmission

| Application | Typical Frequency |
|---|---|
| AM Radio Broadcast | 535 kHz – 1605 kHz |
| FM Radio Broadcast | 88 MHz – 108 MHz |
| VHF Television | 54 MHz – 216 MHz |
| Cellular Mobile (4G LTE) | 700 MHz – 2.6 GHz |
| Wi-Fi (legacy) | 2.4 GHz |
| Bluetooth | 2.4 GHz ISM band |
| Marine / Aviation Communication | 118 MHz – 137 MHz |

### Limitations of Radio Transmission

- **Congestion and interference** in the lower bands.
- **Regulatory licensing** required for most bands.
- **Security weakness** — omnidirectional pattern is easy to intercept.
- **Limited bandwidth** at lower frequencies.

## 2.3 Microwave Transmission

### Operational Characteristics

- **Frequency Band:** **1 GHz – 300 GHz** (effectively L-band through EHF/mmWave).
- **Propagation:** **Directional / Line-of-Sight (LOS)** — parabolic dish antennas focus the energy into a narrow beam.
- **Antenna Size:** much smaller than radio (e.g., 15 cm dish for 2 GHz).
- **Penetration:** poor — blocked by solid objects; absorbed by rain, fog, and atmospheric gases (especially oxygen and water vapour at specific resonant frequencies like **60 GHz**).
- **Range:** up to ~50 km terrestrial, 36,000 km via satellite.

### Operational Logic (How a Microwave Link Works)

1. The transmitter generates a high-frequency carrier (typically **2 GHz, 6 GHz, 11 GHz, 18 GHz, 23 GHz** for terrestrial links).
2. A **parabolic dish antenna** focuses the wave into a narrow pencil-beam (typically 1°–3° beamwidth).
3. The beam travels in a straight line to the receiver dish — **no reflection, no bending**.
4. For longer distances, **repeaters** are placed every 30–50 km.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earth's Curvature Problem:** Because microwave requires true LOS, the Earth's curvature limits the maximum range. For a link of distance $d$ (km), the required antenna height $h$ (metres) is approximately:
> $$h \approx \frac{d^{2}}{8R}$$
> where $R \approx 6371$ km is the Earth's radius. This is why **tall towers** are essential for long terrestrial microwave links.

### Free-Space Loss for Microwave (Friis Equation)

The **Friis Transmission Equation** gives the **received power** in a microwave link:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}$$

where:
- $P_r$ = received power
- $P_t$ = transmitted power
- $G_t$, $G_r$ = transmitter and receiver antenna gains (linear, not dB)
- $d$ = distance between antennas
- $\lambda$ = wavelength

### Atmospheric Absorption Peaks

Microwaves suffer severe attenuation due to molecular absorption:

| Frequency | Cause |
|---|---|
| 22 GHz | Water vapour ($H_2O$) peak |
| 60 GHz | Oxygen ($O_2$) peak |
| 180 GHz | Another $H_2O$ peak |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** At **60 GHz**, attenuation is so severe (~15 dB/km) that signals can only travel a few hundred metres — useful for **short-range, secure, frequency-reuse-dense** 5G and WiGig applications.

### Applications of Microwave Transmission

| Application | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Terrestrial Point-to-Point Links | 2 – 23 GHz |
| Satellite Communication (Uplink) | 6 GHz (C-band), 14 GHz (Ku-band), 30 GHz (Ka-band) |
| Radar Systems | 1 – 40 GHz |
| Wi-Fi (802.11a/n/ac/ax) | 5 GHz |
| 5G mmWave | 24 – 52 GHz |
| WiGig (802.11ad/ay) | 60 GHz |

### Advantages and Limitations of Microwave

**Advantages**
- High bandwidth — supports multi-Mbps to multi-Gbps links.
- No cables required.
- Relatively cheap to deploy compared to fibre.

**Limitations**
- Requires precise alignment (parabolic dishes).
- Affected by **multipath fading** and **rain fade** (especially above 10 GHz).
- Cannot bend around obstacles — strict LOS.

## 2.4 Infrared Transmission

### Operational Characteristics

- **Frequency Band:** **300 GHz – 400 THz** (wavelengths from 1 mm down to 750 nm).
- **Propagation:** **Line-of-Sight**, extremely short range (a few metres).
- **Antenna:** none — uses **LEDs** (transmitter) and **photodiodes** (receiver), or **laser diodes** for narrow beams.
- **Penetration:** **none** — blocked by walls, ceilings, and even dense smoke.
- **Range:** typically < 10 m.

### Operational Logic (How an Infrared Link Works)

1. An **LED or laser diode** emits a modulated infrared carrier wave.
2. The receiver, equipped with a **photodiode** and optical filter, detects the infrared light.
3. The signal is converted back to electrical form for demodulation.
4. Because infrared cannot pass through walls, **each room forms its own cell** — an inherent security feature.

### Two Modes of Infrared Operation

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Directed (Point-to-Point)** | A focused beam aimed directly at the receiver; used for remote controls, IrDA. |
| **Diffuse (Omnidirectional)** | Reflected off ceilings and walls; used for in-room wireless LANs (e.g., IrDA-based systems). |

### Applications of Infrared Transmission

- **TV Remote Controls** — most consumer-grade IR devices operate around **38 kHz modulated carrier** with a 940 nm wavelength LED.
- **IrDA (Infrared Data Association)** — short-range data transfer (1 – 4 Mbps up to 1 m).
- **Indoor Wireless LANs** — diffuse IR for room-scale coverage.
- **Optical Fibre Communication** — although fibre is *guided*, the underlying carrier frequencies fall in the near-infrared range (~ 850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm).

### Advantages and Limitations of Infrared

**Advantages**
- **No licensing required** — spectrum is unregulated globally.
- **Immune to electromagnetic interference** (EMI/RFI) — does not interfere with radio equipment.
- **Secure by default** — cannot pass through walls.
- **Cheap** — LEDs and photodiodes are inexpensive.

**Limitations**
- **Very short range**.
- **Ineffective outdoors** due to solar infrared radiation (sunlight acts as noise).
- **Requires LOS** (in directed mode) or relies on reflections (in diffuse mode).
- **Low bandwidth** compared to microwave and radio.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table contains every formula required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Wireless Transmission. Master these to secure full marks.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency | m |
| 2 | $c = f \lambda$ | Speed of light relation | m/s |
| 3 | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | Free-Space Path Loss (linear) | ratio (unitless) |
| 4 | $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | FSPL in decibels (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 5 | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Friis Transmission Equation (received power) | W |
| 6 | $P_r|_{dBm} = P_t|_{dBm} + G_t|_{dBi} + G_r|_{dBi} - FSPL_{dB}$ | Link budget equation | dBm |
| 7 | $A_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB | dB |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $C = B\log_{2}\!\left(1 + SNR\right)$ | Shannon Channel Capacity | bits/s |
| 10 | $h \approx \dfrac{d^{2}}{8R}$ | Antenna height for LOS | m |
| 11 | $E = hf$ | Photon energy (for IR/light) | J |
| 12 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_{e}}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Antenna gain from effective aperture | linear |
| 13 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Time period of one cycle | s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G Networks** use a combination of **sub-6 GHz radio** for coverage and **mmWave microwave (24–52 GHz)** for capacity — understanding both is essential for next-generation network design.
- **Satellite Internet (Starlink, VSAT)** relies on **Ku-band (12–18 GHz)** and **Ka-band (26–40 GHz)** microwave uplinks/downlinks — Friis equation governs the link budget.
- **IoT & Smart Homes** use **sub-GHz radio (LoRa, Zigbee at 868/915 MHz)** for long-range, low-power telemetry.
- **Li-Fi** research extends IR principles into visible light communication (VLC).
- **Medical telemetry** inside hospitals often uses infrared to avoid EMI with sensitive equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed of Light Relation from Maxwell's Equations

**Given:**
- Maxwell's equation for a plane EM wave in free space:

$$\nabla^{2} \mathbf{E} = \mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\frac{\partial^{2} \mathbf{E}}{\partial t^{2}}$$

**To derive:** $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}} = f\lambda$

**Step 1:** Assume a sinusoidal plane wave solution of the form:

$$E(z,t) = E_{0}\cos(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the wave equation. The second spatial derivative gives:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} E}{\partial z^{2}} = -k^{2}E$$

The second time derivative gives:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} E}{\partial t^{2}} = -\omega^{2}E$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions:

$$-k^{2}E = \mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}(-\omega^{2}E)$$

Simplifying:

$$k^{2} = \mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}\omega^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root. Recall that $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:

$$\frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}}$$

**Step 5:** Define the speed of light:

$$c \equiv \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{c = f\lambda}$$

**Step 6:** Numerically, $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

$$c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(8.854 \times 10^{-12})}} \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Friis Free-Space Path Loss

**Given:** A transmitting isotropic radiator with power $P_t$ radiates uniformly in all directions. A receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ is placed at distance $d$.

**Step 1:** Power Flux Density (PFD) at distance $d$ for an isotropic radiator:

$$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

This follows from the fact that the power spreads over the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$: $A_{sphere} = 4\pi d^{2}$.

**Step 2:** For a directional transmitter antenna of gain $G_t$, the PFD becomes:

$$S_{dir} = G_t \cdot \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The received power is the PFD multiplied by the effective aperture of the receiving antenna:

$$P_r = S_{dir} \cdot A_e$$

**Step 4:** The effective aperture of an antenna is related to its gain by:

$$A_e = G_r \cdot \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the $P_r$ expression:

$$P_r = G_t \cdot \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot G_r \cdot \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

Rearranging:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

This is the **Friis Transmission Equation**.

**Step 6:** Solving for the **path loss** (ratio of transmitted to received power, with unity gains):

$$\boxed{FSPL = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to decibels using $10\log_{10}$:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)$$

Since $\lambda = c/f$:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(c/f)$$

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(c)$$

Plugging in numerical values ($4\pi \approx 12.566$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) and converting to convenient units ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz):

$$\boxed{FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{[km]}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{[MHz]})}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Link Budget for a Microwave Link

**Problem:** A 6 GHz microwave link of 50 km uses parabolic dish antennas of gain 40 dBi each. The transmitter power is 1 W. Compute the received power.

**Step 1:** Transmitted power in dBm:

$$P_t|_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(1000 \text{ mW}) = 30 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute FSPL at $f = 6000$ MHz, $d = 50$ km:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(6000)$$

$$= 32.44 + 20(1.699) + 20(3.778)$$

$$= 32.44 + 33.98 + 75.56$$

$$= 141.98 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** Apply link budget equation:

$$P_r|_{dBm} = P_t|_{dBm} + G_t + G_r - FSPL_{dB}$$

$$= 30 + 40 + 40 - 141.98$$

$$= -31.98 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to milliwatts:

$$P_r = 10^{\,-31.98/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-3.198} \approx 0.000634 \text{ mW}$$

$$\boxed{P_r \approx 0.634 \,\mu\text{W}}$$

This extremely small received power shows why high-gain antennas and sensitive low-noise receivers are mandatory in long-distance microwave links.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Antenna Height for LOS

**Problem:** A 6 GHz terrestrial microwave link spans 40 km. Find the minimum antenna tower height (assuming identical heights on both ends).

**Step 1:** Apply the LOS antenna height formula:

$$h = \frac{d^{2}}{8R}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $d = 40$ km, $R = 6371$ km:

$$h = \frac{(40)^{2}}{8 \times 6371} = \frac{1600}{50968} \approx 0.0314 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to metres:

$$\boxed{h \approx 31.4 \text{ m}}$$

So each tower must be at least ~31 m tall for unobstructed LOS at 40 km.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Radio, Microwave, and Infrared

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 1 — examiners frequently ask for a comparative discussion worth 7–14 marks.

| Parameter | Radio Transmission | Microwave Transmission | Infrared Transmission |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Band** | 3 kHz – 1 GHz | 1 GHz – 300 GHz | 300 GHz – 400 THz |
| **Wavelength** | > 30 cm | 1 mm – 30 cm | 750 nm – 1 mm |
| **Propagation Mode** | Omnidirectional | Directional (LOS) | LOS (directed or diffuse) |
| **Range** | Up to thousands of km | Up to ~50 km terrestrial, 36,000 km satellite | Typically < 10 m |
| **Antenna / Transducer** | Wire dipole, whip | Parabolic dish, horn | LED, laser diode, photodiode |
| **Penetration of Walls** | Yes (strong) | No | No (blocked by most solids) |
| **Bandwidth** | Low to moderate | High (multi-Gbps possible) | Low to moderate |
| **Interference Susceptibility** | High (crowded bands) | Moderate (rain fade, multipath) | Low (immune to EMI; affected by sunlight) |
| **Security** | Poor (broadcast nature) | Good (narrow beam) | Excellent (cannot penetrate walls) |
| **Licensing Required** | Yes (most bands) | Yes (most bands) | No (unregulated) |
| **Atmospheric Effects** | Ionospheric reflection, fading | Rain fade, gaseous absorption | Absorption by dust, smoke |
| **Cost** | Low to moderate | Moderate to high (dishes, towers) | Very low (consumer-grade LEDs) |
| **Typical Applications** | AM/FM radio, TV, cellular, Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz, Bluetooth | Point-to-point links, satellite, radar, Wi-Fi 5 GHz, 5G mmWave | TV remotes, IrDA, in-room LANs |
| **Mobility Support** | Excellent (cellular handover) | Limited (alignment critical) | Limited to stationary/short-range |
| **Line-of-Sight Required** | No (below HF) / Yes (VHF+) | Yes (always) | Yes (always) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Allocation Diagram

The diagram below maps the three wireless modalities against the broader electromagnetic spectrum. Pure alphanumeric node IDs are used per the Mermaid safeguard.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[EM Spectrum] --> B1[Radio Waves 3 kHz to 1 GHz]
    A1 --> B2[Microwaves 1 GHz to 300 GHz]
    A1 --> B3[Infrared 300 GHz to 400 THz]
    A1 --> B4[Visible Light 400 THz to 750 THz]
    B1 --> C1[Ground Wave Propagation]
    B1 --> C2[Sky Wave Propagation]
    B1 --> C3[Line of Sight VHF UHF]
    B2 --> D1[Terrestrial Point to Point]
    B2 --> D2[Satellite Communication]
    B2 --> D3[Radar Systems]
    B2 --> D4[5G mmWave]
    B3 --> E1[Directed Infrared]
    B3 --> E2[Diffuse Infrared]
    B3 --> E3[Optical Fibre Carriers]
    E1 --> E1a[TV Remote Control]
    E1 --> E1b[IrDA Data Link]
    E2 --> E2a[Indoor Wireless LAN]
    E3 --> E3a[850 nm 1310 nm 1550 nm]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wireless Transmission System

The following schematic represents the signal flow from baseband source through the wireless channel. This block diagram maps the architectural interactions common to radio, microwave, and infrared links.

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Baseband Source Data Voice Video] --> enc[Source Encoder Compression]
    enc --> enc2[Channel Encoder Error Correction]
    enc2 --> mod[Modulator Carrier Modulation]
    mod --> txamp[Power Amplifier]
    txamp --> txant[Transmit Antenna or LED or Dish]
    txant --> chan[Wireless Channel Free Space Attenuation Noise Multipath]
    chan --> rxant[Receive Antenna or Photodiode or Dish]
    rxant --> rxamp[Low Noise Amplifier]
    rxamp --> demod[Demodulator Carrier Recovery]
    demod --> dec2[Channel Decoder]
    dec2 --> dec[Source Decoder]
    dec --> sink[Baseband Sink Original Information]
    chan --> noise[Noise Sources Thermal Atmospheric Interference]
    noise --> chan
```

## 4.3 Propagation Mechanism Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1[Radio Transmission]
        r1[Antenna Radiates Omnidirectionally]
        r2[Ground Wave Hugs Earth Surface]
        r3[Sky Wave Reflects Off Ionosphere]
        r4[Line of Sight at VHF UHF]
        r1 --> r2
        r1 --> r3
        r1 --> r4
    end
    subgraph S2[Microwave Transmission]
        m1[Parabolic Dish Focuses Beam]
        m2[Strict Line of Sight]
        m3[Repeaters Every 30 to 50 km]
        m4[Atmospheric Absorption Rain Fade]
        m1 --> m2
        m2 --> m3
        m2 --> m4
    end
    subgraph S3[Infrared Transmission]
        i1[LED or Laser Diode Source]
        i2[Directed Beam or Diffuse Reflection]
        i3[Blocked by Walls Confined to Room]
        i4[Photodiode Receiver with Optical Filter]
        i1 --> i2
        i2 --> i3
        i2 --> i4
    end
```

## 4.4 Microwave Link Path Profile (Block Architecture)

For a long-distance terrestrial microwave link, the end-to-end functional path is mapped as a block-level architecture.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[TX Site Baseband] --> B[Modulator IF Upconverter]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier 1 W]
    C --> D[Parabolic Dish Tx 40 dBi]
    D --> E[Free Space Path Loss 50 km 6 GHz]
    E --> F[Parabolic Dish Rx 40 dBi]
    F --> G[Low Noise Amplifier]
    G --> H[Downconverter IF]
    H --> I[Demodulator]
    I --> J[RX Site Baseband]
    E --> K[Atmospheric Losses Rain Fog]
    K --> E
    E --> L[Multipath Fading]
    L --> E
```
