| Lost ACK | Impossible | Sender timer expires → **resends frame**; receiver detects duplicate via **sequence number** → discards & re-ACKs |
| Duplicate Prevention | Not needed | **Sequence number (0/1)** is mandatory |
| Control Channel | Not required | Requires **reverse logical channel** for ACK/NAK |

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

- **ARQ (Automatic Repeat Request):** A family of protocols where the receiver automatically requests retransmission of erroneous frames.
- **Frame:** The Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the Data Link Layer. Contains header, payload, and trailer (with CRC).
- **Sequence Number ($N_s$):** A 1-bit field (value $\in \{0, 1\}$) used to distinguish a new frame from a retransmitted duplicate.
- **ACK (Positive Acknowledgment):** A small control frame sent by receiver to confirm successful receipt.
- **NAK (Negative Acknowledgment):** A small control frame sent by receiver to explicitly demand retransmission of a damaged frame.
- **Timer:** A countdown mechanism at the sender; on expiry, the sender assumes the frame/ACK was lost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The noisy-channel protocol expands the simple stop-and-wait by introducing **four critical state variables and rules**:

#### A. At the Sender Site
1. **Initialize** sequence number $N_s = 0$.
2. Construct a data frame with $N_s$ embedded in the header.
3. **Send** the frame to the physical layer.
4. **Start a timer** $T_{out}$ (a watchdog countdown).
5. **WAIT** for one of three possible events:
   - **Case 1: ACK arrives for $N_s$** → Stop timer, set $N_s = 1 - N_s$ (toggle), and go to Step 2 (send next frame).
   - **Case 2: NAK arrives** → Stop timer, **immediately retransmit** the SAME frame (do NOT toggle $N_s$), and restart the timer.
   - **Case 3: Timer expires** → Assume the frame (or its ACK) was lost. **Retransmit** the SAME frame, restart timer, and WAIT again.

#### B. At the Receiver Site
1. **Initialize** expected sequence number $N_r = 0$.
2. **WAIT** for an incoming frame.
3. On frame arrival:
   - If frame is **damaged (CRC fails)** → send **NAK** (carrying the $N_r$ it is still waiting for) and go back to Step 2.
   - If frame is **error-free but $N_s \neq N_r$** (this is a duplicate of the previous frame) → **discard the duplicate** and re-send **ACK for the last correctly received frame**.
   - If frame is **error-free and $N_s = N_r$** → **deliver data to network layer**, toggle $N_r = 1 - N_r$, and send **ACK** carrying the new $N_r$ (acknowledging receipt of the current frame).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the 1-Bit Sequence Number

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Reasoning:**
> Why only 0 and 1? Because under Stop-and-Wait, **only one frame can be unacknowledged at any time**. Therefore, the sender and receiver only ever need to distinguish between *"the current frame"* and *"the previous frame"*. A single bit is mathematically sufficient: $N_{next} = 1 - N_{current}$.

If we used 2 bits (0, 1, 2, 3), it would work but **waste bandwidth** — a classic KTU examiner question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission Time ($T_t$) | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| Propagation Time ($T_p$) | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = signal velocity ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper) |
| Parameter $a$ | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Ratio defining link utilization behavior |
| Total Cycle Time (Noisy S&W) | $T_{cycle} = T_t + T_p + T_{proc} + T_{ack} + T_p$ | Includes ACK transmission and processing |
| Protocol Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$ | Upper bound; actual efficiency drops with retransmissions |
| Average Number of Transmissions | $E[\text{transmissions}] = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = frame error probability (Bernoulli trials) |
| Effective Throughput | $S = \eta \cdot R \cdot (1-P)$ | Bits/sec successfully delivered |
| ACK/NAK Frame Length | Negligible (control frame, often 40 bytes) | Assumed to be 0 in simplified derivations |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value notations use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. Example: $\vert N_s \vert$ is written as `\vert N_s \vert`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This protocol, despite its age (Forouzan textbook, 1980s), directly influences:

- **RFID Tag-to-Reader Communication:** Low-bandwidth, high-latency links use stop-and-wait ARQ.
- **Satellite Command Channels (TT&C):** Deep-space probes (NASA's New Horizons) use S&W ARQ for uplink commands.
- **Bluetooth SCO (Synchronous Connection-Oriented) links:** Use stop-and-wait for retransmissions of lost audio packets.
- **HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control):** A descendant of this protocol.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Cycle Time for Noisy Stop-and-Wait

We derive the **time consumed for ONE successful frame delivery** in the noisy channel case:

**Step 1 — Identify all sequential events** in one round-trip:
- Sender transmits the data frame → consumes $T_t = L/R$
- Frame propagates to receiver → consumes $T_p = d/v$
- Receiver processes & generates ACK/NAK → consumes $T_{proc}$ (negligible, often $\approx 0$)
- ACK/NAK frame is transmitted back → consumes $T_{ack}$ (small, often $\approx 0$ for short control frames)
- ACK propagates back to sender → consumes $T_p$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_t + T_p + T_{proc} + T_{ack} + T_p \\
T_{cycle} &= T_t + 2T_p + T_{proc} + T_{ack}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify (KTU standard assumption):**
The KTU textbook (Forouzan) typically assumes $T_{proc} = 0$ and $T_{ack} \approx 0$ because control frames are tiny compared to data frames.

$$
T_{cycle} \approx T_t + 2T_p
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Efficiency $\eta$:**
Efficiency is defined as the **fraction of total channel time spent transmitting useful payload**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\text{Useful time}}{\text{Total time}} \\
\eta &= \frac{T_t}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of the parameter $a = T_p / T_t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2(a \cdot T_t)} \\
\eta &= \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Behavior Analysis (Critical for KTU):**
- If $a \ll 1$ (short link, long frame) → $\eta \approx 1$ (Excellent utilization)
- If $a \gg 1$ (long link, short frame) → $\eta \approx 0$ (Channel mostly idle — **pathological case**)
- If $a = 1$ → $\eta = 1/3$ (only 33% utilization)

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Transmissions under Noise

Let $P$ = probability that a frame is **damaged or lost** during a single transmission attempt.
Let $Q = 1 - P$ = probability of successful delivery on a given attempt.

This follows a **Geometric Distribution** (number of trials until first success):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{attempts}] &= \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P^{k-1} \cdot Q \\
&= \frac{1}{Q} = \frac{1}{1-P}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **effective throughput** $S$ (successful data bits per second) is:

$$
S = \frac{L \cdot (1-P)}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{(1-P)}{1+2a} \cdot R
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A satellite link has $R = 1$ Mbps bandwidth, $d = 36{,}000$ km altitude, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, frame size $L = 1000$ bits, and frame error probability $P = 0.01$. Calculate: (a) $a$, (b) $\eta$, (c) Expected throughput $S$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Parameter $a$:**
First, compute $T_t$ and $T_p$:

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{36 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{120 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 120
$$

**Part (b) — Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 240} = \frac{1}{241} \approx 0.00415 = 0.415\%
$$

**Part (c) — Effective Throughput $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{(1-P)}{1+2a} \cdot R = \frac{0.99}{241} \times 10^6 \approx 4107 \text{ bps} \approx 4.1 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For long-delay satellite links, S&W ARQ is **catastrophically inefficient** — this is why **Sliding Window Protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat)** are mandatory in such environments. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Noiseless S&W | Noisy S&W (ARQ) | Sliding Window (M2 later) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequence Number Bits | 0 | 1 | $k$ bits ($k \geq 3$) |
| Handles Lost Frames | No | Yes | Yes |
| Handles Damaged Frames | No | Yes (via NAK/CRC) | Yes |
| Channel Utilization on Long Links | Poor | Very Poor | Good |
| Receiver Complexity | Trivial | Low | Medium-High |
| Duplicate Detection Required | No | Yes (via $N_s$) | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Sender

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready : Init Ns=0
    Ready --> WaitingAck : Send Frame(Ns) + Start Timer
    WaitingAck --> Ready : Receive ACK for Ns\nToggle Ns = 1 - Ns
    WaitingAck --> WaitingAck : Receive NAK\nRetransmit same Frame\nRestart Timer
    WaitingAck --> WaitingAck : Timer Expires\nRetransmit same Frame\nRestart Timer
    Ready --> [*] : End
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Receiver

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ExpectFrame0 : Init Nr=0
    ExpectFrame0 --> ExpectFrame1 : Frame(0) received OK\nDeliver Data\nSend ACK\nNr = 1 - Nr
    ExpectFrame1 --> ExpectFrame0 : Frame(1) received OK\nDeliver Data\nSend ACK\nNr = 1 - Nr
    ExpectFrame0 --> ExpectFrame0 : Damaged Frame\nSend NAK
    ExpectFrame1 --> ExpectFrame1 : Damaged Frame\nSend NAK
    ExpectFrame0 --> ExpectFrame0 : Frame(1) Duplicate\nDiscard\nResend ACK
    ExpectFrame1 --> ExpectFrame1 : Frame(0) Duplicate\nDiscard\nResend ACK
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline Diagram — Frame Flow with Errors

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    Note over S: Ns = 0
    S->>R: Frame 0 (Data)
    R-->>S: ACK 1 (Frame 0 received)
    Note over S: Ns = 1
    S->>R: Frame 1 (Data)
    Note right of R: Frame 1 DAMAGED in transit
    R-->>S: NAK 1 (Resend Frame 1)
    Note over S: Timer still running
    S->>R: Frame 1 (RETRANSMIT)
    R-->>S: ACK 0 (Frame 1 received)
    Note over S: Ns = 0
    S->>R: Frame 0 (Data)
    Note right of R: Frame 0 LOST completely
    Note over S: Timer EXPIRES
    S->>R: Frame 0 (RETRANSMIT after timeout)
    R-->>S: ACK 1 (Duplicate detected, re-ACK)
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer Payload] --> B[Data Link Layer\nAdd Header + Trailer]
    B --> C{Frame Type?}
    C -->|Data Frame| D[Embed Sequence Number Ns]
    C -->|ACK/NAK| E[Embed Expected Nr]
    D --> F[Physical Layer\nTransmit on Wire]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Channel with Noise BER]
    G --> H{Receiver: CRC Check}
    H -->|OK| I{Is Ns = Nr?}
    H -->|FAIL| J[Send NAK to Sender]
    I -->|YES| K[Deliver to Network Layer\nToggle Nr\nSend ACK]
    I -->|NO| L[Discard Duplicate\nResend ACK]
    J --> M[Sender Retransmit]
    K --> N[Sender Receive ACK\nToggle Ns\nSend Next]
    L --> N
    M --> F
```

### 4.5 Frame Format Diagram (KTU Standard)

```
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------+----------+
|   Flag (8 bits)   |   Header Field    |   Payload   |   CRC    |
|   01111110        |   (Seq No, etc)   |   (Data)    |  (n bit) |
+-------------------+-------------------+-------------+----------+
        \___________ Header _________/  \____________ Trailer ___/
```

**Field-by-field meaning in Noisy S&W:**
- **Flag:** Frame delimiter (bit-stuffing applied).
- **Sequence Number ($N_s$):** 1-bit field, value $\in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Payload:** Actual user data from Network Layer.
- **CRC:** Used by receiver to detect damage. If CRC fails → receiver sends **NAK**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node IDs use purely alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and contain only clean uppercase text labels — no bold, italics, or HTML inside labels, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Why is a sequence number required in the Stop-and-Wait protocol for a noisy channel but NOT required in the noiseless version? What is the minimum number of bits needed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a **noiseless channel**, it is assumed that no frame is lost or duplicated, so the sender never needs to retransmit. Therefore, no sequence number is necessary.

In a **noisy channel**, however, frames (or their ACKs) may be lost. The sender's timer will expire, and it will **retransmit a frame that the receiver has already received successfully**. To prevent the receiver from delivering the same data twice to the Network Layer, a **sequence number** is included.

Since under Stop-and-Wait only **one frame can be unacknowledged at a time**, only **two states** need to be distinguished ("current" and "previous"). Thus, the **minimum number of bits = 1** (values 0 and 1).

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark each for: noisy justification, noiseless justification, and 1-bit conclusion.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Differentiate between ACK and NAK frames in the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol. Why is NAK considered optional?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ACK | NAK |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Positive Acknowledgment — "Frame received correctly" | Negative Acknowledgment — "Frame damaged, please resend" |
| Sent When | Frame passes CRC check AND sequence number matches | Frame fails CRC check |
| Effect on Sender | Sender toggles $N_s$ and sends next frame | Sender retransmits **same** frame (no toggle) |

**Why NAK is "optional":**
Even without a NAK, the sender's **timer will eventually expire** if the frame (or ACK) is lost, and the sender will retransmit automatically. So the protocol could theoretically work using **ACK + timer alone** (using a checksum on the ACK). However, NAK **improves efficiency** by triggering immediate retransmission without waiting for timeout — saving one round-trip delay.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically have an **internal choice**. Below are **TWO** fully independent alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a clear state diagram and frame structure, explain the working of the **Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for a Noisy Channel**. Discuss how lost frames, damaged frames, and lost acknowledgments are handled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kbps and a propagation delay of 20 ms. Frame size is 200 bits. If the frame error rate is $P = 0.1$, calculate: (i) the link utilization parameter $a$, (ii) the protocol efficiency $\eta$, and (iii) the effective throughput in bps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Frame Structure (1 mark)**
Each data frame contains: `Flag | SeqNo (1 bit) | Data | CRC`. ACK frame contains: `Flag | AckNo (1 bit) | CRC`.

**Step 2: Sender Algorithm (2 marks)**
- Start: $N_s = 0$.
- Loop:
  - Construct frame with sequence number $N_s$.
  - Send frame; start timer $T$.
  - Wait for ACK/NAK or timeout.
  - On ACK($N_s$): stop timer, set $N_s = 1 - N_s$, repeat.
  - On NAK: stop timer, **retransmit same frame**, restart timer.
  - On timeout: retransmit same frame, restart timer.

**Step 3: Receiver Algorithm (2 marks)**
- Start: $N_r = 0$.
- Loop:
  - Wait for frame.
  - On damaged frame → send NAK (do not change $N_r$).
  - On error-free frame with $N_s = N_r$ → deliver data, set $N_r = 1 - N_r$, send ACK.
  - On error-free frame with $N_s \neq N_r$ (duplicate) → discard, resend ACK.

**Step 4: State Diagram Discussion (1 mark)**
See SECTION 4 Diagrams (Sender + Receiver state machines) for full pictorial representation.

**Step 5: Loss/Damage/Duplicate Handling Summary (1 mark)**
- **Lost frame** → Timer expires → Retransmit.
- **Damaged frame** → NAK sent → Immediate retransmit (no timer wait).
- **Lost ACK** → Sender timer expires → Retransmit; receiver detects duplicate via $N_s \neq N_r$, discards, re-ACKs.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $R = 4 \text{ kbps} = 4000 \text{ bps}$, $T_p = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.02 \text{ s}$, $L = 200 \text{ bits}$, $P = 0.1$.

**Part (i) — Parameter $a$ (2 marks):**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{200}{4000} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 0.4
$$

**Part (ii) — Efficiency $\eta$ (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.8} = \frac{1}{1.8} \approx 0.556 = 55.6\%
$$

**Part (iii) — Effective Throughput $S$ (3 marks):**

Number of attempts expected:

$$
E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{1}{1-P} = \frac{1}{0.9} \approx 1.111
$$

Total time for ONE successful frame delivery:

$$
T_{success} = 1.111 \times (T_t + 2T_p) = 1.111 \times (0.05 + 0.04) = 1.111 \times 0.09 = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

Throughput:

$$
S = \frac{L}{T_{success}} = \frac{200 \text{ bits}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 2000 \text{ bps} = 2 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Alternative cross-check formula:**

$$
S = \frac{(1-P) \cdot R}{1+2a} = \frac{0.9 \times 4000}{1.8} = 2000 \text{ bps} \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the limitations of the **noiseless Stop-and-Wait protocol** and justify why the **noisy-channel version** with sequence numbers, ACK, NAK, and timer is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a geostationary satellite at 36,000 km. The link bandwidth is 1 Mbps and frames are 4000 bits long. Compute the link utilization and the maximum possible throughput. Suggest a method to improve utilization and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Noiseless Protocol — Assumptions (1 mark)**
- No frame is ever damaged or lost.
- No acknowledgment is needed.
- Sender can transmit continuously without waiting.

**Step 2: Real-World Failure (2 marks)**
In real channels (copper, fiber, wireless), **bit errors** and **frame loss** are inevitable due to:
- Thermal noise, crosstalk, attenuation (copper).
- Signal dispersion, bending loss (fiber).
- Multipath fading, interference (wireless).

**Step 3: Required Enhancements (3 marks)**
The noisy version adds:
1. **CRC** at receiver → detects damage.
2. **Sequence number (1 bit)** → detects duplicates.
3. **ACK frame** → confirms receipt.
4. **NAK frame** → requests fast retransmission.
5. **Timer at sender** → recovers from total loss of frame or ACK.
6. **Reverse logical channel** → for ACK/NAK propagation.

**Step 4: Why This Constitutes an ARQ Protocol (1 mark)**
Because the receiver **Automatically** Requests retransmission — hence **ARQ**.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $d = 36{,}000 \text{ km} = 3.6 \times 10^7$ m, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $R = 10^6$ bps, $L = 4000$ bits.

**Step 1: Compute $T_p$ and $T_t$ (2 marks):**

$$
T_p = \frac{3.6 \times 10^7}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_t = \frac{4000}{10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $a$ and $\eta$ (2 marks):**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{120}{4} = 30
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1+2a} = \frac{1}{1+60} = \frac{1}{61} \approx 0.0164 = 1.64\%
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Throughput (2 marks):**

$$
S_{max} = \eta \cdot R = 0.0164 \times 10^6 \approx 16{,}393 \text{ bps} \approx 16.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4: Improvement Suggestion (1 mark):**
Use a **Sliding Window Protocol (Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat)** with window size $W \geq 2a + 1 = 61$. This allows the sender to keep the channel "full" of frames, raising utilization to nearly **100%** for low-error links.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to toggle $N_s$ on ACK but toggling on NAK** — Many students wrongly toggle $N_s$ on NAK. **Rule:** $N_s$ toggles **only when a valid ACK for the current frame arrives**. NAK and timeout do NOT toggle.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency with throughput** — $\eta$ is dimensionless; $S$ has units of bps. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix them up.
> 3. **Using a multi-bit sequence number** — State explicitly that **1 bit is sufficient** for Stop-and-Wait. Writing "2 bits" loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the round-trip in $T_{cycle}$** — The noisy S&W cycle must include **two** propagation delays (forward for data, return for ACK), not one.
> 5. **Forgetting $T_{ack}$ in derivation** — Even if the final answer uses the simplified form, the **derivation** should show the full $T_{cycle} = T_t + 2T_p + T_{ack}$ first, then simplify.
> 6. **Not stating that the channel must be half-duplex logically** — Simplex data + simplex ACK = effectively a half-duplex logical channel. Examiners expect this term.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Noisy Stop-and-Wait** is an **ARQ protocol** — receiver automatically requests retransmission.
- [x] **Sequence number = 1 bit** (values 0 and 1) is sufficient.
- [x] **Three sender events:** ACK → toggle and proceed; NAK → retransmit (no toggle); Timeout → retransmit.
- [x] **Three receiver events:** Damaged frame → NAK; Correct new frame → deliver, toggle, ACK; Duplicate frame → discard, re-ACK.
- [x] **NAK is "optional"** — timer alone can recover, but NAK saves a round-trip.
- [x] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$, where $a = T_p / T_t$.
- [x] **Effective throughput under noise:** $S = \dfrac{(1-P)}{1+2a} \cdot R$.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $a \ll 1$ → $\eta \approx 1$; $a \gg 1$ → $\eta \approx 0$ (satellite scenario).
- [x] **Sliding Window protocols** are required to overcome poor utilization of Stop-and-Wait on high-$a$ links.
- [x] **Key terms to use in answers:** ARQ, CRC, NAK, ACK, Sequence Number, Round-Trip Time (RTT), Timer, Piggybacking (mentioned in M2 next topics).
- [x] **Mnemonic for sender:** "**S**top-and-**W**ait = **S**end, **W**ait, **A**ck, **N**ak, **T**imeout — remember S.W.A.N.T."
- [x] **Real-world applications:** RFID, satellite TT&C, Bluetooth SCO, HDLC, Wi-Fi ACK.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Window Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sliding Window Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sliding Window Protocol** is a feature of packet-based data transmission protocols that uses a finite-size sender-side and/or receiver-side window of sequence numbers to control the flow of data frames between a sender and a receiver over a noisy or unreliable Data Link Layer channel. It is the principal technique used by the Data Link Layer (Layer 2 of the OSI/TCP-IP model) to enable **pipelining**, whereby multiple frames can be transmitted before requiring an acknowledgment, thereby overcoming the severe throughput limitations of the Stop-and-Wait ARQ mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Data Link Layer flow and error control mechanisms are classified into two broad categories: **Noise-less** (Simplest, Stop-and-Wait) and **Noisy** (Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ). Sliding Window protocols are the **pipelined** successors of Stop-and-Wait.

In a sliding window protocol, both the sender and the receiver maintain a **window** of acceptable sequence numbers. The sender may transmit up to the window size (`Ws`) frames without receiving an acknowledgment. As acknowledgments arrive, the window "slides" forward, enabling transmission of new frames. The receiver window (`Wr`) dictates which frames may be accepted out of order.

### 1.2 Categories of Sliding Window Protocols

The two principal variants mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Go-Back-N ARQ (GBN):** A cumulative-acknowledgment scheme where the receiver discards out-of-order frames and the sender retransmits all frames starting from the lost one. Sender window `Ws = 2^m - 1`, Receiver window `Wr = 1`.
2. **Selective Repeat ARQ (SR):** A selective-acknowledgment scheme where the receiver buffers out-of-order frames and the sender retransmits only the lost/timed-out frames. Sender window `Ws = Wr = 2^{m-1}`.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Conveyor Belt

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Packing Line"**
>
> Imagine a factory where a worker (the **sender**) places labelled boxes on a moving conveyor belt heading to a customer (the **receiver**).
>
> - In **Stop-and-Wait**, the worker places *one* box, then stops and waits for a thumbs-up from the customer before placing the next. The conveyor belt is **idle** most of the time.
> - In **Go-Back-N**, the worker places a row of up to `N` boxes. If the customer says *"Box #5 was damaged, redo from #5 onwards"*, the worker re-feeds boxes #5, #6, #7… (even if they were fine). Simple but wasteful.
> - In **Selective Repeat**, the customer has a side storage shelf. If box #5 is missing, the customer places #6, #7, #8 on the shelf in order, and only asks the worker to **resend just box #5**. Efficient, but requires more bookkeeping (memory).
>
> The "window" is the maximum number of boxes that can be in-flight on the belt at any moment.

### 1.4 Why Sliding Window? — The Problem It Solves

The raw transmission time of a single frame (`Tt`) is often **negligibly small** compared to the propagation delay (`Tp`) on wide-area links (e.g., satellite links: `Tp ≈ 270 ms` one-way, while `Tt` for a 1 Kb frame at 1 Mbps is just 1 ms). In Stop-and-Wait, the link is utilized only for `Tt` out of every `(Tt + 2Tp)` seconds, giving a utilization as low as **0.18%** for satellites. Sliding window protocols allow the pipe to remain **full** of frames, raising utilization toward 100%.

### 1.5 Key Terminology (Must Know)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Frame Transmission Time | $T_t$ | Time to push all bits of one frame onto the link = $\frac{L}{R}$ (L = frame length in bits, R = link bandwidth in bps) |
| Propagation Delay | $T_p$ | Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver = $\frac{d}{v}$ (d = distance, v ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper, $3 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) |
| Acknowledgment Time | $T_a$ | Time to transmit ACK frame (usually very small) |
| Round Trip Time | RTT | $2 \times T_p + T_t + T_a$ |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | BDP | $R \times T_p$ — the number of bits that "fill the pipe" |
| Parameter `a` | $a$ | Ratio $T_p / T_t$ (also sometimes $T_p / T_{frame}$) |
| Sequence Number Bits | $m$ | Number of bits used to number the frames |
| Sender Window | $W_s$ | Max frames sent without ACK |
| Receiver Window | $W_r$ | Max out-of-order frames accepted |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the Constant:** The speed of signal propagation in **vacuum/air** is $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and in **typical copper/fiber media** it is roughly $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ **67% of c**). Examiners love giving this value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Sliding Windows Work

The protocol operates as a **synchronized state machine** between two parties. Let us break down the mechanics:

**A. Sender-Side Logic (Universal):**
1. Maintain a sending window of size `Ws`.
2. Assign a sequence number from `0` to `2^m - 1` (modulo arithmetic) to each frame.
3. A **send pointer** (left edge of window) marks the oldest unacknowledged frame.
4. A **next-frame pointer** (right edge of window) marks the next frame eligible for transmission.
5. As ACKs arrive, the send pointer advances, and new frames are pushed out.
6. On a **timeout** for a frame, the retransmission policy differs: GBN retransmits *that frame and all subsequent*, while SR retransmits *only that frame*.

**B. Receiver-Side Logic (Differs per protocol):**

| Action | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Accept in-order frame | ✅ Yes, send cumulative ACK for next-expected | ✅ Yes, send ACK for that frame |
| Accept out-of-order frame | ❌ Discard, send ACK for next-expected | ✅ Buffer it, send selective ACK |
| Window size | $W_r = 1$ | $W_r = W_s = 2^{m-1}$ |

**C. The "Sliding" Mechanism:**
The window metaphor comes from the fact that the valid sequence number range remains *fixed in size* but *moves forward* over the modulo-$2^m$ number space. Visually, imagine a windowpane sliding over a long tape of repeating sequence numbers 0, 1, 2, … 7, 0, 1, 2 … for `m=3`.

### 2.2 Why These Specific Window Sizes? — The Underlying Math

The window sizes are **not arbitrary**; they are the minimum sizes that prevent ambiguity between a *new* frame and a *retransmission of an old* frame.

- For **Go-Back-N**, the sender might retransmit frames that have already been successfully received but whose ACK was lost. The receiver must be able to distinguish a fresh frame from a duplicate. Setting $W_s \le 2^m - 1$ prevents the sequence number space from "wrapping around" and confusing old frames with new ones.
- For **Selective Repeat**, the receiver keeps out-of-order frames, so the *combination* of sender window + receiver window must not exceed $2^m$. With $W_s = W_r$, this gives $2W_s \le 2^m$, hence $W_s \le 2^{m-1}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All High-Yield Formulas (Print & Memorise)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Condition / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Frame Transmission Time | $T_t = \frac{L}{R}$ | L in bits, R in bps → seconds |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $T_p = \frac{d}{v}$ | d in m, v in m/s → seconds |
| 3 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times T_p$ | bits |
| 4 | Pipeline parameter | $a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Stop-and-Wait Utilization | $U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | $0 < U \le 1$ |
| 6 | Stop-and-Wait Throughput | $\lambda = R \times U$ | frames/sec or bps |
| 7 | Go-Back-N Utilization (no error) | $U = \frac{W_s}{1 + 2a}$ for $W_s < 1+2a$, else $U = 1$ | For $W_s \ge 1+2a$, link stays full |
| 8 | Selective Repeat Utilization (no error) | $U = \frac{W_s}{1 + 2a}$ for $W_s < 1+2a$, else $U = 1$ | Same form as GBN when error-free |
| 9 | GBN Sender Window | $W_s \le 2^m - 1$ | Max value $2^m - 1$ |
| 10 | SR Sender Window | $W_s = 2^{m-1}$ | Strict equality (canonical) |
| 11 | SR Receiver Window | $W_r = 2^{m-1}$ | Same as $W_s$ |
| 12 | Total frames in flight | $N_{pipe} = \lceil \frac{2T_p + T_t}{T_t} \rceil = \lceil 1 + 2a \rceil$ | For full utilization |
| 13 | Go-Back-N: wasted bandwidth on error | All `Ws` frames after loss must be retransmitted | n/a |
| 14 | Selective Repeat: wasted bandwidth on error | Only the lost frame retransmitted | n/a |
| 15 | Minimum bits `m` for sequence number | $m \ge \lceil \log_2(W_s + 1) \rceil$ for GBN; $m \ge \lceil \log_2(2W_s) \rceil$ for SR | Round up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Examiner's Favourite):** When $W_s \ge 1 + 2a$, the sender **never has to stop** — the pipe is permanently full and $U = 1$ (100%). When $W_s < 1 + 2a$, the formula $U = W_s / (1+2a)$ applies. Always state the case before plugging in numbers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control)** and its derivative **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** used in dial-up and early broadband used a sliding window.
- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** at the Transport Layer uses a **variable-size sliding window** for flow control, conceptually descended from Selective Repeat. TCP's window is in *bytes*, not frames.
- **Satellite communications (VSAT)** rely on Selective Repeat because the very high $T_p$ makes Go-Back-N's mass-retransmissions prohibitively expensive.
- **5G NR (New Radio) Data Link Layer** uses Hybrid ARQ (HARQ), a hybrid of ARQ and Forward Error Correction, with a sliding-window-style retransmission buffer.

### 2.5 Piggybacking (Syllabus-Adjacent Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Piggybacking* is the technique where a data frame from receiver → sender carries the acknowledgment for the previously received data frame in its header field, rather than transmitting a separate ACK frame. This saves bandwidth at the cost of a small delay (the ACK waits up to a timer, typically 500 ms, to be "piggybacked").

The trade-off is that if no reverse data is available, a standalone ACK must still be sent (or the timer expires).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Utilization of Stop-and-Wait ARQ

This is the *baseline* against which Sliding Window protocols are compared. We must derive it first.

**Given:** A sender transmits one frame of duration $T_t$. The signal propagates in $T_p$ to the receiver. The receiver immediately returns an ACK (assume ACK transmission time $T_a$ is negligible or included). The ACK takes $T_p$ to return. The cycle then repeats.

**Step 1:** Time for one full cycle (transmit + propagate + ACK + propagate)
$$T_{cycle} = T_t + T_p + T_a + T_p$$

**Step 2:** Since $T_a$ is usually negligible and we define $T_a \to 0$,
$$T_{cycle} \approx T_t + 2T_p$$

**Step 3:** The fraction of time the link is *actually carrying useful data* (i.e., a new data frame) is the data time divided by the cycle time.
$$U_{SAW} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $T_t$, and define $a = T_p / T_t$.
$$U_{SAW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2\,\frac{T_p}{T_t}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

> **Result (boxed):** $\quad U_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$

**Numerical sanity check:** For a geostationary satellite link, $T_p \approx 270$ ms and for a 1 Kb frame at 1 Mbps, $T_t = 1$ ms, so $a = 270$, giving $U = 1 / 541 \approx 0.18\%$. The link is idle **99.82% of the time** — this is the catastrophe that motivates sliding windows.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Utilization of Go-Back-N ARQ (Error-Free Case)

**Given:** The sender may transmit up to $W_s$ frames back-to-back before stopping for an ACK. All $W_s$ frames take $W_s \cdot T_t$ to send. Then the sender waits for the cumulative ACK to return, taking roughly $2T_p$ (we ignore the small ACK time). After the first ACK arrives, the window slides and the next batch is sent.

**Step 1:** Time to push all $W_s$ frames onto the wire:
$$T_{transmit\_all} = W_s \cdot T_t$$

**Step 2:** Time for the ACK of the first frame to return (assuming ACK travels at the speed of the last frame's ACK):
$$T_{wait} \approx 2 T_p + T_a$$

**Step 3:** Total cycle time (one "batch"):
$$T_{cycle\_GBN} = W_s \cdot T_t + 2 T_p$$

**Step 4:** Utilization = (time sending useful data) / (total cycle time):
$$U_{GBN} = \frac{W_s \cdot T_t}{W_s \cdot T_t + 2 T_p}$$

**Step 5:** Divide top and bottom by $T_t$, substitute $a = T_p / T_t$:
$$U_{GBN} = \frac{W_s}{W_s + 2a}$$

> **Result (boxed):** $\quad U_{GBN} = \dfrac{W_s}{W_s + 2a} \quad \text{provided } W_s < 1 + 2a$

**Step 6 (Saturation case):** If the window is so large that an ACK arrives *before* the sender has emptied the window, the sender never idles. The condition for this is:
$$W_s \cdot T_t \ge 2 T_p + T_t \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad W_s \ge 1 + 2a$$
In this regime:
$$U_{GBN} = 1 \quad (100\% \text{ utilization})$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Utilization of Selective Repeat ARQ (Error-Free Case)

**Given:** Same physical setup as GBN. Because in the no-error case, every sent frame is ACKed individually, the cycle analysis is identical to GBN.

**Step 1:** Same derivation as Go-Back-N; the difference between SR and GBN only manifests on **error** (loss) events, not in the no-error case.

**Step 2 (Error case):** Suppose one frame is lost in a batch of $W_s$. GBN retransmits all $W_s$ frames, wasting $W_s$ frame-times. SR retransmits only the 1 lost frame, wasting $1$ frame-time. The probability $P$ that a single frame is in error is $P = 1 - (1 - p)^k$ where $p$ is the bit error rate and $k$ is frame length, OR simply given as $P$ in KTU problems.

**Step 3 (Efficiency formula with error, KTU favourite):**
$$U_{SR} = \frac{W_s \cdot (1 - P)}{(1 + 2a)}$$
when $W_s \ge 1 + 2a$, and
$$U_{GBN} = \frac{W_s \cdot (1 - P)}{(1 + 2a)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (W_s - 1)P}$$
The extra factor in GBN captures the wasted retransmissions.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Window Size to Fully Utilize a Link

**Given:** A link with $T_p$ and frame time $T_t$, find the minimum $W_s$ that achieves $U = 1$.

**Step 1:** Set the saturation condition:
$$W_s \cdot T_t \ge 2 T_p + T_t$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $W_s$:
$$W_s \ge \frac{2 T_p}{T_t} + 1 = 2a + 1$$

**Step 3:** Since $W_s$ must be an integer:
$$W_{s,min} = \lceil 2a + 1 \rceil = \lceil 1 + 2a \rceil$$

> **Result (boxed):** $\quad W_{s,min} = \lceil 1 + 2a \rceil$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Why SR Window Must Be $2^{m-1}$

This is a **classic KTU proof question** worth memorising.

**Given:** Both sender and receiver maintain windows of size $W$ over a sequence number space of $\{0, 1, \dots, 2^m - 1\}$.

**Step 1:** When a frame's ACK is lost, the sender will retransmit it. The receiver, having already advanced its window, must be able to recognize this as a duplicate, **not** as a new frame.

**Step 2:** The maximum span of sequence numbers simultaneously "in the system" equals the sum of the sender window and the receiver window: $W_s + W_r$.

**Step 3:** For the receiver to unambiguously distinguish old from new, the in-flight numbers must not wrap around. Hence:
$$W_s + W_r \le 2^m$$

**Step 4:** In Selective Repeat, $W_s = W_r$ (symmetric design). Therefore:
$$2W_s \le 2^m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W_s \le 2^{m-1}$$

**Step 5:** Setting $W_s$ to its maximum for efficiency:
$$W_s = W_r = 2^{m-1}$$

> **Result (boxed):** $\quad W_s = W_r = 2^{m-1}$ for Selective Repeat.

**Step 6 (Why GBN only needs $2^m - 1$):** In GBN, the receiver window is 1, so the receiver always discards out-of-order frames; it never has to distinguish a "new" frame from a "retransmitted old" frame in its buffer. Hence the constraint is just $W_s < 2^m$, i.e., $W_s \le 2^m - 1$.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-style)

**Example 1:** A channel has $T_p = 20$ ms, $R = 1$ Mbps, $L = 1000$ bits. Find the minimum sender window for full utilization.

- $T_t = L/R = 1000 / 10^6 = 1$ ms.
- $a = T_p / T_t = 20 / 1 = 20$.
- $W_{s,min} = \lceil 1 + 2(20) \rceil = 41$.

**Example 2:** A link uses $m = 4$ bits for sequence numbers. Find the maximum sender window sizes for (a) GBN, (b) SR.

- (a) GBN: $W_s \le 2^4 - 1 = 15$.
- (b) SR: $W_s = W_r = 2^{4-1} = 8$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sliding Window Conceptual Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SND[Sender Side]
        direction TB
        S1[Sequence Numbers 0 to 2^m minus 1] --> S2[Send Window of size Ws]
        S2 --> S3[Frames buffered for transmission]
        S3 --> S4[Timeout Timer per frame]
    end
    subgraph LNK[Unreliable Channel]
        direction TB
        L1[Data Frames in flight] --> L2[Propagation Delay Tp]
        L2 --> L3[ACK frames returning]
    end
    subgraph RCV[Receiver Side]
        direction TB
        R1[Receive Window of size Wr] --> R2[Accept or Discard Policy]
        R2 --> R3[ACK Generator: Cumulative or Selective]
    end
    S4 --> L1
    L3 --> R1
    R3 --> LNK
```

### 4.2 Go-Back-N ARQ Sequence Diagram (Frame Loss Scenario)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Receiver
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Window size Ws = 4, m = 3, sequence 0 to 7
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 0
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 1
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 2
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 1 (cumulative, expecting 2)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 3
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 4
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Frame 4 is LOST in transit
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 5
    Receiver--xSender: Frame 5 discarded, ACK 2 still sent
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 6
    Receiver--xSender: Frame 6 discarded, ACK 2 still sent
    Note over Sender: Timeout fires for Frame 4
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 4 (retransmit)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 5 (retransmit)
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 6 (retransmit)
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 7 (cumulative, all received)
```

### 4.3 Selective Repeat ARQ Sequence Diagram (Frame Loss Scenario)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Receiver
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Window size Ws = Wr = 4, m = 4
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 0
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 1
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 2
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 3
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 0
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 1
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Frame 2 is LOST
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 4
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 3 (buffered 3, 4 out of order)
    Note over Sender: Timeout fires for Frame 2 only
    Sender->>Receiver: Frame 2 (retransmit)
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 2 (now all 0-4 in order, deliver to network)
    Receiver-->>Sender: ACK 4
```

### 4.4 State Machine — Generic Sliding Window Sender

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Transmit: Frame ready and within window
    Transmit --> WaitAck: Frame sent, start timer
    WaitAck --> Ready: ACK received, slide window
    WaitAck --> Timeout: Timer expires
    Timeout --> Retransmit: GBN: resend this and all later, SR: resend only this
    Retransmit --> WaitAck: Restart timer
    Ready --> [*]: All frames ACKed
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison of GBN vs SR

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GBN[Go-Back-N Architecture]
        G1[Sender Buffer: Ws frames] --> G2[Retransmit all from loss point]
        G3[Receiver Buffer: 1 frame only] --> G4[Discard out of order]
        G4 --> G5[Send cumulative ACK]
    end
    subgraph SR[Selective Repeat Architecture]
        S1[Sender Buffer: Ws frames with per frame timer] --> S2[Retransmit only timed out frame]
        S3[Receiver Buffer: Wr frames in order] --> S4[Buffer out of order, mark gaps]
        S4 --> S5[Send selective ACK per frame]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a Sliding Window Protocol. With the help of a diagram, explain the working of a one-bit sliding window protocol.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Sliding Window Protocol allows the sender to transmit multiple frames (up to a window size `Ws`) before needing an acknowledgment, thereby pipelining the channel. Both sender and receiver maintain a window of acceptable sequence numbers; the window "slides" forward as acknowledgments are received, enabling continuous transmission.

In the **one-bit sliding window** (`Ws = Wr = 1`, `m = 1`), the sender tags the frame with a 0 or 1 sequence number, and the ACK also carries an alternating 0/1. If the sender receives a duplicate ACK (because its previous frame or the previous ACK was lost), it retransmits the current frame. The 1-bit alternating tag prevents the receiver from accepting the same frame twice.

> **Marking Tip (KTU pattern):** Definition (1 mark), Working explanation (1 mark), Diagram or example (1 mark). Always draw a small timeline diagram.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ with respect to (i) window size, (ii) receiver behaviour on out-of-order frames, and (iii) bandwidth efficiency on a noisy link.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **(i) Window size** | $W_s \le 2^m - 1$, $W_r = 1$ | $W_s = W_r = 2^{m-1}$ |
| **(ii) Out-of-order frames** | Discarded immediately | Buffered in receiver window |
| **(iii) Efficiency on noisy link** | Poor: one loss wastes $W_s$ frames | High: one loss wastes 1 frame |

> **Marking Tip:** A 3-mark "distinguish" question expects at least 3 distinct points in tabular form. 1 mark per valid point.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the working of **Go-Back-N ARQ** protocol with a suitable diagram. Clearly state the sender and receiver algorithm. (7 marks)

**(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of **1 Mbps** and a one-way propagation delay of **20 ms**. The frame size is **1000 bits**. Find:
- (i) The utilization if Stop-and-Wait ARQ is used.
- (ii) The minimum sender window size needed for Go-Back-N to achieve 100% utilization.
- (iii) The number of bits `m` required in the sequence number field. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer to (a):** *(7 marks)*

**Go-Back-N ARQ** is a sliding window protocol where the sender can transmit up to $W_s$ frames continuously. The receiver only accepts frames **in order**; any out-of-order frame is discarded, and a **cumulative ACK** for the last correctly received in-order frame is sent. On a timeout for frame `i`, the sender retransmits frame `i` and all subsequent frames (i+1, i+2, …) without waiting.

**Sender Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Maintain a sending window of size $W_s$ with sequence numbers $0$ to $2^m - 1$.
2. Assign a sequence number to each frame and start a timer for each.
3. On receiving ACK `n` (cumulative), slide the window forward past frame `n` and stop its timer.
4. On timeout of frame `k`, retransmit frame `k` and all subsequent frames already sent but not ACKed.

**Receiver Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Maintain a receive window of size 1 (next expected sequence number `N`).
2. If received frame has `seq = N`, deliver it, increment `N`, and send ACK for `N`.
3. If received frame has `seq ≠ N`, discard it and re-send ACK for `N` (the current next-expected).

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a sequence diagram showing sender transmitting frames 0, 1, 2, 3, 4; suppose frame 2 is lost; sender times out and retransmits 2, 3, 4 even though 3 and 4 were received and discarded. Cumulative ACKs are shown as ACK 1, ACK 1 (repeated), then ACK 5.

---

**Model Answer to (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $R = 1$ Mbps, $T_p = 20$ ms, $L = 1000$ bits.

**Step 1: Compute $T_t$** (1 mark)
$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute $a$** (1 mark)
$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20}{1} = 20$$

**Step 3: Stop-and-Wait Utilization** (2 marks)
$$U_{SAW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 40} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 \text{ or } 2.44\%$$

**Step 4: Minimum $W_s$ for 100% GBN utilization** (2 marks)
$$W_{s,min} = \lceil 1 + 2a \rceil = \lceil 1 + 40 \rceil = 41$$
So the sender needs a window of at least **41 frames**.

**Step 5: Minimum `m` for sequence number** (1 mark)
We need $W_s \le 2^m - 1$, i.e., $2^m \ge W_s + 1 = 42$.
$$m = \lceil \log_2 42 \rceil = \lceil 5.39 \rceil = 6 \text{ bits}$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) 2.44%, (ii) 41 frames, (iii) 6 bits.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the working of **Selective Repeat ARQ** with a clear diagram showing how lost frames are handled. Why is its window size restricted to $2^{m-1}$? Prove this mathematically. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare Stop-and-Wait ARQ, Go-Back-N ARQ, and Selective Repeat ARQ on the basis of: complexity, buffer requirement, throughput on noisy link, and suitability for satellite links. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer to (a):** *(7 marks)*

**Working (4 marks):** In Selective Repeat ARQ, the sender and receiver both maintain a window of equal size $W_s = W_r = 2^{m-1}$. The sender retransmits **only** the frame that timed out (not subsequent ones), and the receiver **buffers out-of-order frames** until the missing ones arrive. Each frame is ACKed individually (selective ACK).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Show sender sending frames 0, 1, 2, 3; suppose frame 1 is lost. The receiver delivers 0, buffers 2 and 3, and sends ACK 0 only. Sender times out, retransmits frame 1. Receiver now has 1, 2, 3 in order, delivers them, and sends ACK 3.

**Mathematical Proof of $W_s = 2^{m-1}$ (2 marks):**
Let $W_s$ and $W_r$ be the sender and receiver window sizes. The maximum number of sequence numbers that can be "in flight" at any instant is $W_s + W_r$. To prevent the receiver from confusing a brand-new frame (after the window has slid) with a retransmission of an old frame (whose ACK was lost), the in-flight numbers must not cover the entire modulo-$2^m$ space:
$$W_s + W_r \le 2^m$$
Since Selective Repeat is symmetric, $W_s = W_r$, giving $2W_s \le 2^m$, hence:
$$\boxed{W_s \le 2^{m-1}}$$
Setting it to the maximum for best efficiency gives $W_s = 2^{m-1}$.

---

**Model Answer to (b):** *(7 marks)*

| Criterion | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Sender Complexity** | Very Low | Low | High (per-frame timers, resequencing) |
| **Receiver Complexity** | Very Low | Low (no buffer) | High (large buffer for $W_r$ frames) |
| **Buffer Requirement** | 1 frame each | Sender: $W_s$, Receiver: 1 | Sender: $W_s$, Receiver: $W_r$ |
| **Throughput on noisy link** | Very poor (high $T_p$ idle) | Poor (one loss = $W_s$ retransmits) | High (one loss = 1 retransmit) |
| **Satellite link suitability** | Unsuitable | Marginal | Highly suitable |

> **Marking Distribution:** 1.5 marks per filled row, 0.5 mark for the concluding sentence (2 marks for table + 1 mark for conclusion about satellite link).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation case:** Writing $U = W_s / (1+2a)$ even when $W_s \ge 1+2a$. Always state *which case* applies.
> 2. **Confusing GBN and SR window sizes:** $2^m - 1$ vs $2^{m-1}$ is the single most common error. Memorise both, side by side.
> 3. **Units in $T_p$ and $T_t$:** If $T_p$ is given in **ms** and $T_t$ in **μs**, convert both to the same unit *before* computing $a$. A common trap.
> 4. **Not drawing diagrams:** A question on GBN/SR *without* a sequence diagram showing the loss and retransmission typically loses 2 marks automatically.
> 5. **Selective ACK vs Cumulative ACK:** Saying "GBN uses selective ACK" is wrong. GBN uses **cumulative** ACK; SR uses **selective** ACK.
> 6. **Piggybacking definition:** Many students write "sending ACK with data" without mentioning the **timer delay** trade-off.
> 7. **Window size proof:** Students often write the proof of $2^{m-1}$ without explaining *why* the constraint $W_s + W_r \le 2^m$ exists (i.e., to disambiguate old retransmits from new frames).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sliding Window Protocols**

- [ ] Sliding Window = **pipelined** ARQ; solves the idle-link problem of Stop-and-Wait on high-$T_p$ links.
- [ ] **Go-Back-N:** $W_s \le 2^m - 1$, $W_r = 1$, cumulative ACK, receiver **discards** out-of-order frames, sender retransmits from the loss point.
- [ ] **Selective Repeat:** $W_s = W_r = 2^{m-1}$, selective ACK, receiver **buffers** out-of-order frames, sender retransmits only the timed-out frame.
- [ ] The window "slides" forward over a **modulo-$2^m$** sequence number space.
- [ ] **Utilization formulas** (no error):
  - Stop-and-Wait: $U = 1 / (1 + 2a)$
  - GBN / SR: $U = W_s / (W_s + 2a)$ if $W_s < 1+2a$, else $U = 1$
- [ ] **Minimum window for full utilization:** $W_{s,min} = \lceil 1 + 2a \rceil$.
- [ ] **Parameter $a$:** Always defined as $T_p / T_t$. With $T_t = L/R$, $a = (R \cdot T_p) / L$ — i.e., the **bandwidth-delay product in frame-times**.
- [ ] **Bandwidth-Delay Product $BDP = R \cdot T_p$** tells you the number of bits that fill the pipe.
- [ ] **Piggybacking:** Combining a reverse-direction data frame with an ACK. Saves bandwidth, adds a small ACK-delay (timer ≤ ~500 ms).
- [ ] **One-bit sliding window** ($W_s = W_r = 1$, $m = 1$) is a special case used as a teaching tool; the alternating 0/1 tag handles lost ACKs.
- [ ] **Why $W_r = 1$ in GBN?** Because the receiver does not buffer out-of-order frames — simplicity at the cost of bandwidth on errors.
- [ ] **Why $2^{m-1}$ in SR?** Because $W_s + W_r \le 2^m$ with $W_s = W_r$ gives the ceiling.
- [ ] **Satellite links ($T_p \approx 270$ ms):** Always use Selective Repeat; GBN is too wasteful.
- [ ] **Real-world descendants:** TCP's flow control window; HDLC; 5G NR HARQ.
- [ ] **Favourite KTU problem type:** "Given $R$, $L$, $d$, find (i) SAW utilization, (ii) minimum $W_s$ for pipelining, (iii) bits `m` for sequence numbers." Practice at least 3 such problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer: Channel Allocation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer: Channel Allocation Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The MAC Sublayer — Formal Definition
The **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer** is the lower sublayer of the OSI Data Link Layer (Layer 2) responsible for **regulating access to a shared physical transmission medium** (e.g., a copper cable, fiber strand, or radio frequency band). In broadcast networks where multiple nodes share a single channel, the MAC sublayer dictates *who may transmit, when, and for how long* — preventing signal collisions and ensuring orderly, fair utilization of the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Channel Allocation Problem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The *Channel Allocation Problem* is the fundamental challenge in MAC design that asks: *Given a finite shared broadcast channel of capacity $C$ bps and $N$ independent users with variable traffic demands, how do we allocate the channel efficiently to maximize throughput, minimize delay, and ensure fairness?*

### 1.2 The Three Core Conflicting Goals
Every channel allocation scheme must balance the following **three competing objectives**:
1. **Throughput Maximization** — carry as many frames/second as possible.
2. **Delay Minimization** — keep queueing + access delay low.
3. **Fairness** — no single user should monopolize the channel indefinitely.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polite Dinner Table"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Multi-Table Restaurant**
> Imagine 10 friends seated at a single round dinner table trying to have 10 separate conversations simultaneously. The "air" around the table is the **shared medium**. If everyone speaks at once, the result is **noise (collision)** — no message gets through.
> The **Channel Allocation Problem** is essentially: *What rules of conversation should the table adopt so that everyone gets to speak, the dinner finishes quickly (low delay), and no one feels ignored (fairness)?*
> - **Static allocation (FDM)** = assign each friend a fixed 36° wedge of the table to speak into.
> - **Dynamic allocation (ALOHA, CSMA)** = everyone listens, and when silence is detected, someone politely speaks.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Channel Capacity ($C$):** measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Frame Transmission Time ($T$):** time to push one frame onto the medium, where $T = L/C$ (frame length $L$ in bits).
- **Traffic Intensity / Load ($G$):** mean number of transmission attempts per frame time.
- **Throughput ($S$):** mean number of *successful* transmissions per frame time. Unit: **dimensionless (frames/frame-time)**.
- **Propagation Delay ($a$):** ratio of propagation time to transmission time, $a = t_{prop} / t_{trans}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Approaches to Channel Allocation

#### A. Static Channel Allocation
Pre-divides the channel into fixed slices using **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** or **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)**.

**KTU High-Yield Insight:** For $N$ users each requiring $C/N$ bandwidth under FDM:
- Total capacity remains $C$, but each user is **wasted** when idle.
- Best for **deterministic, continuous-traffic** streams (e.g., traditional voice trunks).
- **Major Flaw:** Poor for **bursty** computer traffic (a user idle 99% of the time still holds 1/N of the bandwidth).

#### B. Dynamic Channel Allocation
Five standard assumptions underpinning the analysis (as in Tanenbaum / Kurose-Ross):
1. **Station Model** — $N$ independent stations generating frames; single-channel, single-duplex.
2. **Single Channel Assumption** — one shared broadcast medium, no priority.
3. **Collision Assumption** — if two frames overlap in time, both are garbled (collision).
4. **Continuous Time vs. Slotted Time** — Pure ALOHA vs. Slotted ALOHA.
5. **Carrier Sense** — stations can (CSMA) or cannot (ALOHA) sense the channel before transmitting.

### 2.2 The MAC Protocol Family Tree

1. **Random Access (Contention-Based)**
   - Pure ALOHA
   - Slotted ALOHA
   - CSMA (1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent)
   - CSMA/CD (Ethernet)
   - CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi 802.11)

2. **Controlled Access (Deterministic)**
   - Reservation
   - Polling
   - Token Passing (Token Ring, Token Bus)

3. **Channelization**
   - FDMA, TDMA, CDMA

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Protocol** | **Throughput $S$ formula** | **Max Throughput $S_{max}$** | **Vulnerable Period** | **KTU Exam Tag** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | $2T$ | High-priority |
| Slotted ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | $T$ | High-priority |
| 1-persistent CSMA | $\approx \frac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)}}{G(1+a) + e^{-Ga}}$ | $> 0.5$ (at low $a$) | $2t_{prop} + T$ | Medium |
| Non-persistent CSMA | $\approx \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | $\approx 0.8$ (at low $a$) | Reduced | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Units Convention (KTU Board):** All $S$ and $G$ are **dimensionless** (frames per frame-time). Time values are in **frame-times** (i.e., normalized by $T$). Do not confuse $G$ (load) with $G$ (Giga).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Pure ALOHA:** historical satellite packet networks (ALOHAnet, 1970, Hawaii — Norman Abramson).
- **Slotted ALOHA:** RFID tag-to-reader anti-collision algorithms.
- **CSMA/CD:** 10BASE5/10BASE2 classical Ethernet, half-duplex twisted-pair.
- **CSMA/CA:** IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi, IEEE 802.15.4 ZigBee (collision *avoidance* since wireless cannot detect collisions efficiently).
- **Token Ring:** legacy IBM Token Ring at 4/16 Mbps; FDDI backbone at 100 Mbps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Pure ALOHA Throughput Derivation

We consider a continuous-time channel where all stations transmit frames of fixed length $T$. The **traffic load** $G$ is the mean number of attempted transmissions per frame-time, and we assume the number of attempts in any interval of length $t$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $G \cdot t / T$ (i.e., $G \cdot (t/T)$).

**Step 1 — Poisson Arrival Probability**
The probability of $k$ transmission attempts occurring in a time interval of length $\Delta t$ is:

$$
P[k] = \frac{(G \cdot \Delta t / T)^{k} \cdot e^{-(G \cdot \Delta t / T)}}{k!}
$$

**Step 2 — Vulnerability Window for Pure ALOHA**
In Pure ALOHA, a frame sent at time $t_0$ will be **destroyed** if any other station transmits a frame in the open interval $(t_0 - T, \, t_0 + T)$. This is the **vulnerable period of length $2T$**.

**Step 3 — Probability of Zero Interferers**
For the frame to survive, *no other station* may begin transmission in this $2T$ window. Expected number of attempts in window $2T$ is $2G$ (since $\Delta t / T = 2$). Using Poisson with mean $2G$, the probability of *zero* arrivals is:

$$
P[\text{success} \mid \text{send at } t_0] = \frac{(2G)^{0} \cdot e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput Definition**
Throughput $S$ equals load $G$ multiplied by probability of success per attempt:

$$
S = G \cdot P[\text{success}] = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step analysis of $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$:**
> - When $G \to 0$, the exponential dominates and $S \to 0$ (no one transmits).
> - When $G \to \infty$, $e^{-2G} \to 0$ faster than $G \to \infty$, so $S \to 0$ (total chaos).
> - There is a **single interior maximum** — found by calculus in Step 5.

**Step 5 — Differentiate to Find Maximum**
Setting $\frac{dS}{dG} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0
$$

Solving: $1 - 2G = 0 \Rightarrow G = \frac{1}{2}$.

Substituting $G = 1/2$ back into $S$:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-2 \cdot (1/2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.18394
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** $S_{max} = 1/(2e)$ is for *Pure* ALOHA only. Do not write $1/e$ for Pure ALOHA — that is Slotted ALOHA's value.

---

### 3.2 Slotted ALOHA Throughput Derivation

In Slotted ALOHA, time is **discretized into slots of duration $T$**, and a station may only begin transmission at the start of a slot. The vulnerable period shrinks from $2T$ to $T$.

**Step 1 — Reduced Vulnerability**
A frame now collides only if some *other* station transmits in the *same* slot. Expected number of attempts per slot (other than our own) is $G - 1 \approx G$ for $G \gg 1$, and $G$ for the channel as a whole.

**Step 2 — Poisson Probability of No Collisions**
In a window of length $T$ (one slot), expected attempts = $G$. Probability of *only our frame* (i.e., one attempt total) is:

$$
P[\text{success}] = G \cdot e^{-G} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = e^{-G}
$$

(Using the probability that exactly one of the $G$ Poisson-arriving attempts is the one we already made — by independence, $P[N=1 \text{ others}] \propto G e^{-G}$, simplified to $e^{-G}$ per unit load for the standard normalization.)

**Step 3 — Throughput Formula**

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum**
Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \Rightarrow G = 1
$$

At $G = 1$:

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slotted ALOHA is exactly 2× more efficient than Pure ALOHA:** $1/e \approx 0.368$ vs. $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$. This is the **single most-tested numerical fact** in KTU Module-2 question papers.

---

### 3.3 CSMA Throughput — Key Engineering Intuition

For **1-persistent CSMA**, the throughput (Kleinrock's formula) is:

$$
S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)}}{G(1+a) + e^{-Ga}}
$$

where $a = t_{prop} / t_{trans}$ is the ratio of propagation to transmission time. As $a \to 0$ (LAN with very small propagation), $S_{max} \to 1$ — almost the entire channel capacity can be used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CSMA/CD over coax Ethernet worked so well precisely because $a$ is tiny for short cables. For **satellite links** with $a \approx 270$ (e.g., 50,000 km GEO round-trip / 1500-byte frame), Pure ALOHA may outperform CSMA!

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Metric** | **Pure ALOHA** | **Slotted ALOHA** | **1-persistent CSMA** | **Non-persistent CSMA** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Throughput $S_{max}$ | $0.184$ | $0.368$ | Up to $0.53$ | Up to $0.85$ |
| Best at $a$ | All $a$ | All $a$ | $a \to 0$ | All $a$ |
| Vulnerable Period | $2T$ | $T$ | $2t_{prop} + T$ | Smaller |
| Real-time Complexity | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | **Very High** | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CSMA State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Idle --> Sense: Ready to send
    Sense --> Transmit: Channel idle 1 persistent

    Wait --> Sense: Backoff timer expires
    Transmit --> DetectCD: Listen while sending CSMA CD
    DetectCD --> Jam: Collision detected
    DetectCD --> Success: Transmission completes
    Jam --> Backoff: Send jam signal
    Backoff --> Sense: Binary exponential backoff

```
