<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Channel Allocation Problem. List the three primary goals of any MAC protocol.*

**Model Answer:**
The Channel Allocation Problem is the MAC-layer challenge of fairly, efficiently, and rapidly distributing a single shared broadcast channel of capacity $C$ among $N$ contending users with stochastic traffic demands. The three primary goals are:
1. **Maximizing throughput** (successful frames/second on the medium).
2. **Minimizing average packet delay** (waiting + transmission + propagation time).
3. **Ensuring fairness** (no station starved indefinitely, bounded worst-case waiting time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark per correctly stated goal. Always number them — do not write a paragraph.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is Slotted ALOHA exactly twice as efficient as Pure ALOHA? State the maximum throughput of each.*

**Model Answer:**
Slotted ALOHA forces all stations to begin transmissions only at **discretized slot boundaries** of length $T$ (one frame-time). This reduces the **vulnerable period** of a frame from $2T$ (Pure ALOHA) to $T$ (Slotted ALOHA), halving the window during which a collision can occur.

$$
S_{max}^{\text{Pure ALOHA}} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184, \quad S_{max}^{\text{Slotted ALOHA}} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

Since $\frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2$, Slotted ALOHA achieves **double** the maximum throughput.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the throughput equation $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ for Pure ALOHA. Show that maximum throughput is $1/(2e)$ at $G = 1/2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear diagram, explain the operation of **Slotted ALOHA**. Compare its maximum throughput and vulnerable period with Pure ALOHA. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution A(a):**
1. Assume Poisson arrival of frames with mean $G$ per frame-time $T$. (1 mark)
2. Vulnerable period for a frame starting at $t_0$ is the interval $(t_0 - T, t_0 + T)$, length $2T$. (1 mark)
3. Expected arrivals in $2T$ window is $2G$. (1 mark)
4. Probability of zero arrivals in this window: $P[0] = e^{-2G}$. (1 mark)
5. Throughput $S = (\text{attempts per frame-time}) \times P[\text{no collision}] = G \cdot e^{-2G}$. (1 mark)
6. Differentiate: $dS/dG = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 \Rightarrow G = 1/2$. (1 mark)
7. Substitute: $S_{max} = (1/2) \cdot e^{-1} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$. (1 mark)

#### **Model Solution A(b):**
- **Diagram:** Show time axis divided into equal slots of length $T$; stations transmit only at slot start. (2 marks)
- **Operation:** (1) A station waits for the next slot boundary; (2) transmits one frame in the slot; (3) waits for ACK within 1 slot; (4) if no ACK, randomly retransmits in a future slot. (2 marks)
- **Comparison table:** (3 marks)

| **Property** | **Pure ALOHA** | **Slotted ALOHA** |
|---|---|---|
| Time | Continuous | Discrete slots |
| Vulnerable Period | $2T$ | $T$ |
| $S_{max}$ | $0.184$ | $0.368$ |
| Synchronization | Not required | **Required** |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (clock sync) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** What is the Channel Allocation Problem? Compare static (FDM/TDM) and dynamic channel allocation methods. State **two advantages and two disadvantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A group of $N = 50$ stations share a 1 Mbps pure ALOHA channel. Each station generates a frame of size 1000 bits at an average rate of 5 frames/second. **(i)** Compute the traffic load $G$. **(ii)** Find the throughput $S$. **(iii)** State whether the channel is overloaded. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution B(a):**
**Channel Allocation Problem:** (2 marks) The MAC-layer challenge of dividing a finite shared broadcast channel of capacity $C$ among $N$ contending users so as to maximize throughput, minimize delay, and ensure fairness. See Section 1.1 above.

**Static vs Dynamic Allocation:** (5 marks total)

| **Aspect** | **Static (FDM/TDM)** | **Dynamic (ALOHA/CSMA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Allocation | Fixed at design | Demand-based, per-packet |
| Best for | Steady, continuous traffic | Bursty computer traffic |
| Flexibility | **Low** | **High** |
| Utilization under bursty load | **Poor** (idle slices wasted) | **High** |
| Implementation | Simple | Complex (sensing, backoff) |
| Delay under low load | Higher (waits for slot) | Lower |

**Two advantages of Static:** (a) Predictable delay, (b) No collisions, (c) Simple hardware.
**Two disadvantages of Static:** (a) Bandwidth wasted when idle, (b) Inflexible to load changes, (c) Number of users capped at design time.

**Two advantages of Dynamic:** (a) Adapts to bursty traffic, (b) Higher utilization, (c) Scales to variable $N$.
**Two disadvantages of Dynamic:** (a) Collisions possible, (b) Higher delay under overload, (c) More complex MAC logic.

#### **Model Solution B(b):**
**Given:** $C = 1$ Mbps, $L = 1000$ bits, $\lambda = 5$ frames/sec per station, $N = 50$ stations.

**(i) Compute frame transmission time $T$ and load $G$:** (3 marks)

$$
T = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/sec}} = 10^{-3} \text{ sec} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

Total offered load per frame-time:

$$
G = N \cdot \lambda \cdot T = 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.25
$$

**(ii) Pure ALOHA throughput:** (2 marks)

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G} = 0.25 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.25 \times 0.6065 = 0.1516
$$

**(iii) Channel status:** (2 marks)
Since $G = 0.25 < G_{opt} = 0.5$, the channel is **underloaded** and operating on the *rising slope* of the $S$–$G$ curve. Throughput will increase if load increases. The channel is **not** overloaded; collisions are infrequent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2 MAC):**
> 1. **Mixing up formulas** — $G e^{-2G}$ is for Pure, $G e^{-G}$ is for Slotted. A 3-mark deduction for swapping them.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — $T = L/C$ must be in seconds, $G$ is dimensionless. Mixing $G$ (load) with $G$ (Giga) loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "why vulnerable period is $2T$" explanation** — board expects a labelled timeline diagram, not just the formula.
> 4. **Writing $S_{max} = 0.184$ without specifying "Pure ALOHA"** — you must name the protocol every time.
> 5. **Confusing $a$ (propagation ratio) with $t_{prop}$** in CSMA problems — they are NOT the same variable.
> 6. **CSMA/CD requires minimum frame size $2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot C$** to ensure collision detection — a frequent Module 2 Part-B sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PCITT402 Module 2 (MAC):**
> - [ ] **Channel Allocation Problem** = allocate shared channel $C$ among $N$ users → 3 goals: throughput, delay, fairness.
> - [ ] **Static (FDM/TDM):** fixed slices, predictable but inefficient for bursty traffic.
> - [ ] **Dynamic:** demand-based, 3 families — random, controlled, channelization.
> - [ ] **Pure ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$, vulnerable period $= 2T$.
> - [ ] **Slotted ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$ at $G = 1$, vulnerable period $= T$, requires slot synchronization.
> - [ ] **Slotted is 2× Pure** — the single most-tested numerical fact.
> - [ ] **CSMA:** carrier sensing reduces collisions; throughput $\to 1$ as $a \to 0$.
> - [ ] **1-persistent** transmits immediately when idle; **non-persistent** waits random backoff; **p-persistent** transmits with probability $p$ on idle slot.
> - [ ] **CSMA/CD** (Ethernet): detects collisions, sends jam signal, uses **binary exponential backoff**; requires $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_{prop} \cdot C$.
> - [ ] **CSMA/CA** (Wi-Fi 802.11): cannot detect collisions on wireless → uses RTS/CTS + ACK + NAV.
> - [ ] **Key parameter** $a = t_{prop}/t_{trans}$: small $a$ = LAN, large $a$ = satellite.
> - [ ] **Traffic load formula:** $G = N \cdot \lambda \cdot T$ where $T = L/C$.
> - [ ] **Throughput per user** = $S / N$; throughput in frames/sec = $S / T$.
> - [ ] **Controlled access:** Reservation, Polling, Token Passing (deterministic, no collisions, but token loss = failure).
> - [ ] **Channelization:** FDMA, TDMA, CDMA — used in cellular, satellite, GPS.
> - [ ] **Historical context:** ALOHAnet (1970, Hawaii) — first wireless packet network, by Norman Abramson at the University of Hawaii.
> - [ ] **Kurose-Ross 5 assumptions** of the dynamic allocation model are often asked as 3-mark Part-A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Protocols (ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access Protocols (ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Multiple Access Protocols?

**Multiple Access Protocols** are a set of rules and procedures that govern how multiple independent users (nodes/stations) share a common communication medium (channel) in a computer network. Since the channel is a *shared, finite resource*, only one (or a few) stations can transmit successfully at any given time without causing interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** Multiple access protocols belong to the **Data Link Layer (Sublayer – MAC / Medium Access Control)** of the OSI reference model. They define the algorithm used by nodes to determine *when* and *how* they may transmit on a shared broadcast channel, thereby resolving contention and maximizing channel utilization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cocktail Party

Imagine a **cocktail party** with 50 guests in a single room:

- The **room (air)** = the shared wireless channel
- The **guests** = the transmitting stations
- **Speaking etiquette** = the multiple access protocol

**Three possible party rules:**

1. **Pure ALOHA Party:** Every guest speaks whenever they feel like it. If two people talk simultaneously, they both stop and wait a random time before trying again. *(Very chaotic, lots of collisions.)*
2. **Slotted ALOHA Party:** A host blows a whistle every fixed interval. Guests may *only* speak immediately after a whistle. *(Slightly better, but still messy.)*
3. **CSMA Party (Carrier Sense):** Before speaking, every guest *listens* to check if the room is silent. If silent → they speak. If someone is already talking → they wait. *(Polite, efficient, and how real meetings work.)*

> [!NOTE]
> The entire evolution from **Pure ALOHA → Slotted ALOHA → CSMA → CSMA/CD → CSMA/CA** is essentially the engineering journey of teaching machines to be *polite party guests* on a shared channel.

### 1.3 Classification of Multiple Access Protocols

Multiple access protocols are broadly classified into three families (per the **KTU 2024 syllabus**):

| Family | Sub-Techniques | Channel Sensing? | Collision Handling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Random Access** | Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA | Optional (CSMA does) | Detect & retransmit |
| **Controlled Access** | Reservation, Polling, Token Passing | No contention | Centralized or token-based |
| **Channelization** | FDMA, TDMA, CDMA | No contention | Frequency / Time / Code division |

This module focuses on the **Random Access** family, which is the most heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The three fundamental metrics used by examiners worldwide:

- **Throughput (S):** The fraction of channel capacity used for *successful* transmission. Measured in **frames per frame-time**. Ranges from **0 to 1**.
- **Offered Load / Traffic Intensity (G):** The average number of transmission attempts (successes + collisions) per frame-time.
- **Vulnerable Time:** The window during which a collision can occur if two stations transmit.
- **Propagation Delay (a):** Ratio of propagation time to transmission time. Defines the *spatial* extent of a collision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** When `G = 1` (one attempt per frame-time on average), it is *not* the same as `S = 1` (full utilization). Collisions consume channel capacity and reduce throughput.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PURE ALOHA — The Original Random Access Protocol

**Invented at the University of Hawaii in 1971** for the ALOHANET radio network. It is the *grandparent* of all random access protocols.

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

1. A station has a frame to send → it transmits **immediately** (no listening, no waiting).
2. After transmitting, it waits for an **acknowledgment (ACK)** from the receiver.
3. If **ACK is received** within a timeout → success.
4. If **ACK is NOT received** (or a NACK comes) → the station assumes a **collision** occurred.
5. The station waits a **random backoff time** (each station picks a random `k` and waits `k × T_prop` or a slotted backoff) and **retransmits**.
6. The process repeats until success (with a maximum retry limit to avoid starvation).

### 2.1.2 Vulnerable Time in Pure ALOHA

A collision occurs if **any other station transmits a frame whose bits overlap** with the current frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerable Time for Pure ALOHA = 2 × T_frame**
>
> *Why?* A collision can happen if another frame starts **T_frame seconds before** *or* **T_frame seconds after** the start of our frame. The total danger window is `2T`.

### 2.1.3 Throughput Analysis (Poisson Model)

Assume frame arrivals follow a **Poisson distribution** with mean `G` attempts per frame-time.

The probability of **zero other arrivals** in the vulnerable window `2T` is:

$$P(\text{success}) = e^{-2G}$$

Therefore, the throughput is:

$$S = G \times e^{-2G}$$

To find the **maximum throughput**, differentiate with respect to `G` and set to zero:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} - 2G \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0$$

Solving gives **`G = 1/2`**, and substituting back:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184 \quad \text{(or 18.4\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At optimum, `G = 0.5` and `S = 0.184`. This means the channel is *idle* (1 − 0.184 = 81.6%) or *colliding* (0.5 − 0.184 = 31.6%) most of the time. **Only ~18% efficiency** — a strong motivation for improvement.

---

## 2.2 SLOTTED ALOHA — The Time-Discrete Improvement

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle

Invented by **Roberts (1972)**. Introduces a **discrete time-slotted channel**:

1. Time is divided into equal **slots of duration T_frame**.
2. A station **may only transmit at the BEGINNING of a slot** (must wait for slot boundary).
3. A central clock or sync mechanism (pilot tone) coordinates all stations.
4. If collision occurs → random backoff to a *future* slot, then retransmit.

### 2.2.2 Vulnerable Time in Slotted ALOHA

Since transmission can *only* start at a slot boundary, a frame can collide with another frame **only if it starts in the same slot**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerable Time for Slotted ALOHA = T_frame (exactly)**
>
> *Why?* Only other transmissions starting in our slot can collide. Transmissions starting T_frame later arrive *after* our frame has finished.

### 2.2.3 Throughput Analysis

Probability of **zero other arrivals** in our slot of duration `T` is `e^{-G}`.

$$S = G \times e^{-G}$$

Maximizing:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1$$

$$S_{max} = 1 \times e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \quad \text{(or 36.8\%)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slotted ALOHA is EXACTLY 2× the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA** (36.8% vs 18.4%). This is the single most-asked numerical fact in KTU exams.

### 2.2.4 Graphical Comparison

| Load G | Pure ALOHA S = Ge^(−2G) | Slotted ALOHA S = Ge^(−G) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 0.0819 | 0.0905 |
| 0.5 | **0.1839 (peak)** | 0.3033 |
| 1.0 | 0.1353 | **0.3679 (peak)** |
| 2.0 | 0.0366 | 0.2707 |
| 5.0 | 0.0337 | 0.0337 |

---

## 2.3 CARRIER SENSE MULTIPLE ACCESS (CSMA)

**Key Insight:** Both ALOHA schemes are wasteful because stations *transmit blindly* even when the channel is busy. CSMA improves this by **listening before talking** (Listen Before Talk — LBT).

### 2.3.1 CSMA Operational Principle

1. Station wants to transmit → **Carrier Sense**: it *listens* to the channel.
2. If channel is **IDLE** → station transmits.
3. If channel is **BUSY** → station defers transmission based on its **persistence strategy**.

> [!NOTE]
> CSMA still suffers from **propagation delay collisions**: if station A begins transmitting, the signal takes time `T_prop` to reach station B. If B starts transmitting within that propagation window, a collision occurs. This is the fundamental limitation CSMA/CD aims to solve.

### 2.3.2 The 3 Variants of CSMA

#### **(a) 1-Persistent CSMA**
- Station senses channel.
- If **idle** → transmits **immediately with probability 1**.
- If **busy** → waits until idle, then transmits with probability 1.
- **Pros:** Low delay when idle. **Cons:** If multiple stations wait for an idle channel, **all transmit simultaneously** → guaranteed collision.

#### **(b) Non-Persistent CSMA**
- Station senses channel.
- If **idle** → transmits.
- If **busy** → waits a **random backoff time** before sensing again.
- **Pros:** Reduces collisions. **Cons:** Wastes channel time during the random wait (channel may be idle while stations are sleeping).

#### **(c) p-Persistent CSMA**
*(Used on slotted channels.)*
- Station senses channel.
- If **idle** → transmits with probability `p`, defers to next slot with probability `(1-p)`.
- If **busy** → waits until next slot, then repeats.
- **Pros:** Balances delay and collision. **Cons:** Requires slotted channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The persistence parameter `p` controls the throughput-delay trade-off. Higher `p` → lower delay, higher collision risk. Lower `p` → higher delay, lower collision risk.

---

## 2.4 CSMA / CD (Collision Detection)

CSMA alone cannot *stop* a collision once it has started — it can only sense the channel *before* transmission. **CSMA/CD (Ethernet's protocol)** improves this by detecting collisions *during* transmission and aborting immediately.

### 2.4.1 Operational Principle

1. Station senses the channel; if idle, begins transmitting.
2. **While transmitting**, it *continuously monitors* the signal on the medium.
3. If the monitored signal energy **exceeds the transmitted energy** → a collision is detected (because some other station's signal is being added).
4. On detection → station **immediately aborts transmission**, transmits a **32-bit jam sequence** (to ensure all other stations also detect the collision), then enters **binary exponential backoff**.
5. After the backoff, the cycle repeats.

### 2.4.2 Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)

After the `i`-th consecutive collision, the station picks a random `k` from the set:

$$k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^i - 1\}$$

and waits `k × 512 bit times` (for IEEE 802.3 Ethernet). After 10 collisions, `i` is capped at 10; after 16 collisions, the frame is dropped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "exponential"?** The window doubles after every collision: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ..., 1024 slots. This adapts to network load — few stations = short waits, many stations = long waits.

### 2.4.3 CSMA/CD Throughput

For a network with `a = T_{prop} / T_{trans}`:

$$S_{CSMA/CD} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4a} \quad \text{(approximation for Ethernet)}$$

For `a → 0` (LANs), throughput approaches 1. For large `a` (WANs), throughput drops sharply.

---

## 2.5 CSMA / CA (Collision Avoidance)

Used in **wireless networks (IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi)** where **collision detection is impractical** (a station cannot simultaneously transmit and listen on the same radio frequency — the local transmission overwhelms any incoming signal).

### 2.5.1 CSMA/CA Strategy

1. Sense the channel.
2. If **idle for a DIFS** (Distributed Inter-Frame Space) → transmit.
3. If **busy** → wait a random backoff (using a **Contention Window** with a **Network Allocation Vector / NAV**).
4. After waiting, sense again; if still idle → transmit.
5. **Positive ACKs:** receiver sends ACK after a SIFS. If sender does not receive ACK within timeout → assumes collision → doubles contention window and retries.

> [!NOTE]
> **CSMA/CA trades efficiency for safety:** it avoids collisions proactively (using ACKs and backoffs) because it *cannot* detect them. The 802.11 DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) is the canonical example.

---

## 2.6 KTU FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU question on this topic maps to one of these formulas.**

| # | Protocol | Throughput Formula `S = f(G)` | Max Throughput `S_max` | Optimum Load `G_opt` | Vulnerable Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pure ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | $G = 0.5$ | $2 \cdot T_{frame}$ |
| 2 | **Slotted ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | $G = 1.0$ | $T_{frame}$ |
| 3 | **1-Persistent CSMA** | No closed form; ≈ Slotted ALOHA × 0.5 | ~ 0.529 (approx) | — | $2 \cdot T_{prop}$ |
| 4 | **Non-Persistent CSMA** | No closed form | ~ 0.815 (approx) | — | $2 \cdot T_{prop}$ |
| 5 | **CSMA/CD** | $S \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4a}$ where $a = T_{prop}/T_{trans}$ | $\to 1$ as $a \to 0$ | — | $2 \cdot T_{prop}$ |
| 6 | **CSMA/CA** | $S \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{backoff terms}}$ | Lower than CSMA/CD | — | $2 \cdot T_{prop} + \text{DIFS}$ |

### Boundary Conditions & Engineering Utility

- **$e$ (Euler's number) ≈ 2.71828** appears in every ALOHA throughput derivation — it comes from the Poisson assumption of random arrivals.
- **`a` (propagation-to-transmission ratio):** Small `a` means a small, fast network (LAN); large `a` means long, slow network (WAN or satellite). For Ethernet, `a` must be < 1 for CSMA/CD to work (the frame must be at least `2 × T_{prop}` long).
- **Real-world utility:** Slotted ALOHA is used in **RFID** and **satellite uplink** systems. CSMA/CD is the heart of **classical Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**. CSMA/CA powers **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive and KTU-board-ready**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Throughput of Pure ALOHA

**Given:** Frames arrive according to a Poisson process with mean rate `λ` per frame-time `T`. Total offered load is `G = λ × T` attempts per frame-time.

**Step 1 — Define the probability of zero arrivals.**
For a Poisson process with mean `G` over a window of duration `2T` (vulnerable time):

$$P[k \text{ arrivals in } 2T] = \frac{e^{-G \cdot 2} \cdot (2G)^k}{k!}$$

For a successful transmission of a *single* frame, **zero other frames** must start in the vulnerable window `2T`:

$$P[\text{success} \mid \text{our frame started}] = \frac{e^{-2G} \cdot (2G)^0}{0!} = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 2 — Express throughput.**
Throughput = (offered load) × (probability of success):

$$S = G \times e^{-2G}$$

**Step 3 — Find the maximum.**
Differentiate with respect to `G`:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = \frac{d}{dG}\left[ G \cdot e^{-2G} \right]$$

Apply the product rule `(uv)' = u'v + uv'`:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = (1) \cdot e^{-2G} + G \cdot e^{-2G} \cdot (-2)$$

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} - 2G \cdot e^{-2G}$$

Factor out `e^{-2G}` (which is never zero):

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} \cdot (1 - 2G)$$

**Step 4 — Set derivative to zero and solve.**

$$e^{-2G} \cdot (1 - 2G) = 0$$

Since $e^{-2G} \neq 0$ for any finite `G`:

$$1 - 2G = 0 \implies G = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 5 — Confirm it is a maximum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^2S}{dG^2} = \frac{d}{dG}\left[ e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) \right]$$

$$= -2 e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) + e^{-2G}(-2)$$

$$= e^{-2G}\left[ -2(1 - 2G) - 2 \right] = e^{-2G}[-2 + 4G - 2] = e^{-2G}(4G - 4)$$

At $G = 0.5$:

$$\frac{d^2S}{dG^2}\bigg|_{G=0.5} = e^{-1}(4 \times 0.5 - 4) = e^{-1}(-2) = -\frac{2}{e} < 0$$

**Confirmed: maximum.**

**Step 6 — Compute the maximum throughput value.**

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times e^{-2 \times \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{2} \times e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx \frac{1}{5.4366} \approx 0.18394$$

$$\boxed{S_{max}^{\text{Pure ALOHA}} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Throughput of Slotted ALOHA

**Given:** Same Poisson model, but transmission is restricted to the start of slots.

**Step 1 — Vulnerable time is now just one slot `T`.**
The probability of zero other arrivals in the slot of duration `T` is `e^{-G}`.

**Step 2 — Throughput equation:**

$$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G)$$

**Step 4 — Set to zero:**

$$1 - G = 0 \implies G = 1$$

**Step 5 — Second derivative test:**

$$\frac{d^2S}{dG^2} = -e^{-G}(1 - G) + e^{-G}(-1) = e^{-G}(-2 + G)$$

At $G = 1$: $e^{-1}(-1) = -1/e < 0$. **Maximum confirmed.**

**Step 6 — Compute maximum throughput:**

$$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.36788$$

$$\boxed{S_{max}^{\text{Slotted ALOHA}} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the ratio:**

$$\frac{S_{max}^{\text{Slotted}}}{S_{max}^{\text{Pure}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2$$

Slotted ALOHA achieves **exactly double** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA. **This is a guaranteed KTU board question.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vulnerable Time of Pure ALOHA (Visual Proof)

Let `T_frame = t_f` and let station A start transmitting at time `t = 0`. The frame occupies $[0, t_f]$.

A collision happens if *any* other station B transmits a frame that overlaps with A's frame.

**Case 1:** B starts at time $t \in [-t_f, 0]$ (B started *before* A).
B's frame occupies $[t, t + t_f]$. For overlap with A's $[0, t_f]$:

$$t + t_f \geq 0 \implies t \geq -t_f$$

So the B-start time must lie in $[-t_f, 0]$, a window of length $t_f$.

**Case 2:** B starts at time $t \in [0, t_f]$ (B starts *after* A).
B's frame is $[t, t + t_f]$. Overlap requires $t < t_f$ (i.e., B starts before A finishes), so $t \in [0, t_f]$, again a window of length $t_f$.

**Total vulnerable window = $t_f + t_f = 2 t_f$.**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{vuln}}^{\text{Pure ALOHA}} = 2 \cdot T_{frame}}$$

For Slotted ALOHA, transmission only happens at slot boundaries, so only **Case 2** applies (Case 1 cannot happen because B couldn't have started mid-slot):

$$\boxed{T_{\text{vuln}}^{\text{Slotted ALOHA}} = T_{frame}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Throughput at Various Loads

| `G` | Pure ALOHA `S = G·e^(−2G)` | Slotted ALOHA `S = G·e^(−G)` | Channel Idle % (Pure) | Channel Collision % (Pure) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 0.0819 | 0.0905 | 90.0% | 1.8% |
| 0.2 | 0.1224 | 0.1637 | 80.0% | 7.8% |
| 0.3 | 0.1492 | 0.2222 | 70.0% | 15.1% |
| **0.5** | **0.1839 (MAX)** | 0.3033 | 60.0% | 31.6% |
| 0.7 | 0.1744 | 0.3476 | 50.0% | 32.6% |
| **1.0** | 0.1353 | **0.3679 (MAX)** | 30.0% | 36.5% |
| 1.5 | 0.0747 | 0.3347 | 15.0% | 27.5% |
| 2.0 | 0.0366 | 0.2707 | 5.0% | 19.3% |
| 5.0 | 0.000457 | 0.0337 | ~0% | ~1.0% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Beyond `G = 1` in Slotted ALOHA (or `G = 0.5` in Pure), throughput *decreases* despite *more* attempts. This is **channel saturation collapse** — too many collisions destroy efficiency. The KTU board loves to test this concept.

---

## 3.5 CSMA/CD Minimum Frame Size Derivation

**Statement (KTU 2024 syllabus):** For CSMA/CD to function, a frame must be long enough that the **transmission time exceeds 2 × one-way propagation delay**.

**Proof sketch:**

Suppose station A starts transmitting at time $t = 0$. The frame takes time $T_{trans}$ to be fully sent. The collision could occur at the **farthest point** in the network at time $T_{prop}$. The collision signal must then travel *back* to A, arriving at time $2 T_{prop}$.

For A to *detect* this collision, it must still be transmitting:

$$T_{trans} \geq 2 \cdot T_{prop}$$

This is the **IEEE 802.3 minimum frame size rule** of 64 bytes for a 10 Mbps Ethernet with 2500 m max segment length:

$$T_{trans,min} = \frac{512 \text{ bits}}{10 \text{ Mbps}} = 51.2 \text{ μs}$$

$$\implies 2 T_{prop, max} \leq 51.2 \text{ μs} \implies T_{prop,max} \approx 25.6 \text{ μs}$$

For signal propagation at $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable, this yields $\approx 5000$ m round-trip / $\approx 2500$ m one-way — the classic Ethernet 5-4-3 rule.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Multiple Access Protocols. List and briefly explain the categories of multiple access protocols.**

**Model Answer:**

Multiple Access Protocols are a set of rules operating at the **Data Link Layer (MAC sublayer)** that determine how multiple stations share a common broadcast medium. They prevent or manage collisions and ensure fair, efficient channel use.

The three main categories are:

1. **Random Access Protocols:** No station has control; each transmits at will, with rules to handle collisions. Examples: ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA.
2. **Controlled Access Protocols:** A controller (or a token) explicitly grants channel access. Examples: Reservation, Polling, Token Passing.
3. **Channelization Protocols:** The channel's capacity is partitioned by frequency, time, or code. Examples: FDMA, TDMA, CDMA.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is the maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA exactly twice that of Pure ALOHA? Justify with the vulnerable time concept.**

**Model Answer:**

The maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA is $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$, while that of Pure ALOHA is $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$. The ratio is exactly **2**.

**Justification:** In Pure ALOHA, a station may transmit at *any* instant. A collision occurs if another transmission begins within a vulnerable window of **$2 T_{frame}$** (one frame-time *before* and one frame-time *after* the start of our frame). In Slotted ALOHA, stations may *only* transmit at the start of a discrete slot, so the vulnerable window is reduced to **$T_{frame}$** — exactly half. Halving the vulnerable time halves the collision probability, and the resulting throughput is doubled:

$$\frac{S_{max}^{\text{Slotted}}}{S_{max}^{\text{Pure}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### **(a) Explain the working of Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA protocols in detail. Compare their maximum throughput. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pure ALOHA:**
- Developed at the University of Hawaii in 1971 for the ALOHANET.
- A station with a ready frame **transmits immediately** without checking the channel.
- After transmission, it waits for an **acknowledgment (ACK)**.
- If ACK is not received within a timeout, the station assumes a **collision** and retransmits after a **random backoff**.
- The vulnerable time is $2 T_{frame}$: any other transmission starting within one frame-time before or after our start will collide.

The probability of a successful transmission follows the Poisson model:

$$S_{\text{Pure}} = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$

Differentiating to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 \implies G = 0.5$$

$$S_{max}^{\text{Pure}} = 0.5 \times e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$$

**Slotted ALOHA:**
- Proposed by Roberts (1972) to improve Pure ALOHA.
- Time is divided into **discrete slots of duration $T_{frame}$** synchronized across all stations.
- A station **may transmit only at the beginning of a slot**; otherwise it must wait.
- Vulnerable time is reduced to $T_{frame}$: only transmissions starting in the *same* slot can collide.

The throughput equation is:

$$S_{\text{Slotted}} = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1$$

$$S_{max}^{\text{Slotted}} = 1 \times e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$$

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Time | Continuous | Discrete slots |
| Vulnerable time | $2 T_{frame}$ | $T_{frame}$ |
| Throughput formula | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ |
| Optimum `G` | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| Maximum throughput | $1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$ | $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ |
| Complexity | Low | Requires synchronization |

$$\boxed{\frac{S_{max}^{\text{Slotted}}}{S_{max}^{\text{Pure}}} = 2}$$

#### **(b) For Pure ALOHA, derive the expression for throughput and find the maximum throughput value. If the channel is operating at a load G = 0.3, calculate the throughput and the probability of collision. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Throughput (already shown in Section 3.1):**

Using the Poisson arrival model with mean offered load `G` per frame-time, the probability that no other frame starts in the vulnerable window $2T$ is:

$$P[\text{no collision}] = e^{-2G}$$

Throughput = (number of attempts per frame-time) × (probability of success):

$$\boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-2G}}$$

**Maximum throughput:**
At $G = 0.5$:

$$S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839 \quad \text{(or 18.4%)}$$

**At G = 0.3:**

Throughput:

$$S = 0.3 \cdot e^{-2 \times 0.3} = 0.3 \cdot e^{-0.6}$$

Using $e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$:

$$S = 0.3 \times 0.5488 = 0.1646 \quad \text{(or 16.46%)}$$

Probability of collision (per attempt):

$$P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - P[\text{success}] = 1 - e^{-2G} = 1 - e^{-0.6}$$

$$P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - 0.5488 = 0.4512 \quad \text{(or 45.12%)}$$

**Number of frames successfully transmitted per frame-time:** $S = 0.1646$

**Number of frames colliding per frame-time:** $G \times P_{\text{collision}} = 0.3 \times 0.4512 = 0.1354$

**Frame transmissions wasted on collisions:** $(0.3 - 0.1646) = 0.1354$ per frame-time (which matches).

$$\boxed{S \approx 0.1646, \quad P_{\text{collision}} \approx 0.4512}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

#### **(a) Explain the working of CSMA protocols. Compare 1-persistent, non-persistent, and p-persistent CSMA. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA):**
- The fundamental improvement over ALOHA: stations **listen to the channel before transmitting**.
- If the channel is sensed **busy**, the station defers transmission based on its **persistence strategy**.
- CSMA drastically reduces collisions by avoiding the "blind transmission" of ALOHA.

**1-Persistent CSMA:**
- On sensing the channel:
  - If **idle** → transmit immediately (with probability 1).
  - If **busy** → continuously monitor until idle, then transmit immediately.
- **Advantage:** Minimum delay when the channel is idle.
- **Disadvantage:** If two or more stations are waiting for the channel to go idle, they will *all* transmit the moment it goes idle, causing a **guaranteed collision**. Performance is poor under high load.

**Non-Persistent CSMA:**
- On sensing the channel:
  - If **idle** → transmit immediately.
  - If **busy** → wait a **random backoff interval**, then re-sense.
- **Advantage:** Significantly fewer collisions (random wait desynchronizes stations).
- **Disadvantage:** Wastes channel time — the channel may be idle while stations are in their random wait, increasing **average delay**.

**p-Persistent CSMA** (used on **slotted** channels):
- On sensing the channel:
  - If **idle** → transmit with probability `p`; defer to next slot with probability `(1-p)`.
  - If **busy** → wait until next slot, then repeat.
- **Advantage:** Splits the difference — lower collision than 1-persistent, lower delay than non-persistent.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires **synchronized slotted channel**; `p` must be chosen carefully (small `p` = good under high load, large `p` = good under low load).

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | 1-Persistent | Non-Persistent | p-Persistent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmission on idle | Immediate (prob 1) | Immediate (prob 1) | Probability `p` |
| Action on busy | Wait until idle, then go | Random backoff, re-sense | Wait one slot, then re-sense |
| Collisions | High | Low | Moderate, tunable |
| Delay | Low (when idle) | High (random waits) | Moderate |
| Channel type | Continuous | Continuous | Slotted |

#### **(b) Explain CSMA/CD with a neat diagram. Discuss Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm. A network using CSMA/CD has a bandwidth of 10 Mbps. If the maximum propagation delay is 25.6 μs, find the minimum frame size. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection):**
Used in **classical Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**. Adds *collision detection* to CSMA — a station can sense a collision *while* it is still transmitting and aborts immediately.

**Working Steps:**
1. **Carrier sense:** Listen to channel.
2. **If idle** → start transmitting.
3. **While transmitting** → continuously monitor the signal energy on the medium.
4. **If signal energy exceeds the transmitted energy** → a **collision** is detected (another station's signal is being superimposed).
5. **Abort transmission immediately**, send a **32-bit jam sequence** to ensure all other stations also detect the collision.
6. **Binary Exponential Backoff** — wait a random time and retry.
7. **Wait for ACK**; if not received within timeout, assume lost and re-enter backoff.

**Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) Algorithm:**

After the `i`-th consecutive collision (capped at `i = 10`):

1. Choose a random integer `k` uniformly from $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^i - 1\}$.
2. Wait for `k × Slot_Time` (Slot_Time = 512 bit times for IEEE 802.3).
3. After waiting, re-sense the channel and attempt retransmission.
4. If collision recurs, double the window.
5. After 16 consecutive collisions, **drop the frame** (report error to upper layer).

**The window sizes progress as:** $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512$ slots.

**Why "exponential"?** Light traffic = short wait (few collisions). Heavy traffic = long wait (spread out retries). This adaptively handles dynamic network loads.

**Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth `B = 10 Mbps = 10 \times 10^6$ bits/sec
- Maximum propagation delay $T_{prop,max} = 25.6\ \mu s = 25.6 \times 10^{-6}$ s

**Formula for minimum frame size in CSMA/CD:**

The frame must be long enough that its transmission time exceeds $2 \times T_{prop}$ (so the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns):

$$T_{trans,min} \geq 2 \cdot T_{prop,max}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $2 \cdot T_{prop,max}$:**

$$2 T_{prop,max} = 2 \times 25.6\ \mu s = 51.2\ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute minimum frame size:**

$$L_{min} = B \times 2 T_{prop,max} = (10 \times 10^6) \times (51.2 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$L_{min} = 10 \times 51.2 = 512\ \text{bits}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes:**

$$L_{min} = \frac{512\ \text{bits}}{8\ \text{bits/byte}} = 64\ \text{bytes}$$

$$\boxed{L_{min} = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** This is exactly the IEEE 802.3 standard minimum frame size of **64 bytes**. Any frame shorter than this is illegal and would be padded by the sender (a "runt frame").

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing `S` and `G`:** Students often mistakenly write `G = e^{−2G}` for Pure ALOHA. The throughput is `G × e^{−2G}`, *not* `e^{−2G}` alone.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in vulnerable time:** Always explicitly show that Pure ALOHA's vulnerable time is `2T`, not `T`. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for sloppy justification.
> 3. **Wrong optimum `G`:** Maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA occurs at `G = 0.5`, NOT at `G = 1`. Confusing the two is a classic mark-loser.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Always quote the maximum throughput as `1/(2e) ≈ 0.184` **or** `18.4%`, not just `0.184` (the unit interpretation matters).
> 5. **CSMA/CD minimum frame calculation:** Students often forget to **multiply by 2** the propagation delay. The collision signal must travel there AND back, hence the `2T_{prop}` requirement.
> 6. **BEB description:** Examiners want the *complete* algorithm — including the cap at 10 and the frame drop after 16 retries. Skipping these loses 2-3 marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Multiple Access Protocols** operate at the **MAC sublayer** of the **Data Link Layer**.
- [x] Three families: **Random Access**, **Controlled Access**, **Channelization**.
- [x] **Pure ALOHA:** `S = G·e^(−2G)`, `S_max = 1/(2e) ≈ 18.4%` at `G = 0.5`, vulnerable time = `2T`.
- [x] **Slotted ALOHA:** `S = G·e^(−G)`, `S_max = 1/e ≈ 36.8%` at `G = 1.0`, vulnerable time = `T`.
- [x] **Slotted ALOHA throughput = 2 × Pure ALOHA throughput.** (Memorize this ratio!)
- [x] **CSMA** adds *listen-before-talk* to reduce collisions.
- [x] **1-persistent CSMA:** transmit immediately on idle → high collision risk.
- [x] **Non-persistent CSMA:** random backoff on busy → fewer collisions, higher delay.
- [x] **p-persistent CSMA:** transmit with probability `p` on idle → tunable, needs slotted channel.
- [x] **CSMA/CD:** collision *detection* + jam signal + **Binary Exponential Backoff**. Used in Ethernet (IEEE 802.3).
- [x] **CSMA/CA:** collision *avoidance* using DIFS, NAV, contention window, ACKs. Used in Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11).
- [x] **BEB:** window size = $2^i$ where `i` = number of collisions (capped at 10). Drop after 16 failures.
- [x] **Minimum frame size rule:** $T_{trans} \geq 2 T_{prop} \implies L_{min} = 2 \times B \times T_{prop}$.
- [x] For 10 Mbps Ethernet, $L_{min} = 64$ bytes.
- [x] **`a` parameter:** $a = T_{prop}/T_{trans}$. CSMA/CD requires `a < 1` to function.
- [x] **Poisson assumption** underlies all ALOHA throughput derivations — the appearance of $e$ in the formulas is a direct consequence.
- [x] **CSMA** has no closed-form throughput equation but is approximately bounded by slotted ALOHA (above) and 1 (below).
- [x] **CSMA/CD throughput** ≈ $\dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4a}$ — drops sharply for WANs, ideal for LANs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision-Free Protocols - Bit Map Protocol, Token Passing, Binary Countdown, Wireless LAN Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collision-Free Protocols - Bit Map Protocol, Token Passing, Binary Countdown, Wireless LAN Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are Collision-Free Protocols?

A **Collision-Free Protocol** is a class of **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sub-layer protocols that mathematically *guarantee* that no two stations will ever transmit simultaneously on a shared broadcast channel. Unlike contention-based protocols (Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD) where collisions are *expected* and recovered from, collision-free protocols **proactively schedule** or **arbitrate** channel access so that collisions are structurally impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Collision-free protocols allocate the channel to one station at a time, either through a centralized controller, a token-passing mechanism, or by using the binary address bits of competing stations to resolve contention without any wastage of bandwidth.*

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Collision-Free Protocols?

To fully appreciate collision-free protocols, we must understand the *tax* that contention-based protocols pay for their simplicity:

| Issue in Contention Protocols | Practical Consequence |
|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Collision waste** (Slotted ALOHA max efficiency = 37%) | Half the bandwidth lost in best case |
| **CSMA/CD's minimum frame size** (512 bits for 10Mbps Ethernet) | Cannot be used in wireless (cannot sense while transmitting) |
| **Wired cable-length limitation** | CSMA/CD fails for >2.5 km segments |
| **No QoS guarantee** | Voice/video suffer jitter under contention |
| **Unbounded backoff delay** | Unacceptable for real-time traffic |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Round-Table Meeting"

Imagine **N colleagues** sitting around a single shared microphone, and only one person can speak at a time:

- **Pure ALOHA / CSMA** → Everyone just *shouts* when they have something to say. If two people talk at once, both stop, wait a random time, and try again. (Chaotic, wastes time, sometimes nothing gets said).
- **Collision-Free Protocols** → There is a *structured rule* ensuring exactly one person gets the mic at a time:
  - **Bit Map** → A list is circulated; "I want to speak?" is declared in advance.
  - **Token Passing** → A physical "talking stick" is passed around; only the holder speaks.
  - **Binary Countdown** → All who want to speak simultaneously state their *priority number*; the highest priority wins instantly.
  - **Wireless (MACA/MACAW)** → Before speaking, you say "Request to Send"; the receiver says "Clear to Send"; only then you speak. Neighbours hearing these signals stay quiet.

### 1.4 Classification of Collision-Free Protocols

```
COLLISION-FREE PROTOCOLS
│
├── 1. RESERVATION-BASED
│       ├── Bit-Map Protocol (Tanenbaum's classic)
│       └── Reservation Protocols (sub-channel signaling)
│
├── 2. TOKEN-BASED
│       ├── Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)
│       ├── Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) – obsolete
│       └── Token Passing (logical ring on any topology)
│
├── 3. CONTENTION-RESOLUTION (no waste)
│       ├── Binary Countdown (highest bit wins)
│       └── Limited-Contention Protocols (hybrid)
│
└── 4. WIRELESS-SPECIFIC
        ├── MACA (RTS/CTS only)
        ├── MACAW (RTS/CTS/DS/ACK)
        └── IEEE 802.11 DCF (uses CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Exam Insight:** All collision-free protocols trade-off **predictable delay** for **lower peak throughput under low load**. Token ring, for example, is inefficient when only one station has data (token must still circulate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bit-Map Protocol (Reservation Protocol)

**Operational Principle:**
- Time is divided into **contention slots** (each slot = 1 bit time) and **transmission slots** (variable length).
- During the **contention period** (N bit-times for N stations), each station that has a frame to send broadcasts a "1" bit in its assigned slot. Stations with nothing to send broadcast a "0".
- The order of the contention period is *fixed* (station 0, then 1, ..., then N−1).
- After the bitmap is broadcast, **every station knows exactly who wishes to transmit**, and they transmit in numerical order.

**Bit-by-Bit Mechanics:**

| Phase | Duration | Action |
|-------|----------|--------|
| Contention Period | N bit-times | Each station asserts "1" if it has a frame, else "0" |
| Transmission Window | Variable (d frames) | Stations transmit in order of their bitmap "1" bits |
| Idle Period | None | All willing stations have transmitted |
| Next Cycle | Repeats | New bitmap is started |

**Performance Characteristics:**
- **Low load** (only 1 of N stations has a frame): efficiency ≈ **1/N** (terrible! 9 out of 10 bits are overhead).
- **High load** (all N stations have frames): efficiency = **N / (2N) = 50%** (still capped at 50%).
- **Asymmetry** is naturally handled: a heavy user simply appears more often in the bitmap.
- **No collisions are possible** because the bitmap acts as a global distributed reservation system.

**Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
This is exactly how a **class register works in a school**. The teacher goes through the roll-call list ("Is Aditya present?" "Yes"), and only those who said "Yes" get to read their answer. The total time wasted on roll call grows with class size — same as the N-bit overhead.

### 2.2 Token Passing Protocol

**Operational Principle:**
- A small control frame called a **TOKEN** (3-byte frame in IEEE 802.5) circulates logically (or physically) among stations in a pre-defined order — typically forming a **logical ring**.
- A station may **only transmit when it holds the token**. After transmitting (or after a maximum token-holding time), it passes the token to its logical successor.
- If a station has nothing to send, it forwards the token immediately.
- **Token recovery mechanisms** handle token loss (e.g., a station monitors for an "orphan" frame; if detected, it regenerates a token after a timeout — known as the **"beaconing" process** in Token Ring).

**Critical Performance Formulas:**

Let:
- $T_h$ = **Token Holding Time (THT)** — max time a station may hold the token (typically **10 ms** in IEEE 802.5)
- $T_t$ = **Token Transmission Time** — time to transmit the 3-byte token
- $T_p$ = **Propagation delay** between adjacent stations
- $N$ = number of stations
- $L$ = length of one data frame in bits
- $R$ = channel bit rate

**Token Walk Time (Rotation Time without transmission):**
$$T_{walk} = N \cdot T_p + N \cdot T_t$$

**Effective Utilization for 1 station, 1 frame:**
$$U = \frac{L / R}{T_{walk} + L / R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Exam Question:** *"Why is Token Ring inefficient at low load?"*
> **Answer:** The token must complete a full rotation around the ring ($T_{walk}$ in the order of milliseconds) even when only one station has data, and no station can transmit during that rotation.

**Fault Tolerance — Token Loss and Recovery:**
1. **Active Monitor (AM)** — One designated station is the "ring boss". It sets the AM_present flag and monitors for token.
2. If AM does not see a token for a full **"no-token timer"** period (e.g., 2.6 s in 16 Mbps Token Ring), it assumes the token is lost.
3. AM **purges the ring** and injects a fresh token.
4. **Claim Token process** (IEEE 802.5) — if a station detects the ring is dead, it sends a "Claim Token" frame; the highest-address claimant wins.

**Token Ring vs Token Bus (obsolete):**
- **Token Ring (802.5)** — physical ring topology, token insertion via relays, MAU (Multistation Access Unit).
- **Token Bus (802.4)** — physical bus, but a *logical ring* is established by passing token in MAC address order. Used in MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol) industrial networks. **Now obsolete.**

### 2.3 Binary Countdown Protocol

**Operational Principle — The "Elegant" Protocol:**

Binary Countdown is a **bit-by-bit contention protocol** that is collision-free *and* wastes *no bandwidth on overhead bits* (unlike bit-map).

**Rules:**
1. All stations that have a frame to send **broadcast their full binary station address simultaneously**, one bit at a time, starting from the **most significant bit (MSB)**.
2. All stations listen to the channel.
3. After each bit-time, any station whose bit was a "0" *but the channel showed a "1"* realizes a higher-priority station is contending → it **withdraws immediately** and remains silent for the rest of the round.
4. The station whose complete address wins transmits its frame.

**Why It Works (The Math):**
- The MSB position is the most decisive. If station 4 (binary `100`) and station 7 (binary `111`) contend, at the MSB both show "1" → tie continues. At bit 2, station 4 shows "0" but station 7 shows "1" → station 4 withdraws. Station 7 wins.
- Because the channel acts as a **logical OR** of all bits, the channel value equals the OR of all contending addresses, which equals the *maximum* address.
- **No bit is wasted** — every bit-time yields useful information (a definitive OR result).

**Efficiency:**
- Channel is used for **N bit-times** of contention (where N = address length, e.g., 48 bits) **plus the full frame transmission**.
- For high load, efficiency approaches **1.0** (100% of bandwidth used for data).
- For low load, the cost is N bit-times per *single* frame, which is much smaller than Bit Map's N bit-times per *round of d frames* — hence binary countdown is more efficient at low load too.

**Comparison Insight for KTU:**
| Property | Bit Map | Binary Countdown |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| Overhead per round | N bits | 0 (waste-free) |
| Overhead per frame | N/d bits | log₂N bits (address bits) |
| Fairness | Strict round-robin | **Unfair — high addresses always win** |
| Station priority | All equal | Implicit (by address) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "Binary Countdown guarantees fairness." — **FALSE.** Higher-addressed stations systematically win. This is a deliberate *priority* mechanism, not a fair one. To make it fair, station addresses can be *logically rotated* (each station periodically renumbers itself).

### 2.4 Wireless LAN Protocols — MACA and MACAW

**The Problem with CSMA in Wireless:**

Wireless networks **cannot use CSMA/CD** because:
- A station cannot sense the carrier *and* transmit simultaneously (the transmitted signal is ~100,000× stronger than any received signal → swamps the receiver).
- Two hidden stations may both sense the channel as idle, transmit simultaneously, and collide at the receiver — neither knew.

**Two Fundamental Wireless Problems:**

**(a) Hidden Terminal Problem:**
```
[A]  --- 20m ---  [B]  --- 20m ---  [C]
        (A's range)         (C's range)
A and C are out of each other's range (hidden), but both can reach B.
A senses idle (cannot hear C), transmits to B.
C senses idle (cannot hear A), transmits to B.
→ Collision at B.
```

**(b) Exposed Terminal Problem:**
```
[A]  ---  [B]  ---  [C]  ---  [D]
A wants to send to B. C wants to send to D.
A transmits RTS to B → B replies CTS (heard by C).
C defers unnecessarily → exposed terminal waste.
```

#### 2.4.1 MACA (Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) — Karn, 1990

**Karn's Solution:** Use short *control frames* to *reserve the channel around the receiver*, not the transmitter.

**Handshake Protocol:**

1. **Sender → Receiver:** `RTS` (Request to Send) — short frame, contains frame length.
2. **Receiver → Sender:** `CTS` (Clear to Send) — short frame, contains the *same* frame length.
3. **All other stations** hearing the CTS *or* the RTS stay silent for the duration implied by the frame length (this duration field is called the **NAV — Network Allocation Vector**).
4. Sender transmits the data frame.
5. **No ACK** in original MACA — this was a flaw.

**Why This Solves Hidden Terminal:**
- C hears B's CTS → C knows B is busy → C defers. C never needed to hear A's RTS; the CTS from B was sufficient.

**Why Exposed Terminal is Partially Solved:**
- C, hearing only B's CTS but wanting to send to D, can still try — but most implementations defer anyway (NAV sets B→A duration). True exposed-terminal resolution is imperfect in MACA.

#### 2.4.2 MACAW (MACA for Wireless) — Bharghavan, 1994

**MACAW Improvements over MACA:**

| Frame | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| **RTS** | Sender requests |
| **CTS** | Receiver confirms |
| **DS** (Data Sending) | Announces data is coming (helps exposed terminals detect) |
| **ACK** | Receiver confirms data (adds reliability — MACA lacked this) |
| **RRTS** (Receiver-Ready-To-Send) | Resolves exposed terminal more cleanly |

**MACAW Backoff Algorithm:** Replaces MACA's binary exponential backoff with a **per-link, per-destination** copy called the **"Mildly Backed-Off"** algorithm — each (sender, receiver) pair maintains its own backoff counter, leading to higher aggregate throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) Connection:** Modern WiFi (802.11 DCF — Distributed Coordination Function) is essentially **CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS + ACK**, derived directly from MACAW. So when answering KTU questions, you may say: *"802.11 is a commercialized evolution of MACAW."*

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Constants | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Bit Map: contention bits | $C = N$ bit-times | $N$ = number of stations | Fixed overhead per round |
| 2 | Bit Map: efficiency | $U_{BM} = \dfrac{d}{d + N}$ | $d$ = number of transmitting stations | $d = 1 \Rightarrow$ worst; $d = N \Rightarrow$ 50% max |
| 3 | Bit Map: max efficiency | $\lim_{d \to N} U_{BM} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Dimensionless | Capped at 50% regardless of $N$ |
| 4 | Binary Countdown: contention bits | $C = \log_2 N$ bit-times | Per *frame*, not per round | Saves overhead vs Bit Map |
| 5 | Binary Countdown: efficiency (high load) | $U_{BC} \approx 1 - \dfrac{\log_2 N}{F}$ | $F$ = frame length in bits | Approaches 1 as $F \gg \log_2 N$ |
| 6 | Token Walk Time | $T_w = N \cdot T_p + N \cdot T_t$ | seconds | Token transit + propagation |
| 7 | Token Ring efficiency (1 station, 1 frame) | $U_{TR} = \dfrac{L / R}{T_w + L / R}$ | Dimensionless | Low under light load |
| 8 | IEEE 802.5 Token Holding Time | $T_{THT} = 10$ **ms** | milliseconds | KTU must-know constant |
| 9 | IEEE 802.5 Token Size | **3 bytes** (24 bits) | bytes | Start delimiter, access control, end delimiter |
| 10 | MACA/MACAW handshake | RTS $\to$ CTS $\to$ DATA $\to$ (ACK) | frames | Solves hidden terminal via CTS broadcast |
| 11 | NAV (Network Allocation Vector) | Virtual carrier-sense counter | microseconds | Updated by every overheard duration field |
| 12 | Address bits needed in Binary Countdown | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | bits | Equals number of contention bit-times |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam warning on Bit Map efficiency:** Many students incorrectly write $U = N/(2N)$ for "low load" or "general case". The *general* formula is $U = d/(d+N)$, valid for any $d$ from 1 to $N$. The 50% is a *limiting* case, not the general formula.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bit Map** → Foundational for understanding **IEEE 802.15.4 (LR-WPAN/Zigbee)** beacon-enabled superframes, where the coordinator broadcasts a beacon listing active devices.
- **Token Passing** → Still used in **FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface)** for campus backbone networks, and in industrial fieldbuses (PROFIBUS, Token Ring's modern descendant).
- **Binary Countdown** → Forms the basis of the **Data Sense Multiple Access (DSMA)** and is conceptually similar to the **IEEE 802.12 (100BASE-VG / 100VG-AnyLAN)** demand-priority protocol.
- **MACA/MACAW** → Directly evolved into **IEEE 802.11 (WiFi)**. Without Karn's MACA (1990), there would be no WiFi collision avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Map Protocol Channel Efficiency

**Given:**
- $N$ stations total
- $d$ stations have a frame to send in a given round ($1 \le d \le N$)
- Each contention slot = 1 bit-time
- Each data frame = $F$ bit-times (assume equal size for analysis)
- Channel bit rate = $R$ bps

**Step 1 — Total Bits Used in One Complete Round:**

In one round, the channel carries:
- The bitmap itself: $N$ bit-times (always exactly N, because every station declares itself)
- The data frames: $d \times F$ bit-times (only the d willing stations transmit)

$$\text{Total time per round} = T_{round} = N \cdot \frac{1}{R} + d \cdot F \cdot \frac{1}{R}$$

$$\text{Total bits per round} = N + dF$$

**Step 2 — Useful Bits per Round:**

The "useful" bits are the $dF$ data bits. The $N$ bitmap bits are overhead.

$$\text{Useful bits} = dF$$

**Step 3 — Channel Utilization (Efficiency):**

$$U_{BM} = \frac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Total bits}}$$

$$U_{BM} = \frac{dF}{N + dF}$$

If we assume the bitmap overhead is amortized equally across the d data frames, we can also write:

$$U_{BM} = \frac{d}{d + N} \quad \text{(when } F = 1 \text{ normalized unit, or amortized form)}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting Cases:**

*Case (i): Low load* — Only one station has data, $d = 1$:

$$U_{BM}\vert_{d=1} = \frac{1}{1 + N}$$

For $N = 8$: $U = 1/9 \approx 11.1\%$ (terrible)

For $N = 100$: $U = 1/101 \approx 0.99\%$ (catastrophic)

*Case (ii): High load* — All stations have data, $d = N$:

$$U_{BM}\vert_{d=N} = \frac{N}{N + N} = \frac{N}{2N} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The Bit Map Protocol **never exceeds 50% efficiency**, even with maximum load. The reason: half the time is always spent on the bitmap overhead (the N bits of contention), and the other half on actual data. The bitmap is essentially "double-bookkeeping" — the contention declares who wants to send, but this declaration is the *same size* as one round of data.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> *"Consider 10 stations using the Bit-Map Protocol. The frame size is 1000 bits. If exactly 4 stations have frames to send, calculate the channel efficiency."*

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $N = 10$, $d = 4$, $F = 1000$ bits

**Step 2 — Bitmap overhead bits:**

$$\text{Overhead} = N = 10 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Data bits transmitted:**

$$\text{Data} = d \times F = 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Total bits on channel:**

$$\text{Total} = 10 + 4000 = 4010 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**

$$U = \frac{4000}{4010} = 0.9975 = 99.75\%$$

**Step 6 — Verification using compact formula:**

$$U = \frac{dF}{N + dF} = \frac{4000}{10 + 4000} = \frac{4000}{4010} = 0.9975 \checkmark$$

**Why is this so high?** Because the per-round overhead (10 bits) is tiny compared to the total data (4000 bits). The 50% cap only applies when each "frame" is just 1 bit!

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Binary Countdown Efficiency

**Given:**
- $N$ stations, address length = $a = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ bits
- $k$ stations have frames, with $1 \le k \le N$
- Each frame = $F$ bits

**Step 1 — Contention Phase Duration:**

All $k$ contenders broadcast their $a$-bit address simultaneously. After $a$ bit-times, the winner is known.

$$\text{Contention bits} = a = \log_2 N$$

**Step 2 — Data Phase Duration:**

Only the winner transmits its frame (length $F$). The losers stay silent.

$$\text{Data bits} = F$$

**Step 3 — Total per "contention cycle" (one frame successfully sent):**

$$\text{Total bits} = \log_2 N + F$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$U_{BC} = \frac{F}{F + \log_2 N}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Case for Large Frames:**

$$\lim_{F \to \infty} U_{BC} = 1 \quad \text{(approaches 100%)}$$

For $F = 1500$ bytes (standard Ethernet MTU) = 12,000 bits and $N = 1024$ ($a = 10$):

$$U_{BC} = \frac{12000}{12000 + 10} = 0.99917 = 99.92\%$$

**Step 6 — Why this is better than Bit Map at low load:**

Bit Map spends $N$ bits of overhead per *round of d frames*. Binary Countdown spends only $\log_2 N$ bits per *single frame*. For $N = 1024$:

- Bit Map overhead per round: **1024 bits**
- Binary Countdown overhead per frame: **10 bits**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Binary Countdown's efficiency scales with $F / \log_2 N$, whereas Bit Map's scales with $dF / (N + dF)$. Binary Countdown is **always** more efficient per individual frame because its overhead is logarithmic, not linear in $N$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Token Ring Walk Time

**Given:**
- $N$ stations on a logical ring
- Propagation delay between adjacent stations = $T_p$ seconds
- Token length = 24 bits (3 bytes); at bit rate $R$, token transmission time $T_t = 24/R$
- One station wants to send one frame of $L$ bits

**Step 1 — Walk Time:**

The token must visit all $N$ stations to return to the sender. Total walk = $N$ propagation segments + $N$ token transmissions.

$$T_w = N \cdot T_p + N \cdot T_t = N(T_p + T_t)$$

**Step 2 — Frame Transmission Time:**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Utilization (1 station, 1 frame):**

$$U = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_w + T_{frame}} = \frac{L/R}{N(T_p + T_t) + L/R}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

$N = 50$ stations, $T_p = 5\,\mu s$ (typical for a 1 km ring with $c = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable), $R = 16$ Mbps, $L = 4000$ bits (500 bytes).

$$T_t = \frac{24}{16 \times 10^6} = 1.5\,\mu s$$

$$T_w = 50 \times (5 + 1.5)\,\mu s = 50 \times 6.5\,\mu s = 325\,\mu s$$

$$T_{frame} = \frac{4000}{16 \times 10^6} = 250\,\mu s$$

$$U = \frac{250}{325 + 250} = \frac{250}{575} = 0.4348 = 43.48\%$$

**Interpretation:** With 50 stations, a single sender, and a 4 kb frame, only ~43% of the channel is used for actual data. The rest is spent waiting for the token to return.

**Step 5 — Maximum Efficiency (all N stations have one frame):**

Each station transmits for $T_{frame} = L/R$ and passes the token. Total cycle = $N \cdot T_{frame} + T_w$. Useful time = $N \cdot T_{frame}$.

$$U_{max} = \frac{N \cdot T_{frame}}{N \cdot T_{frame} + T_w} = \frac{N \cdot L/R}{N \cdot L/R + N(T_p + T_t)} = \frac{L/R}{L/R + (T_p + T_t)}$$

For our example: $U_{max} = 250 / (250 + 6.5) = 97.46\%$ (excellent under saturation).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Four Protocols at a Glance

| Property | Bit Map | Token Passing | Binary Countdown | MACA / MACAW |
|----------|---------|---------------|------------------|--------------|
| **Collision possibility** | Zero | Zero | Zero | Near-zero (RTS/CTS collision only) |
| **Overhead type** | N bits/round | 1 token (24 bits)/hop | log₂N bits/frame | RTS + CTS + (ACK + DS in MACAW) |
| **Efficiency @ low load** | Poor (≈1/N) | Poor (walk time wasted) | Good | Good |
| **Efficiency @ high load** | **Capped at 50%** | Excellent (≈100%) | Excellent (≈100%) | Moderate (≈80–90% practical) |
| **Fairness** | Strict round-robin | Strict round-robin | **Unfair (high addr wins)** | Best-effort (per-link backoff) |
| **Centralized?** | No (distributed) | No (token is) | No (distributed) | No (peer-to-peer) |
| **Topology** | Bus (shared medium) | Ring or logical ring | Bus (shared medium) | Wireless (any) |
| **Suitability for wireless?** | No (needs sensing) | Limited | No (needs sensing) | **Yes — designed for it** |
| **Solves hidden terminal?** | N/A | N/A | N/A | **Yes (via CTS broadcast)** |
| **Solves exposed terminal?** | N/A | N/A | N/A | Partially (DS in MACAW) |
| **KTU-recommended example** | Tanenbaum Fig 4-6 | IEEE 802.5 | Tanenbaum Fig 4-8 | Karn 1990 / Bharghavan 1994 |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Comparing Bit Map and Binary Countdown for KTU Exam

> *"A network has 8 stations. Each station has a 1024-bit frame to send. Compare the total channel time and efficiency of (a) Bit-Map Protocol and (b) Binary Countdown Protocol, assuming all 8 stations have data."*

**Given:** $N = 8$, $F = 1024$ bits, $d = N = 8$.

**Part (a) — Bit-Map:**

Bitmap overhead: $N = 8$ bits.
Data: $d \cdot F = 8 \times 1024 = 8192$ bits.
Total: $8 + 8192 = 8200$ bits.

$$U_{BM} = \frac{8192}{8200} = 0.99902 = 99.90\%$$

(Note: The 50% cap applies only when $F$ is normalized to 1 bit. For real frames, efficiency is near 100%.)

**Part (b) — Binary Countdown:**

Address length: $a = \log_2 8 = 3$ bits.
Contention overhead: 3 bits (just once, since winner takes the channel for the whole round of 8 frames — wait, no, each frame needs a separate contention!).

**Important correction:** In Binary Countdown, contention happens **per frame**, not per round.

Per frame:
- Contention: 3 bits
- Data: 1024 bits
- Total: 1027 bits

For 8 frames:
- Total: $8 \times 1027 = 8216$ bits

$$U_{BC} = \frac{8 \times 1024}{8216} = \frac{8192}{8216} = 0.99708 = 99.71\%$$

**Comparison:** Bit Map is **slightly more efficient** when ALL stations have data, because its N-bit overhead is amortized over d = N frames, while Binary Countdown's log₂N overhead is paid per frame. However, Binary Countdown wins at low load (only 1 station has data):

For 1 frame of 1024 bits in an 8-station network:
- Bit Map: $N + F = 8 + 1024 = 1032$ bits; $U = 1024/1032 = 99.22\%$
- Binary Countdown: $3 + 1024 = 1027$ bits; $U = 1024/1027 = 99.71\%$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Answer Pattern:** Always state both the formula and the numerical result. Examiners give partial marks for correct formula substitution even if arithmetic slips.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bit-Map Protocol — Frame Sequence Diagram

The diagram below shows how the Bit-Map Protocol allocates the channel when stations {2, 5, 7} want to send (out of N=8 stations).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ch as Shared Channel
    participant S0 as Station 0
    participant S1 as Station 1
    participant S2 as Station 2
    participant S3 as Station 3
    participant S4 as Station 4
    participant S5 as Station 5
    participant S6 as Station 6
    participant S7 as Station 7

    Note over Ch: === CONTENTION PERIOD (8 bit-times) ===
    S0->>Ch: Bit 0 (no data) = 0
    S1->>Ch: Bit 1 (no data) = 0
    S2->>Ch: Bit 2 (HAS DATA) = 1
    S3->>Ch: Bit 3 (no data) = 0
    S4->>Ch: Bit 4 (no data) = 0
    S5->>Ch: Bit 5 (HAS DATA) = 1
    S6->>Ch: Bit 6 (no data) = 0
    S7->>Ch: Bit 7 (HAS DATA) = 1

    Note over Ch: Channel carries: 0 0 1 0 0 1 0 1 (the bitmap)

    Note over Ch: === TRANSMISSION PHASE (in bitmap order) ===
    S2->>Ch: FRAME from Station 2
    S5->>Ch: FRAME from Station 5
    S7->>Ch: FRAME from Station 7

    Note over Ch: === NEXT ROUND BEGINS ===
```

**Reading the diagram:** The bitmap `00100101` (read left-to-right) tells all stations that stations 2, 5, and 7 have frames, in that order. The total round consists of 8 contention bits + 3 frames. No station can transmit unless it sees its "1" in the bitmap.

---

### 4.2 Token Passing Protocol — Logical Ring Architecture

The diagram below shows the logical ring structure of a Token Passing network with 6 stations. Note that the physical topology can be a star (using a MAU), but the *logical* token-passing order is the ring.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef active fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef silent fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666,color:#333
    classDef token fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff

    S0["Station 0<br/>Holds Token"]:::active
    S1["Station 1<br/>Idle"]:::silent
    S2["Station 2<br/>Has Data Queue"]:::active
    S3["Station 3<br/>Idle"]:::silent
    S4["Station 4<br/>Has Data Queue"]:::active
    S5["Station 5<br/>Idle"]:::silent
    T(("TOKEN")):::token

    S0 -->|"Pass token"| S1
    S1 -->|"Pass token"| S2
    S2 -->|"Pass token"| S3
    S3 -->|"Pass token"| S4
    S4 -->|"Pass token"| S5
    S5 -->|"Pass token back to 0"| S0
```

**Annotations:**
- The red TOKEN frame physically circulates.
- S0 currently holds it. Since S0 is active (highlighted yellow), it will transmit its queued frame, then pass the token to S1.
- S1 and S3 are silent (gray) — they have no data, so they forward the token almost immediately.
- S2 and S4 will transmit when the token reaches them.
- The maximum time a station can hold the token is **THT = 10 ms** (IEEE 802.5).

---

### 4.3 Binary Countdown Protocol — Bit-by-Bit Contention

This diagram shows 3 stations (addresses 5 = `101`, 6 = `110`, 3 = `011`) contending. Station 6 (binary `110`) should win.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Round Begins: 3 stations want to send]) --> Bit1

    Bit1["Bit-Time 1: MSB<br/>Station 3 sends 0<br/>Station 5 sends 1<br/>Station 6 sends 1<br/>Channel OR = 1"]:::activeBit
    Bit1 -->|Check| Decision1{Any station sent 0<br/>but channel was 1?}

    Decision1 -->|"Yes — Station 3 (sent 0, saw 1)"| Withdraw1[Station 3 WITHDRAWS]
    Decision1 -->|"No — Stations 5 and 6 are still in"| Bit2

    Bit2["Bit-Time 2: Middle bit<br/>Station 5 sends 0<br/>Station 6 sends 1<br/>Channel OR = 1"]:::activeBit
    Bit2 -->|Check| Decision2{Any station sent 0<br/>but channel was 1?}

    Decision2 -->|"Yes — Station 5 (sent 0, saw 1)"| Withdraw2[Station 5 WITHDRAWS]
    Decision2 -->|"No — Station 6 is sole survivor"| Win

    Win["Station 6 is the WINNER"]
    Win -->|Transmit| Frame[Station 6 transmits its DATA FRAME]
    Frame -->|Done| Next([Next round — remaining stations 3 and 5 may contend again])

    classDef activeBit fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef withdrawNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class Withdraw1,Withdraw2 withdrawNode
```

**Reading the diagram:** At each bit position, stations whose transmitted bit (0) is "overruled" by the channel (which is 1) immediately drop out. Only the highest-addressed station survives all bit positions. This is **inherently unfair** — station 7 always beats station 0, regardless of traffic.

---

### 4.4 MACA / MACAW — Hidden Terminal Resolution via RTS/CTS

This is the most important diagram for KTU. It shows the handshake mechanism that solves the hidden terminal problem.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A<br/>(Sender)
    participant B as Station B<br/>(Receiver)
    participant C as Station C<br/>(Hidden Terminal)
    participant D as Station D<br/>(Exposed Terminal)

    Note over A,C: Initial state: A and C both have frames for B<br/>(A cannot hear C; C cannot hear A — they are HIDDEN)

    A->>B: RTS (Request to Send)<br/>Duration: 30ms
    Note over C: C does NOT hear A's RTS<br/>(A is out of C's range)<br/>C's NAV stays unset

    B->>A: CTS (Clear to Send)<br/>Duration: 25ms
    Note over C: C HEARS B's CTS<br/>C sets NAV = 25ms
    Note over D: D HEARS B's CTS<br/>D sets NAV = 25ms (deferring)

    Note over A: A transmits DATA frame<br/>(duration 20ms, fits in NAV window)

    A->>B: DATA frame
    Note over C: C stays SILENT<br/>(NAV countdown active)

    B->>A: ACK (in MACAW only)
    Note over C: After 25ms, C may contend<br/>— but for B, not A<br/>This avoids collision at B!

    Note over A,C: ✓ Hidden terminal problem SOLVED<br/>✓ Exposed terminal D stays silent (acceptable loss)
```

**Critical reading:**
1. A's RTS only reaches B (not C).
2. B's CTS is broadcast and heard by C.
3. The **CTS** is the key — it tells every neighbour of the receiver to stay quiet.
4. C's transmission, if it had occurred, would have collided at B with A's data. By overhearing CTS, C avoids this.
5. **MACAW adds ACK** for reliability and **DS** so that exposed terminals like D can be more intelligently handled.

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Comparing the Four Protocols

Since the four protocols serve different environments, a unified block diagram shows their functional contrast:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Wired["WIRED ENVIRONMENTS (Bus / Ring)"]
        direction TB
        A1[Bit-Map Protocol<br/>Function: Distribute N-bit reservation list<br/>+ N stations each get one bit-slot]
        A2[Token Passing<br/>Function: Circulate single 3-byte control frame<br/>+ Holder has exclusive right to transmit]
        A3[Binary Countdown<br/>Function: Bit-wise OR of station addresses<br/>+ Highest address wins collision-free]
    end

    subgraph Wireless["WIRELESS ENVIRONMENTS"]
        direction TB
        A4[MACA / MACAW<br/>Function: Channel reservation via handshake<br/>+ RTS-CTS-(-DS)-DATA-(-ACK)]
    end

    Common["SHARED PROPERTY: Collision-Free Operation"]:::sharedStyle
    Wired --> Common
    Wireless --> Common

    Common --> Use1[Predictable maximum delay]
    Common --> Use2[No retransmission due to collision]
    Common --> Use3[QoS-friendly behaviour]

    classDef sharedStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Block interpretation:**
- The three **wired** protocols differ mainly in *how* they resolve the "who speaks next" question — bitmap (declarative), token (sequential), binary countdown (priority).
- The **wireless** protocols (MACA/MACAW) are architecturally different because they cannot sense the carrier during transmission — hence the explicit reservation handshake.
- All four converge on the same engineering goal: **collision-free, predictable channel access**.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
> **Define the Bit-Map Protocol. Why is its maximum channel efficiency capped at 50% even under maximum load?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Bit-Map Protocol is a collision-free reservation-based MAC protocol in which, during each round, all N stations broadcast a single bit ("1" if they have a frame, "0" otherwise) in their pre-assigned slot, and stations with "1" transmit in numerical order.
- **Mechanism (1 mark):** Each round consists of N contention bit-times plus d × F data bit-times, where d is the number of willing stations and F is the frame length.
- **50% cap explanation (1 mark):** When all N stations have data (d = N), total bits per round = N (bitmap) + N·F (data). For F = 1 (normalized), the overhead N is exactly half of the total 2N bits, giving U = N/(N+N) = 0.5 = 50%. The bitmap overhead is always half the channel time when amortized to per-frame basis with unit frames.

---

> **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
> **Explain the Hidden Terminal Problem in wireless networks. How does MACA solve it using RTS/CTS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hidden Terminal Definition (1 mark):** Stations A and C are both within range of receiver B, but A and C are out of each other's transmission range. Both sense the channel as idle (cannot sense each other) and transmit simultaneously, causing a collision at B.
- **MACA's RTS/CTS Solution (1.5 marks):** A first sends a short RTS frame to B. B replies with a CTS frame that is broadcast (heard by all stations in B's range, including C). The CTS contains the duration of the upcoming data transmission, and all hearing stations set their **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** to defer for that duration.
- **Why it works (0.5 marks):** Although C cannot hear A's RTS, C can hear B's CTS, so C knows the channel will be busy at B and stays silent. The reservation is made at the **receiver**, not the transmitter, which is the key insight of MACA.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Part B — Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> **(a)** Describe the **Token Passing Protocol** used in IEEE 802.5 in detail. Explain its operational mechanism, the structure of the token, and the procedures for token loss recovery. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Token Ring network has 50 stations. The propagation delay between adjacent stations is 5 μs. The channel bit rate is 16 Mbps. The token is 3 bytes long. If a station transmits one frame of 4000 bits, calculate the **token walk time** and the **channel efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Token Passing Protocol in IEEE 802.5 — 7 marks:**

*1. Token Structure (2 marks):*
- A token in IEEE 802.5 is a **3-byte (24-bit)** control frame consisting of:
  - **Start Delimiter (SD)** — 1 byte, signals frame start; uses differential Manchester encoding with deliberate code violations.
  - **Access Control (AC)** — 1 byte, contains the **T (Token) bit**, **M (Monitor) bit**, **P (Priority) bits (3 bits)**, and **R (Reservation) bits (3 bits)**.
  - **End Delimiter (ED)** — 1 byte, marks frame end; also uses code violations.
- The token is the *only* mechanism granting channel access. **No station may transmit without holding it**.

*2. Operational Mechanism (2 marks):*
- Stations are connected in a **logical ring** (the physical topology is usually a star via a MAU — Multistation Access Unit).
- When a station has a frame to send, it waits for the token. Upon capture, it **converts the token into a data frame** by setting the T bit, appends its data, and transmits.
- After transmission, or after the **Token Holding Time (THT = 10 ms)** expires (whichever comes first), the station issues a new token to its downstream neighbour.
- A station with no data forwards the token in microseconds.

*3. Token Loss Recovery (2 marks):*
- One station is designated the **Active Monitor (AM)**. It sets the **M-bit** in every token/frame it sees, asserting its supervisory role.
