- The AM maintains a **no-token timer** (e.g., 2.6 seconds in 16 Mbps TR). If no token is seen within this period, the AM assumes the token is lost.
- The AM **purges the ring** (sends purge frames) and **injects a fresh token**.
- A backup recovery procedure called **Claim Token** is used when a station detects the AM itself has failed. The station sends a Claim Token frame containing its address. After the ring traversal, the highest-addressed claimant becomes the new AM and injects a new token.

*4. Priority Mechanism (1 mark, optional but high-yield):*
- The 3 P-bits and 3 R-bits in the Access Control byte allow 8 priority levels. A high-priority station can *preempt* a low-priority frame by reserving the next token at a higher priority.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

*Given:* $N = 50$, $T_p = 5\,\mu s$, $R = 16$ Mbps, $T_t = 24/16\text{Mbps} = 1.5\,\mu s$, $L = 4000$ bits.

*Step 1 — Token Transmission Time:*
$$T_t = \frac{24 \text{ bits}}{16 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1.5\,\mu s$$

*Step 2 — Token Walk Time:*
$$T_w = N(T_p + T_t) = 50 \times (5 + 1.5)\,\mu s = 50 \times 6.5\,\mu s = 325\,\mu s$$

*Step 3 — Frame Transmission Time:*
$$T_{frame} = \frac{4000}{16 \times 10^6} = 250\,\mu s$$

*Step 4 — Channel Efficiency (1 station, 1 frame):*
$$U = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_w + T_{frame}} = \frac{250}{325 + 250} = \frac{250}{575} = 0.4348$$

$$\boxed{U = 43.48\%}$$

*Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):*
The channel is used only 43.48% of the time for actual data; the remaining 56.52% is spent waiting for the token to circulate. This demonstrates the **low-load inefficiency** of Token Ring.

---

#### **Part B — Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Binary Countdown Protocol** in detail. How does it ensure collision-free operation? Why is it considered inherently unfair? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Hidden Terminal and Exposed Terminal problems** in wireless networks. With a clear sequence diagram (use steps), explain how the **MACAW protocol** resolves them. List all the frame types used in MACAW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Binary Countdown Protocol — 7 marks:**

*1. Mechanism (3 marks):*
- All stations that have a frame to send **broadcast their full binary station address simultaneously**, one bit per bit-time, starting from the **most significant bit (MSB)**.
- The shared channel acts as a **logical OR** of all transmitted bits. Since ORing with 1 yields 1 and ORing with 0 leaves the other bit unchanged, the channel value at any bit-time equals the highest bit being broadcast.
- After each bit-time, any station that transmitted a "0" but observed a "1" on the channel realizes that a higher-priority station is contending. That station **withdraws immediately** and stays silent for the remainder of the contention.
- After $\log_2 N$ bit-times, exactly **one station survives** — the one with the highest binary address. That station transmits its data frame.

*2. Collision-Free Guarantee (2 marks):*
- The OR-based arbitration resolves contention in a *deterministic* number of bit-times ($\log_2 N$), with no actual collision of data bits.
- The only "collision" is the OR result, which is **intentional** and is the arbitration signal itself — not a waste, since the winner is uniquely determined.

*3. Unfairness (2 marks):*
- A station with address `1111...1` (all 1s) will **always win**, regardless of how long a low-address station (e.g., `0000...1`) has been waiting.
- This is *priority by identity*, not by arrival time or queue length. A high-addressed station can **starve** low-addressed stations under sustained load.
- **Mitigation (KTU bonus point):** Address rotation (logical renumbering) can be used, where each station periodically changes its transmitted address to share the load more fairly.

**(b) Hidden & Exposed Terminal + MACAW — 7 marks:**

*1. Hidden Terminal (1.5 marks):*
Stations A and C are out of each other's range but both can transmit to receiver B. A senses the channel idle (cannot detect C), C senses idle (cannot detect A), and both transmit — collision occurs at B.

*2. Exposed Terminal (1.5 marks):*
Stations A and C are within each other's range, but A transmits to B and C wants to transmit to D. A's transmission causes C to defer unnecessarily, even though C's transmission to D would not interfere at B.

*3. MACAW Frame Types (1.5 marks):*
- **RTS** (Request to Send) — sender requests reservation.
- **CTS** (Clear to Send) — receiver confirms.
- **DS** (Data Sending) — sender announces data is on the way; helps exposed terminals detect.
- **DATA** — the actual frame.
- **ACK** — receiver's acknowledgment (lacking in original MACA, which is why MACAW was invented).
- **RRTS** (Receiver-Ready-To-Send) — receiver asks a third party to retry later; helps exposed terminals.

*4. MACAW Resolution Steps (2.5 marks):*
- **Step 1:** A sends RTS to B. A's neighbours (including C if A→C range overlaps, but not in pure hidden-terminal case) defer.
- **Step 2:** B replies with CTS, broadcast to all in B's range. C hears CTS and sets NAV → C stays silent.
- **Step 3:** A transmits DATA. B replies with **ACK** (added in MACAW for reliability).
- **Step 4:** If a station hears a DS frame from an *unrelated* sender, it knows that sender is about to transmit, and may use **RRTS** to coordinate with a third party — this is the main exposed-terminal improvement of MACAW over MACA.
- **Step 5:** MACAW uses **per-link backoff counters** (the "Mildly Backed-Off" algorithm), giving each (sender, receiver) pair independent contention state, which raises aggregate throughput by ~30–50% over MACA's per-station backoff.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Collision-Free Protocol Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA:** Many students write "CSMA/CD is used in wireless" — **this loses full credit**. Wireless uses **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)**. CSMA/CD is **wired Ethernet only**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that the CTS is the key frame in MACA:** When explaining hidden-terminal resolution, students often say "RTS solves the problem". Wrong. The **CTS** is what reaches the hidden terminal, not the RTS. The CTS broadcast to the receiver's neighbours is the *actual* collision-avoidance mechanism.
>
> 3. **Writing "Binary Countdown is fair":** False. Higher addresses systematically win. If you write "it is fair" without qualification, you will lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing Token Ring THT (10 ms) with the no-token timer (2.6 s):** These are different values for different purposes. THT is the max time a station may *hold* the token. No-token timer is the timeout after which the AM declares the token lost. Don't mix them up.
>
> 5. **Bit Map efficiency formula error:** Writing $U = d/(N+d)$ is correct. Writing $U = dF/(N+dF)$ is also correct (more general). Writing $U = N/(2N) = 50\%$ **as a general formula** is wrong — that is only the limiting case when $d = N$ AND $F$ is normalized to 1. Always derive the specific formula for the given parameters.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exams):**
>
> ✅ **Bit-Map Protocol**
> - Reservation-based, N-bit contention period, fixed station order.
> - Efficiency: $U = d/(d + N)$ or $U = dF/(N + dF)$.
> - **Max efficiency 50%** (only when $d = N$ and $F = 1$).
> - **Low load efficiency poor** (≈1/N).
>
> ✅ **Token Passing (IEEE 802.5)**
> - **3-byte token** circulates in a logical ring.
> - **THT = 10 ms** (Token Holding Time).
> - **Walk time** $T_w = N(T_p + T_t)$.
> - **AM (Active Monitor)** handles token loss recovery via no-token timer.
> - Inefficient at low load; excellent at high load.
>
> ✅ **Binary Countdown**
> - **OR-based arbitration** of station addresses.
> - Contention overhead = $\log_2 N$ bits per frame.
> - **Unfair** — high addresses always win.
> - Efficiency ≈ 1 for large frames; excellent at both low and high load.
>
> ✅ **MACA / MACAW (Wireless)**
> - **MACA** = RTS + CTS only (no ACK — unreliable).
> - **MACAW** = RTS + CTS + DS + DATA + ACK (+ RRTS).
> - **Solves Hidden Terminal** via CTS broadcast to receiver's neighbours.
> - **Partially solves Exposed Terminal** (DS + RRTS in MACAW).
> - **Per-link backoff** in MACAW = better aggregate throughput than MACA.
> - **Direct ancestor of IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) DCF**.
>
> ✅ **Key Constants to Memorize**
> - Token size: **3 bytes / 24 bits**.
> - Token Holding Time (THT): **10 ms**.
> - No-token timer (16 Mbps): **2.6 s**.
>
> ✅ **Common Exam Themes**
> - "Why is Bit Map inefficient at low load?" → $N$ bits of overhead per round.
> - "Why is Binary Countdown unfair?" → Address-based priority.
> - "Why is CSMA/CD unsuitable for wireless?" → Cannot sense carrier while transmitting.
> - "What does MACA's CTS solve?" → Hidden terminal problem.
> - "How does MACAW improve MACA?" → ACK for reliability, DS for exposed terminal, per-link backoff.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethernet: Frame Format, Types - Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet, 10-Gigabit Ethernet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethernet: Frame Format, Types - Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet, 10-Gigabit Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Ethernet?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Ethernet** is a family of wired LAN (Local Area Network) technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.3** standard, employing **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) as the medium access control protocol. It specifies the physical cabling, electrical signalling, data frame structure, and MAC sub-layer rules that allow multiple devices to share a common broadcast medium fairly and efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Emphasis:** When asked "What is Ethernet?", always mention **IEEE 802.3**, **CSMA/CD**, and its evolution from **10 Mbps → 10 Gbps**. Examiners look for the standard reference, not just the brand name.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Letter Metaphor

Imagine Ethernet communication as **sending a registered postal letter** between two office buildings:

| Ethernet Component | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Preamble** | The postman announcing arrival (rings doorbell pattern) |
| **SFD (Start Frame Delimiter)** | Knocking twice — "Start of letter!" |
| **Destination MAC Address** | The recipient's building & flat number |
| **Source MAC Address** | The sender's return address |
| **Length/Type** | The label saying "this is a 2-page letter" |
| **Data Payload** | The actual letter content inside the envelope |
| **Pad (Padding)** | Backing paper to fill an almost-empty envelope |
| **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** | The wax seal — if broken, the letter is rejected |

The **MAC address** (48-bit hardware address) is the unique, factory-burned "identity card" of every network interface card (NIC) — no two NICs in the world share the same one.

### 1.3 Why Ethernet Dominates

- **Inexpensive** — uses cheap twisted-pair copper.
- **Backward compatible** — old 10 Mbps NICs interoperate with new switches.
- **Scalable** — evolved from 10 Mbps (1980) to 400 Gbps (today).
- **Simple** — plug-and-play with auto-negotiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Timeline (Must-Know for KTU):**
> - **1973** — Bob Metcalfe invents Ethernet at Xerox PARC.
> - **1983** — IEEE standardises as **IEEE 802.3** (10BASE5 Thick Ethernet).
> - **1995** — **Fast Ethernet** IEEE 802.3u (100 Mbps).
> - **1998** — **Gigabit Ethernet** IEEE 802.3z (1 Gbps).
> - **2002** — **10-Gigabit Ethernet** IEEE 802.3ae (10 Gbps).
> - **2010+** — 40G, 100G, 400G Ethernet for data centres.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ethernet Frame Format (IEEE 802.3) — Field-by-Field Analysis

The classic **IEEE 802.3** frame has a maximum size of **1518 bytes** and minimum size of **64 bytes** (excluding the Preamble and SFD).

```
+---------+-----+--------+--------+----------+----------+-----+-----+
|PREAMBLE | SFD |  DA    |  SA    | LEN/TYPE |   DATA   | PAD | FCS |
| 7 B     | 1 B | 6 B    | 6 B    |   2 B    | 46-1500B | 0-46B| 4 B |
+---------+-----+--------+--------+----------+----------+-----+-----+
                  <---------- 64 B (min) to 1518 B (max) -------->
```

#### Field-by-Field Explanation:

**1. Preamble (7 bytes = 56 bits)**
- Pattern: `10101010 10101010 ...` (repeating 7 times)
- Purpose: Allows the **receiver's clock to synchronise** with the sender's clock (clock recovery / bit synchronisation).
- The alternating `1-0-1-0` pattern creates a square wave that the receiver PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) locks onto.

**2. SFD — Start Frame Delimiter (1 byte)**
- Pattern: `10101011`
- Purpose: Marks the **end of preamble** and the **start of the actual frame**. The last two bits `11` break the alternating pattern, signalling "frame starts here."

**3. Destination Address (DA) — 6 bytes (48 bits)**
- The **MAC address** of the intended receiver.
- First bit: `0` = Unicast, `1` = Multicast, all `1`s = Broadcast (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`).
- Globally unique; assigned by **IEEE** to manufacturers (OUI — first 24 bits).

**4. Source Address (SA) — 6 bytes**
- The MAC address of the sender. Always a **unicast** address.

**5. Length / Type Field (2 bytes)**
- **Original IEEE 802.3**: indicates the **length of the data field** in bytes (0–1500).
- **DIX (Ethernet II)**: indicates the **protocol type** (e.g., `0x0800` = IPv4, `0x86DD` = IPv6, `0x0806` = ARP).
- Since the value 1536 (`0x0600`) and above is used for type, and below for length, the two formats are **distinguishable** on the wire.

**6. Data / Payload (46 to 1500 bytes)**
- The actual upper-layer packet (IP, ARP, etc.).
- **Minimum 46 bytes**: ensures the frame is at least 64 bytes total, so collisions are detected before transmission ends.
- If the upper-layer data is less than 46 bytes, **padding** is added.

**7. Pad (0 to 46 bytes)**
- Zeros added to make the data field reach the 46-byte minimum.

**8. FCS — Frame Check Sequence (4 bytes = 32 bits)**
- A **CRC-32** (Cyclic Redundancy Check) computed over DA, SA, Length, Data, and Pad.
- The receiver recomputes the CRC; if it doesn't match, the frame is **silently dropped**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame Size Recap (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Minimum frame = 64 bytes** (header 18 + data 46) — required for collision detection in 10/100 Mbps half-duplex.
> - **Maximum frame = 1518 bytes** (header 18 + data 1500) — limits buffer sizes and ensures fairness.
> - **Jumbo Frames = up to 9000 bytes** — non-standard, used in data centres for efficiency.

---

### 2.2 The Three High-Speed Ethernet Generations

#### A. Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps) — IEEE 802.3u (1995)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Goal:** 10× speed of 10BASE-T while keeping the **same frame format**, **same MAC protocol**, and **same minimum/maximum frame sizes** for backward compatibility.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Bit rate:** 100 Mbps
- **Bit time:** 1/100 µs = **10 ns** (10× shorter than 10 Mbps)
- **Slot time:** 512 bit times = **5.12 µs**
- **Network diameter:** ~205 m (much shorter than 10BASE-T's 2500 m, due to shorter bit time)

**Physical Layer Variants (the "100BASE-X" family):**

| Standard | Medium | Segments | Topology | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **100BASE-TX** | 2 pairs Cat 5 UTP / STP | 100 m | Star | Most popular |
| **100BASE-FX** | 2 strands multimode fiber (62.5/125 µm) | 412 m (half-duplex) / 2 km (full-duplex) | Star | For long runs, EMI-prone areas |
| **100BASE-T4** | 4 pairs Cat 3 UTP | 100 m | Star | **Deprecated** — used legacy Cat 3 |
| **100BASE-T2** | 2 pairs Cat 3 UTP | 100 m | Star | Rarely implemented |

**Encoding:**
- **100BASE-TX** uses **4B/5B** block coding followed by **MLT-3** (Multi-Level Transmit) signalling on each pair → reduces bandwidth requirement to 31.25 MHz.
- **100BASE-FX** uses **4B/5B** with **NRZ-I** (Non-Return to Zero Inverted) optical signalling.

**Auto-Negotiation (IEEE 802.3u):**
- Allows two linked devices to **negotiate the highest common speed** (10/100 Mbps) and duplex (half/full).
- Uses **Fast Link Pulses (FLP)** — a burst of 17 NLPs (Normal Link Pulses) carrying a 16-bit link code word.
- Modern devices **always enable** auto-negotiation by default.

---

#### B. Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps) — IEEE 802.3z (1998) & 802.3ab (1999)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Goal:** 10× speed of Fast Ethernet. **Backward compatible** at the frame level. **Bit time = 1 ns**, slot time retained at 512 bit times = **512 ns** for CSMA/CD compatibility.

**Why Keep Slot Time at 512 Bits?**
To preserve the same **minimum frame size (64 bytes)** and **maximum network diameter**, ensuring interoperability with existing 10/100 Mbps equipment. The bit time is 1/1000 µs, so a 512-bit slot = 0.512 µs. To extend the network diameter, two tricks are used: **Carrier Extension** and **Frame Bursting**.

**Physical Layer Variants:**

| Standard | IEEE | Medium | Max Distance | Encoding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1000BASE-SX** | 802.3z | Multimode fiber (62.5/50 µm) | 275–550 m | 8B/10B + NRZ |
| **1000BASE-LX** | 802.3z | Single-mode / Multimode fiber | 5 km (SM) / 550 m (MM) | 8B/10B + NRZ |
| **1000BASE-CX** | 802.3z | Twinaxial copper (150 Ω) | 25 m | 8B/10B |
| **1000BASE-T** | 802.3ab | 4 pairs Cat 5e/6 UTP | 100 m | **PAM-5 (5-level)** + Trellis coding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1000BASE-T Secret:** Uses all 4 pairs simultaneously, each pair carrying 250 Mbps, with **PAM-5** (Pulse Amplitude Modulation with 5 voltage levels) → 2 bits per symbol × 125 Mbaud = 250 Mbps per pair. **Full-duplex** is mandatory in practice.

**Carrier Extension (for Half-Duplex only):**
- The frame is padded to a minimum length of **512 bytes (4096 bit times)** at the PHY layer.
- The receiving station strips the extension symbols; original frame is unchanged.
- Without this, a 64-byte frame would be transmitted in only 512 ns — too short to detect a collision on a 200 m network.

**Frame Bursting (for Half-Duplex only):**
- After transmitting the first frame (with extension), the station may send **up to 7 more frames** consecutively without contending for the medium.
- This amortises the contention overhead across multiple short frames.
- Total burst ≤ 65,536 bit-times (8192 bytes).

**Full Duplex in Gigabit Ethernet:**
- With switches, **CSMA/CD is disabled** (point-to-point links, no collisions possible).
- **No carrier extension or bursting needed** → maximum throughput is true 1 Gbps in both directions.

---

#### C. 10-Gigabit Ethernet (10 Gbps) — IEEE 802.3ae (2002)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paradigm Shift:** 10 Gigabit Ethernet is **FULL-DUPLEX ONLY**. **CSMA/CD is officially abandoned.** It is no longer a LAN technology — it is used for **LANs, MANs, and WANs**.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Bit rate:** 10 Gbps
- **Bit time:** 0.1 ns
- **Slot time:** no longer meaningful (no CSMA/CD)
- **Maximum frame size:** extended to **2000 bytes** (jumbo support) in some variants
- **WAN PHY** + **LAN PHY** → bridges Ethernet natively with **OC-192c / STM-64** SONET/SDH networks (9.58464 Gbps).

**Physical Layer Variants:**

| Standard | Medium | Max Distance | Encoding | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10GBASE-SR** | Multimode fiber (850 nm) | 26–300 m | 64B/66B | "Short Range" — data centres |
| **10GBASE-LR** | Single-mode fiber (1310 nm) | 10 km | 64B/66B | "Long Range" — campus |
| **10GBASE-ER** | Single-mode fiber (1550 nm) | 40 km | 64B/66B | "Extended Range" — MAN |
| **10GBASE-LX4** | Multimode/Single-mode fiber | 300 m / 10 km | WWDM (4 λ) | Older 4-lane design |
| **10GBASE-CX4** | Twinaxial copper | 15 m | 8B/10B | IEEE 802.3ak; Infiniband-style |
| **10GBASE-T** | Cat 6a / Cat 7 UTP | 100 m (Cat 6a) | **PAM-16 (DSQ-128)** + LDPC | IEEE 802.3an (2006) |
| **10GBASE-SW/LW/EW** | Fiber | as above | 64B/66B | **WAN PHY** versions (SONET/SDH compatible) |

**Encoding: 64B/66B**
- 64 bits of data encoded into a 66-bit block (2-bit header + 64-bit payload).
- Only **3.125% overhead** (vs 25% for 8B/10B in 1G) → enables high serial-rate transmission.
- 10 Gbps data rate = 10.3125 Gbps line rate (because of 64/66 expansion).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Compare Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet, and 10-Gigabit Ethernet in terms of speed, standard, media, CSMA/CD status, and encoding."* — Memorise the table above.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Bit time (1 bit duration) | seconds |
| $T_{frame} = \dfrac{L \times 8}{R}$ | Transmission time of a frame of $L$ bytes at rate $R$ | seconds |
| $E_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{data}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Frame efficiency (useful data % of total bits) | percent |
| $T_{slot} = 512 \times T_{bit}$ | Slot time for CSMA/CD | seconds |
| $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \times T_{slot}}{2}$ | Max network diameter (round-trip) | metres |
| $v = 2 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$ | Signal propagation speed in copper/fiber | m/s |
| Throughput (half-duplex) | $R \times E_{frame}$ | bps |
| Throughput (full-duplex) | $2R \times E_{frame}$ | bps |

**CRITICAL:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` in tables to avoid markdown breakage — never write absolute-value bars as raw pipes inside a table cell.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data centres** — 10G/40G/100G Ethernet has replaced Fibre Channel as the storage & server fabric.
- **Service provider backbones** — Metro Ethernet (10G/100G) replaces legacy SONET/SDH.
- **Enterprise LANs** — 1G to the desk, 10G between floors, 40G/100G between buildings.
- **5G xHaul** — fronthaul and backhaul of 5G base stations use 10G/25G Ethernet.
- **Industrial automation** — Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) extends Ethernet for real-time control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frame Transmission Time

> **Problem:** A 1500-byte Ethernet frame is sent over 1 Gbps Ethernet. Find the **transmission time** and the **frame efficiency**.

**Given:**
- $L = 1500$ bytes (payload data)
- $R = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$
- Total frame on wire = 1500 (data) + 18 (header) + 8 (preamble + SFD) = 1526 bytes

**Step 1 — Convert bytes to bits.**

$$
L_{total} = 1526 \ \text{bytes} \times 8 \ \text{bits/byte} = 12{,}208 \ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute transmission time.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{L_{total}}{R} = \frac{12{,}208 \ \text{bits}}{10^9 \ \text{bits/s}}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 12.208 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s} = 12.208 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute frame efficiency.**

$$
E_{frame} = \frac{L_{data}}{L_{total}} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{12{,}208} = \frac{12{,}000}{12{,}208}
$$

$$
E_{frame} \approx 0.9830 = 98.30\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
At 1 Gbps, a 1500-byte frame achieves 98.3% efficiency — overhead is negligible at high speeds. This is why jumbo frames are *not* required for 10G+ Ethernet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Frame Size from Collision Detection

> **Derivation:** Why is the minimum Ethernet frame 64 bytes?

**Given:**
- 10BASE-T propagation speed: $v = 2 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- Maximum network diameter: $d_{max} = 2500 \ \text{m}$ (10BASE5 coax limit)
- Bit rate: $R = 10 \ \text{Mbps}$

**Step 1 — Compute one-way propagation delay for the maximum distance.**

$$
t_{prop} = \frac{d_{max}}{v} = \frac{2500 \ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}} = 12.5 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute round-trip propagation delay (sender must still be transmitting when collision signal returns).**

$$
t_{rtt} = 2 \times t_{prop} = 25 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum number of bits that must be transmitted to cover this round-trip time at 10 Mbps.**

$$
L_{min} = R \times t_{rtt} = 10 \times 10^6 \ \text{bits/s} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s}
$$

$$
L_{min} = 250 \ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for safety margin (cable repeater delays, preamble transmission, etc.).**

The IEEE chose **512 bits = 64 bytes** as the **slot time**, which corresponds to a maximum diameter of $\approx 2500$ m. So the **minimum frame = 64 bytes** ensures a transmitting station is still on the wire when the collision signal returns from the farthest station.

**Step 5 — The slot time / network-diameter relationship.**

$$
d_{max} = \frac{v \times T_{slot}}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^8 \times 512 \times 10^{-7}}{2}
$$

$$
d_{max} = 5120 \ \text{m} \ (\text{theoretical limit at 10 Mbps})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 64 bytes isn't enough at 100 Mbps or 1 Gbps:**
> - 100 Mbps bit time = 10 ns, so 64 bytes (512 bits) is on the wire for only $5.12 \ \mu s$ — covers a network of diameter $d_{max} = 512$ m max.
> - 1 Gbps bit time = 1 ns, so 64 bytes is on the wire for only $512$ ns — covers a network of $d_{max} = 51.2$ m.
> - Hence Gigabit Ethernet uses **Carrier Extension** to 512 bytes and **Frame Bursting** to maintain the same slot time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Diameter for Fast Ethernet

> **Problem:** For 100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet, what is the maximum network diameter in half-duplex mode?

**Given:**
- $R = 100 \ \text{Mbps}$
- Slot time $T_{slot} = 512 \ \text{bit times}$
- $v = 2 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$

**Step 1 — Convert slot time to seconds.**

$$
T_{slot} = 512 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 512 \times 10^{-8} = 5.12 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum one-way propagation delay.**

$$
t_{prop}^{max} = \frac{T_{slot}}{2} = 2.56 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum diameter.**

$$
d_{max} = v \times t_{prop}^{max} = 2 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s}
$$

$$
d_{max} = 512 \ \text{m} \ (\text{theoretical})
$$

**Step 4 — Apply practical limits.**
Each repeater adds $\approx 0.7 \ \mu s$ delay and the maximum of 2 repeaters is allowed. Practical diameter is **$\approx 205$ m** with 2 Class II repeaters.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time to Transmit a 64-Byte Frame at 10 Gbps

> **Problem:** Find how long a 64-byte minimum frame takes to transmit on 10GBASE-LR.

**Step 1 — Total bits including preamble and SFD.**

$$
L_{total} = (64 + 8) \ \text{bytes} = 72 \ \text{bytes} = 576 \ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute transmission time.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{576 \ \text{bits}}{10 \times 10^9 \ \text{bps}} = 57.6 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{s}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 57.6 \ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute frame efficiency for the smallest frame.**

$$
E_{frame} = \frac{46 \times 8}{72 \times 8} = \frac{46}{72} = 0.6389 = 63.89\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
At 10 Gbps, even the smallest legal frame is 57.6 ns on the wire — for a 10 km fiber link, the round-trip propagation is $100 \ \mu s$, which is **1700× longer** than the frame itself. This is why CSMA/CD is **physically impossible** at 10G and is removed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | 10BASE-T (Std) | 100BASE-TX (Fast) | 1000BASE-T (Gigabit) | 10GBASE-T (10 Gig) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard** | 802.3 | 802.3u | 802.3ab | 802.3an |
| **Speed** | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1 Gbps | 10 Gbps |
| **Bit Time** | 100 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns | 0.1 ns |
| **Slot Time** | 51.2 µs | 5.12 µs | 512 ns (kept same) | Not used |
| **Min Frame** | 64 B | 64 B | 64 B (with ext. to 512 B) | 64 B |
| **Max Diameter (HD)** | 2500 m | 205 m | 25 m (with switches, full) | Full-duplex only |
| **Encoding** | Manchester | 4B/5B + MLT-3 | 8B/10B + PAM-5 | 64B/66B + PAM-16 + LDPC |
| **CSMA/CD** | Yes | Yes | Yes (with ext./bursting) | **No** (Full-duplex only) |
| **Pairs/Medium** | 2 pairs Cat 3 | 2 pairs Cat 5 | 4 pairs Cat 5e/6 | 4 pairs Cat 6a/7 |
| **Auto-Negotiation** | No (older) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Year** | 1990 | 1995 | 1999 | 2006 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Format (Block-Level Anatomy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PREAM["PREAMBLE - 7 BYTES"]
        P1["10101010"]
        P2["10101010"]
        P3["10101010"]
        P4["10101010"]
        P5["10101010"]
        P6["10101010"]
        P7["10101010"]
    end
    subgraph SFD["SFD - 1 BYTE"]
        SF["10101011"]
    end
    subgraph HEAD["MAC HEADER - 14 BYTES"]
        DA["DESTINATION MAC - 6 BYTES"]
        SA["SOURCE MAC - 6 BYTES"]
        LT["LENGTH OR TYPE - 2 BYTES"]
    end
    subgraph PAY["PAYLOAD - 46 TO 1500 BYTES"]
        DAT["UPPER LAYER DATA"]
        PAD["PADDING - 0 TO 46 BYTES"]
    end
    subgraph TAIL["TRAILER - 4 BYTES"]
        FCS["FRAME CHECK SEQUENCE - CRC-32"]
    end
    PREAM --> SFD --> HEAD --> PAY --> TAIL
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> SF
    DA --> SA --> LT
    DAT --> PAD
```

**How to read this diagram:** Data flows from left → right. The **total size (excluding preamble and SFD) is 64–1518 bytes**, broken into header (14) + payload (46–1500) + trailer (4). Each box is a self-explanatory field, the way a KTU examiner would sketch on the answer sheet.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Ethernet Family Tree (Evolution)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["IEEE 802.3 ETHERNET FAMILY"]
    ROOT --> A["STANDARD 10 MBPS - 1983"]
    ROOT --> B["FAST 100 MBPS - 1995 - 802.3u"]
    ROOT --> C["GIGABIT 1 GBPS - 1998-1999"]
    ROOT --> D["10 GIGABIT 10 GBPS - 2002-2006"]

    A --> A1["10BASE5 Thick Coax"]
    A --> A2["10BASE2 Thin Coax"]
    A --> A3["10BASE-T Twisted Pair"]

    B --> B1["100BASE-TX - Cat 5"]
    B --> B2["100BASE-FX - Fiber"]
    B --> B3["100BASE-T4 - Cat 3 DEPRECATED"]

    C --> C1["1000BASE-T - Cat 5e UTP 802.3ab"]
    C --> C2["1000BASE-SX - MM Fiber 802.3z"]
    C --> C3["1000BASE-LX - SM Fiber 802.3z"]

    D --> D1["10GBASE-SR - Short Range"]
    D --> D2["10GBASE-LR - Long Range"]
    D --> D3["10GBASE-ER - Extended Range"]
    D --> D4["10GBASE-T - Cat 6a Copper 802.3an"]
    D --> D5["10GBASE-SW-LW-EW - WAN PHY"]

    style ROOT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#90EE90
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style D fill:#FFA07A
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — CSMA/CD Process and Why It Fails at 10G

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA_A as Station A
    participant BUS as Shared Medium
    participant STA_B as Station B

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: 10BASE-T / 100BASE-TX / 1000BASE-T (Half-Duplex)
    STA_A->>BUS: 1. Sense carrier - idle
    STA_A->>BUS: 2. Begin transmitting frame
    STA_B->>BUS: 3. Begin transmitting simultaneously
    BUS-->>STA_A: 4. Collision detected - voltage spike
    STA_A->>STA_A: 5. Send jam signal
    STA_A->>STA_A: 6. Wait backoff time
    STA_A->>BUS: 7. Retry transmission

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: 10GBASE - Full Duplex Only
    Note over BUS: Point to point link via Switch<br/>No shared medium<br/>No collisions possible<br/>CSMA CD disabled
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Auto-Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Port comes up - link is detected"]
    START --> FL["Transmit Fast Link Pulses FLP"]
    FL --> EX["Exchange 16-bit Link Code Words"]
    EX --> MATCH["Compare capabilities with partner"]
    MATCH --> DECIDE{"Highest common<br/>denominator?"}
    DECIDE -->|10 Half| H10["10BASE-T HDX"]
    DECIDE -->|10 Full| F10["10BASE-T FDX"]
    DECIDE -->|100 Half| H100["100BASE-TX HDX"]
    DECIDE -->|100 Full| F100["100BASE-TX FDX"]
    DECIDE -->|1000 Full| F1000["1000BASE-T FDX"]
    DECIDE -->|No match| FAIL["Parallel Detection - default to 10/100 HDX"]
    H10 --> DONE["Link established"]
    F10 --> DONE
    H100 --> DONE
    F100 --> DONE
    F1000 --> DONE
    FAIL --> DONE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

**Q1. With a neat diagram, explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format. Mention the size of each field.**
*(3 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame consists of the following fields (see diagram in Section 4.1):

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Preamble | 7 bytes | Clock synchronisation (`10101010` pattern) |
| SFD | 1 byte | Marks start of frame (`10101011`) |
| Destination MAC | 6 bytes | Recipient's hardware address |
| Source MAC | 6 bytes | Sender's hardware address |
| Length/Type | 2 bytes | Length of payload or protocol type |
| Data | 46–1500 bytes | Upper-layer payload |
| Pad | 0–46 bytes | Ensures 64-byte minimum |
| FCS | 4 bytes | CRC-32 error detection |

**Total size (excluding preamble + SFD):** Minimum **64 bytes**, Maximum **1518 bytes**.

> The 7-byte preamble plus 1-byte SFD are not counted in the frame's official size. The data field is padded with zeros to reach the 46-byte minimum so that the total frame is always at least 64 bytes, which is required for proper CSMA/CD collision detection on 10/100 Mbps Ethernet.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List and briefly explain any three physical layer variants of Fast Ethernet.**
*(3 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u, 100 Mbps) has the following physical layer variants:

1. **100BASE-TX** — Uses **2 pairs of Cat 5 UTP (or STP)** with a maximum segment length of **100 m**. Encoding is **4B/5B block coding** followed by **MLT-3** signalling. Most widely deployed Fast Ethernet variant.
2. **100BASE-FX** — Uses **2 strands of multimode fiber** (62.5/125 µm). Maximum segment length is **412 m in half-duplex** or **2 km in full-duplex**. Encoding is **4B/5B + NRZ-I**. Used for longer cable runs and in EMI-prone environments.
3. **100BASE-T4** — Uses **4 pairs of Cat 3 UTP** with a maximum segment length of **100 m**. It was designed to support legacy Cat 3 cabling but is now **deprecated** due to its complexity.
4. **100BASE-T2** (bonus) — Uses **2 pairs of Cat 3 UTP** but is rarely implemented in practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **100BASE-TX** (Cat 5) with **100BASE-T4** (Cat 3) and write the wrong cable type. Remember: **TX → Cat 5**, **T4 → Cat 3 (4 pairs)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

#### ✅ CHOICE 1: Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format** in detail. Discuss the purpose of each field and state the minimum and maximum frame sizes.  
*(7 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Ethernet, defined by IEEE 802.3, is the dominant wired LAN technology. Every frame on the wire follows a strict structure that ensures synchronisation, addressing, payload delivery, and error detection.

**Frame Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Field-by-field explanation (3 marks):**
- **Preamble (7 B):** Bit pattern `10101010` × 7; allows the receiver to lock its clock onto the sender's bit stream.
- **SFD (1 B):** `10101011`; breaks the preamble pattern, marks start of the actual frame.
- **DA (6 B):** Destination MAC address; can be unicast, multicast, or broadcast.
- **SA (6 B):** Source MAC address; always unicast.
- **Length/Type (2 B):** Either length of data (802.3) or protocol type (Ethernet II). Distinguishable by value $\geq 1536$ = type.
- **Data (46–1500 B):** Upper-layer PDU; padded to minimum 46 B.
- **Pad (0–46 B):** Zeros to ensure minimum frame size.
- **FCS (4 B):** CRC-32 for error detection.

**Sizes (1 mark):**
- Minimum frame = **64 bytes** (header 14 + data 46 + FCS 4).
- Maximum frame = **1518 bytes** (header 14 + data 1500 + FCS 4).
- Including preamble + SFD, the on-wire size is 72–1526 bytes.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u)** standard in detail. List all physical layer variants, the encoding used, and the concept of auto-negotiation.  
*(7 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Fast Ethernet (IEEE 802.3u, 1995) is the 100 Mbps evolution of 10BASE-T. It preserves the same MAC frame format, MAC address structure, and CSMA/CD protocol for full backward compatibility. The bit time is reduced from 100 ns to 10 ns, which shortens the network diameter to about 205 m in half-duplex mode.

**Physical Layer Variants (3 marks):**

1. **100BASE-TX** — 2 pairs Cat 5 UTP, 100 m, **4B/5B + MLT-3** encoding.
2. **100BASE-FX** — 2 strands multimode fiber, 412 m HDX / 2 km FDX, **4B/5B + NRZ-I** encoding.
3. **100BASE-T4** — 4 pairs Cat 3 UTP, 100 m, **8B/6T** encoding (now deprecated).
4. **100BASE-T2** — 2 pairs Cat 3 UTP, 100 m (rarely used).

**Encoding (1 mark):**
- **4B/5B** maps every 4 data bits into a 5-bit code with guaranteed transitions for clock recovery (efficiency = 80%).
- **MLT-3** uses 3 voltage levels (–V, 0, +V) to keep the signal spectrum below 30 MHz.

**Auto-Negotiation (2 marks):**
- Defined in IEEE 802.3u clause 28.
- Two linked devices exchange **Fast Link Pulses (FLP)** containing a 16-bit **link code word**.
- They negotiate the highest common **speed (10/100 Mbps)** and **duplex (half/full)**.
- If negotiation fails, the device falls back to **parallel detection** (default 10/100 HDX).
- Auto-negotiation is **mandatory** for twisted-pair ports today.

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 2: Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Discuss the architecture of **Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3z/802.3ab)** with reference to physical layer variants, encoding, and the techniques (carrier extension and frame bursting) used to maintain CSMA/CD compatibility.  
*(7 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps) is defined by two IEEE standards: **802.3z** (1998) for fiber and coaxial, and **802.3ab** (1999) for copper. It retains the **same frame format** and **same minimum 64-byte frame** as 10/100 Mbps Ethernet for full backward compatibility. The bit time is now 1 ns.

**Physical Layer Variants (2 marks):**

| Standard | IEEE | Medium | Distance | Encoding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1000BASE-SX | 802.3z | Multimode fiber (850 nm) | 275–550 m | 8B/10B + NRZ |
| 1000BASE-LX | 802.3z | SM / MM fiber (1310 nm) | 5 km / 550 m | 8B/10B + NRZ |
| 1000BASE-CX | 802.3z | Twinaxial copper | 25 m | 8B/10B |
| 1000BASE-T | 802.3ab | 4 pairs Cat 5e/6 UTP | 100 m | 8B/10B + **PAM-5 + Trellis** |

**Encoding (1 mark):**
- All 1G PHYs (except 1000BASE-T) use **8B/10B** block coding → 20% overhead, line rate = 1.25 Gbaud.
- **1000BASE-T** uses all 4 pairs simultaneously, **PAM-5** (5 voltage levels) → 2 bits per symbol, 125 Mbaud, **250 Mbps per pair**, Trellis-coded modulation for error correction.

**Carrier Extension (2 marks):**
- In **half-duplex** mode, a 64-byte frame would be on the wire for only **512 ns** — shorter than the time needed to detect a collision on a 200 m network.
- The MAC layer **extends** short frames with non-data symbols to make every frame at least **512 bytes (4096 bit times)** long.
- The receiver strips the extension symbols; the original 64-byte frame is delivered unchanged.
- Maintains the **same 512-bit slot time** as 10/100 Ethernet.

**Frame Bursting (1 mark):**
- After sending the first (extended) frame, the station may immediately send **up to 7 additional frames** without backing off, as long as the total burst is $\leq 65{,}536$ bit times (8192 bytes).
- This amortises the contention overhead and improves throughput for bursty traffic.
- In **full-duplex** (switched) mode, no carrier extension or bursting is needed — true 1 Gbps in each direction.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **10-Gigabit Ethernet (IEEE 802.3ae)** in detail. Why is CSMA/CD abandoned, and what is the WAN PHY concept? List any four physical layer variants.  
*(7 Marks — KTU CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
10 Gigabit Ethernet (10 Gbps) was standardised in **IEEE 802.3ae (2002)** for fiber, **802.3ak (2004)** for twinaxial copper, and **802.3an (2006)** for Cat 6a/7 UTP. It is the first Ethernet standard designed exclusively for **full-duplex** operation, marking Ethernet's transition from a LAN-only technology to LAN/MAN/WAN use.

**Why CSMA/CD is Abandoned (2 marks):**
- At 10 Gbps, a 64-byte frame is on the wire for only **57.6 ns**.
- For a 10 km fiber link, the round-trip propagation is **$\approx 100 \ \mu s$** — nearly **2000× longer** than the frame itself.
- A station would have finished transmitting before a collision signal could return from the far end.
- Hence collision detection is **physically impossible**, and the standard mandates **full-duplex, switched** operation.
- A slot time is no longer defined.

**Physical Layer Variants (2 marks):**
1. **10GBASE-SR** — Multimode fiber, 850 nm, 26–300 m. "Short Range" for data centres.
2. **10GBASE-LR** — Single-mode fiber, 1310 nm, up to 10 km. "Long Range" for campus.
3. **10GBASE-ER** — Single-mode fiber, 1550 nm, up to 40 km. "Extended Range" for MAN.
4. **10GBASE-T** — 4 pairs Cat 6a/7 UTP, 100 m, uses **PAM-16 (DSQ-128) + LDPC** coding. Standardised as 802.3an (2006).
5. **10GBASE-CX4** — Twinaxial copper, 15 m, 4 lanes, 8B/10B encoding (802.3ak).
6. **10GBASE-LX4** — Wide-Wavelength-Division-Multiplexed (WWDM) over MM/SM fiber, 4 λ.

**WAN PHY Concept (2 marks):**
- 10G Ethernet comes in two flavours: **LAN PHY** (10.3125 Gbps) and **WAN PHY** (9.58464 Gbps).
- The **WAN PHY** line rate matches **OC-192c / STM-64** SONET/SDH framing at $\approx 9.5846$ Gbps.
- The 64B/66B block code is wrapped inside an **SDH/SONET frame** at the WAN PHY interface.
- This allows service providers to carry 10G Ethernet traffic natively over existing **SONET/SDH** optical transport networks without a separate router.
- Variants: **10GBASE-SW, 10GBASE-LW, 10GBASE-EW** (the "W" suffix indicates WAN PHY).

**Encoding:** **64B/66B** block code (only 3.125% overhead) — a major improvement over 8B/10B (20% overhead) used in 1G Ethernet. Line rate = 10.3125 Gbps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 8-byte preamble + SFD** when stating frame sizes. The "64-byte minimum" excludes them, but the *on-wire* minimum is 72 bytes.
> 2. **Writing "Manchester encoding for Fast Ethernet"** — WRONG! 10BASE-T uses Manchester, but **100BASE-TX uses 4B/5B + MLT-3**. This is a 3-mark blunder.
> 3. **Confusing 100BASE-TX (Cat 5) with 100BASE-T4 (Cat 3)** and writing the wrong cable type.
> 4. **Stating that CSMA/CD is used in 10G Ethernet** — 10G is full-duplex only, no CSMA/CD.
> 5. **Mixing up LAN PHY (10.3125 Gbps) and WAN PHY (9.5846 Gbps)** in 10G Ethernet. The "S/L/E-W" suffix is WAN PHY; "S/L/E-R" suffix is LAN PHY.
> 6. **Forgetting Carrier Extension and Frame Bursting** when discussing Gigabit Ethernet half-duplex operation. These two mechanisms are the heart of the 802.3z design.
> 7. **Writing the formula $d_{max} = v \times T_{slot}$** (without dividing by 2) — the slot time is round-trip, so the diameter uses $T_{slot}/2$.
> 8. **Confusing the FCS** with a checksum — FCS uses **CRC-32**, not a simple sum.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist to your wall for the last 24 hours before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Ethernet = IEEE 802.3**, MAC sublayer, CSMA/CD for shared media.
- [ ] **Frame = 64 B (min) to 1518 B (max)**, excluding 8 B preamble+SFD.
- [ ] **Header (14 B):** DA(6) + SA(6) + Length/Type(2).
- [ ] **Trailer (4 B):** CRC-32 FCS.
- [ ] **Preamble pattern:** `10101010` × 7; **SFD:** `10101011`.
- [ ] **MAC address:** 48 bits, globally unique, first 24 bits = OUI.
- [ ] **Fast Ethernet (802.3u, 100 Mbps):** 100BASE-TX = 4B/5B + MLT-3 on Cat 5; 100BASE-FX = fiber; auto-negotiation via FLP.
- [ ] **Gigabit Ethernet (802.3z/ab, 1 Gbps):** 1000BASE-T = PAM-5 + Trellis on 4-pair Cat 5e; **Carrier Extension** pads frames to 512 B; **Frame Bursting** chains up to 8 frames.
- [ ] **10-Gigabit Ethernet (802.3ae, 10 Gbps):** **Full-duplex only**, **no CSMA/CD**, 64B/66B encoding (3.125% overhead), LAN PHY & WAN PHY, fiber + Cat 6a/7.
- [ ] **Slot time = 512 bit times** at 10/100/1000 Mbps; **undefined at 10G**.
- [ ] **Network diameter** $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \times T_{slot}}{2}$; 10BASE-T = 2500 m; 100BASE-TX ≈ 205 m; 1G HDX ≈ 25 m; 10G = switched only.
- [ ] **Bit time = 1/R**: 10 Mbps = 100 ns; 100 Mbps = 10 ns; 1 Gbps = 1 ns; 10 Gbps = 0.1 ns.
- [ ] **Encoding evolution:** Manchester (10M) → 4B/5B+MLT-3 (100M) → 8B/10B+PAM-5 (1G) → 64B/66B+PAM-16+LDPC (10G).
- [ ] **WAN PHY rate = 9.58464 Gbps** (matches OC-192/STM-64).
- [ ] **Auto-negotiation** is mandatory on modern twisted-pair Ethernet ports.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Layer Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Layer Design Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Network Layer** is the third layer of the **OSI Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Internet Protocol Suite**, responsible for end-to-end packet delivery across multiple interconnected networks (internets). The *design issues* of the network layer refer to the set of architectural decisions that govern how packets are routed, forwarded, addressed, and delivered — independent of the underlying physical topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The five classical *Design Issues* of the Network Layer (as per Tanenbaum & Wetherall, standard KTU reference) are:
> 1. **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching**
> 2. **Services Provided to the Transport Layer**
> 3. **Implementation of Connectionless Service**
> 4. **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service**
> 5. **Comparison of Virtual-Circuit (VC) Subnets vs. Datagram (DG) Subnets**

---

## 1.2 The Five Design Issues — One-Line Definitions

| # | Design Issue | One-Line Definition |
|---|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Store-and-Forward Switching | Every router must receive the *entire* packet before forwarding it. |
| 2 | Services to Transport Layer | Should the network be **connectionless** or **connection-oriented**? |
| 3 | Connectionless Implementation | Each packet is routed **independently** (Datagram subnets / Internet). |
| 4 | Connection-Oriented Implementation | A **virtual circuit** is set up first; all packets follow the same path. |
| 5 | VC vs. Datagram Comparison | Trade-off analysis between **connection-oriented** and **connectionless** internets. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy: Postal Mail vs. Phone Call**
>
> - **Datagram Subnet (Connectionless)** → Think of the **postal system**. You write a letter, put a *full address* on it (source + destination), and drop it in the mailbox. The post office (router) decides the route *independently for each letter*. Two letters from the same sender to the same receiver may take different paths and arrive out of order.
> - **Virtual-Circuit Subnet (Connection-Oriented)** → Think of a **traditional telephone call**. First you *dial* (connection setup), a dedicated path is established, and all your words (packets) follow the *same established route*. After you hang up (teardown), the path is released.
>
> This single analogy covers ~80% of KTU exam questions on this topic.

### 1.3.1 What is a "Subnet"?

A **subnet** (short for *sub-network*) is a collection of **routers, links, and hosts** operating under a *common network-layer administration*. The Internet itself is a *network of subnets*.

- **Datagram Subnet** → uses the **Internet Protocol (IP)** → Internet
- **Virtual-Circuit Subnet** → uses protocols like **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM** (legacy/enterprise)

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Memorization)

- **Packet** → basic unit of data at the network layer
- **Datagram** → a packet in a connectionless network
- **Virtual Circuit (VC)** → a logical path in a connection-oriented network
- **Router** → Layer-3 switching device (also called **Gateway** in older literature)
- **TTL (Time To Live)** → hop counter to prevent infinite looping
- **Hops** → number of routers traversed between source and destination

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Issue 1: Store-and-Forward Packet Switching

### Operational Logic
1. A **source host** transmits a packet to its directly-connected router.
2. The router receives the *complete packet* into its **input buffer** (this is the *store* step).
3. The router examines the destination address, consults its **routing table**, and decides the outgoing interface.
4. The router transmits the packet on the chosen link (this is the *forward* step).
5. This process **repeats at every intermediate router** until the destination is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Store-and-Forward"?** Because the router *stores* the ENTIRE packet in memory before *forwarding* it. If a packet is corrupted in transit, only the *last hop's* link is wasted — the corrupted packet is dropped and the previous router can retransmit it from its buffer. This is the foundation of **reliability and hop-by-hop error recovery**.

### Key Engineering Insight
- **Cut-Through Switching** (used in some switches) → starts forwarding as soon as the destination address is read (faster but less reliable).
- **Store-and-Forward** is the **standard for routers** and is what KTU exams assume.

---

## 2.2 Issue 2: Services Provided to the Transport Layer

This is the **central architectural debate** of the network layer. Two camps exist:

### 2.2.1 The Two Schools of Thought

| School | Argument | Backed By |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| **Internet (Datagram) School** | "The network is inherently unreliable. Keep the network simple; push complexity to the hosts (end-to-end principle)." | **Internet Community** (Cerf, Kahn, Clark) |
| **Telecom (VC) School** | "The network should provide a reliable, connection-oriented service like the telephone system." | **Telecom Companies** (X.25, ATM) |

### 2.2.2 The Five Canonical Service Goals (Saltzer, Reed, Clark — End-to-End Arguments)
The network layer service should ideally:
1. Be **independent of router technology** (vendor neutrality).
2. **Shield the transport layer** from the number, type, and topology of routers.
3. Use **uniform addressing** across LANs and WANs.
4. Satisfy application requirements (throughput, delay, jitter).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"List the services provided to the transport layer,"* always mention the above 4 points **plus** the choice between **connectionless / connection-oriented** services.

---

## 2.3 Issue 3: Connectionless Service (Datagram Subnet)

### Operational Flow
- No setup phase — packets are sent immediately.
- Each packet is called a **datagram**.
- Routers maintain **routing tables** that map destination prefixes to outgoing interfaces.
- **Packets can take different paths** and **arrive out of order**.
- The **destination host** is responsible for reordering (handled by TCP, not the network).

### Technical Characteristics
- **No state in routers** beyond routing tables (stateless forwarding).
- **Routing decisions are made *per-packet*** (not per-flow).
- **Congestion control** is *end-to-end* (TCP).
- Used by the **Internet** (IP Protocol).

---

## 2.4 Issue 4: Connection-Oriented Service (Virtual-Circuit Subnet)

### Operational Flow
1. **Connection Setup:** Source sends a *CALL REQUEST* packet; a path is established through the network.
2. **Data Transfer:** All subsequent packets carry a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** instead of full destination addresses. Each router uses the VCI to look up its forwarding table.
3. **Connection Teardown:** A *CLEAR REQUEST* packet is sent to release resources.

### Technical Characteristics
- **Stateful routers** maintain a Virtual Circuit Table (VCI → outgoing interface, new VCI).
- **All packets follow the same path** (resource reservation possible).
- **In-order delivery** is guaranteed by the network.
- Used by **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM** (legacy), and modern **MPLS** (Multi-Protocol Label Switching).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VC ≠ Dedicated Physical Path.** A *virtual circuit* is a **logical** path — the underlying physical links can be shared with other VCs through **statistical multiplexing**.

---

## 2.5 Issue 5: Comparison of VC Subnets vs. Datagram Subnets (HIGH-YIELD)

This is the **most frequently asked** comparison in KTU exams. Memorize the table below.

| Property | Datagram Subnet | Virtual-Circuit Subnet |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **Setup phase** | Not required | Required |
| **Addressing** | Each packet carries full source + destination address | Each packet carries a short **VCI** |
| **Routing** | Per-packet (independent) | Per-connection (fixed path) |
| **Router state** | Routing tables only | Routing + VC tables (stateful) |
| **Effect of router failure** | Minimal — routes can be recomputed | All VCs through that router are **terminated** |
| **Congestion control** | Difficult | Easy (resources reserved at setup) |
| **Quality of Service (QoS)** | Hard to guarantee | Easier to guarantee |
| **Implementation complexity** | In the **hosts** | In the **network** |
| **Scalability** | Excellent (Internet scale) | Poor (state doesn't scale) |
| **Packet ordering** | Not guaranteed by network | Guaranteed by network |
| **Examples** | **IP / Internet** | **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS** |
| **Billing** | Difficult (per-packet) | Easy (per-VC connection time) |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Store-and-Forward Delay

For a packet of size $L$ bits traversing a path with $N$ links, each of rate $R$ bps, the **total store-and-forward delay** is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R}
$$

The **transmission delay at a single hop** is:

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

The **propagation delay per link** (length $d$, speed $s$) is:

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Store-and-forward *queues* packets at routers, giving rise to the classic **M/M/1 queuing** model in network engineering. Under high load, delay grows **asymptotically** toward infinity. This is why congestion control exists at both router (RED/ECN) and end-host (TCP) levels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Delay for Store-and-Forward Transmission

Consider a packet of size $L$ bits transmitted across a path of $N - 1$ intermediate routers, giving $N$ store-and-forward hops. Each link has a transmission rate of $R$ bps.

### Step 1 — Identify the delays involved
At each hop, the packet must be:
- (a) **Transmitted** onto the link (serialization delay) = $L / R$
- (b) **Propagated** across the physical medium = $d / s$
- (c) **Processed** at the router (lookup, queueing) = $T_{\text{proc}}$

> For KTU Module 3, we focus on the *transmission* and *propagation* delays (processing delay is often neglected in basic problems).

### Step 2 — Write delay for one hop

$$
T_{\text{hop}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}}
$$

Assuming $T_{\text{proc}} \approx 0$ (a common KTU simplification):

$$
T_{\text{hop}} \approx \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum over $N$ hops

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{hop},i} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{d_i}{s_i}
$$

If all links are identical (same $R$, $d$, $s$):

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \frac{L}{R} + N \cdot \frac{d}{s}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A 1500-byte packet travels through 4 routers. Each link has a bandwidth of 1 Mbps and length 2000 km. The signal propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Find the total end-to-end delay.

**Given:**
- $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 12000 \text{ bits}$
- $N = 5$ hops (4 routers between source and destination)
- $R = 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $d = 2000 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$
- $s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 4a — Transmission delay per hop:**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^6} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4b — Propagation delay per link:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4c — Total per hop:**

$$
T_{\text{hop}} = 12 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} = 22 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4d — Total end-to-end delay:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_{\text{hop}} = 5 \cdot 22 \text{ ms} = 110 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget to count the **last hop to the destination** as a transmission. A path with $k$ routers has $k + 1$ links and therefore $k + 1$ transmission events. In the example above, 4 routers ⇒ 5 links ⇒ 5 × 22 ms = 110 ms.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit Decision Tree

Use this matrix as a *decision tree* when answering design questions:

$$
\text{Question} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Recommendation}
$$

| Application Requirement | Datagram Subnet? | VC Subnet? |
|------------------------|------------------|------------|
| Bursty, unpredictable traffic (e.g., web) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Real-time, low-jitter traffic (e.g., VoIP, video) | ❌ Poor fit | ✅ Yes (with QoS) |
| Highly scalable internetwork (millions of nodes) | ✅ Yes | ❌ State explosion |
| Need for guaranteed bandwidth | ❌ No (best-effort) | ✅ Yes (resource reservation) |
| Router failure tolerance required | ✅ Excellent (recompute routes) | ❌ Poor (VC tear-down) |
| Simple host-side design | ❌ No (TCP complexity) | ✅ Yes (network handles it) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Proof: Why Datagram Subnets Need Full Addresses

In a **datagram subnet**, the $i$-th packet from host A to host B carries:

$$
\text{Header fields} = \{\text{Source IP}, \text{Destination IP}, \text{TTL}, \text{Protocol}, \ldots\}
$$

Header overhead per packet $= H_d$ bits.

In a **VC subnet**, the same packet carries:

$$
\text{Header fields} = \{\text{VCI}, \text{other small fields}, \ldots\}
$$

Header overhead per packet $= H_v$ bits, with $H_v \ll H_d$.

For $P$ packets of payload $L$ each, the **total bandwidth consumed** is:

$$
B_{\text{datagram}} = P \cdot (L + H_d)
$$

$$
B_{\text{VC}} = P \cdot (L + H_v) + O_{\text{setup}}
$$

> The datagram subnet **pays a per-packet header tax** of $H_d - H_v$ bits. This is the *price of stateless forwarding* — acceptable for the Internet because it enables **massive scalability**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Store-and-Forward Packet Switching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Host A Source] -->|Packet starts| r1[Router 1 Store]
    r1 -->|Buffer full then forward| r2[Router 2 Store]
    r2 -->|Buffer full then forward| r3[Router 3 Store]
    r3 -->|Final hop| dst[Host B Destination]

    style src fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style r1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style r2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style r3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style dst fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each yellow box represents a *store* event; each arrow represents a *forward* event. The packet cannot be forwarded until it has been **completely received** at the router.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Connectionless (Datagram) Subnet Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host A] -->|Packet 1 with full dest addr| R1[Router 1]
    R1 -->|Route decision per packet| R2a[Router 2 Path A]
    R1 -->|Route decision per packet| R2b[Router 3 Path B]
    R2a --> R4[Router 4]
    R2b --> R4
    R4 --> B[Host B Reorders if needed]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style R1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style R2a fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style R2b fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style R4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

> **Key takeaway:** Packet 1 and Packet 2 from A→B may traverse **different paths**. Host B (or TCP) is responsible for reordering.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit) Subnet Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Host A Source
    participant R1 as Router 1
    participant R2 as Router 2
    participant R3 as Router 3
    participant D as Host B Destination

    S->>R1: CALL REQUEST VCI 5
    R1->>R2: Forward with VCI 11
    R2->>R3: Forward with VCI 7
    R3->>D: CALL ACCEPTED
    Note over S,D: VC Established all packets use same path
    S->>R1: Data Packet VCI 5
    R1->>R2: VCI 11
    R2->>R3: VCI 7
    R3->>D: Deliver
    S->>R1: CLEAR REQUEST
    Note over S,D: VC Released
```

> **Key takeaway:** The VCI is **rewritten at every router** (e.g., VCI 5 → VCI 11 → VCI 7). All packets follow the *same logical path*.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Functional Architecture of Network Layer Design Choices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Network Layer Design Decisions]

    root --> S1[Store and Forward Switching]
    root --> S2[Service to Transport Layer]
    root --> S3[Connectionless Service]
    root --> S4[Connection Oriented Service]

    S1 --> S1a[Per hop buffering]
    S1 --> S1b[Error recovery at each hop]

    S2 --> S2a[Independent of router tech]
    S2 --> S2b[Uniform addressing]
    S2 --> S2c[Choose connectionless or connection oriented]

    S3 --> S3a[Datagram subnet]
    S3 --> S3b[Routing table only]
    S3 --> S3c[Per packet routing decision]
    S3 --> S3d[Example Internet IP]

    S4 --> S4a[Virtual Circuit subnet]
    S4 --> S4b[VC table per router]
    S4 --> S4c[Setup data teardown phases]
    S4 --> S4d[Example X.25 ATM MPLS]

    style root fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style S3 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style S4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **canonical taxonomy** Tanenbaum presents in Chapter 5 — knowing this hierarchy helps you answer any *design-issue* question, even if it is phrased unusually.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] (3 Marks)**
**List the five design issues of the network layer.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** — Routers receive the complete packet before forwarding.
2. **Services Provided to the Transport Layer** — Define whether service is connectionless or connection-oriented.
3. **Implementation of Connectionless Service** — Datagram-based routing.
4. **Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service** — Virtual-Circuit-based routing.
5. **Comparison of Virtual-Circuit and Datagram Subnets** — Trade-off analysis.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between datagram and virtual-circuit subnets (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Datagram Subnet | Virtual-Circuit Subnet |
|------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | No connection setup is required. | Connection setup is required before data transfer. |
| 2 | Each packet carries full source and destination address. | Each packet carries a short Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI). |
| 3 | Routing decision is taken for **every packet** independently. | Routing decision is taken **once** during setup; all packets follow the same path. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the five design issues of the network layer in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Store-and-Forward Packet Switching:**
In this mechanism, a router must receive the *entire* packet into its buffer before it can begin transmitting the packet on the outgoing link. This ensures hop-by-hop error detection and reliable buffering. If a packet is corrupted during transmission on a particular link, only that link's bandwidth is wasted — the previous router can retransmit from its stored copy.

**2. Services Provided to the Transport Layer:**
The network layer must offer a well-defined service to the transport layer (TCP/UDP). The design goals include:
- Independence from router/subnet technology
- Uniform addressing across heterogeneous networks
- Choice between connectionless and connection-oriented services

**3. Implementation of Connectionless Service:**
A datagram subnet routes each packet independently. The destination address in every packet is consulted by every router along the path, and a routing decision is made *per packet*. Routers maintain only routing tables. The Internet (IP) is the prime example.

**4. Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service:**
A virtual-circuit subnet first establishes a logical path. A short VCI label is then carried in each packet, allowing routers to perform fast lookup. Three phases exist: setup, data transfer, teardown. X.25, ATM, and MPLS are examples.

**5. Comparison of Datagram and Virtual-Circuit Subnets:**
See the detailed table in SECTION 2.5 above. Key trade-offs: scalability (DG wins) vs. QoS guarantees (VC wins).

---

**(b) A 4000-byte packet is sent across a network path with 3 intermediate routers. Each link has a bandwidth of 2 Mbps. The signal propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s and the physical length of each link is 1000 km. Calculate the total end-to-end delay. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 4000 \text{ bytes} = 32000 \text{ bits}$
- Number of intermediate routers = 3 → **Number of links $N = 4$**
- $R = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$
- $d = 1000 \text{ km} = 10^6 \text{ m}$ per link
- $s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Transmission delay per hop:**

$$
T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{32000}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation delay per link:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Delay per hop:**

$$
T_{\text{hop}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} = 16 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} = 21 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total end-to-end delay:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_{\text{hop}} = 4 \cdot 21 \text{ ms} = 84 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistake in delay problems is **forgetting the final link to the destination host**. If the problem says "$k$ intermediate routers," the number of links is $k + 1$, NOT $k$. Losing 2–3 marks here is routine.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (14 Marks)**

**(a) Compare datagram subnets and virtual-circuit subnets. List the advantages and disadvantages of each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Datagram Subnet | Virtual-Circuit Subnet |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Circuit setup | Not needed | Required |
| Addressing | Full source + dest in every packet | Short VCI per packet |
| State in routers | Only routing tables | Routing + VC tables |
| Routing | Per-packet | Per-connection |
| Router failure | Tolerated (routes recomputed) | Catastrophic (VCs die) |
| QoS | Hard | Easier |
| Congestion control | Difficult | Easier (resources reserved) |
| Host complexity | High (end-to-end TCP) | Low (network handles it) |
| Best for | Bursty, scalable traffic (Internet) | Real-time, QoS-sensitive traffic |

**Advantages of Datagram Subnets:**
- Highly **scalable** (no per-flow state)
- **Robust** to router/link failures
- Simple router design

**Disadvantages of Datagram Subnets:**
- Higher per-packet header overhead
- No QoS guarantees
- Packets may arrive out of order

**Advantages of Virtual-Circuit Subnets:**
- Predictable performance
- Guaranteed in-order delivery
- Easier billing (per-connection time)
- Supports **resource reservation** for QoS

**Disadvantages of Virtual-Circuit Subnets:**
- **State explosion** in routers
- Catastrophic failure if a router dies
- Complex setup, less scalable

---

**(b) Explain store-and-forward packet switching with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred over cut-through switching in the network layer? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In **store-and-forward packet switching**, each router along the path receives the *complete packet* into its buffer, performs error checking on the entire frame, consults its routing/forwarding table, and then transmits the packet onto the outgoing link. This is repeated at every intermediate router.

**Why preferred over cut-through switching?**
1. **Error Detection:** The full packet is buffered, so CRC/checksum errors are detected before forwarding. Cut-through may forward corrupted frames.
2. **Congestion Management:** Buffers allow queueing and active queue management (e.g., RED, ECN).
3. **Variable Output Rates:** A slow outgoing link can absorb a fast incoming link's traffic via buffering.
4. **Routing Table Update:** Time to update the forwarding table during congestion.
5. **Network Layer Mandate:** Routers operate at the network layer where reliability, not raw speed, is the primary concern.

*Diagram:* See SECTION 4.1 above (Diagram A).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "VCI" with "VPI"** — VCI is a *Virtual Channel Identifier*; VPI is *Virtual Path Identifier* (ATM-specific). Don't mix them.
> 2. **Calling IP "connectionless" but forgetting it offers NO reliability.** Internet Protocol is *unreliable*, *connectionless*, and *best-effort*.
> 3. **Saying "VC = dedicated path"** — Wrong! VC is a *logical* path over *shared* physical links (statistical multiplexing).
> 4. **Forgetting the $k+1$ link count** in delay problems.
> 5. **In VC, packets can arrive out of order if the network has multiple parallel VCs** — the *single* VC guarantees order, not multiple VCs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Network Layer Design Issues**

- ✅ The **5 design issues** are: Store-and-Forward, Services to Transport, Connectionless, Connection-Oriented, Comparison.
- ✅ **Store-and-Forward** = entire packet buffered before forwarding.
- ✅ **Datagram Subnet** = Connectionless, per-packet routing, Internet (IP) is the canonical example.
- ✅ **Virtual-Circuit Subnet** = Connection-oriented, fixed path, 3 phases (setup/data/teardown), VCI-based forwarding.
- ✅ **VCI is rewritten at every router** in a VC subnet.
- ✅ **Datagram wins on scalability and robustness**; **VC wins on QoS and simplicity for hosts**.
- ✅ **Total delay** for $N$ links: $T = N \cdot (L/R + d/s)$.
- ✅ Number of **links = routers + 1** in a path.
- ✅ **IP** is **unreliable, connectionless, best-effort** — never say "IP is reliable."
- ✅ **MPLS** is a modern, hybrid VC-style protocol (uses labels, but is connectionless from the customer's view).
- ✅ Internet uses the **end-to-end principle**: keep the network dumb, push intelligence to the edges.
- ✅ **ATM and X.25** are legacy VC protocols — useful only as historical/KTU reference.
- ✅ Router failure in VC subnets **terminates all VCs** through that router — a critical scalability weakness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle, Shortest Path Routing, Flooding, Distance Vector Routing, Link State Routing, Multicast Routing, Routing for Mobile Hosts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle, Shortest Path Routing, Flooding, Distance Vector Routing, Link State Routing, Multicast Routing, Routing for Mobile Hosts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Routing Algorithm?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Routing Algorithm** is a distributed algorithmic procedure used by network layer devices (routers/gateways) to determine the optimal path for forwarding data packets from a source to a destination across one or more networks. It builds and maintains a **routing table** containing destination addresses and the corresponding next-hop (output interface) information.

The routing algorithm is the **brain** of the Network Layer. While the data plane simply forwards packets using lookup tables, the **control plane** (routing algorithm) is responsible for *intelligently deciding* which path the packet must take.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classification Snapshot — Adaptive vs Non-Adaptive:**
> - **Non-Adaptive (Static) Routing:** Decisions are pre-computed and loaded at boot time. Example: Shortest Path Routing with fixed weights.
> - **Adaptive (Dynamic) Routing:** Decisions change with traffic load or topology. Examples: Distance Vector Routing, Link State Routing.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Navigator

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy (GPS Navigation):**
> Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** and your GPS (think Google Maps) must choose a route. The GPS considers:
> 1. **Distance** of each road segment.
> 2. **Traffic congestion** (load).
> 3. **Road closures** (link failures).
> 4. **Toll charges** (cost metric).
>
> The routing algorithm in a router does **exactly the same thing** — it treats every router as a city, every link as a road, and the cost (distance, delay, hops) as the toll/traffic. The router constantly re-evaluates the "best highway" to forward your packet, just like a GPS recalculates when you miss a turn.

---

### 1.3 Routing Algorithm Goals (KTU Board Perspective)

| Goal | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Packet must reach its intended destination. |
| **Simplicity** | Low computational overhead on the router CPU. |
| **Robustness** | Must adapt gracefully to link/router failures. |
| **Stability** | Must converge to a steady state (no oscillation). |
| **Fairness & Optimality** | No single stream should monopolize resources. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse the terms "**Routing**" (building the map/table) with "**Forwarding**" (using the table to move a packet). *Routing = Control Plane.* *Forwarding = Data Plane.* Examiners award marks for this distinction.

---

### 1.4 The Optimality Principle — Foundation Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Optimality Principle (Statement):**
> If router **J** lies on the optimal (shortest-cost) path from router **I** to router **K**, then the optimal path from **J** to **K** also lies along the same route. Formally:
> $$\text{If } P(I \to K) = I \to \cdots \to J \to \cdots \to K \text{ is optimal, then } P(J \to K) = J \to \cdots \to K \text{ is also optimal.}$$

**Intuition:** Suppose a better path existed from J to K. We could simply *splice* it onto the I→J portion to produce a strictly cheaper I→K path, contradicting the assumption that the original I→K path was already optimal. This is a proof by contradiction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sink Tree (Destination Tree):**
> A direct consequence of the Optimality Principle is that the set of all optimal paths from **every** source to a **single fixed destination** forms a tree rooted at the destination. This is called a **Sink Tree**. Routing algorithms strive to approximate an all-sink-trees set of forwarding tables (the *Grotta* of trees).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain Analogy:** Think of a banyan tree with the destination as the root and each source as a leaf. Every drop of water (packet) flowing from a leaf has exactly one deterministic, optimal path down the tree branches to the root. No cycles, no loops, no backtracking.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Shortest Path Routing (Dijkstra's Algorithm)

This is the **foundational shortest-path algorithm** in routing. The router runs it locally to compute the least-cost path to every other node.

**Operational Steps (Router-Side Logic):**
1. The network topology is modeled as a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = routers and $E$ = links with cost metric $w(u,v)$.
2. A "source" router $S$ is selected. Initially, every other node $v$ is assigned a **tentative** distance $D(v) = \infty$ and predecessor $P(v) = \text{null}$.
3. The source $S$ is marked **permanent** with $D(S) = 0$.
4. From the most recently permanently labeled node $u$, examine every neighbour $v$. Update:
$$D(v) = \min\bigl(D(v),\; D(u) + w(u,v)\bigr)$$
5. The unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance is promoted to permanent.
6. Repeat until all nodes are permanent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Box:**
> - **Permanent Label (P):** A finalized shortest distance. No further updates allowed.
> - **Tentative Label (T):** A best-so-far guess, subject to revision.
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ for naive implementation; $O((E+V)\log V)$ with min-heap (Fibonacci heap gives $O(E+V\log V)$).

---

### 2.2 Flooding

**Definition:** Every incoming packet is transmitted on **every outgoing line** except the one on which it arrived.

**Theoretical Rule-Set:**
- Rule 1 — **Hop Counter:** Each packet carries a TTL (Time-To-Live) field. Decremented at every hop; discarded at 0.
- Rule 2 — **Sequence Numbering:** Every router remembers recently seen sequence numbers; duplicates are dropped.
- Rule 3 — **Selective Flooding:** The router only forwards on lines that are "approximately" in the right direction (saves bandwidth).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine spilling water from a single point. Water flows in *every* direction, finds *every* possible path, and the first droplet to reach the destination wins. Used in military networks and emergency broadcast systems where robustness trumps efficiency.

---

### 2.3 Distance Vector Routing (Bellman–Ford)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Recursive Equation (The Bellman–Ford Equation):**
> $$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \bigr\}$$
> Where $D_x(y)$ is the best-known distance from router $x$ to destination $y$, $v$ iterates over all neighbours of $x$, and $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the direct link $x \to v$.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Each router $x$ maintains a vector $(D_x(y_1), D_x(y_2), \ldots)$ for **every** destination $y_i$.
2. Periodically (typically every 30 s) and on topology changes, the router sends its entire distance vector to **immediate neighbours only**.
3. Upon receiving an updated vector from neighbour $v$, the router recomputes its own vector using the Bellman–Ford minimization.
4. If the local table changes, the router immediately informs its neighbours.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Count-to-Infinity Problem:**
