> A classic instability issue. Suppose the link $A \to B$ fails. $A$ advertises a distance of $\infty$ to $B$ via $C$, but $C$ still thinks it can reach $B$ via $A$ with cost 2. Thus $A$ updates to 3, then $C$ updates to 4, then $A$ to 5… counting slowly up to infinity. *Kills convergence.*
>
> **Heuristics used in practice:**
> - **Split Horizon:** Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom you learned it.
> - **Poisoned Reverse:** Advertise that route back with cost $\infty$.
> - **Triggered Updates:** Send an update immediately upon change, not waiting for the timer.

---

### 2.4 Link State Routing (Modern Standard — OSPF, IS-IS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Canonical Steps of Link State Routing:**
> 1. **Discover Neighbours** — Hello protocol; learn direct neighbours.
> 2. **Measure Cost to Each Neighbour** — Round-trip-time (RTT) measured via ECHO packets.
> 3. **Construct Link State Packet (LSP)** — Contains router ID, neighbour list, sequence number, age.
> 4. **Distribute LSP via Reliable Flooding** — Every router eventually receives every LSP.
> 5. **Compute Shortest Paths** — Run Dijkstra's algorithm locally to populate the forwarding table.

**Why Link State Converges Faster than Distance Vector:**
- Every router has a **complete map** of the topology (not just neighbours' views).
- LSPs are flooded **immediately** on change (no 30-second wait).
- Dijkstra's algorithm is loop-free by construction.
- *Trade-off:* More memory (stores the full graph) and more CPU (Dijkstra) per router.

---

### 2.5 Multicast Routing

**Problem Statement:** Efficiently deliver a single source packet stream to a set of *subscribed* receivers (a multicast group), avoiding per-receiver unicast duplication.

**Two Foundational Mechanisms:**
- **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF):** Router accepts a multicast packet from $S$ only if it arrived on the link that is on $S$'s own shortest path back to $S$. Otherwise, it is silently dropped. This prunes loops without a spanning tree.
- **Spanning Tree / Pruning:** Build a *distribution tree* from source to all group members. Prune branches with no listeners (controlled by IGMP prune messages).

---

### 2.6 Routing for Mobile Hosts

**The Mobility Problem:** Traditional IP routing assumes the host's network prefix is fixed. A mobile host crossing into a new subnet must remain reachable at its **home address**.

**Three Entities:**
- **Home Network** — The subnet where the mobile host *natively* belongs.
- **Home Agent (HA)** — A router in the home network that tunnels packets to the mobile's current location.
- **Foreign Agent (FA)** — A router in the visited (foreign) network that delivers tunneled packets to the mobile host and acts as its proxy.
- **Care-of Address (CoA)** — The temporary IP address acquired by the mobile host in the foreign network.

> [!WARNING]
> **Triangle Routing Problem:** The path Sender → HA → Mobile is non-optimal when the Sender is physically close to the Mobile. The triangle's three legs represent wasted bandwidth. *Solution:* Mobile-IP route optimization allows the Sender to learn the CoA and tunnel directly.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — these are the equations examiners test.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optimality Principle (splicing) | $D(I,K) = D(I,J) + D(J,K)$ when $J$ lies on optimal $I \to K$ | Sink Tree proof |
| 2 | Dijkstra's relaxation | $D(v) = \min\{D(v), D(u) + w(u,v)\}$ | Shortest Path Routing |
| 3 | Bellman–Ford | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Distance Vector |
| 4 | Count-to-Infinity Bound | Converges in $O(\text{network diameter})$ iterations | DV pitfall |
| 5 | Dijkstra Time Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive; $O(E + V \log V)$ with heap | LS Routing |
| 6 | Flooding Duplication Bound | $N!$ duplicates in worst case, $N-1$ copies per node | Flooding |
| 7 | Mobile-IP Tunneling (logical path) | Sender $\to$ HA $\to$ FA $\to$ MH | Mobile Routing |
| 8 | RPF Prune Rule | Drop packet if $\text{arrival\_port} \neq \text{shortest\_path\_port\_to\_source}$ | Multicast |
| 9 | Distance Vector Memory | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ for full table across all routers | DV |
| 10 | LS Storage | $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert)$ per router (full topology) | LS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on set notation:** Per KTU typography norms, $\vert V \vert$ denotes cardinality of the vertex set $V$ and $\mid$ denotes the conditional divider in minimisation (e.g., $\min_v$).

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm | Internet Protocol | Engineering Deployment |
|---|---|---|
| Distance Vector | **RIP** (Routing Information Protocol) | Small intranets, legacy systems |
| Link State | **OSPF** (Open Shortest Path First), **IS-IS** | ISP core, enterprise backbones |
| Path Vector | **BGP** (Border Gateway Protocol) | Inter-AS routing (the Internet's spine) |
| Mobile IP | **Mobile IPv4 / Mobile IPv6** | 4G/5G handover, VoWiFi, IoT |
| Multicast | **PIM-SM**, **PIM-DM**, **DVMRP** | IPTV, financial tickers, stock feeds |
| Flooding (controlled) | Used in **ad hoc networks** (AODV, DSR) | Disaster recovery, military MANETs |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Dijkstra's Algorithm on a 6-Node Graph

**Network Topology (with link costs):**

We use a small Internet-like topology:

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | 4 | B–C | 2 | C–D | 3 |
| A–E | 1 | C–E | 5 | D–E | 6 |
| B–E | 3 | A–D | 7 | C–F | 8 |
| D–F | 2 | E–F | 10 | | |

Source: **A**. Compute shortest path from A to every other node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in tables:** $(P, T)$ means $P$ = predecessor (the node we came *from*), $T$ = tentative cumulative distance from A. A **bold** entry has been promoted to **permanent**.

**Step 0 — Initialization:**

Working with $A$ as source.

| Node | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Label | (A, **4**) | (–, $\infty$) | (A, **7**) | (A, **1**) | (–, $\infty$) |
| Status | T | T | T | **P** | T |

A is marked permanent with $D(A) = 0$. Examine neighbours of A: B(4), D(7), E(1). Smallest tentative → E gets promoted to permanent.

**Step 1 — Promote E (distance 1):**

Check E's neighbours B, C, F.

| Node | New candidate via E | Existing | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | $1 + 3 = 4$ | 4 (via A) | Tie — keep first |
| C | $1 + 5 = 6$ | $\infty$ | Update B's record via E |
| F | $1 + 10 = 11$ | $\infty$ | Update to (E,11) |

Resulting table (tentative unless bold):

| Node | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Label | (A, **4**) | (E, **6**) | (A, **7**) | (–, **1**) | (E, **11**) |
| Status | **P** | T | T | **P** | T |

Smallest tentative is C with distance 6.

**Step 2 — Promote C (distance 6):**

Check C's neighbours: B(2), D(3), E(5), F(8).

| Node | New candidate via C | Existing | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | $6 + 2 = 8$ | 4 (via A) | No improvement |
| D | $6 + 3 = 9$ | 7 (via A) | No improvement |
| E | already permanent | 1 | No change |
| F | $6 + 8 = 14$ | 11 (via E) | No improvement |

Table after Step 2: B(4,P), C(6,P), D(7,T), E(1,P), F(11,T). Smallest tentative = B(4).

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** B is already permanent, so we skip it. Move on to next smallest tentative = D(7).

**Step 3 — Promote D (distance 7):**

D's neighbours: A, C(3), E(6), F(2).

| Node | New candidate via D | Existing | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| F | $7 + 2 = 9$ | 11 (via E) | **Improvement** → update to (D,9) |

**Step 4 — Promote B (distance 4):**

B's neighbours: A, C(2), E(3). No new improvements.

**Step 5 — Promote F (distance 9, after D's update):**

F's neighbours: C, D, E — all already permanent. No improvements.

**Final Result — Sink Tree from A:**

| Destination | Distance | Predecessor (next hop) | Forwarding Table Entry at A |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | 4 | A direct | Send to B via port to A's link AB |
| C | 6 | E | Send via E, then E→B→C (or directly E→C) |
| D | 7 | A direct | Send via A's link AD |
| E | 1 | A direct | Send directly to E |
| F | 9 | D | Send via A's link AD, then D→F |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **sink tree** to justify your predecessor chain. The KTU evaluator checks that your predecessor forms an acyclic path back to the source. A common mistake is forgetting to backtrack to construct the actual next-hop.

---

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Convergence of the Bellman–Ford Equation

We want to show that the iterative update rule
$$D_x^{(k+1)}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl\{ c(x,v) + D_v^{(k)}(y) \bigr\}$$
converges to the true shortest-path distance after at most $h$ iterations, where $h$ is the maximum number of hops in any shortest path.

**Step-by-step justification:**

1. **Base Case ($k = 0$):** A node knows only its neighbours. So $D_x^{(0)}(y) = c(x,y)$ if $y$ is a direct neighbour, and $\infty$ otherwise. This is trivially correct for paths of 1 hop.

2. **Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume after $k$ iterations, $D_x^{(k)}(y)$ equals the length of the shortest path from $x$ to $y$ that uses at most $k$ hops.

3. **Inductive Step ($k+1$):** Any path from $x$ to $y$ using at most $k+1$ hops must first traverse a direct link to some neighbour $v$, then a path of at most $k$ hops from $v$ to $y$. The optimal choice minimises over $v$:
$$D_x^{(k+1)}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl\{ c(x,v) + D_v^{(k)}(y) \bigr\}$$

4. **Termination:** Since the network has finite diameter $h$, no shortest path needs more than $h$ hops (each hop is at least 1). After $h$ iterations, no further changes occur:
$$\lim_{k \to \infty} D_x^{(k)}(y) = D_x^{*}(y) \quad \text{(true shortest distance)}$$

5. **Complexity:** Each iteration is $O(\mid V \mid \cdot \mid E \mid)$, giving a total worst case of $O(h \cdot \mid V \mid \cdot \mid E \mid)$.

$$\boxed{\;\therefore \text{Bellman–Ford converges in at most } h = \text{network diameter iterations.}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Count-to-Infinity (3-Node Topology)

**Topology:**
- $A \to B$: cost **1**
- $B \to C$: cost **1**
- $A \to C$: cost **2** (direct, but routers don't know this initially)

**Initial Distance Vectors (each row is "advertised to neighbours"):**

| Time | $D_A$ to B, C | $D_B$ to A, C | $D_C$ to A, B |
|---|---|---|---|
| t=0 | (0, 1) | (1, 0) | (1, 1) |
| t=1 (link B–C fails!) | (0, 1) | (1, $\infty$) | (1, 1) |

After failure, B's vector to C becomes $\infty$. But C still believes it can reach B with cost 1.

**Iteration-by-iteration count-up:**

| Step | A's view of C | B's view of C | C's view of B |
|---|---|---|---|
| t=1 | min(2, $1+\infty$) = 2 | $\infty$ | 1 (stale) |
| t=2 | min(2, $1+3$) = **3** | min($\infty, 1+1$) = **2** | 1 (stale) |
| t=3 | min(3, $1+3$) = **3** | min(2, $1+2$) = **2** | min(1, $2+1$) = 3 (catches up) |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| t=k | k+1 | k | k-1 |

**Convergence to infinity** — the cost slowly increments by 1 each exchange. With RIP, infinity is capped at 16 hops. This is the **count-to-infinity** problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Mitigation (Poisoned Reverse Demonstration):**
> If $C$ applies *split horizon with poison reverse*, then when $C$ advertises back to $B$, it states $D_C(B) = \infty$ (because $C$ learned that route from $B$). B then immediately realises that the route is dead. Convergence time drops from $O(h)$ to $O(1)$.

---

### 3.4 Mathematical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| Property | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) | Path Vector (BGP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **View of Network** | Neighbours only | Full topology | Full AS path |
| **Update Trigger** | Periodic + on change | Event-driven (flood) | Incremental |
| **Convergence** | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (seconds) | Slow (minutes) |
| **Memory** | $O(\mid V \mid)$ | $O(\mid E \vert + \mid V \mid)$ | $O(\text{paths})$ |
| **CPU** | Bellman–Ford ($O(VE)$) | Dijkstra ($O(V^2)$) | Path processing |
| **Hierarchy** | Flat | Two-level (area) | Inter-AS |
| **Loop-Free?** | No (slow loops) | Yes (Dijkstra) | Yes (full path) |
| **Algorithm Used** | Bellman–Ford | Dijkstra | Best-path selection |
| **Standard Example** | RIP v1/v2 | OSPF, IS-IS | BGP-4 |
| **Stability Heuristics** | Split horizon, poison reverse | Sequence numbers, aging | Route flap damping |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `r1`, `ls1`, `fa1`) — never reserved keywords. Labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

### 4.1 The Optimality Principle — Splicing Argument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeI[I - SOURCE] -->|cost a| nodeJ[J - ON OPTIMAL PATH]
    nodeJ -->|cost b| nodeK[K - DESTINATION]
    nodeJ -. OPTIMAL SUFFIX .-> nodeK
    nodeI -. OPTIMAL PREFIX .-> nodeJ
    classDef opt fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeJ,nodeK opt
```

> **Reading aid:** The solid path I→J→K is *proven* optimal. If there were a *cheaper* route from J to K (say J→L→K), we would splice it onto I→J to get a cheaper I→K path, contradicting optimality.

### 4.2 Sink Tree Rooted at Destination K

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootK((K - DEST ROOT))
    rootK --> rJ1[J1]
    rootK --> rJ2[J2]
    rootK --> rJ3[J3]
    rJ1 --> rI1[I1]
    rJ1 --> rI2[I2]
    rJ2 --> rI3[I3]
    rJ2 --> rI4[I4]
    rJ3 --> rI5[I5]
    rJ3 --> rI6[I6]
    classDef root fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef mid fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef leaf fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333
    class rootK root
    class rJ1,rJ2,rJ3 mid
    class rI1,rI2,rI3,rI4,rI5,rI6 leaf
```

> **Reading aid:** All optimal paths to K form a tree (no cycles, single root at K). The forwarding table at each internal node picks *one* parent edge — the one on the sink tree.

### 4.3 Link State Routing — Five-Step Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ls1[STEP 1: DISCOVER NEIGHBOURS\nHELLO PACKETS] --> ls2[STEP 2: MEASURE LINK COSTS\nECHO/ECHO REPLY RTT]
    ls2 --> ls3[STEP 3: BUILD LSP\nLINK STATE PACKET]
    ls3 --> ls4[STEP 4: RELIABLE FLOOD LSP\nTO ALL ROUTERS]
    ls4 --> ls5[STEP 5: DIJKSTRA SHORTEST PATH\nPOPULATE FORWARDING TABLE]
    classDef step fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    class ls1,ls2,ls3,ls4,ls5 step
```

### 4.4 Distance Vector — Information Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dv1[ROUTER X HOLDS VECTOR DX]
    dv2[NEIGHBOUR V ADDS CVX]
    dv3[X MINIMISES: DX Y EQUALS MIN CVX PLUS DV Y]
    dv4[IF CHANGED BROADCAST TO NEIGHBOURS]
    dv4 --> dv2
    classDef dv fill:#e6f9ff,stroke:#006699,stroke-width:2px
    class dv1,dv2,dv3,dv4 dv
```

### 4.5 Mobile Host Routing — Home Agent / Foreign Agent Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    senderNode[SENDER HOST] -->|packet to home IP| homeNet[HOME NETWORK]
    homeNet --> homeAgent[HOME AGENT HA]
    homeAgent -->|encapsulated tunnel via CoA| foreignNet[FOREIGN NETWORK]
    foreignNet --> foreignAgent[FOREIGN AGENT FA]
    foreignAgent -->|decapsulated delivery| mobileHost[MOBILE HOST MH]
    mobileHost -. reply direct .-> senderNode
    classDef sender fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    classDef home fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600
    classDef foreign fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003399
    classDef mobile fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#990066
    class senderNode sender
    class homeNet,homeAgent home
    class foreignNet,foreignAgent foreign
    class mobileHost mobile
```

> **Reading aid:** Notice the asymmetry — packets *toward* the mobile host travel Sender→HA→FA→MH (the long triangle leg), but packets *from* the mobile host go directly back to the sender. This is the **triangle routing problem** in Mobile IPv4.

### 4.6 Multicast Routing — Reverse Path Forwarding Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    msrc((SOURCE S))
    msrc --> m1[R1]
    msrc --> m2[R2]
    m1 --> m3[R3]
    m1 --> m4[R4 - NOT A LISTENER PRUNED]
    m2 --> m5[R5]
    m3 --> m6[R6 - LISTENER]
    m5 --> m7[R7 - LISTENER]
    m4 -. PRUNED .-> m4x(( ))
    classDef src fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef keep fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    classDef prune fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#999999,stroke-dasharray:5
    class msrc src
    class m1,m2,m3,m5,m6,m7 keep
    class m4,m4x prune
```

> **Reading aid:** R4 has no multicast listeners, so it sends an **IGMP Prune** message upstream. R1 then stops forwarding multicast packets on that branch — saving bandwidth. The shaded branch is the pruned "raptor claw" that is no longer active.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain the Optimality Principle as applied to routing algorithms. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Optimality Principle** states that if router **J** lies on the *optimal* (shortest-cost) path from router **I** to router **K**, then the optimal path from **J** to **K** is the *suffix* of the same route. Formally:
> $$D(I,K) = D(I,J) + D(J,K)$$
>
> **Justification (by contradiction):** Suppose a strictly cheaper path $J \to K'$ exists. Splicing it onto the optimal $I \to J$ prefix would produce a strictly cheaper $I \to K$ path — contradicting optimality. Hence, the suffix from $J$ onward must itself be optimal.
>
> **Consequence:** The set of all optimal paths to a single destination forms a *tree* rooted at the destination — called the **Sink Tree**. A router that knows the sink tree has all the information it needs to forward packets optimally.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Distance Vector (DV) | Link State (LS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **View of network** | Sees only neighbours' distances. | Holds complete topology. |
| 2 | **Update trigger** | Periodic + triggered. | Event-driven flooding. |
| 3 | **Algorithm** | Bellman–Ford equation. | Dijkstra's algorithm. |
| 4 | **Convergence** | Slow (count-to-infinity). | Fast (seconds). |
| 5 | **Memory** | $O(\mid V \mid)$ per router. | $O(\mid E \mid + \mid V \mid)$ per router. |
| 6 | **Example protocol** | RIP, IGRP. | OSPF, IS-IS. |

> A concise 1-line exam-ready summary: *DV tells "how far," LS tells "how the network looks."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Modified Pattern]`**

**(a)** Apply Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm to compute the shortest path tree (sink tree) from source node **A** for the following weighted graph. Show all intermediate label updates. **(7 Marks)**

```
        4
   A ------- B
   | \       | \
 7 |  \1     |3  2
   |   \     |   \
   D    E----+    C
   |   /|    5    |
   |  6 |         | 8
   | /  |10       |
   F----+         F
      \2/
        (Note: F connects to D with cost 2 and to E with cost 10; C connects to F with cost 8)
```

> Simplified adjacency for clarity:
> - $A\text{-}B = 4$, $A\text{-}D = 7$, $A\text{-}E = 1$
> - $B\text{-}C = 2$, $B\text{-}E = 3$
> - $C\text{-}E = 5$, $C\text{-}F = 8$
> - $D\text{-}E = 6$, $D\text{-}F = 2$
> - $E\text{-}F = 10$

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Permanent Node | B | C | D | E | F | Predecessor Chain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A (0) | (A,4) | (–,$\infty$) | (A,7) | (A,1) | (–,$\infty$) | — |
| 1 | E (1) | (A,4) | (E,6) | (A,7) | — | (E,11) | E from A |
| 2 | B (4) | — | (B,**6**) | (A,7) | — | (E,11) | B from A direct |
| 3 | C (6) | — | — | (A,7) | — | (E,11)→(C,14) no update | C from E |
| 4 | D (7) | — | — | — | — | (D,**9**) ← improved | D from A direct |
| 5 | F (9) | — | — | — | — | — | F from D |

**Final Sink Tree from A:**

- $A \to B$: distance 4, next-hop B
- $A \to C$: distance 6, next-hop E
- $A \to D$: distance 7, next-hop D
- $A \to E$: distance 1, next-hop E
- $A \to F$: distance 9, next-hop D (then D→F)

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five steps of Link State Routing** as used in OSPF. Discuss why it converges faster than Distance Vector Routing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Five Steps of Link State Routing:**

1. **Discover Neighbours** — On boot, the router sends HELLO packets on each point-to-point link and learns the identity (router ID) of every directly attached neighbour.

2. **Measure Link Cost** — The router sends an ECHO packet and divides the round-trip-time by 2 to estimate delay. Alternatively, the link cost is statically configured (e.g., inversely proportional to bandwidth).

3. **Build the Link State Packet (LSP)** — An LSP contains:
   - Router ID (sender).
   - Sequence number and age (TTL).
   - List of neighbours and the cost to each.
   - Flags (e.g., for area border routers).

4. **Distribute LSP via Reliable Flooding** — The LSP is sent (or re-sent) to *every* router in the area, not just neighbours. Each receiving router acknowledges the LSP and re-floods it onward. Sequence numbers and ageing prevent duplicates and discard stale LSPs.

5. **Compute Shortest Paths** — Each router runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** over its complete LSP database to build the sink tree and the forwarding table.

**Block Diagram (functional):**

```
[HELLO / ECHO]  →  [LSP Builder]  →  [Reliable Flooder]  →  [LSP Database]
                                                                       |
                                                                       v
                                          [Forwarding Table] ← [Dijkstra Engine]
```

**Why Link State Converges Faster:**

| Reason | Distance Vector | Link State |
|---|---|---|
| Network view | Each router's view is a *partial, distance-only* snapshot. | Each router has the *full* topology, hence identical global view. |
| Update propagation | Hop-by-hop, neighbour-to-neighbour. | One-shot flooding — every router learns in one round. |
| Information content | Only distances; not the actual links. | LSPs include complete link state. |
| Loop detection | Cannot detect loops; counts to infinity. | Dijkstra produces a loop-free sink tree by construction. |
| Convergence time | $O(h)$ iterations (slow). | $O(1)$ LSP-flood + Dijkstra run (fast). |

> Hence, for large intranets (e.g., enterprise WANs), Link State Routing is preferred.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Modified Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain **Distance Vector Routing** with the Bellman–Ford equation. Use a 3-router example to demonstrate the **count-to-infinity problem** and show how **poisoned reverse** mitigates it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Distance Vector Routing — Definition:**
Each router $x$ maintains a vector $D_x = (D_x(y_1), D_x(y_2), \ldots, D_x(y_n))$ giving its current best estimate of the distance to every destination $y_i$ in the network. Vectors are exchanged with *immediate neighbours only*, periodically (e.g., every 30 s) and on any local topology change.

**Bellman–Ford Recursion:**
$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \bigl\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \bigr\}$$

Where $N(x)$ is the set of direct neighbours of $x$, and $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the direct link from $x$ to $v$.

**3-Router Example Demonstrating Count-to-Infinity:**

> Topology: $A \to B$ cost 1, $B \to C$ cost 1. (At time $t=0$, all links work.)

**Initial Distance Vectors:**

| Router | $D(\cdot)$ to A | $D(\cdot)$ to B | $D(\cdot)$ to C |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| B | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| C | 2 | 1 | 0 |

> At $t=1$, link $B \to C$ fails. New cost $c(B,C) = \infty$.

**Count-to-Infinity Trace:**

| t | A's view of C | B's view of C | C's view of B |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | min(2, 1+$\infty$) = 2 | $\infty$ | 1 (stale) |
| 2 | min(2, 1+2) = 3 | min($\infty$, 1+1) = 2 | 1 (stale) |
| 3 | min(3, 1+3) = 4 | min(2, 1+2) = 3 | min(1, 2+1) = 3 |
| 4 | 5 | 4 | 4 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| k | k+1 | k | k-1 |

The cost **counts up** by 1 each exchange cycle. In RIP, $\infty$ is capped at 16 hops.

**Poisoned Reverse Mitigation:**
When $C$ advertises back to $B$, instead of saying "I can reach B with cost 1", it says "I can reach B with cost **$\infty$**" (poisoned reverse). Then $B$ immediately knows that the route via $C$ is dead, since $C$ itself denies it. **Convergence drops to $O(1)$ for the failed link.**

---

**(b)** Explain the **Mobile IP architecture** for routing packets to a mobile host that has moved to a foreign network. Use a diagram. Discuss the **triangle routing problem** and its solution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Mobility Problem:**
A mobile host (MH) has a permanent **home address** in its home network. When it moves to a *foreign* network, classical IP routing (which assumes host-to-prefix binding is fixed) cannot deliver packets to it. **Mobile IP** solves this with three core entities:

| Entity | Role |
|---|---|
| **Home Agent (HA)** | Resides in the home network; intercepts packets destined to the MH and tunnels them to the MH's current location. |
| **Foreign Agent (FA)** | Resides in the visited network; decapsulates the tunnel and delivers the packet to the MH. |
| **Care-of Address (CoA)** | The temporary IP address identifying the MH in the foreign network (either an FA's address — *foreign-agent CoA*, or a locally assigned address — *co-located CoA*). |

**Packet-Forwarding Flow:**

```
SENDER  →  HOME NETWORK  →  HOME AGENT (encapsulate, tunnel)
                                      ↓
                          FOREIGN NETWORK  →  FOREIGN AGENT
                                                  ↓
                                          MOBILE HOST (decapsulate, deliver)
```

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Registration:** The MH, on entering the foreign network, hears FA advertisements and registers its CoA with the FA. The FA relays this to the HA, which binds the home address ↔ CoA in a mobility binding table.

2. **Tunneling:** The sender transmits a packet to the MH's *home address*. The HA receives it (intercepts on the home link), encapsulates the original packet inside a new IP header addressed to the CoA, and tunnels it across the Internet to the FA.

3. **Decapsulation:** The FA strips the outer IP header and delivers the original packet to the MH over the foreign link.

4. **Reverse path:** When the MH replies, it can send directly to the sender (no tunneling needed), since the sender's address is fixed.

**Triangle Routing Problem:**
> The forward path is: Sender → HA (far away) → FA → MH. But often the *Sender* is physically close to the *MH*, and a direct Sender → MH path would be far cheaper.

**Solution — Route Optimization (Mobile IPv6 / RFC 3775):**
- After registration, the HA sends a **Binding Update** to the Sender (and any correspondent node).
- The Sender then tunnels packets *directly* to the CoA, bypassing the HA.
- The triangle becomes a straight line: Sender → FA → MH.
- Security note: Binding Updates are authenticated to prevent hijacking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ✘ Do **not** confuse the **care-of address (CoA)** with the **home address**. The CoA is a *temporary* address; the home address is *permanent* and the one used as the upper-layer identifier (e.g., for TCP).
> - ✘ Do **not** claim that Mobile IP eliminates the need for a Home Agent. The HA is **mandatory** for at least the initial registration and binding. In Mobile IPv6, route optimization is *additive*, not a replacement.
> - ✘ Do **not** omit the foreign agent when describing foreign-agent CoA. A *co-located* CoA does not need an FA, but the *foreign-agent* CoA does.
> - ✘ For count-to-infinity, do **not** claim that "split horizon alone" solves it — *split horizon* prevents the loop, but *poisoned reverse* is the *stronger* version that explicitly marks the bad route as $\infty$.
> - ✘ In Dijkstra's, do **not** forget to update **predecessor labels** as well as distance labels. Examiners check both.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Save this list to memory!**

- [ ] **Routing vs Forwarding:** Routing = Control Plane (decision-making); Forwarding = Data Plane (packet movement).
- [ ] **Optimality Principle:** Subpaths of optimal paths are themselves optimal. *Proof by contradiction via splicing.*
- [ ] **Sink Tree:** All optimal paths to a destination form a tree rooted at the destination.
- [ ] **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Used in **Link State**. Tentative → Permanent label promotion. Complexity $O(\mid V \vert^2)$ naive, $O(E + V \log V)$ with heap.
- [ ] **Flooding:** Every incoming packet is sent on every outgoing line. Use *hop count* and *sequence numbers* to limit damage. Use selectively for efficiency.
- [ ] **Distance Vector (RIP):** Uses **Bellman–Ford** recursion $D_x(y) = \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$. Sends full table to neighbours only. Suffers from **count-to-infinity**.
- [ ] **Count-to-Infinity Fixes:** *Split horizon*, *Poisoned Reverse*, *Triggered Updates*. RIP caps infinity at 16 hops.
- [ ] **Link State (OSPF, IS-IS):** 5 steps — Discover, Measure, Build LSP, Flood, Run Dijkstra. **Converges fast**, more memory.
- [ ] **Multicast Routing:** Uses **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** to prune loops; **IGMP Prune** to cut off branches with no listeners.
- [ ] **Mobile IP Entities:** Home Agent, Foreign Agent, Care-of Address. Forwarding uses **tunneling** (encapsulation) from HA to FA.
- [ ] **Triangle Routing:** Sender → HA → FA → MH. Solve with **Route Optimization** (Binding Update → direct tunnel).
- [ ] **BGP** is a *path vector* protocol (not DV, not LS) — used for inter-AS routing; **KAM** key fact: BGP *prevents* loops by carrying the full AS path.
- [ ] **Ad Hoc Routing** (AODV, DSR) uses *controlled flooding* with sequence numbers — a direct application of Flooding + DV ideas.
- [ ] **Convergence Comparison:** DV slow ($O(h)$), LS fast (single flood + Dijkstra), Path Vector slow (policy-driven).
- [ ] **Memory Comparison:** DV = $O(\mid V \mid)$; LS = $O(\mid E \vert + \mid V \mid)$; Path Vector = $O(\text{paths})$.
- [ ] **Engineering Examples to Memorize:** DV → **RIP**; LS → **OSPF, IS-IS**; Path Vector → **BGP**; Multicast → **PIM, DVMRP**; Mobile → **Mobile IPv6**.

> **One-line mnemonic for the 7 sub-topics:**
> *Optimality Paves the way → Shortest Path tells the cost → Flooding saturates → DV gossips via Bellman-Ford → LS disseminates with Dijkstra → Multicast prunes with RPF → Mobile tunnels with HA/FA.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Congestion Control Algorithms: Approaches to Congestion Control - Choke Packets, Random Early Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Congestion Control Algorithms: Approaches to Congestion Control - Choke Packets, Random Early Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Congestion Control Algorithms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Network Congestion?

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, **network congestion** is formally defined as a condition in which the demand for network resources (such as bandwidth, buffer space, and processing capacity at intermediate routers) exceeds the available capacity of the network path, resulting in **degraded Quality of Service (QoS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (PCITT402):**
> *Congestion occurs when the number of packets being transmitted through the network approaches the packet-handling capacity of the network. It is a state where the performance collapses because routers begin dropping packets, retransmissions multiply, and end-to-end delay grows unbounded.*

Symptoms observed when congestion sets in:
- **Increased packet delay** (queues overflow)
- **Increased packet loss** (buffers overflow)
- **Degradation of throughput** (effective data rate drops)
- **Blockage of new connections** (call admission fails)

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Approaches to Congestion Control

The KTU syllabus bifurcates congestion control into two grand philosophies:

| Approach | Decision-Making Entity | Reaction Style | Core Philosophy |
|----------|----------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Open-Loop Congestion Control** | Pre-planned, by policy/algorithm | Preventive ("stop it before it happens") | Uses predefined rules to prevent congestion from ever occurring. No feedback used. |
| **Closed-Loop Congestion Control** | Reactive, based on network feedback | Reactive ("fix it once it is detected") | Monitors the network, detects congestion, and feeds back information to sources to throttle traffic. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Choke Packets** and **Random Early Detection (RED)** are both **Closed-Loop congestion control techniques**. The former is a *Source-Based* closed-loop method, while RED is a *Router-Based* (destination-side) preventive mechanism.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic" Analogy

Imagine a **4-lane national highway** merging into a **2-lane tunnel** during a long weekend:
- The tunnel is the **bottleneck router/link**.
- Cars are the **packets**.
- Traffic police stationed at the tunnel mouth are the **congestion control algorithms**.

**Choke Packets** = A traffic policeman at the tunnel sends a message *backward* (toward the highway entry) telling approaching cars to "slow down or stop." The cars (senders) themselves are responsible for slowing their pace.

**Random Early Detection (RED)** = Smart traffic lights at the tunnel mouth start blinking yellow earlier and earlier as the queue grows, randomly tapping *some* cars to halt *before* total gridlock. This is preventive, gentle, and avoids the brutal "all-cars-stopped" traffic jam.

---

## 1.4 Closed-Loop Congestion Control — Sub-Taxonomy

Closed-loop mechanisms are further classified based on how the feedback loop is closed:

1. **Backpressure** — Propagates hop-by-hop toward the source (used in virtual-circuit networks).
2. **Choke Packet** — A router sends a control packet directly to the source telling it to throttle.
3. **Implicit Signaling** — Sender infers congestion from observed events (e.g., delayed ACKs, timeouts). Used by **TCP**.
4. **Explicit Signaling** — Router inserts a flag in the data packet itself (e.g., ECN bit), informing the end host.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Classify congestion control approaches with examples."* Memorize this exact four-fold taxonomy for Part A.

---

## 1.5 Congestion Control vs. Flow Control — A Critical Distinction

| Property | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Scope** | End-to-end (sender ↔ receiver) | Network-wide (senders ↔ network) |
| **Purpose** | Prevent sender from overwhelming the receiver | Prevent senders from overwhelming the network |
| **Mechanism** | Receiver window (rwnd) | Network feedback (ECN, RED, choke packets) |
| **Analogy** | A funnel controlling how fast a tap fills a glass | Traffic control preventing gridlock on a road |

This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

## 1.6 The Congestion Control Loop — Five Standard Phases

Every closed-loop congestion control scheme (including choke packets and RED) executes the following canonical sequence:

$$\text{Step 1: Monitor} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Detect} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Adjust} \rightarrow \text{Step 5: Verify}$$

The KTU board examiners specifically award marks to students who name these five phases explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Control Loop — Operational Mechanics

Let us dissect each phase in the closed-loop congestion control mechanism:

### Phase 1 — Monitoring
Each router continuously measures metrics such as:
- **Queue length** (instantaneous or average)
- **Output link utilization** (line occupancy percentage)
- **Packet loss rate** (drops per second)
- **Average packet delay** (per-hop queuing delay)

### Phase 2 — Detection
A congestion condition is declared when a monitored metric crosses a **predefined threshold** (e.g., buffer fill > 80%). The detection may be:
- **Hard threshold** (used in Choke Packets)
- **Soft probabilistic threshold** (used in RED)

### Phase 3 — Feedback Transmission
Information about congestion is conveyed to the source via:
- A **separate control packet** (Choke Packet model)
- A **bit in the data packet header** (Explicit Congestion Notification — ECN)
- An **implicit signal** (e.g., timeout or duplicate ACK in TCP)

### Phase 4 — Adjustment by the Source
Upon receiving feedback, the source reduces its transmission rate. Common adjustments:
- Reduce the **congestion window (cwnd)**
- Decrease the **sending rate** by a multiplicative factor
- Halt transmission entirely for a backoff period

### Phase 5 — Verification
The system monitors whether the rate reduction resolved the congestion. If not, the loop reiterates.

---

## 2.2 Choke Packets — Detailed Theoretical Treatment

### 2.2.1 Definition

A **Choke Packet** is a specially formatted control packet generated by a congested router and sent **back to the source** to instruct it to reduce its transmission rate. It is the most classical form of explicit feedback in closed-loop congestion control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Original RFC / Academic Reference:**
> The choke-packet mechanism was formally described by **Nagle (1987)** in *RFC 970* and is treated extensively in **Tanenbaum's Computer Networks** and **Kurose & Ross** — both standard KTU references.

### 2.2.2 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step)

1. **Router R** observes that its outgoing queue length exceeds the configured threshold $\text{Q}_{\text{max}}$.
2. **R** generates a Choke Packet addressed to the source host **S**.
3. The Choke Packet is forwarded **backward** through the path (often using the reverse routing tables or by flipping source/destination addresses at each hop).
4. On receiving the Choke Packet, host **S** reduces its window size or sending rate by a factor (commonly **50% multiplicative decrease**).
5. **S** waits for a **cooldown interval** before checking whether it may resume normal operation.

### 2.2.3 Variants of Choke Packets

| Variant | Behavior |
|---------|----------|
| **Hard Choke** | Source stops sending completely until receiving a "Resume" packet. |
| **Soft Choke** | Source merely reduces its rate; it can gradually recover. |
| **Hop-by-Hop Choke** | Each upstream router throttles the next downstream one, propagating back-pressure. |
| **Source-Directed Choke** | The congested router directly addresses the source. (Most common KTU form.) |

### 2.2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Choke Packets

**Advantages:**
- Conceptually simple
- Works on both virtual-circuit and datagram networks
- Provides explicit, deterministic feedback

**Disadvantages:**
- Increases **reverse-path traffic**
- Adds **processing overhead** at routers
- **Slow to react** to short-burst congestion (because the choke packet must travel the full round-trip)
- Risk of **choke-packet storms** under sustained overload

---

## 2.3 Random Early Detection (RED) — Detailed Theoretical Treatment

### 2.3.1 Definition

**Random Early Detection (RED)**, proposed by **Floyd & Jacobson (1993)** in their seminal IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking paper, is a **router-based, active queue management (AQM) algorithm** designed to detect congestion *before* queues actually overflow and to notify sources probabilistically *before* the buffer is full.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's specific phrasing: *"RED is a congestion avoidance algorithm that randomly drops or marks packets even before the queue is full, in order to signal sources to slow down proactively."*

### 2.3.2 The Two Thresholds of RED

RED maintains two thresholds on the **average queue length** $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}$:

- $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ — *Minimum threshold*
- $\text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ — *Maximum threshold*

The decision logic is summarized below:

| Region | Condition on $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}$ | RED Action |
|--------|-----------------------------------|------------|
| **Normal** | $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} < \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ | **No drop.** Admit all packets. |
| **Congestion Avoidance** | $\text{Min}_{\text{th}} \le \text{Q}_{\text{avg}} < \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ | **Probabilistic drop.** Drop probability rises linearly from **0 to MaxP**. |
| **Congestion Control** | $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} \ge \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ | **Drop every arriving packet** (drop probability = 1). |

### 2.3.3 The RED Drop Probability Function

Within the congestion-avoidance region, the drop probability is computed as:

$$P_b = \text{MaxP} \cdot \frac{\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}$$

The **actual** packet-drop probability is then:

$$P_a = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}$$

where `count` is the number of packets admitted since the last drop. This ensures that drops are **spaced out** and not clustered.

### 2.3.4 Computing the Average Queue Length — The EWMA Formula

To avoid reacting to transient bursts, RED uses an **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)**:

$$\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = (1 - w_q) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}} + w_q \cdot q$$

where:
- $q$ = **current instantaneous queue length**
- $w_q$ = **queue weight**, with $0 \le w_q \le 1$ (typically $0.001$ to $0.01$)

This is the **most tested formula** on RED in KTU exams.

### 2.3.5 The Six Canonical Advantages of RED

1. **Avoids global synchronization** — by dropping packets from different flows at different times.
2. **Reduces queuing delay** — by keeping the average queue small.
3. **Allows short bursts** — because the EWMA smooths the measurement.
4. **Provides early notification** — drops happen *before* the buffer is full.
5. **Compatible with ECN** — instead of dropping, RED can set the ECN bit.
6. **Simple to implement** — requires only per-queue state, no per-flow state.

### 2.3.6 The Three Principal Disadvantages of RED

1. **Parameter tuning is delicate** — $w_q$, $\text{Min}_{\text{th}}$, $\text{Max}_{\text{th}}$, and MaxP must be set correctly.
2. **Cannot guarantee fairness** across flows.
3. **Performance collapses** if the traffic mix changes dramatically.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is your **last-minute revision sheet**. Memorize these equations verbatim.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Average queue length (EWMA) | $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = (1 - w_q)\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} + w_q \cdot q$ | Smoothed estimate of queue |
| 2 | Linear drop probability (intermediate region) | $P_b = \text{MaxP} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}$ | Base drop probability |
| 3 | Actual drop probability (with spacing) | $P_a = \dfrac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}$ | Spaced-out drop probability |
| 4 | Admit all packets condition | $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} < \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$ | No-drop region |
| 5 | Drop all packets condition | $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} \ge \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$ | Forced-drop region |
| 6 | Recommended $w_q$ range | $0.001 \le w_q \le 0.01$ | Router tuning |
| 7 | Multiplicative decrease (TCP) | $\text{cwnd} \leftarrow \dfrac{\text{cwnd}}{2}$ | On congestion event |
| 8 | Choke packet rate reduction | $\text{Rate}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\text{Rate}_{\text{old}}}{2}$ | Standard reaction |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Choke Packets** → Prototype in **DECnet**, used conceptually in **ATM ABR (Available Bit Rate)** congestion control.
- **RED** → Implemented in **Cisco IOS** (as `random-detect`), **Linux** (via the `fq_codel` family), and forms the basis of modern **AQM** algorithms like **BLUE**, **PIE**, and **CoDel**.
- Used in **data-center fabrics**, **5G scheduling**, and **satellite link QoS** architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RED Average Queue Length (EWMA)

Let $q_t$ denote the instantaneous queue length observed at time $t$, and let $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t)}$ denote the smoothed average at the same instant.

**Step 1 — Definition of the smoothing filter.**
A first-order low-pass filter is applied to the queue-length signal:

$$\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t)} = \alpha \cdot q_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-1)}$$

**Step 2 — Rename the smoothing constant.**
In the RED literature, $\alpha$ is conventionally called $w_q$ (the *queue weight*):

$$\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t)} = w_q \cdot q_t + (1 - w_q) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-1)}$$

**Step 3 — Unroll the recursion** for the previous $k$ samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-1)} &= w_q \cdot q_{t-1} + (1-w_q) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-2)} \\
\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-2)} &= w_q \cdot q_{t-2} + (1-w_q) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t-3)} \\
&\;\;\vdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute repeatedly** into the first equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t)} &= w_q \cdot q_t \\
&\;\; + (1-w_q) \cdot w_q \cdot q_{t-1} \\
&\;\; + (1-w_q)^2 \cdot w_q \cdot q_{t-2} \\
&\;\; + (1-w_q)^3 \cdot w_q \cdot q_{t-3} \\
&\;\; + \;\cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the canonical EWMA form:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}^{(t)} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} w_q \cdot (1 - w_q)^i \cdot q_{t-i}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Verify that the weights sum to 1:**

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} w_q (1-w_q)^i = \frac{w_q}{1 - (1-w_q)} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Thus, the EWMA is a **convex combination** (weighted average) of all past samples, with exponentially decaying influence of older samples.

> [!NOTE]
> The **time constant** of the filter — i.e., the time over which past samples significantly contribute — is given by $\tau = \dfrac{1}{w_q}$ packet arrivals. With $w_q = 0.002$, the time constant is **500 packets**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Linear Drop Probability Curve

Let $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}$ be the smoothed queue length. The desired drop probability $P_b$ must satisfy three conditions:

1. $P_b = 0$ when $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$.
2. $P_b = \text{MaxP}$ when $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = \text{Max}_{\text{th}}$.
3. $P_b$ is linear in $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}$ between the two thresholds.

**Step 1 — Write the linear equation** in slope-intercept form:

$$P_b(\text{Q}_{\text{avg}}) = m \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}} + c$$

**Step 2 — Apply condition 1:**

$$0 = m \cdot \text{Min}_{\text{th}} + c \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c = -m \cdot \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply condition 2:**

$$\text{MaxP} = m \cdot \text{Max}_{\text{th}} - m \cdot \text{Min}_{\text{th}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the slope $m$:**

$$m = \frac{\text{MaxP}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back** to get the closed-form:

$$P_b = \text{MaxP} \cdot \frac{\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}{\text{Max}_{\text{th}} - \text{Min}_{\text{th}}}$$

This matches the formula in Section 2.3.3. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RED Drop Probability

**Given:**
- $\text{Min}_{\text{th}} = 10$ packets
- $\text{Max}_{\text{th}} = 30$ packets
- $\text{MaxP} = 0.10$
- Current $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = 22$ packets
- $\text{count} = 4$ (packets admitted since last drop)

**Step 1 — Verify we are in the intermediate region:**

$$10 \le 22 < 30 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (congestion-avoidance region)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the base drop probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.10 \cdot \frac{22 - 10}{30 - 10} \\
&= 0.10 \cdot \frac{12}{20} \\
&= 0.10 \cdot 0.60 \\
&= 0.06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the actual drop probability** (with spacing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{1 - 4 \cdot 0.06} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{1 - 0.24} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{0.76} \\
&\approx 0.0789
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **7.89%** of incoming packets are dropped at this instant. This early notification tells TCP senders to back off *before* the queue reaches the upper threshold of 30.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Choke Packet Source Reaction

**Given:**
- Source S sends at $R_{\text{old}} = 10$ Mbps.
- Router R detects its queue exceeding the threshold and sends a **Choke Packet** to S.
- On receiving the choke packet, S applies a **50% multiplicative decrease**.

**Step 1 — Apply the multiplicative-decrease rule:**

$$R_{\text{new}} = \frac{R_{\text{old}}}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 2 — Cooldown interval:**
S waits a predetermined period (e.g., 200 ms) before probing for available bandwidth.

**Step 3 — Recovery:**
After the cooldown, S gradually ramps up its rate, often using an **additive-increase** policy:

$$R_{\text{next}} = R_{\text{new}} + \Delta R, \quad \Delta R = 0.5 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium behavior (AIMD pattern):**

$$R(t+1) = \frac{R(t)}{2} \;\;\text{(on congestion)} \quad \text{and} \quad R(t+1) = R(t) + \Delta R \;\;\text{(on success)}$$

This combined policy is called **Additive-Increase Multiplicative-Decrease (AIMD)** and is the cornerstone of TCP's congestion control (e.g., **TCP Reno**, **TCP Tahoe**).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Analytical Parameter | Choke Packets | Random Early Detection (RED) |
|----------------------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| **When does it react?** | After the queue exceeds a hard threshold | Before the queue is full, in the probabilistic region |
| **Drop pattern** | Source-based, post-facto | Distributed, preventive |
| **Signaling mechanism** | Separate control packet | Implicit (drop) or ECN-marked |
| **State required** | Per-source | Per-queue |
| **Computation cost** | Low | Moderate (EWMA + probability) |
| **Reactivity to bursts** | Slow | Fast (due to EWMA tuning) |
| **Avoids global sync?** | No (single source receives all feedback) | Yes (random selection of flows) |
| **KTU mark weight** | High in theory (3–7 marks) | High in numerics (7–14 marks) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Congestion Control Taxonomy — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONGESTION CONTROL ALGORITHMS] --> B[OPEN LOOP PREVENTIVE]
    A --> C[CLOSED LOOP REACTIVE]

    B --> B1[Traffic Shaping]
    B --> B2[Leaky Bucket]
    B --> B3[Token Bucket]
    B --> B4[Admission Control Policy]

    C --> C1[Backpressure Hop by Hop]
    C --> C2[Choke Packet Source Directed]
    C --> C3[Implicit Signaling Example TCP Timeout]
    C --> C4[Explicit Signaling Example ECN]

    C2 --> C2a[Hard Choke Stop Completely]
    C2 --> C2b[Soft Choke Reduce Rate]

    C4 --> C4a[RED Random Early Detection]
    C4 --> C4b[ECN Explicit Congestion Notification]
    C4 --> C4c[BLUE PIE CoDel Modern AQM]
```

---

## 4.2 Choke Packet Operational Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Source Host S
    participant R1 as Router R1
    participant R2 as Congested Router R2
    participant D as Destination D

    S->>R1: Data Packet 1
    R1->>R2: Data Packet 1
    Note over R2: Queue exceeds Qmax threshold
    R2-->>S: CHOKE PACKET address S
    R2->>R2: Drop or buffer incoming packets
    S->>S: Reduce cwnd by 50 percent
    S->>R1: Data Packet 2 at reduced rate
    R1->>R2: Data Packet 2 at reduced rate
    R2->>D: Data Packet 2 forwarded
    D-->>S: ACK
    S->>S: After cooldown slowly ramp up via AIMD
```

---

## 4.3 RED Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Incoming Packet] --> Q{Average Queue Size Qavg}
    Q -->|Qavg less than MinTh| ADMIT[ADMIT PACKET]
    Q -->|MinTh to MaxTh| PROB[Compute Drop Probability Pb]
    Q -->|Qavg at or above MaxTh| DROP1[DROP ALL PACKETS]
    PROB --> PA[Compute Pa using count]
    PA --> RNG{Random Number less than Pa}
    RNG -->|Yes| DROP2[MARK OR DROP PACKET]
    RNG -->|No| ADMIT
    ADMIT --> OUT[Forward to Output Link]
    DROP1 --> OUT2[Discard]
    DROP2 --> OUT2
```

---

## 4.4 RED Drop Probability Curve — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NormalRegion["NORMAL REGION Qavg less than MinTh"]
        A1[Drop Probability = 0]
    end
    subgraph AvoidRegion["CONGESTION AVOIDANCE REGION"]
        A2["Pb rises linearly from 0 to MaxP"]
    end
    subgraph ControlRegion["CONGESTION CONTROL REGION Qavg at or above MaxTh"]
        A3[Drop Probability = 1]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
```

---

## 4.5 The Congestion Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sender] -->|1 Send Data| N[Network Routers]
    N -->|2 Detect Congestion| C[Control Entity]
    C -->|3 Choke Packet or ECN| S
    S -->|4 Reduce Rate| N
    N -->|5 Verify Improvement| C
    C -->|6 Resume or Further Throttle| S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the two main approaches to congestion control. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop congestion control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Congestion control strategies are classified into two main categories:

**1. Open-Loop Congestion Control (Preventive):**
- Decisions are made *before* congestion occurs, based on predefined policies.
- The network does not use any feedback from the current state.
- Examples: **Traffic Shaping, Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Admission Control**.
- Suitable for real-time traffic where deterministic guarantees are needed.

**2. Closed-Loop Congestion Control (Reactive):**
- Decisions depend on the *current state* of the network.
- A feedback loop is established: monitor → detect → feedback → adjust → verify.
- Examples: **Choke Packets, Backpressure, Implicit Signaling (TCP), Explicit Signaling (ECN, RED)**.

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Decision basis | Pre-defined | Real-time feedback |
| Reaction | Preventive | Reactive |
| Adaptability | Low | High |
| Example | Token bucket | TCP, RED |

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for differentiation table.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a choke packet? How does it help in congestion control?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **choke packet** is a special control packet sent by a congested router back to the source host, instructing it to reduce its transmission rate.

**How it helps:**
1. The congested router detects that its output queue has exceeded the threshold.
2. It generates a choke packet addressed to the source.
3. The source, upon receiving it, performs a **multiplicative decrease** of its sending rate (commonly halving the congestion window).
4. The source waits for a cooldown interval before probing for available bandwidth.
5. This prevents further buffer overflow and stabilizes the network.

Choke packets are an example of **explicit signaling** in closed-loop congestion control and are most effective in **virtual-circuit networks**.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for working steps, 1 mark for example/context.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (a) confusing congestion control with flow control, (b) forgetting the EWMA formula derivation, and (c) failing to label the three regions of the RED drop-probability curve clearly in diagrams. Always include the **formula** AND the **operational region** in your answer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`
#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With neat diagrams, explain the working of Random Early Detection (RED) algorithm. Mention the role of the two thresholds MinTh and MaxTh.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**In a router using RED, the minimum threshold is 10 packets, maximum threshold is 30 packets, and MaxP is 0.1. If the current average queue length is 22 packets and 4 packets have been admitted since the last drop, calculate the actual drop probability.**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Working of RED Algorithm (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
RED is an **Active Queue Management (AQM)** algorithm proposed by Floyd and Jacobson (1993). It detects congestion *early* and probabilistically drops/marks packets *before* the queue is full, signalling sources to slow down proactively.

**2. Two Thresholds (1 mark):**
- **MinTh** — Below this, no packets are dropped.
- **MaxTh** — Above this, every arriving packet is dropped.
- Between MinTh and MaxTh, packets are dropped with a probability that grows linearly with the average queue length.

**3. Average Queue Length Calculation (1 mark):**
The router computes an exponentially weighted moving average:

$$\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = (1 - w_q) \cdot \text{Q}_{\text{avg}} + w_q \cdot q$$

where $w_q$ is the queue weight and $q$ is the current queue size.

**4. Three Operational Regions (2 marks):**

| Region | Condition | RED Action |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Normal | Qavg < MinTh | No drops; admit all |
| Avoidance | MinTh ≤ Qavg < MaxTh | Drop with probability Pb |
| Control | Qavg ≥ MaxTh | Drop every packet |

**5. Drop Probability Computation (1 mark):**

$$P_b = \text{MaxP} \cdot \frac{\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} - \text{MinTh}}{\text{MaxTh} - \text{MinTh}}$$

**6. Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the three-region drop-probability graph (Section 4.4).

**Advantages:** Avoids global synchronization, reduces queuing delay, provides early notification.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- MinTh = 10 packets
- MaxTh = 30 packets
- MaxP = 0.1
- Qavg = 22 packets
- count = 4

**Step 1 — Identify the region (1 mark):**
Since 10 ≤ 22 < 30, we are in the **congestion-avoidance** region.

**Step 2 — Compute base drop probability Pb (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.1 \cdot \frac{22 - 10}{30 - 10} \\
&= 0.1 \cdot \frac{12}{20} \\
&= 0.1 \cdot 0.6 \\
&= 0.06
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute actual drop probability Pa (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{1 - 4 \cdot 0.06} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{1 - 0.24} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{0.76} \\
&\approx 0.0789
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
Approximately **7.89%** of incoming packets are dropped. This early drop informs TCP senders to back off, preventing queue overflow at MaxTh = 30.

**Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** Show the three-region graph with Qavg = 22 marked in the avoidance region.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of choke packets in congestion control. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast open-loop and closed-loop congestion control mechanisms. Which approach is used by TCP and why?**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Choke Packets (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A **choke packet** is a control packet generated by a congested router and sent back to the source host, instructing it to reduce its sending rate.

**2. Operational Steps (2 marks):**
1. Router R observes queue length > Qmax.
2. R generates a choke packet addressed to source S.
3. Choke packet is reverse-forwarded to S.
4. S reduces its congestion window (multiplicative decrease).
5. S waits for cooldown interval.
6. S gradually resumes via additive increase (AIMD).

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** Sequence diagram as in Section 4.2.

**4. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple, explicit feedback
- Works in datagram and virtual-circuit networks
- Reduces buffer overflow

**5. Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Adds reverse-path overhead
- Slow reaction (full RTT delay)
- Risk of choke-packet storms
- Does not prevent global synchronization

---

### Part (b) — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop + TCP Rationale (7 Marks)

**1. Open-Loop (2 marks):**
- Preventive; no feedback used
- Decisions based on traffic descriptors and QoS contracts
- Examples: Token bucket, leaky bucket, admission control
- Pros: Predictable, low overhead
- Cons: Inflexible, cannot adapt to dynamic load

**2. Closed-Loop (2 marks):**
- Reactive; uses feedback from the network
- Five phases: Monitor → Detect → Feedback → Adjust → Verify
- Examples: TCP, RED, choke packets, ECN
- Pros: Adaptive, robust
- Cons: Higher overhead, more complex

**3. TCP uses Closed-Loop — Justification (2 marks):**
- TCP operates over the **best-effort Internet**, where no QoS guarantees exist.
- It must adapt to dynamic conditions, varying RTT, and heterogeneous links.
- Closed-loop allows TCP to infer congestion from **timeouts and duplicate ACKs** (implicit signaling) and to react via **AIMD**.
- Modern TCP variants (Reno, Cubic, BBR) extend this closed-loop philosophy with refined feedback mechanisms.
- Open-loop is unsuitable because the Internet has no admission control.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Comparison table as in Section 1.3.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Congestion** = demand for resources > available capacity; symptoms are increased delay, packet loss, throughput collapse.
- **Two approaches:** Open-loop (preventive, no feedback) vs. Closed-loop (reactive, uses feedback).
- **Closed-loop phases:** Monitor → Detect → Feedback → Adjust → Verify.
- **Choke packet** = router-originated control packet sent to the source to throttle.
- **Choke packet reaction** = multiplicative decrease of rate (typically halve).
- **RED** = Router-based **Active Queue Management (AQM)** algorithm by Floyd & Jacobson (1993).
- **RED's two thresholds:** MinTh and MaxTh; three regions: Normal, Avoidance, Control.
- **EWMA formula (MUST memorize):** $\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} = (1 - w_q)\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} + w_q \cdot q$.
- **Drop probability formula:** $P_b = \text{MaxP} \cdot \dfrac{\text{Q}_{\text{avg}} - \text{MinTh}}{\text{MaxTh} - \text{MinTh}}$.
- **Actual drop probability (with spacing):** $P_a = \dfrac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}$.
- **RED's biggest advantage:** Avoids **global synchronization** by dropping packets from random flows.
- **RED's biggest disadvantage:** Parameter tuning ($w_q$, MinTh, MaxTh, MaxP) is delicate.
- **Congestion control ≠ flow control:** Congestion is network-wide; flow control is end-to-end.
- **Implicit vs. Explicit signaling:** Implicit = source infers (TCP timeout); Explicit = network signals (ECN, choke packet).
- **AIMD** = Additive-Increase Multiplicative-Decrease; cornerstone of TCP and many closed-loop schemes.
- **Modern AQM descendants of RED:** BLUE, PIE, CoDel, fq_codel — all derive from Floyd & Jacobson's 1993 paper.
- **KTU favourite 3-mark question:** *"Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop congestion control."* (Memorize the table.)
- **KTU favourite 14-mark question:** RED with thresholds and a numerical computation of drop probability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality of Service - Application Requirements, Traffic Shaping - Leaky and Token Buckets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality of Service - Application Requirements, Traffic Shaping - Leaky and Token Buckets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Quality of Service (QoS) — Formal Definition

**Quality of Service (QoS)** is the set of networking technologies, mechanisms, and policies used to manage network resources (bandwidth, buffer space, CPU cycles at routers) to provide **predictable, guaranteed, and differentiated** service delivery to specific network traffic flows, ensuring that performance-critical applications meet their stringent timing, throughput, and reliability requirements.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, QoS is a **service-level commitment framework** where the network is treated as a finite-resource system that must be intelligently rationed among competing applications — rather than as a best-effort pipeline that simply forwards packets as fast as it can.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental objective of QoS is to resolve the *contention problem* — when multiple applications (voice, video, file transfer, email) simultaneously demand network resources, QoS provides the rules to **prioritize, reserve, shape, and police** traffic to satisfy the most stringent application requirements.

### 1.2 Application Requirements — Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multi-lane expressway connecting several major cities** (Mumbai, Pune, Bangalore). On this highway:
- **Ambulances** carrying critical patients must reach the hospital in **exactly 12 minutes** (strict delay bound).
- **Wedding cars** must reach the venue by a **fixed time**, but a 5-minute delay is forgivable.
- **Daily commuters** need *consistent travel time*; they hate unpredictability (jitter) more than absolute speed.
- **Goods trucks** carrying bulk grain don't care about delay — they just need **high throughput** and zero loss.

The highway authority (the "network") installs:
- **Dedicated emergency lanes** (bandwidth reservation),
- **Speed governors** that smoothen bursts from individual lanes (traffic shaping),
- **Toll booths** that drop excess vehicles if a lane exceeds its quota (traffic policing),
- **Minimum service guarantees** for premium customers (service-level agreements).

This is precisely what **QoS** does for computer networks — it provides differentiated, application-aware service.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Application Requirements

Every networked application has a unique fingerprint of needs, characterized by four fundamental metrics:

**1. Throughput (Bandwidth Demand)**
- The minimum sustained data rate the application requires to function acceptably.
- Measured in **bits per second (bps)**, typically **Mbps** or **Gbps**.
- Example: 4K streaming Netflix requires **25 Mbps**, while VoIP needs only **64 kbps**.

**2. Delay (Latency)**
- The maximum tolerable one-way end-to-end packet travel time.
- Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- Real-time voice tolerates ≤ **150 ms**; video conferencing ≤ **400 ms**; bulk file transfer is *insensitive* to delay.

**3. Jitter (Delay Variation)**
- The variability in inter-packet arrival times.
- Measured as the standard deviation or peak-to-peak variation of delay.
- Caused by queuing, route changes, congestion. A jitter buffer at the receiver smoothens it, but at the cost of added delay.

**4. Loss (Packet Error/Loss Tolerance)**
- The maximum fraction of packets that may be lost/dropped without service degradation.
- Email tolerates arbitrary loss; VoIP can typically tolerate **≤ 1%**; TCP guarantees zero loss via retransmission (at the cost of delay).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (for KTU exams):** These four metrics are **mutually constraining**. Higher throughput often implies larger packets, which means longer delay and higher jitter. Loss recovery via retransmission adds delay. Understanding this **trade-off matrix** is essential for exam answers.

### 1.4 Application Classification Based on Tolerance

| Application Class | Throughput | Delay | Jitter | Loss |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Real-time interactive (VoIP, Video conf.)** | Low–Medium | **Strict** (≤ 150 ms) | **Strict** (≤ 30 ms) | Strict (≤ 1%) |
| **Streaming (IPTV, Netflix)** | High | Tolerant | Tolerant | Tolerant |
| **Bulk Data Transfer (FTP)** | High | **None** | None | Strict (reliable) |
| **Transactional (HTTP, Banking)** | Medium | Medium | None | Strict |
| **Best-Effort (Email)** | None | None | None | None |

> [!NOTE]
> **Best-Effort Service** is the default IP service model — no guarantees. **Integrated Services (IntServ)** and **Differentiated Services (DiffServ)** are the two architectural frameworks that *add* QoS guarantees to the Internet.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Need for Traffic Shaping — Burstiness Problem

Network traffic is inherently **bursty**. A source may transmit nothing for several seconds, then suddenly send a massive burst (e.g., a database query response, a video frame burst, a backup operation). This burstiness causes:
- **Congestion** at intermediate routers,
- **Packet loss** when buffer overflows,
- **Unfair sharing** of bandwidth with other flows,
- **Jitter spikes** for delay-sensitive traffic sharing the same queue.

**Traffic Shaping** is a congestion-management technique that **regulates the average rate and burstiness** of a traffic flow to ensure it conforms to a predefined traffic contract (specified by a *traffic descriptor* or *leaky/spec parameters*).

**Two Pillars of Congestion Management:**

1. **Traffic Shaping (Leaky/Token Bucket)** — *Smoothing & delay tolerance*: Sends traffic into the network at a *smoothed, controlled average rate*. Excess traffic is **buffered** (delayed). Operates on the **outgoing** side. **Packet never discarded** by shaper.

2. **Traffic Policing** — *Strict enforcement & immediate action*: Drops or marks packets that violate the contract. Operates on the **incoming** side. **Packet may be discarded** by policer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *Shaping is Polite (waits and delays); Policing is Police (arrests and discards).*

### 2.2 The Leaky Bucket Algorithm — Operational Logic

The **Leaky Bucket Algorithm** is a **traffic shaping** mechanism that models an interface as a bucket with a constant drain rate, regardless of the input rate.

**Conceptual Model:**
- A bucket holds packets (the water).
- The bucket has a **fixed capacity** $C$ (in bytes/packets).
- Water (packets) leaks out of a **small hole at the bottom** at a **constant rate** $\rho$ (the output rate).
- If the bucket is full, **newly arriving packets are discarded** (or dropped).

**Operational Rules (Why and How):**

- **Why constant output rate?** To smooth out burstiness and present a perfectly paced, deterministic traffic stream to the downstream network.
- **How is burst handled?** Bursts fill the bucket quickly, but the constant drain means the output remains at rate $\rho$. The bucket *absorbs* the burst over time, delaying subsequent packets.
- **When is loss triggered?** When the input rate exceeds the sum of (output rate + remaining bucket capacity drained during the interval). At steady state, if input rate $> \rho$, the bucket eventually overflows.

**Traffic Contract:** Defined by the pair $(\rho, C)$ where:
- $\rho$ = bucket output (drain/leak) rate in **bytes/second** or **packets/second**.
- $C$ = bucket depth (capacity) in **bytes** or **packets**.

### 2.3 The Token Bucket Algorithm — Operational Logic

The **Token Bucket Algorithm** is a **traffic shaping/policing** mechanism that allows traffic to transmit at the average rate *plus* a controlled burst.

**Conceptual Model:**
- A bucket holds **tokens** (not packets), generated at a rate of $r$ tokens per second.
- The bucket has a **maximum capacity** $B$ tokens.
- To transmit a packet of size $L$ bytes, the sender must **remove $L$ tokens** from the bucket.
- If the bucket has **fewer tokens than required**, the packet must **wait** (shaping) or be **discarded/marked** (policing).
- If the bucket is full, newly generated tokens are **discarded** (bucket cannot hold more than $B$).

**Operational Rules:**

- **Why allow bursts?** Real applications (web servers, video encoders) are inherently bursty. The token bucket provides a *credit-based* mechanism: a flow that was idle accumulates tokens, then uses them to send a burst up to the bucket size.
- **Maximum burst size** = bucket capacity $B$.
- **Long-term average rate** = token generation rate $r$.
- **How is it different from Leaky Bucket?** Token bucket allows controlled bursts up to $B$ bytes, while Leaky Bucket forces a perfectly smooth output.

**Traffic Contract:** Defined by the pair $(r, B)$ where:
- $r$ = token generation (fill) rate in **tokens/second** (or bytes/second).
- $B$ = bucket capacity in **tokens** (or bytes).

### 2.4 Comparison Matrix — Leaky Bucket vs. Token Bucket

| Parameter | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| **Output rate** | Constant (smooth) | Average $r$, burst up to $B$ |
| **Burst handling** | Absorbs and smooths | Absorbs and preserves |
| **Maximum burst** | $C$ (bucket capacity in packets) | $B$ (bucket capacity in tokens/bytes) |
| **Discarding** | Discards arriving packets when full | Discards excess tokens when full |
| **Long-term rate** | $\rho$ | $r$ |
| **Typical use** | ATM VBR, smoothing video | IETF RFC 2697/2698, QoS schedulers |
| **KTU 2024 categorization** | Traffic Shaping | Traffic Shaping & Policing |
| **Memory model** | Continuous fluid drain | Discrete credit-based |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is used below to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Leaky bucket drain (output) rate | packets/sec or bytes/sec |
| $C$ | Leaky bucket capacity (max buffer) | packets or bytes |
| $r$ | Token bucket fill (token generation) rate | tokens/sec |
| $B$ | Token bucket capacity | tokens or bytes |
| $L$ | Packet size (length) | bytes or bits |
| $T$ | Time interval (observation window) | seconds |
| $S_{\max}$ | Maximum burst size | bytes or packets |
| $D_{\text{trans}}$ | Link transmission delay | seconds |
| $D_{\text{prop}}$ | Link propagation delay | seconds |
| $D_{\text{queue}}$ | Queuing delay | seconds |
| $D_{\text{end-to-end}}$ | Total end-to-end delay | seconds |
| $R$ | Link bandwidth (transmission rate) | bits/sec |
| $\mu$ | Average service rate of the queue | packets/sec |
| $\lambda$ | Average arrival rate | packets/sec |
| $\rho_{\text{util}}$ | Traffic intensity / utilization | dimensionless |

**Core Governing Equations:**

**Equation 1 — Output Rate of Leaky Bucket:**
The output of a leaky bucket, in the long run, equals the drain rate:
$$\text{Output Rate}_{\text{Leaky}} = \rho$$

**Equation 2 — Maximum Burst a Token Bucket Can Support:**
The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted in a single burst equals the bucket capacity:
$$S_{\max} = B$$

**Equation 3 — Maximum Time a Burst of Size $S$ Takes to Drain (Leaky):**
$$T_{\text{drain}} = \frac{S}{\rho}$$

**Equation 4 — Burst Tolerance Time (Token Bucket):**
The maximum length of time a sender can transmit at line rate $R$ using stored tokens:
$$T_{\text{burst}} = \frac{B}{R - r}$$

**Equation 5 — Maximum Number of Packets in Bucket (Leaky):**
$$N_{\max} = C \quad \text{(packets)}$$

**Equation 6 — Total End-to-End Delay (Sum of Components):**
$$D_{\text{end-to-end}} = N \cdot (D_{\text{trans}} + D_{\text{prop}} + D_{\text{proc}} + D_{\text{queue}})$$
where $N$ is the number of links, $D_{\text{trans}} = L/R$.

**Equation 7 — Average Packet Transmission Delay:**
$$D_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}$$

**Equation 8 — Utilization / Traffic Intensity (Stability Condition):**
$$\rho_{\text{util}} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$$

**Equation 9 — Token Count Evolution (Token Bucket):**
At time $t$, the token count $T(t)$ is:
$$T(t) = \min \left( B, \, T(t_0) + r \cdot (t - t_0) - \text{Data Sent} \right)$$

**Equation 10 — Leaky Bucket Steady-State Output:**
For input rate $\lambda(t)$ and drain rate $\rho$, the bucket depth $B(t)$ evolves as:
$$\frac{dB(t)}{dt} = \lambda(t) - \rho$$

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Leaky Bucket** is used in ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) networks for traffic shaping at the **CBR (Constant Bit Rate)** and **VBR (Variable Bit Rate)** service classes.
- **Token Bucket** is the **algorithmic backbone** of nearly every modern QoS system: Linux **tc (traffic control)** uses TBF (Token Bucket Filter); Cisco routers use **CAR (Committed Access Rate)** and **QoS policing** based on token buckets.
- **RFC 2697** (Single Rate Three Color Marker) and **RFC 2698** (Two Rate Three Color Marker) — both used in **DiffServ** networks — are built on token bucket mathematics.
- **TCP congestion control** is, in essence, a distributed token/leaky bucket system where the congestion window $cwnd$ acts as the bucket.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Burst Drain Time in a Leaky Bucket

**Problem Setup:**
A leaky bucket has drain rate $\rho = 2$ MB/s and capacity $C = 5$ MB. A burst of 10 MB enters the bucket instantaneously. Compute the time required for the bucket to fully drain back to zero.

**Analytical Strategy:**
We model the bucket as a continuous fluid system. Once the burst enters, the drain operates at constant rate $\rho$. We apply conservation of mass (data) to compute the drain time.

**Step 1: Identify initial bucket state.**
At $t = 0$, after the burst arrives, the bucket contains:
$$B_0 = \min(C, \, \text{Burst Size}) = \min(5 \text{ MB}, 10 \text{ MB}) = 5 \text{ MB}$$

*Explanation:* The bucket can only hold $C = 5$ MB, so the extra $10 - 5 = 5$ MB is **dropped** (this is a critical exam point — leaky bucket discards excess).

**Step 2: Identify the drain process.**
For $t > 0$, the bucket drains at constant rate $\rho = 2$ MB/s. The bucket content at time $t$ is:
$$B(t) = B_0 - \rho \cdot t = 5 - 2t \quad \text{(in MB)}$$

**Step 3: Find the time when $B(t) = 0$.**
Setting $B(t) = 0$ and solving for $t$:
$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= 5 - 2t \\
2t &= 5 \\
t &= \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify against alternative total drain method.**
The bucket contains 5 MB after the burst is absorbed; draining at 2 MB/s:
$$T_{\text{drain}} = \frac{5 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ MB/s}} = 2.5 \text{ seconds} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{\text{drain}} = 2.5$ seconds.

**Note on packet-vs-fluid:** If we model discrete packets of size 1 MB each, the last packet transmits at $t = 2.5$ s, finishing at $t = 2.5 + 1/2 = 3.0$ s including its own transmission time. The fluid model approximates the system for large $C$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Token Bucket Burst Capacity

**Problem Setup:**
A token bucket is configured with token generation rate $r = 10$ tokens/s and bucket capacity $B = 50$ tokens. Each token represents 1 byte. The host link transmits at $R = 100$ Mbps. Compute:
1. The maximum burst size in bytes.
2. The maximum time the burst can be sustained at peak rate $R$.
3. The time to refill the bucket from empty to full.

**Analytical Strategy:**
We apply the token bucket evolution equation and convert between time-scales of token generation and high-speed transmission.

**Step 1: Maximum burst size.**
Each token = 1 byte, and the bucket can hold at most $B = 50$ tokens. Hence:
$$S_{\max} = B = 50 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Maximum burst duration at line rate.**
At line rate $R = 100$ Mbps, the time to send $S_{\max}$ bytes (converting to bits: $50 \times 8 = 400$ bits) is:
$$T_{\text{burst}} = \frac{S_{\max} \times 8}{R} = \frac{400 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3: Time to refill the bucket from empty.**
Starting from $T(0) = 0$, we need to generate $B = 50$ tokens at rate $r = 10$ tokens/s. The refill time is:
$$T_{\text{refill}} = \frac{B}{r} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4: Sanity check — long-term rate.**
