| Live video (RTP/UDP) | Low transit delay, tolerates residual error | Real-time constraint; small loss acceptable |
| Email (SMTP/TCP) | Very low residual error, low priority | Reliability over speed |
| DNS lookup | Very low setup overhead | Connectionless preferred (UDP) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing "Request" with "Indication":** Many students write `T_CONNECT.indication` is issued by the *client*. Wrong. The *indication* is delivered to the *server*. The *request* comes from the *client*. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4th primitive (RESPONSE/CONFIRM):** A common omission. You lose 1 mark instantly.
> 3. **Mixing "Connection-Oriented" with "Reliable":** They are *not* synonymous in strict OSI terminology. A service can be connection-oriented but unreliable (e.g., for fast setup), and connectionless but reliable (e.g., with application-layer ACK).
> 4. **Not drawing the primitive exchange diagram** in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners explicitly demand a sketch.
> 5. **Calling the primitive types "calls" instead of "primitive types"** — terminology matters; "types" is the formal term in ISO 8072.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall List (read before entering the exam hall):**

- **Transport Service** = services + primitives exposed by Layer 4 to upper layers.
- **Two service types:** Connection-Oriented (TCP) and Connectionless (UDP).
- **Five core primitives:** LISTEN, CONNECT, SEND, RECEIVE, DISCONNECT.
- **Four primitive flavors:** REQUEST, INDICATION, RESPONSE, CONFIRM.
- **TSAP** = Transport Service Access Point; realized as `(IP, port)`.
- **QoS parameters** to memorize: establishment delay, establishment failure probability, throughput, transit delay, residual error rate, transfer failure probability.
- **BSD Socket mapping:** `socket()` + `bind()` + `listen()` → `accept()`; client uses `connect()`; `send()`/`recv()`; `close()`/`shutdown()`.
- **State machine:** IDLE → CONNECTING → ESTABLISHED → DISCONNECTING → IDLE.
- **Connection-Oriented ≠ Always Reliable** — reliability is a separate orthogonal axis.
- **UDP = Connectionless + Unreliable + Datagram-oriented; TCP = Connection-Oriented + Reliable + Byte-stream.**
- **TSAP multiplexing** allows many upper-layer apps to share one IP.
- **Expedited data** primitive exists for out-of-band signaling (Ctrl-C, abort).
- **Graceful disconnect** waits for pending data; **abrupt disconnect** doesn't.
- **OSI Transport Service Definition** = ISO 8072; **Protocol Specification** = ISO 8073 (referenced for context, not detailed).
- **Exam tip:** Always draw the **client–server primitive timeline** for full marks in 14-mark questions.
- **Exam tip:** Always mention **at least 5 QoS parameters** in a 7-mark question; missing parameters = missing marks.
- **Mnemonic for primitives:** **L**isten, **C**onnect, **S**end, **R**eceive, **D**isconnect → **"LCSRD"** or "**LCDSR**".
- **Mnemonic for primitive flavors:** **R**equest, **I**ndication, **R**esponse, **C**onfirm → **"RIRC"**.
- **Key distinction:** Primitive = *what*; Protocol = *how* (TCP/UDP are protocols; `SEND`/`RECEIVE` are primitives).
- **Expedited data** is *separate* from regular data — it bypasses normal flow control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Socket Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Socket Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Socket Programming** is the technique of enabling bidirectional communication between two processes running on a network using the **socket API (Application Programming Interface)**, which is an endpoint defined by an **IP address** and a **port number**. A socket is a software abstraction that serves as a communication endpoint between two machines over a network, allowing applications to send and receive data through standard transport-layer protocols such as **TCP** or **UDP**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, socket programming is studied as the practical mechanism by which the Transport Layer services (provided by TCP/UDP) are exposed to the Application Layer processes. The most widely adopted standard is the **Berkeley Sockets API** (BSD Sockets), used in Unix/Linux/Windows systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition of a Socket:**
> A socket is uniquely identified by a **5-tuple**:
> $$\{Protocol, Local\_IP, Local\_Port, Remote\_IP, Remote\_Port\}$$
> For connectionless (UDP) sockets, the connection state is omitted.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large corporate office building where hundreds of employees (applications) work. To receive mail from outside, the company must set up:

1. **Building Address (IP Address)** — Identifies the specific building/machine on the internet.
2. **Desk Number / Office Number (Port Number)** — Identifies the specific employee/application inside that building.
3. **Mailbox (Socket)** — The actual endpoint where mail is placed.

Every time a letter (data packet) arrives, the postman (network stack) looks at the building address (IP) and desk number (port) to deposit the letter in the correct mailbox (socket). Two employees can then communicate by writing letters to each other via their respective mailboxes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** A socket is *not* the network itself; it is simply the door/window through which an application talks to the network. Without sockets, an application has no way to receive or send data over TCP/UDP.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Socket** | Endpoint of a bidirectional network communication |
| **IP Address** | **32-bit (IPv4)** or **128-bit (IPv6)** logical network address |
| **Port Number** | **16-bit** unsigned integer (0–65535) identifying the application |
| **IP Socket** | Combination of IP + Port (used in UNIX domain and INET) |
| **Socket Descriptor** | Integer handle returned by `socket()` system call (like a file descriptor) |
| **Domain / Address Family** | Specifies addressing scheme: `AF_INET`, `AF_INET6`, `AF_UNIX` |
| **Socket Type** | Specifies semantics: `SOCK_STREAM` (TCP) or `SOCK_DGRAM` (UDP) |
| **Protocol** | Typically `0` (auto), `IPPROTO_TCP`, or `IPPROTO_UDP` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Port Number Categories (IANA Reserved Ranges):**
> - **0–1023:** Well-Known Ports (HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23)
> - **1024–49151:** Registered Ports
> - **49152–65535:** Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports (assigned by OS for client side)

## 1.4 Types of Sockets (Critical for KTU)

1. **Stream Socket (`SOCK_STREAM`)** — Uses TCP. Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream, ordered.
2. **Datagram Socket (`SOCK_DGRAM`)** — Uses UDP. Connectionless, unreliable, message-oriented.
3. **Raw Socket (`SOCK_RAW`)** — Allows direct access to underlying protocols (ICMP, etc.); used for ping/traceroute.
4. **Sequential Packet Socket (`SOCK_SEQPACKET`)** — Connection-oriented but message-bounded (rare).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Socket Lifecycle — Operational Concept

Every socket-based communication passes through a well-defined lifecycle. The lifecycle differs slightly between **TCP** (connection-oriented) and **UDP** (connectionless).

### 2.1.1 TCP Server Lifecycle (Listen-Bind-Accept Loop)

1. **`socket()`** — Create a new socket descriptor.
2. **`bind()`** — Bind the socket to a specific (IP, Port). Tells the OS: "All packets destined to this IP+Port must come to me."
3. **`listen()`** — Mark the socket as **passive**, willing to accept incoming connection requests. Maintains a **backlog queue** for pending connections.
4. **`accept()`** — **Blocking call**. Waits for a client to connect. Returns a **new socket descriptor** for the established connection. The original socket continues listening.
5. **`recv()` / `read()`** — Receive data from the connected client.
6. **`send()` / `write()`** — Send data to the connected client.
7. **`close()`** — Terminate the connection and free resources.

### 2.1.2 TCP Client Lifecycle

1. **`socket()`** — Create a new socket descriptor.
2. **`connect()`** — Actively initiate a **3-way handshake** with the server (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK).
3. **`send()` / `write()`** — Send data.
4. **`recv()` / `read()`** — Receive response.
5. **`close()`** — Terminate.

### 2.1.3 UDP Lifecycle (Symmetric — No Listen/Accept)

- **Server:** `socket()` → `bind()` → `recvfrom()` → `sendto()` → `close()`
- **Client:** `socket()` → `sendto()` → `recvfrom()` → `close()`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> UDP does **not** call `listen()`, `accept()`, or `connect()`. It is **stateless** and **fire-and-forget** by design.

## 2.2 The Berkeley Socket API — KTU Reference Table

| Function | Header File | Purpose | TCP/UDP |
|---|---|---|---|
| `socket()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Create endpoint | Both |
| `bind()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Bind to (IP, Port) | Both |
| `listen()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Mark socket as passive | TCP only |
| `accept()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Accept incoming connection | TCP only |
| `connect()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Initiate connection | TCP only (optional UDP) |
| `send()` / `recv()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Stream I/O | TCP only |
| `sendto()` / `recvfrom()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Datagram I/O | UDP primarily |
| `close()` | `<unistd.h>` | Release socket | Both |
| `select()` / `poll()` | `<sys/select.h>` | I/O Multiplexing | Both |
| `gethostbyname()` | `<netdb.h>` | DNS Resolution | Both |
| `htons()` / `ntohs()` | `<arpa/inet.h>` | Host-to-Network Byte Order conversion | Both |
| `inet_aton()` / `inet_ntoa()` | `<arpa/inet.h>` | IP Address string ↔ binary | Both |

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The socket structure `sockaddr_in` (IPv4):**
>
> $$\text{sockaddr\_in} = \{\text{sin\_family},\ \text{sin\_port},\ \text{sin\_addr},\ \text{sin\_zero}[8]\}$$
>
> - `sin_family` = `AF_INET`
> - `sin_port` = Port in **Network Byte Order (Big-Endian)** → use `htons(port)`
> - `sin_addr.s_addr` = IP Address in **Network Byte Order** → use `inet_aton()` or `inet_addr()`
> - `sin_zero[8]` = Padding to match size of generic `struct sockaddr`

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| End-to-End Delay over TCP | $D = N \cdot \frac{L}{R}$ | $D$=Delay, $N$=#hops, $L$=packet size, $R$=bandwidth |
| Socket Descriptor Type | `int` | Returns $-1$ on error |
| Port Number Range | $0 \le Port \le 65535$ | Unsigned 16-bit |
| Concurrent Server Pool | $N_{conn} = $ `accept()` returns $\rightarrow$ `fork()` | POSIX Threads alternative |
| Byte-Order Conversion | Host (Little-Endian) $\xleftrightarrow{htons/htonl}$ Network (Big-Endian) | Mandatory in C |
| Backlog Queue Length | `listen(sockfd, backlog)` | Kernel-dependent max |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Servers (Apache/Nginx):** Use socket programming to accept HTTP connections on port **80/443**.
- **Chat Applications (WhatsApp/Slack):** TCP sockets ensure reliable message delivery.
- **Video Streaming (YouTube/Netflix):** Use **UDP/RTP sockets** for low-latency, drop-tolerant transmission.
- **DNS Resolvers:** Use **UDP port 53** for fast queries.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Lightweight sockets for sensor data over Wi-Fi.
- **Network Testing Tools:** `ping` uses **Raw Sockets (ICMP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless — KTU Distinction:**
> - **TCP:** Handshake, acknowledgments, retransmission, flow control, congestion control.
> - **UDP:** No handshake, no ACKs, no retransmission, no order guarantee. Low overhead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analysis of a TCP Server Program (C / POSIX)

Below is the **step-by-step logical flow** of a TCP echo server using the Berkeley Socket API. Each step is annotated with the system call, its purpose, and the resulting kernel state.

### Step 1: Include Headers

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
```

These headers expose the socket-related structures (`sockaddr_in`, `in_addr`) and constants (`AF_INET`, `SOCK_STREAM`, etc.).

### Step 2: Create the Socket — `socket()`

```c
int sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
if (sockfd < 0) {
    perror("Socket creation failed");
    exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
}
```

**Logic Step:** The kernel allocates a new file descriptor, allocates an internal socket object of type `TCP`, and assigns it to the process's open-file table.

**Parameters:**
- `AF_INET` → IPv4
- `SOCK_STREAM` → Reliable, byte-stream (TCP)
- `0` → Kernel chooses protocol (IPPROTO_TCP)

### Step 3: Construct and Zero the Address Structure

```c
struct sockaddr_in serv_addr;
memset(&serv_addr, 0, sizeof(serv_addr));
serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
serv_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY;   // 0.0.0.0 — any incoming interface
serv_addr.sin_port = htons(8080);
```

**Logic Step:**
- `memset` zeroes the structure to avoid garbage values in `sin_zero[]`.
- `INADDR_ANY` binds to all available network interfaces (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Loopback).
- `htons(8080)` converts the port from **Host Byte Order (Little-Endian on x86)** to **Network Byte Order (Big-Endian)**.

### Step 4: Bind the Socket — `bind()`

```c
int ret = bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));
if (ret < 0) {
    perror("Bind failed");
    close(sockfd);
    exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
}
```

**Logic Step:** The kernel marks the socket as belonging to the (IP, Port) tuple. If the port is already in use (e.g., TIME_WAIT state), `bind()` returns `-1` with errno `EADDRINUSE`.

### Step 5: Listen for Connections — `listen()`

```c
listen(sockfd, 5);   // backlog = 5 (max pending connections)
```

**Logic Step:** The kernel transitions the socket state from `CLOSED` → `LISTEN`. It allocates **two queues** internally:
- **Incomplete Connection Queue:** Holding SYN-received (half-open) sockets.
- **Completed Connection Queue:** Holding fully established (3-way handshake complete) sockets, waiting for `accept()`.

### Step 6: Accept a Client — `accept()`

```c
int newsockfd;
struct sockaddr_in cli_addr;
socklen_t cli_len = sizeof(cli_addr);
newsockfd = accept(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&cli_addr, &cli_len);
```

**Logic Step:**
- This is a **blocking** call. The kernel suspends the process until a completed connection appears in the queue.
- On success, the kernel returns a **new socket descriptor** (`newsockfd`) tied to the specific client (IP, Port).
- The original `sockfd` continues listening for other clients.

### Step 7: Communicate — `read()` / `write()`

```c
char buffer[256];
ssize_t n = read(newsockfd, buffer, sizeof(buffer) - 1);
if (n > 0) {
    buffer[n] = '\0';
    write(newsockfd, buffer, n);  // Echo back
}
```

**Logic Step:**
- `read()` returns the number of bytes actually received (could be < requested size).
- Returns `0` when the client sends FIN (graceful close).
- Returns `-1` on error (e.g., connection reset).

### Step 8: Close the Connection — `close()`

```c
close(newsockfd);
close(sockfd);
```

**Logic Step:** Sends FIN packet, frees the socket descriptor, and the kernel transitions through `FIN_WAIT_1`, `FIN_WAIT_2`, `TIME_WAIT` states.

## 3.2 Mathematical Justification of `htons()`

Let the host machine's CPU be **Little-Endian** (e.g., x86). For port `8080`:

$$\text{Binary of 8080} = 0001\ 1111\ 1001\ 0000_2$$

On Little-Endian (low byte first):

$$8080_{LE} = 90\ 1F\ (hex) \quad \text{in memory}$$

Network standard demands **Big-Endian** (high byte first):

$$8080_{BE} = 1F\ 90\ (hex) \quad \text{in memory}$$

Therefore:

$$htons(8080) = \text{swap\_bytes}(8080)$$

This byte-swap ensures that regardless of the host CPU's native endianness, packets on the wire carry port numbers in a universally agreed-upon format.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | TCP (SOCK_STREAM) | UDP (SOCK_DGRAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Setup | 3-Way Handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | None — Fire and Forget |
| `listen()`/`accept()` Required | Yes | No |
| `connect()` Required | Yes | Optional |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery, retransmission | No guarantee |
| Order of Delivery | Preserved | Not preserved |
| Flow Control | Yes (Sliding Window) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes (Reno, Cubic, BBR) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (without options) | **8 bytes** |
| Typical Use | HTTP, FTP, SSH, Email (SMTP) | DNS, VoIP, Video, Gaming |
| Byte/Message Boundary | Stream (no boundaries) | Message-preserving |
| Overhead | High | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement:**
> "TCP sockets provide a **reliable, connection-oriented, full-duplex byte stream**, while UDP sockets provide an **unreliable, connectionless, message-oriented** service with minimal overhead."

## 3.4 I/O Multiplexing — The `select()` Mechanism

When a server must handle **multiple clients simultaneously** without spawning threads, it uses `select()` to monitor multiple file descriptors.

```c
fd_set readfds;
FD_ZERO(&readfds);
FD_SET(sockfd, &readfds);          // Server socket
FD_SET(newsockfd, &readfds);       // Client socket

int maxfd = (sockfd > newsockfd) ? sockfd : newsockfd;
int activity = select(maxfd + 1, &readfds, NULL, NULL, NULL);
```

**Logic Step:** `select()` blocks until *any* of the watched descriptors becomes ready for I/O, eliminating busy-wait loops.

**Maximum descriptors monitored:** `FD_SETSIZE = 1024` (default on Linux).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TCP Server-Client Socket Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Server: socket] --> B[Server: bind IP:Port]
    B --> C[Server: listen backlog=5]
    C --> D{Server: accept blocks}
    D -- TCP 3-Way Handshake --> E[Client: connect]
    E -- SYN --> F[Server Kernel: SYN-RECEIVED]
    F -- SYN-ACK --> G[Client Kernel: ESTABLISHED]
    G -- ACK --> H[Server Kernel: ESTABLISHED]
    H --> I[Server: accept returns newsockfd]
    I --> J[Both sides: send and recv data]
    J --> K[Client: close sends FIN]
    K --> L[Server: close sends FIN-ACK]
    L --> M[TIME_WAIT then CLOSED]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Socket Communication Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        A1[User Process like HTTP Client]
    end
    subgraph SOCKAPI["Socket API Layer"]
        B1[socket call]
        B2[bind call]
        B3[connect call]
        B4[send recv call]
    end
    subgraph TRANS["Transport Layer"]
        C1[TCP Segment Header]
        C2[UDP Datagram Header]
    end
    subgraph NET["Network Layer"]
        D1[IP Packet Header]
    end
    subgraph LINK["Data Link Layer"]
        E1[Ethernet Frame]
    end
    subgraph PHY["Physical Layer"]
        F1[Electrical or Optical Signals on Wire]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
```

## 4.3 TCP Socket State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : server calls listen
    LISTEN --> SYN_RECEIVED : receive SYN
    SYN_RECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED : receive ACK
    LISTEN --> SYN_SENT : client calls connect
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : receive SYN-ACK and send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : close or send FIN
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : receive FIN and send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : receive FIN
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : send FIN
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : wait 2 MSL timeout
```

## 4.4 Client-Server Communication with `select()` Multiplexing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRV["Server Process"]
        S1[socket] --> S2[bind]
        S2 --> S3[listen]
        S3 --> S4[select monitors fds]
        S4 -- ready to read sockfd --> S5[accept new client]
        S5 --> S6[add newsockfd to fd set]
        S4 -- ready to read newsockfd --> S7[recv from that client]
        S7 --> S8[process and reply]
    end
    subgraph CLT1["Client A"]
        C1A[socket] --> C2A[connect to server]
        C2A --> C3A[send receive data]
    end
    subgraph CLT2["Client B"]
        C1B[socket] --> C2B[connect to server]
        C2B --> C3B[send receive data]
    end

    C3A <--> S7
    C3B <--> S7
```

## 4.5 UDP Server-Client Datagram Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    Note over S: socket() -> bind() -> recvfrom() blocks
    Note over C: socket() -> sendto() does not connect
    C->>S: UDP Datagram (dest IP, dest Port)
    Note over S: Kernel demultiplexes by Port, delivers to bound socket
    S->>C: sendto() reply with client IP and Port
    C->>S: next datagram (independent, no state)
    Note over S: Server maintains no per-client state
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define a socket. Explain the difference between a TCP socket and a UDP socket.**

**Model Answer:**

A **socket** is an endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network, uniquely identified by the tuple `{Protocol, Local_IP, Local_Port, Remote_IP, Remote_Port}`. It is the interface through which applications send and receive data over TCP or UDP.

| Aspect | TCP Socket | UDP Socket |
|---|---|---|
| Type | `SOCK_STREAM` | `SOCK_DGRAM` |
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-Way Handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed, in-order | Best-effort, no guarantee |
| Use Cases | HTTP, FTP, Email | DNS, Video Streaming, VoIP |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: What is the purpose of the `bind()` and `listen()` system calls in TCP socket programming?**

**Model Answer:**

- **`bind()`**: Associates the socket with a specific **IP address and port number** at the local machine. It tells the OS kernel to deliver all incoming packets destined for this (IP, Port) to this socket. Without `bind()`, the socket has no local identity.

- **`listen()`**: Converts an active socket into a **passive listening socket**. It allocates kernel queues (incomplete and completed connection queues) with a specified backlog. The server then waits for client connections using `accept()`.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the TCP client-server communication using socket programming. List the system calls used at the server and client side and explain any five of them in detail.**

#### Part (a) — Diagram and System Call List (7 Marks)

**TCP Client-Server Communication Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as TCP Client
    participant S as TCP Server
    Note over S: socket() -> bind() -> listen()
    S->>S: accept() blocks
    Note over C: socket() -> connect()
    C->>S: SYN
    S->>C: SYN-ACK
    C->>S: ACK
    Note over S: accept() returns new socket
    C->>S: send("Hello")
    S->>C: recv and process
    S->>C: send("Hi Back")
    C->>S: close (FIN)
    S->>C: close (FIN-ACK)
```

**System Calls at Server:** `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, `recv()`, `send()`, `close()`

**System Calls at Client:** `socket()`, `connect()`, `send()`, `recv()`, `close()`

**(5 marks for diagram + list)**

#### Part (b) — Detailed Explanation of Five System Calls (7 Marks)

**1. `socket(int domain, int type, int protocol)`**
Creates a new socket and returns a file descriptor. Example: `int s = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);`. Returns `-1` on error.

**2. `bind(int sockfd, const struct sockaddr *addr, socklen_t addrlen)`**
Assigns the local address (IP + Port) to the socket. Example: `bind(s, (struct sockaddr*)&addr, sizeof(addr));`. Fails if the port is already in use.

**3. `listen(int sockfd, int backlog)`**
Marks the socket as passive, willing to accept connections. `backlog` defines the maximum queue length of pending connections.

**4. `accept(int sockfd, struct sockaddr *addr, socklen_t *addrlen)`**
Blocking call that extracts the first connection request from the completed queue, creates a new socket for the connection, and returns its file descriptor. The original `sockfd` remains listening.

**5. `connect(int sockfd, const struct sockaddr *addr, socklen_t addrlen)`**
Used by the client to initiate a TCP three-way handshake with the server at the specified address. Returns `-1` on failure.

**(7 marks — 1.4 marks per system call)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between connection-oriented (TCP) and connectionless (UDP) socket programming. Write the algorithm for a UDP client and server in pseudo-code.**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Parameter | TCP Socket | UDP Socket |
|---|---|---|
| Socket Type | `SOCK_STREAM` | `SOCK_DGRAM` |
| Connection Setup | 3-Way Handshake required | None |
| Server Calls | `socket`, `bind`, `listen`, `accept`, `recv`, `send` | `socket`, `bind`, `recvfrom`, `sendto` |
| Client Calls | `socket`, `connect`, `send`, `recv` | `socket`, `sendto`, `recvfrom` |
| Reliability | ACK, retransmission, sequencing | No ACK, no retransmission |
| Header Size | 20 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Data Unit | Byte stream (no boundaries) | Datagram (message boundaries preserved) |
| Flow / Congestion Control | Yes | No |
| Examples | HTTP, FTP, SSH | DNS, TFTP, RTP |

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — UDP Client and Server Algorithms (7 Marks)

**UDP Server Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM: UDP_Server
1. CREATE socket sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
2. INITIALIZE serv_addr with:
       sin_family = AF_INET
       sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY
       sin_port = htons(SERVER_PORT)
3. bind(sockfd, serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr))
4. LOOP forever:
       a. recvfrom(sockfd, buffer, size, 0, cli_addr, len)
       b. PROCESS buffer (e.g., convert to uppercase)
       c. sendto(sockfd, processed_buffer, len, 0, cli_addr, len)
5. close(sockfd)
END
```

**UDP Client Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM: UDP_Client
1. CREATE socket sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
2. INITIALIZE serv_addr with:
       sin_family = AF_INET
       sin_port = htons(SERVER_PORT)
       inet_aton(SERVER_IP, &serv_addr.sin_addr)
3. LOOP:
       a. READ input from user
       b. sendto(sockfd, input, len, 0, serv_addr, len)
       c. recvfrom(sockfd, response, len, 0, NULL, NULL)
       d. PRINT response
4. close(sockfd)
END
```

**(7 marks — 3.5 marks each)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `htons()`:** Writing `serv_addr.sin_port = 8080;` instead of `htons(8080)` causes binding to port **0** (since 8080 in little-endian is not valid). **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `accept()` for UDP:** UDP does **not** call `accept()`. Examiner will deduct full marks if mistakenly used.
> 3. **Confusing `INADDR_ANY` (0.0.0.0) with `127.0.0.1`:** First binds to all interfaces, second only loopback.
> 4. **Not checking return values of `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`:** In KTU answers, mentioning error handling (returning `-1`, `errno`) earns bonus credit.
> 5. **Mixing up `send`/`sendto` and `recv`/`recvfrom`:** TCP uses `send/recv` on connected socket; UDP uses `sendto/recvfrom` with explicit address.
> 6. **Omitting `memset` of `sockaddr_in`:** Garbage bytes in `sin_zero` may cause unexpected behaviour. Mentioning this is appreciated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Socket Programming:**

- **Socket = IP + Port**, identified by a 5-tuple after connection.
- **TCP** uses `SOCK_STREAM`; **UDP** uses `SOCK_DGRAM`.
- **Server sequence (TCP):** `socket()` → `bind()` → `listen()` → `accept()` → `recv()/send()` → `close()`.
- **Client sequence (TCP):** `socket()` → `connect()` → `send()/recv()` → `close()`.
- **UDP is symmetric:** Both sides use `sendto()/recvfrom()`. **No `listen`/`accept`/`connect`**.
- **Port range:** 0–65535. **htons()** mandatory to convert to network byte order.
- **Backlog queue:** Two queues managed by kernel — incomplete (SYN received) and completed (handshake done).
- **`accept()` is blocking** and returns a **new socket descriptor** for the connected client.
- **TCP 3-Way Handshake:** SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK (state transitions: CLOSED → LISTEN → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED).
- **TCP 4-Way Termination:** FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK (TIME_WAIT = **2 × MSL**).
- **Concurrent servers:** Use `fork()` (POSIX) or threads or `select()/poll()` to handle multiple clients.
- **`select()`** monitors multiple file descriptors; default `FD_SETSIZE = 1024`.
- **Byte Order:** Network is **Big-Endian**; x86 host is **Little-Endian**. Use `htons`/`htonl` and `ntohs`/`ntohl`.
- **Common headers:** `<sys/socket.h>`, `<netinet/in.h>`, `<arpa/inet.h>`, `<unistd.h>`, `<netdb.h>`.
- **TCP guarantees:** Reliability, ordering, no-duplicates, flow control, congestion control.
- **UDP is best-effort:** Used in DNS (port 53), VoIP, video, gaming — where speed > reliability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Datagram Protocol (UDP) - User Datagram, Checksum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** User Datagram Protocol (UDP) - User Datagram, Checksum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — User Datagram Protocol (UDP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (RFC 768):**
> *User Datagram Protocol (UDP)* is a **connectionless, unreliable, message-oriented transport layer protocol** defined in **IETF RFC 768** (released **28 August 1980** by J. Postel). It provides a thin wrapper around the Internet Protocol (IP), adding only **port multiplexing, optional checksum, and length information** — but no handshaking, no sequencing, and no retransmission.

UDP sits directly on top of IP (Protocol Number **17** in the IP header) and below the application layer. It is the simplest of the two standard Internet transport protocols — the other being TCP.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a college fest, and you want to shout out the **campus placement schedule** to all of your friends standing in a nearby open ground.

- You **do not** walk up to each friend, shake their hand, confirm they are listening, and then speak (**that is TCP** — connection-oriented, handshaking, reliable).
- Instead, you simply **shout once, loudly**, into the crowd (**that is UDP** — connectionless, fire-and-forget).

The message reaches whoever is close enough to hear. If the wind is loud (network congestion) or the ground is too big (long distance), some friends might **miss the announcement** — and that is perfectly acceptable because the lost data is not life-critical.

| Feature | Real-World Shout | UDP Datagram |
|---|---|---|
| Connection setup | None — you just shout | No 3-way handshake |
| Acknowledgement | None | Receiver does not ACK |
| Order guarantee | None | Datagrams may arrive out of order |
| Speed | Instant | Lowest possible overhead |
| Use case | Announcements, live scores | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> UDP is the canonical example of a **"best-effort delivery"** protocol. It trades **reliability for speed and low overhead**, making it ideal for real-time, loss-tolerant applications.

### Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **UDP Header Size:** **8 bytes (64 bits)** — fixed, never optional fields except checksum.
- **Maximum Theoretical Datagram Size:** **65,507 bytes** (65,535 − 20 IP header − 8 UDP header).
- **Practical Limit:** Often capped at **~1,472 bytes** on standard Ethernet (to avoid IP fragmentation).
- **Protocol Number:** **17** (carried in the IP header's *Protocol* field).
- **Default Port Range:** **0 – 65,535** (Well-known: **0 – 1,023**, Registered: **1,024 – 49,151**, Dynamic/Private: **49,152 – 65,535**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Characteristics of UDP

UDP is best understood by enumerating what it **does not** do (because what TCP does, UDP omits). The following structured list maps each operation to its engineering reality:

- **Connectionless Service**
  - *Why:* No state is maintained between sender and receiver. Each datagram is independent.
  - *How:* The sender transmits a UDP segment inside an IP datagram without prior negotiation.

- **Unreliable / Best-Effort Delivery**
  - *Why:* There is no acknowledgement, no retransmission, and no timeout.
  - *How:* Lost segments are silently dropped; the application must detect and recover (if at all).

- **No Ordering Guarantee**
  - *Why:* Each datagram may travel a different route.
  - *How:* Datagrams are stamped only with source/destination ports, not a sequence number.

- **No Flow Control**
  - *Why:* The sender does not know if the receiver is overwhelmed.
  - *How:* The application layer (e.g., a video codec) modulates the bit rate, not UDP itself.

- **No Congestion Control**
  - *Why:* UDP does not back off when the network is congested.
  - *How:* This can cause **congestion collapse** in extreme cases — which is why TCP-friendly protocols exist.

- **Port Multiplexing / Demultiplexing**
  - *Why:* Multiple applications on one host need to share the IP layer.
  - *How:* A **16-bit source port** and **16-bit destination port** uniquely identify the communicating endpoints.

- **Optional Checksum (Optional, but recommended)**
  - *Why:* Detect bit-errors introduced by lower layers (link, physical).
  - *How:* End-to-end **16-bit one's complement checksum** computed over a *pseudo-header* + UDP header + data.

### 2.2 UDP Header Format — Bit-Level Anatomy

The UDP header is exactly **four fields of 16 bits each = 64 bits = 8 bytes**.

| Field | Size (bits) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Source Port | 16 | Optional; **0** if not used |
| Destination Port | 16 | Mandatory; identifies the target application |
| Length | 16 | Length of **UDP header + data** in bytes (min **8**) |
| Checksum | 16 | 16-bit one's complement checksum; **0** means checksum disabled (in IPv4) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** reference for UDP-related numerical problems in KTU exams.

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| UDP Header size | $H_{UDP} = 8\ \text{bytes}$ | Fixed |
| Maximum UDP datagram | $L_{max} = 65{,}535 - 20 - 8 = 65{,}507\ \text{bytes}$ | $2^{16}-1$ minus IP+UDP headers |
| Length field value | $L_{UDP} = H_{UDP} + D_{payload}$ | $D_{payload}$ = data length in bytes |
| Checksum (sender) | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{16\text{-bit words}} w_i}$ | One's complement of the **16-bit one's complement sum** |
| Checksum (receiver) | $\text{Accept if } \sum_{16\text{-bit words}}(\text{incl. checksum field}) = 0xFFFF$ | All-ones means no errors detected |
| Pseudo-header size | $12\ \text{bytes}$ | Used **only for checksum computation**, never transmitted |
| Pseudo-header sum range | $96\ \text{bits} = 6 \times 16\text{-bit words}$ | Source IP, Dest IP, Zero, Protocol, UDP Length |
| Protocol field value | $\text{Protocol} = 17$ | Fixed for UDP inside the pseudo-header |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** Note that the pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or simply written in words above. Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the column delimiter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

UDP is the backbone of the modern internet's most latency-sensitive services:

- **DNS (Port 53):** A single query is small; TCP's overhead of opening a connection is wasteful.
- **VoIP (e.g., SIP on 5060, RTP on dynamic ports):** Re-transmitting old voice packets is useless — by the time they arrive, the conversation has moved on.
- **Live Video Streaming (RTP/RTSP):** Dropping a frame is preferable to stalling the entire stream.
- **Online Gaming (multiplayer FPS):** Position updates must arrive *now*; a delayed update is misinformation.
- **QUIC/HTTP3:** Even modern reliable protocols use UDP as the substrate to avoid *head-of-line blocking* of TCP.
- **TFTP, SNMP, NTP:** Simple, low-overhead request-response protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The UDP Checksum — The Most Important Derivation for KTU

Unlike TCP, where the checksum is mandatory, in **IPv4 UDP** the checksum is **optional** (sender sets it to 0 to disable). However, in **IPv6** the UDP checksum is **mandatory**. The checksum covers three regions concatenated together:

1. **UDP Pseudo-Header** (12 bytes — used only for computation)
2. **UDP Header** (8 bytes)
3. **UDP Data / Payload** (variable)

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Construct the Pseudo-Header

The pseudo-header is **not transmitted** but is prefixed to the UDP segment *only* at the checksum computation stage. It is the device by which UDP "borrows" the IP layer's addressing for end-to-end protection.

| Field | Size (bytes) | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Source IP Address | 4 | Taken from the IP header |
| Destination IP Address | 4 | Taken from the IP header |
| Zero | 1 | All **zeros** (padding) |
| Protocol | 1 | **17** for UDP |
| UDP Length | 2 | Same value as the UDP *Length* field |

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Pad the Combined Block to a 16-bit Boundary

If the **UDP Data** has an **odd number of bytes**, append a single **0x00 byte** at the end. The total block to checksum is:

$$B_{total} = \underbrace{12}_{\text{pseudo}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{UDP header}} + \underbrace{L_{data}}_{\text{data}} + \underbrace{\delta}_{\delta \in \{0,1\}}$$

where $\delta = 1$ if $L_{data}$ is odd, else $\delta = 0$.

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the 16-bit One's Complement Sum

Break the block $B_{total}$ into a sequence of $N$ **16-bit words** $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N$. Compute the ordinary binary (one's complement) addition:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \quad \text{(in one's complement arithmetic)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One's Complement Arithmetic Rule:**
> When an overflow bit (carry-out of the 17th bit) is produced, **wrap it around and add 1** to the 16-bit result. This is called **end-around carry**.

Mathematically:

$$S = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i\right) \bmod 2^{16} + \left\lfloor \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}{2^{16}} \right\rfloor$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Take the One's Complement to Get the Checksum

$$\boxed{\text{Checksum} = \overline{S} = \text{bitwise NOT}(S) = 0xFFFF - S}$$

This value is placed in the **Checksum field of the UDP header** before transmission.

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Verification at the Receiver

The receiver repeats the **same process** — pseudo-header + UDP header (with the checksum field included) + data — and computes the sum. If the result is:

- **All 1s (0xFFFF)**: No error detected → datagram accepted.
- **Anything else**: Error detected → datagram **silently discarded** (no NACK in UDP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** A correct checksum $\overline{S}$ is the bitwise NOT of $S$. Therefore $S + \overline{S} = 0xFFFF$. Including the checksum field in the receiver's sum reproduces $0xFFFF$ if the data is intact. This is a beautiful property of **one's complement arithmetic**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Checksum Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the UDP checksum for a segment with the following parameters:

- Source IP: `192.168.1.10`
- Destination IP: `192.168.1.20`
- Source Port: `0x1A2B` (decimal 6699)
- Destination Port: `0x0050` (decimal 80)
- Length: `0x001C` (decimal 28 → 8 header + 20 data)
- Data (20 bytes): `0x41 42 43 44 45 46 47 48 49 4A 4B 4C 4D 4E 4F 50 51 52 53 54` (the ASCII string `"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRST"`)

#### Solution Step A — Assemble the Pseudo-Header

Source IP `192.168.1.10` in hex: `C0 A8 01 0A`
Destination IP `192.168.1.20` in hex: `C0 A8 01 14`
Zero byte: `00`
Protocol: `11` (decimal 17)
UDP Length: `00 1C`

Pseudo-header as 16-bit words (6 words = 96 bits):

$$w_1 = 0xC0A8, \quad w_2 = 0x010A, \quad w_3 = 0xC0A8, \quad w_4 = 0x0114, \quad w_5 = 0x0011, \quad w_6 = 0x001C$$

#### Solution Step B — Assemble the UDP Header (with Checksum = 0 initially)

$$w_7 = 0x1A2B \quad (\text{src port}), \quad w_8 = 0x0050 \quad (\text{dst port})$$
$$w_9 = 0x001C \quad (\text{length}), \quad w_{10} = 0x0000 \quad (\text{checksum initially set to 0})$$

#### Solution Step C — Assemble the Data (20 bytes → 10 × 16-bit words)

Data bytes: `41 42 43 44 45 46 47 48 49 4A 4B 4C 4D 4E 4F 50 51 52 53 54`

$$w_{11} = 0x4142, \quad w_{12} = 0x4344, \quad w_{13} = 0x4546, \quad w_{14} = 0x4748$$
$$w_{15} = 0x494A, \quad w_{16} = 0x4B4C, \quad w_{17} = 0x4D4E, \quad w_{18} = 0x4F50$$
$$w_{19} = 0x5152, \quad w_{20} = 0x5354$$

Total data length is **20 bytes (even)** → no padding needed.

#### Solution Step D — Compute the One's Complement Sum

Adding all 20 words in **16-bit binary**:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{partial}} &= 0xC0A8 + 0x010A + 0xC0A8 + 0x0114 + 0x0011 + 0x001C \\
&\quad + 0x1A2B + 0x0050 + 0x001C + 0x0000 \\
&\quad + 0x4142 + 0x4344 + 0x4546 + 0x4748 + 0x494A + 0x4B4C \\
&\quad + 0x4D4E + 0x4F50 + 0x5152 + 0x5354
\end{aligned}$$

Grouping for clarity:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pseudo-Header sum } &= 0xC0A8 + 0x010A + 0xC0A8 + 0x0114 + 0x0011 + 0x001C \\
&= 0x182F4 + 0xC0A8 + 0x0114 + 0x0011 + 0x001C \\
&= 0x182F4 + 0xC0A8 = 0x2439C \\
&\Rightarrow 0x2439C + 0x0114 = 0x244B0 \\
&\Rightarrow 0x244B0 + 0x0011 = 0x244C1 \\
&\Rightarrow 0x244C1 + 0x001C = 0x244DD
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Header sum } &= 0x1A2B + 0x0050 + 0x001C + 0x0000 = 0x1A97
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Data sum } &= 0x4142 + 0x4344 + 0x4546 + 0x4748 + 0x494A + 0x4B4C + 0x4D4E + 0x4F50 + 0x5152 + 0x5354
\end{aligned}$$

Computing the data sum step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
0x4142 + 0x4344 &= 0x8486 \\
0x8486 + 0x4546 &= 0xC9CC \\
0xC9CC + 0x4748 &= 0x11114 \xrightarrow{\text{wrap}} 0x1115 \\
0x1115 + 0x494A &= 0x5A5F \\
0x5A5F + 0x4B4C &= 0xA5AB \\
0xA5AB + 0x4D4E &= 0xF2F9 \\
0xF2F9 + 0x4F50 &= 0x14249 \xrightarrow{\text{wrap}} 0x424A \\
0x424A + 0x5152 &= 0x939C \\
0x939C + 0x5354 &= 0xE6F0
\end{aligned}$$

So the data sum is $0xE6F0$.

#### Solution Step E — Total Sum (with End-Around Carry)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 0x244DD + 0x1A97 + 0xE6F0 \\
&= 0x244DD + 0x1A97 = 0x25F74 \xrightarrow{\text{wrap}} 0x5F75 \\
&\Rightarrow 0x5F75 + 0xE6F0 = 0x14665 \xrightarrow{\text{wrap}} 0x4666
\end{aligned}$$

So $S = 0x4666$.

#### Solution Step F — Compute the Checksum

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Checksum} &= \overline{S} = 0xFFFF - 0x4666 = 0xB999
\end{aligned}$$

**The UDP checksum is `0xB999`**, which is placed in the *Checksum* field of the UDP header before transmission.

#### Solution Step G — Verification at Receiver

The receiver re-computes the sum but with $w_{10} = 0xB999$ instead of $0x0000$. The new total is:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{new}} &= 0x4666 + 0xB999 = 0xFFFF
\end{aligned}$$

Since the result is **all 1s ($0xFFFF$)**, the datagram is **accepted as error-free** — confirming our derivation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use UDP vs TCP

| Parameter | UDP | TCP |
|---|---|---|
| Header overhead | **8 bytes** | **20 – 60 bytes** |
| Connection setup | None | 3-way SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK |
| Reliability | None (best-effort) | ACK, retransmit, timeout |
| Ordering | Not guaranteed | Byte-stream order preserved |
| Flow control | None | Sliding window |
| Congestion control | None | Slow-start, AIMD, etc. |
| Speed | Very high | Moderate |
| Use cases | DNS, VoIP, gaming, video | Web, email, file transfer |
| Multicast support | Yes | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UDP Datagram Encapsulation Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Application Layer Data / Payload"] --> B["UDP Header 8 bytes"]
    B --> C["IP Header 20 bytes"]
    C --> D["Ethernet Frame Header 14 bytes"]
    D --> E["Physical Layer Bits on Wire"]
    E --> F["Receiver Physical Layer"]
    F --> G["Receiver Ethernet Frame"]
    G --> H["Receiver IP Layer"]
    H --> I["Receiver UDP Layer"]
    I --> J["Receiver Application"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram illustrates the **end-to-end encapsulation** of a UDP datagram from the application layer down to the physical layer and back up. Notice how the **pseudo-header used for checksum calculation exists only at the endpoints** — it is **never transmitted** on the wire.

### 4.2 UDP Header Bit-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP["Source Port 0 to 15"] --> DP["Destination Port 16 to 31"]
    DP --> LN["Length 32 to 47"]
    LN --> CS["Checksum 48 to 63"]
    SP -.16 bits.-> SP
    DP -.16 bits.-> DP
    LN -.16 bits.-> LN
    CS -.16 bits.-> CS
```

### 4.3 UDP Checksum Computation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    PH["Pseudo Header 12 bytes"] --> CONC["Concatenation Buffer"]
    UH["UDP Header 8 bytes checksum = 0"] --> CONC
    DATA["UDP Data N bytes"] --> PAD["Padding Logic add 0x00 if odd length"]
    PAD --> CONC
    CONC --> SPLT["Split into 16-bit Words"]
    SPLT --> SUM["Ones Complement Adder 16 bit with End-Around Carry"]
    SUM --> COMP["Bitwise NOT"]
    COMP --> OUT["Final Checksum 16 bits"]
    OUT --> CSFIELD["Insert into UDP Header Checksum Field"]
```

### 4.4 Receiver-Side Verification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    RX["Received Datagram IP + UDP + Data"] --> RPH["Reconstruct Pseudo Header 12 bytes"]
    RPH --> RCONC["Concatenation Buffer with original Checksum Field included"]
    UH2["UDP Header 8 bytes including received Checksum"] --> RCONC
    DATA2["UDP Data N bytes"] --> RPAD["Padding if odd"]
    RPAD --> RCONC
    RCONC --> RSPLT["Split into 16-bit Words"]
    RSPLT --> RSUM["Ones Complement Sum with End-Around Carry"]
    RSUM --> DEC{"Result equals 0xFFFF"}
    DEC -->|Yes| ACC["Accept Datagram Deliver to Application"]
    DEC -->|No| DROP["Silently Discard no NACK"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU 14-mark answers:**
> In board exam answers, even a hand-drawn version of the above block diagram is worth **2 to 3 marks** by itself. KTU examiners specifically reward "labelled diagrams" because they demonstrate structural understanding of the protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any six features of UDP that distinguish it from TCP. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. UDP is **connectionless** — no handshaking is performed before data transfer.
2. UDP is **unreliable** — there is no acknowledgement, retransmission, or timeout mechanism.
3. UDP has a **small fixed 8-byte header**, whereas TCP uses a 20-byte (minimum) header.
4. UDP provides **no ordering guarantee** — datagrams may arrive out of order.
5. UDP has **no flow control** and **no congestion control** mechanisms.
6. UDP supports **unicast, multicast, and broadcast**, whereas TCP supports only unicast.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is the UDP checksum computed over a pseudo-header? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The UDP pseudo-header contains the **Source IP Address, Destination IP Address, and the Protocol Number (17)** sourced from the IP layer. It is included in the checksum computation to:

1. **End-to-end error detection across layer boundaries** — without it, a misrouted datagram delivered to the wrong host would still pass the checksum.
2. **Verify that the segment belongs to the correct IP datagram** — preventing an attacker from injecting UDP segments with spoofed port numbers into a foreign conversation.
3. **Protect the IP addressing information** — the pseudo-header ensures that any corruption of source/destination IP addresses (e.g., by an erroneous router) is detected by the destination.

> [!NOTE]
> The pseudo-header is **not transmitted**; it is reconstructed at the receiver from the IP header fields.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *UDP Datagram Structure + Checksum Computation*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **UDP user datagram format** in detail. List the function of each field. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The UDP user datagram consists of an **8-byte header** followed by the **data payload**.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP["Source Port 16 bits"] --> DP["Destination Port 16 bits"]
    DP --> LN["Length 16 bits"]
    LN --> CS["Checksum 16 bits"]
    CS --> DA["Data Payload variable bytes"]
    SP -.identifies the sending application.-> SP
    DP -.identifies the receiving application.-> DP
    LN -.header plus data in bytes.-> LN
    CS -.16 bit ones complement checksum.-> CS
    DA -.carries the application layer message.-> DA
```

**Field Functions:**

| Field | Function |
|---|---|
| Source Port (16 bits) | Identifies the port of the sending application. May be set to 0 if the sender does not require a reply. |
| Destination Port (16 bits) | Identifies the port of the intended receiver. Used by the kernel for **demultiplexing** to the correct application socket. |
| Length (16 bits) | The length, in bytes, of the entire UDP datagram — header + data. Minimum value is **8** (header alone, no data). |
| Checksum (16 bits) | Optional in IPv4, mandatory in IPv6. 16-bit one's complement checksum over pseudo-header + header + data. |
| Data (variable) | The actual application layer message, opaque to UDP. |

**(b)** Compute the UDP checksum for the following segment: **(7 Marks)**

Given:
- Source IP: `10.0.0.1`
- Destination IP: `10.0.0.2`
- Source Port: `1050`
- Destination Port: `2000`
- Data (4 bytes): `0xDE 0xAD 0xBE 0xEF`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all fields to hexadecimal 16-bit words.**

| Item | Hex Words |
|---|---|
| Source IP `10.0.0.1` | `0x0A00, 0x0001` |
| Dest IP `10.0.0.2` | `0x0A00, 0x0002` |
| Zero + Protocol | `0x0011` |
| UDP Length | header(8) + data(4) = 12 = `0x000C` |
| Source Port 1050 | `0x041A` |
| Dest Port 2000 | `0x07D0` |
| Length field | `0x000C` |
| Checksum field (initial) | `0x0000` |
| Data `DE AD BE EF` | `0xDEAD, 0xBEEF` |

**Step 2 — Sum all 16-bit words (one's complement addition):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 0x0A00 + 0x0001 + 0x0A00 + 0x0002 + 0x0011 + 0x000C \\
  &\quad + 0x041A + 0x07D0 + 0x000C + 0x0000 + 0xDEAD + 0xBEEF
\end{aligned}$$

Grouped stepwise:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pseudo-header sum } &= 0x0A00 + 0x0001 + 0x0A00 + 0x0002 + 0x0011 + 0x000C = 0x1420 \\
\text{UDP header sum (with cksum = 0) } &= 0x041A + 0x07D0 + 0x000C + 0x0000 = 0x0BF6 \\
\text{Data sum } &= 0xDEAD + 0xBEEF = 0x19D9C \xrightarrow{\text{wrap}} 0x9D9D
\end{aligned}$$

Total:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 0x1420 + 0x0BF6 + 0x9D9D \\
  &= 0x1420 + 0x0BF6 = 0x2016 \\
  &\Rightarrow 0x2016 + 0x9D9D = 0xBDB3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Checksum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Checksum} &= 0xFFFF - 0xBDB3 = 0x424C
\end{aligned}$$

**The UDP checksum is `0x424C`.**

**Step 4 — Verification at Receiver:** When the receiver sums the same words but replaces the checksum field `0x0000` with `0x424C`, the new total is:

$$0xBDB3 + 0x424C = 0xFFFF$$

Result is all 1s ⇒ **datagram accepted**, no error detected. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *UDP vs TCP + Application Suitability*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice]**

**(a)** Compare UDP and TCP in terms of header size, reliability, ordering, flow control, congestion control, connection setup, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | UDP | TCP |
|---|---|---|
| Header Size | **8 bytes (fixed)** | **20 – 60 bytes** (variable options) |
| Connection Setup | **None** — connectionless | **3-way handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) |
| Reliability | Unreliable — no ACK, no retransmit | Reliable — ACK + retransmission on timeout |
| Ordering | Not preserved | Byte-stream order strictly preserved |
| Flow Control | None | **Sliding window** protocol (receiver advertised window) |
| Congestion Control | None | **Slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/recovery** |
| Multicast Support | **Yes** | No |
| Typical Applications | **DNS, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming, SNMP, TFTP** | **HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SSH, Telnet** |

**Conclusion:** UDP trades reliability for **speed and low overhead**, making it ideal for **latency-sensitive, loss-tolerant** applications. TCP is preferred for **loss-intolerant, throughput-sensitive** workloads.

**(b)** Explain with examples why DNS uses UDP and not TCP. Also discuss three scenarios where TCP is the only sensible choice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why DNS prefers UDP (Port 53):**

1. **Small payload size:** A typical DNS query/response is **under 512 bytes** — well within UDP's typical MTU. TCP's 20-byte header overhead is significant for such small messages.
2. **Low latency required:** DNS resolution must be near-instantaneous. TCP's three-way handshake adds **1.5 round-trip times (RTT)** before any data is exchanged, while UDP sends the query in **0.5 RTT**.
3. **Statelessness:** A DNS server handles thousands of queries per second. UDP's connectionless nature avoids the memory and CPU cost of maintaining **thousands of TCP sockets**.
4. **Retry is cheap:** If a DNS query is lost, the client simply **re-queries** after a short timeout. There is no need for TCP's retransmission machinery.

**When TCP is mandatory — Three Scenarios:**

1. **Large File Transfer (FTP, HTTP file downloads):** If a packet is lost, TCP retransmits *only the missing segment*, not the entire file. UDP would either drop the segment silently (corrupt file) or require the application to implement retransmission from scratch.

2. **Email (SMTP, IMAP, POP3):** Emails can be **megabytes in size** with attachments. Reliability and ordering are non-negotiable — a 5 MB email delivered out of order or with holes is unusable.

3. **Secure Shell (SSH) and Banking Web Pages (HTTPS):** These require **strict byte-stream semantics** — every command and every response must be processed in the correct order. SSH would be unusable if a typed command arrived before the previous prompt.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the end-around carry** during checksum addition. UDP checksum uses **one's complement** addition, not two's complement. If a 16-bit sum overflows beyond `0xFFFF`, the carry-out **must be wrapped and added back**. Students often lose **2 marks** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Setting the checksum field to 0 in the receiver's calculation.** The receiver **includes the received checksum value** in its summation. If the result is `0xFFFF`, the datagram is good. If you sum without the checksum, you cannot verify.
>
> 3. **Including the Length field twice in confusion.** The UDP length appears in the pseudo-header *and* in the actual header. Both must be included (with the same value).
>
> 4. **Confusing UDP's *Length* with the IP *Total Length*.** The UDP *Length* field is **UDP header + data only**, not the IP-level total length.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the pseudo-header entirely.** Many students only checksum the header + data, which is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU answers. Always state clearly that a **12-byte pseudo-header is used**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — UDP User Datagram & Checksum**

- **UDP is defined in RFC 768**, uses **IP Protocol Number 17**, and is **connectionless + unreliable**.
- The UDP datagram consists of an **8-byte header** + variable-length data.
- Header fields: **Source Port (16), Destination Port (16), Length (16), Checksum (16)**.
- **Length field = UDP header (8) + data length in bytes** (minimum value 8).
- The UDP checksum is **16-bit one's complement**, computed over: **pseudo-header (12 B) + UDP header (8 B) + data (padded to even)**.
- **Pseudo-header fields:** Src IP, Dst IP, Zero (1 byte), Protocol (17), UDP Length.
- The **pseudo-header is not transmitted** — it is reconstructed at the receiver.
- Checksum is **optional in IPv4** (set to 0 to disable) but **mandatory in IPv6**.
- **End-around carry** is mandatory in one's complement addition.
- **Checksum = 0xFFFF − Sum** (i.e., one's complement of the sum).
- **Receiver verifies by checking that the total sum (with the checksum field included) equals 0xFFFF.**
- Maximum UDP datagram size = **65,507 bytes**; practical Ethernet-friendly size ≈ **1,472 bytes**.
- **UDP is preferred for**: DNS, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming, SNMP, TFTP, QUIC.
- **TCP is preferred for**: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, SSH, banking — anywhere reliability/ordering matter.
- **UDP supports multicast/broadcast**; TCP does not.
- **No flow control, no congestion control** in UDP — the application must manage rate.
- **No NACK on error** — receiver silently discards corrupt datagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Transport Protocols (RTP, RTCP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Transport Protocols (RTP, RTCP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Real-Time Transport Protocol (RTP)

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
The **Real-Time Transport Protocol (RTP)** is an application-layer protocol (standardized in **RFC 1889 / RFC 3550**) designed to provide **end-to-end network transport functions** suitable for applications transmitting real-time data, such as audio, video, and simulation data, over multicast or unicast network services. RTP does **not** guarantee Quality of Service (QoS) or reliable delivery — it merely provides the mechanisms (sequence numbers, timestamps, payload identification) needed by the application to handle these issues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tested Definition:**
> *RTP is an application-layer protocol that provides end-to-end delivery services for real-time traffic (audio/video) over UDP. It includes payload type identification, sequence numbering, time-stamping, and delivery monitoring.*

## 1.2 RTP Control Protocol (RTCP)

**Definition:**
The **RTP Control Protocol (RTCP)** is the companion control protocol to RTP. It operates by periodically transmitting **control packets to all participants in a session**, using the same distribution mechanism as the data packets. The primary functions of RTCP are to provide **feedback on the quality of data distribution** (QoS feedback), carry persistent transport-level identifiers (CNAME), control the **rate of RTCP packets**, and convey minimal session control information.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Live News Broadcast" Model

Imagine you are watching a **live news broadcast** on television:

- **The Video Stream = RTP** 🎥
  The actual video frames and audio keep flowing to your screen continuously. If one frame is dropped, the show still goes on — you do not stop the broadcast to retransmit the lost frame. RTP behaves exactly like this: it ships media packets as they come, using sequence numbers (to detect loss) and timestamps (to re-order and synchronize playback).

- **The Broadcast Statistics & Studio Intercom = RTCP** 🎙️
  In the newsroom, the production team periodically sends out **viewer ratings, signal-quality reports, and studio announcements** (e.g., "We are now going live to the field reporter"). Similarly, every receiver in an RTP session periodically sends back **Receiver Reports (RR)** containing packet loss, jitter, and round-trip time. The sender uses this to adjust encoding bitrate or switch codecs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** RTP transports the **media**; RTCP transports the **meta-information** (statistics, feedback, identification). They are **sister protocols** — never used independently.

## 1.4 Standard Port Assignments (UDP)

| Protocol | Port Number | Direction |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| RTP      | **Even port number** (e.g., 5004) | Data Plane |
| RTCP     | **Next higher odd port number** (e.g., 5005) | Control Plane |

> [!TIP]
> A common trick question in KTU exams: *"Why is RTP placed on an even port while RTCP on the next odd port?"* — Answer: To allow a single UDP socket pair to demultiplex RTP and RTCP traffic with minimal overhead, following the convention that RTP uses an even port and RTCP uses the **next higher odd port**.

## 1.5 Why RTP Runs Over UDP (and Not TCP)

RTP is deliberately designed to run over **UDP** (User Datagram Protocol) because:
- TCP's **retransmission** mechanism is harmful for real-time media (a late packet is worse than a lost one).
- TCP's **congestion control** causes variable delays ("jitter") unacceptable for streaming.
- TCP's **in-order delivery** contradicts the design where the application can handle out-of-order packets.
- UDP gives RTP the **low-latency, best-effort** semantics real-time applications need.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RTP Operational Workflow (Why & How)

The "Why" behind each RTP mechanism:

- **Sequence Numbering → Why?** To allow the receiver to detect **packet loss** and **restore packet order** without requiring TCP-style retransmission.
- **Timestamping → Why?** To allow the receiver to **synchronize playback** of audio/video samples (e.g., 8000 samples/sec for a G.711 voice stream means each timestamp unit = 1/8000 s). It also enables **inter-media synchronization** (lip-sync between audio and video).
- **Payload Type (PT) → Why?** To tell the receiver **which codec/encoding** is being used (e.g., PT = 0 for PCMU, PT = 26 for JPEG, PT = 96–127 for dynamic types). Without this, the receiver cannot decode the payload.
- **SSRC Identifier → Why?** To uniquely identify **each synchronization source** in a session (e.g., two cameras in a video conference). It allows the receiver to separate streams from different senders.
- **CSRC List → Why?** When a **mixer** combines multiple sources into one stream, the CSRC list records the identities of the contributing original sources so receivers know "who is speaking".
- **Marker (M) Bit → Why?** To mark significant events in the stream, e.g., the **start of a talkspurt** in voice, or the **end of a video frame**.

## 2.2 RTP Header Structure (12 bytes minimum + optional CSRC + extension)

| Field | Width (bits) | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|---------|
| V (Version) | **2** | RTP version, currently **2** |
| P (Padding) | **1** | Packet contains padding octets at the end |
| X (Extension) | **1** | Header extension present after CSRC list |
| CC (CSRC Count) | **4** | Number of CSRC identifiers that follow the fixed header (0–15) |
| M (Marker) | **1** | Application-defined marker (e.g., frame boundary) |
| PT (Payload Type) | **7** | Format of the payload (codec identification) |
| Sequence Number | **16** | Increments by 1 per RTP packet; used to detect loss |
| Timestamp | **32** | Sampling instant of the first octet of the payload |
| SSRC | **32** | Synchronization Source Identifier (random, unique per session) |
| CSRC \[0..15\] | **32 each** | Contributing Source identifiers (used by mixers) |
| Header Extension (optional) | **Variable** | Application-specific extension header |
| Payload | **Variable** | The actual media data (e.g., encoded audio/video) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Fixed Header Size** = 12 bytes = 96 bits.
> The **minimum RTP packet** = 12 (header) + payload (no padding, no CSRC, no extension).

## 2.3 RTCP Packet Types (Five Defined Types)

| Type Value | Abbreviation | Name | Purpose |
|------------|--------------|------|---------|
| **200** | SR | Sender Report | Sent by active senders; contains sender + reception stats |
| **201** | RR | Receiver Report | Sent by receivers (who are not active senders); reception stats only |
| **202** | SDES | Source Description | CNAME, NAME, EMAIL, PHONE, LOC, TOOL, NOTE, PRIV |
| **203** | BYE | Goodbye | Indicates end of participation |
| **204** | APP | Application-defined | Application-specific functions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sender Report (SR) is the most frequently asked RTCP packet in KTU exams.** It contains three sections: a header, sender information, and a list of reception report blocks (one per source from which the sender has received data in the last interval).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — RTP/RTCP Quantitative Metrics

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **RTCP Total Bandwidth** ≤ **5%** of session bandwidth | Hard upper bound; recommended by RFC 3550 |
| 2 | RTCP allocation: **25% to senders**, **75% to receivers** | Default split of the 5% RTCP bandwidth |
| 3 | Average RTCP packet size: $\text{avg\_size} \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{size}_i$ | $n$ = number of RTCP packets observed |
| 4 | RTCP Interval: $T = \max\!\left(T_{\min},\; n \cdot \frac{\text{avg\_size}}{B}\right)$ | $B$ = RTCP bandwidth share, $T_{\min}$ = minimum interval (5 sec) |
| 5 | Inter-Arrival Jitter: $D(i,j) = (R_j - R_i) - (S_j - S_i)$ | $R$ = receive time, $S$ = RTP timestamp; absolute value taken |
| 6 | Cumulative Jitter: $J(i) = J(i-1) + \frac{\vert D(i-1,i) \vert - J(i-1)}{16}$ | Exponentially weighted smoothing (RFC 3550) |
| 7 | Fraction Lost: $\text{frac\_lost} = \dfrac{\text{packets lost}}{\text{packets expected}}$ | Expressed as an 8-bit fixed-point in RR/SR |
| 8 | Extended Highest Seq: $\text{extended\_max} = \text{seq\_cycles} \times 2^{16} + \text{highest\_seq}$ | Handles 16-bit sequence number wraparound |
| 9 | NTP ↔ RTP timestamp mapping: $\text{RTP} = \text{NTP}_{sec} \times \text{clock\_rate} + \Delta$ | Used for cross-media synchronization |
| 10 | Minimum Header Size | $12 \text{ bytes} = 96 \text{ bits}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember:** Bandwidth $B$ is in **bytes per second (B/s)**, average size in **bytes**, interval $T$ in **seconds**. Jitter $D$ and $J$ are in the **same units as timestamps** (typically samples or milliseconds).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of RTP/RTCP |
|--------|-----------------|
| **VoIP (Voice over IP)** | SIP + RTP for call media; RTCP-XR for call quality |
| **Video Conferencing** | WebRTC, Zoom, Microsoft Teams use SRTP (Secure RTP) |
| **Live Streaming** | RTSP + RTP for IPTV surveillance, H.264 streaming |
| **Stock Tickers / Feeds** | Real-time market data with sub-second timestamps |
| **Online Gaming** | Voice chat modules; minimal jitter is critical |
| **DVB / ATSC Television** | Professional broadcast contribution feeds |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Minimum RTP Packet Size

A student must know how to compute the size of a minimal RTP packet for a 2-mark or 3-mark KTU question.

**Problem:** Calculate the size (in bytes) of a minimal RTP packet with no CSRC list, no extension header, and no payload.

**Step 1 — Identify the fixed header components:**

The fixed RTP header contains exactly **12 mandatory fields**. The fixed fields are: V, P, X, CC, M, PT (combined 16 bits = 2 bytes), Sequence Number (16 bits = 2 bytes), Timestamp (32 bits = 4 bytes), and SSRC (32 bits = 4 bytes).

**Step 2 — Sum the bit widths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header Size} &= \underbrace{2+1+1+4+1+7}_{V,P,X,CC,M,PT} + \underbrace{16}_{\text{Seq}} + \underbrace{32}_{\text{Timestamp}} + \underbrace{32}_{\text{SSRC}} \\
&= 16 + 16 + 32 + 32 \\
&= 96 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Header Size} &= \frac{96 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 12 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add payload and any CSRC/extension:**

The total RTP packet size is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Size} = 12 + (\text{CSRC Count} \times 4) + \text{Extension} + \text{Payload Size}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a packet with 0 CSRC, no extension, and a payload of $L$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Size} = 12 + 0 + 0 + L = (12 + L) \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** A 20-byte G.711 voice sample wrapped in RTP → $12 + 20 = 32$ bytes total per packet.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Calculating the RTCP Transmission Interval

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):** A video conferencing session has a session bandwidth of **2 Mbps** dedicated to media + control. There are **20 active participants**. Calculate the **minimum RTCP packet transmission interval** if the average RTCP packet size is **100 bytes**. Assume the default 25/75 sender/receiver split and that all 20 participants are senders.

**Step 1 — Compute the total RTCP bandwidth budget (5% of session):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{RTCP,total}} &= 0.05 \times 2\,\text{Mbps} \\
&= 0.05 \times 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/sec} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{5} \text{ bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-sender RTCP share (25% allocation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{sender}} &= 0.25 \times B_{\text{RTCP,total}} \\
&= 0.25 \times 10^{5} \\
&= 25{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert average RTCP packet size to bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{avg\_size} &= 100 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \\
&= 800 \text{ bits/packet}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time to send one RTCP packet (in seconds):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{one}} &= \frac{\text{avg\_size}}{B_{\text{sender}}} \\
&= \frac{800 \text{ bits}}{25{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}} \\
&= 0.032 \text{ seconds} = 32 \text{ milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the RFC 3550 interval formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \max\!\left( T_{\min},\; n \cdot \frac{\text{avg\_size}}{B} \right) \\
&= \max\!\left( 5 \text{ sec},\; 20 \times 0.032 \right) \\
&= \max\!\left( 5,\; 0.64 \right) \\
&= 5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the mathematical "ideal" interval is only **0.64 seconds**, the **minimum allowed interval** of **5 seconds** dominates. This is by RFC design — to prevent RTCP from flooding the network in large sessions.

**Step 6 — Calculate the per-participant RTCP packet rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{pps}} &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ packets/second} \\
&= 12 \text{ packets/minute per participant}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Sequence Number Wraparound and Extended Highest Sequence

**Problem:** A source's RTP sequence number counter has rolled over **3 times**, and the highest 16-bit sequence number received so far is **45231**. Compute the **Extended Highest Sequence Number**.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{extended\_max} = \text{seq\_cycles} \times 2^{16} + \text{highest\_seq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{extended\_max} &= 3 \times 65{,}536 + 45{,}231 \\
&= 196{,}608 + 45{,}231 \\
&= 241{,}839
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the technique RR/SR packets use to communicate 32-bit-equivalent sequence counts without growing the RTP header.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for RTP/RTCP Comparison

| Dimension | RTP | RTCP |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Purpose** | Transport of real-time media | Control + statistics |
| **Packet type** | Single type (variable payload) | 5 types (SR, RR, SDES, BYE, APP) |
| **Transport** | UDP (typically even port) | UDP (typically next higher odd port) |
| **Direction** | Sender → Receiver(s) | Both directions (multicast) |
| **Frequency** | Continuous (one per media frame) | Periodic (every 5 sec minimum) |
| **Bandwidth share** | ~95% of session | ≤5% of session |
| **Reliability** | None — best effort | None — best effort |
| **Key fields** | Seq#, Timestamp, PT, SSRC | Fraction lost, Cumulative lost, Jitter, LSR, DLSR |
| **OSI layer** | Application (sits on UDP) | Application (sits on UDP) |
| **Replaces?** | Nothing (sister to RTCP) | Nothing (sister to RTP) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Derivation 4 — Inter-Arrival Jitter Calculation

**Problem:** Three consecutive RTP packets arrive with the following data:

| Packet $i$ | RTP Timestamp $S_i$ | Receive Time $R_i$ (seconds) |
|------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| 1          | 1000                | 0.000                        |
| 2          | 2020                | 0.105                        |
| 3          | 3040                | 0.190                        |

Compute the **inter-arrival jitter** for the second packet.

**Step 1 — Apply the D(i,j) formula:**

For packet 2 vs packet 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(1,2) &= (R_2 - R_1) - (S_2 - S_1) \\
&= (0.105 - 0.000) - (2020 - 1000) \\
&= 0.105 - 1020 \\
&= -1019.895
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Take absolute value and accumulate via smoothing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(2) &= J(1) + \frac{\vert D(1,2) \vert - J(1)}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

Initial $J(1) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(2) &= 0 + \frac{\vert -1019.895 \vert - 0}{16} \\
&= \frac{1019.895}{16} \\
&\approx 63.74 \text{ timestamp units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Jitter is reported in **RTP timestamp units**, not seconds. To convert to seconds, divide by the **clock rate** of the payload (e.g., 8000 Hz for PCMU audio). Here: $63.74 / 8000 \approx 7.97$ ms of jitter.

## 4.3 RTP Session — End-to-End Media + Feedback Flow

This block diagram shows the data and control flows between two endpoints in a typical RTP session.

    end

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **asymmetric feedback loop**: media flows Sender → Receiver continuously, but RTCP reports flow in **both directions** so each side knows the quality the other is experiencing.

## 4.5 RTP Mixer Architecture (Multi-Source Convergence)

A **mixer** combines multiple RTP streams into a single stream, often translating encodings. This is essential in conference bridges.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Source 1\nSSRC A"]:::src
    S2["Source 2\nSSRC B"]:::src
    S3["Source 3\nSSRC C"]:::src
    MIX["RTP MIXER\nCombines audio\nNew SSRC X\nCSRC A B C"]:::mix
    NET["Network Multicast"]:::net
    R1["Receiver 1"]:::rcv
    R2["Receiver 2"]:::rcv

    S1 --> MIX
    S2 --> MIX
    S3 --> MIX
    MIX --> NET
    NET --> R1
    NET --> R2

    classDef src fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef rcv fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mixer's outgoing packet has **SSRC = X (its own new ID)** and a **CSRC list \[A, B, C\]** so receivers know the three original speakers that were mixed into this packet.
