## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the role of the Domain Name System (DNS) in the Internet. Why is a fully distributed database preferred over a centralized one for name resolution?*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Role of DNS:** DNS provides a **distributed, hierarchical naming service** that maps human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) to machine IP addresses (e.g., `203.0.113.45`) and other resource records. It is critical because humans cannot easily memorize 32-bit IPv4 or 128-bit IPv6 addresses, while applications need these numeric addresses for actual packet delivery. *(1 mark)*

2. **Why distributed?** A single centralized server would be a single point of failure, would suffer from massive traffic bottlenecks, and would be physically distant from most users. *(1 mark)*

3. **Distributed benefits:** Hierarchical distribution across **root → TLD → authoritative** servers ensures **fault tolerance** (anycast, replication), **scalability** (each zone operator maintains its own data), **low latency** (caching at local resolvers), and **administrative delegation** (each domain is managed independently). *(1 mark)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Briefly explain the functions of SMTP, POP3, and IMAP in an e-mail system. State one key difference between POP3 and IMAP.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol, TCP/25):** Handles **pushing** mail from sender's MTA to receiver's MTA. It is the *inter-server* and *submission* protocol. *(1 mark)*

2. **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3, TCP/110):** Used by the MUA to **download** mail from the mailbox server. Default mode is "download-and-delete," making it a *one-way retrieval* protocol. *(1 mark)*

3. **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol, TCP/143):** Used by the MUA to **access and synchronize** mail *on the server*. Supports server-side folders, partial fetch, and multi-device state sync. *(1 mark for key difference)*

**Key Difference (must state one):**
- POP3 **downloads mail to local device and (typically) deletes from server**, while **IMAP keeps mail on the server** and synchronizes state (read/unread, flags, folders) across multiple devices.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — DNS Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **hierarchical organization of the DNS namespace**. List the four main categories of DNS servers and state their role. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **iterative** and **recursive DNS queries** with sequence diagrams. A host `H` sends a recursive query to its local DNS server to resolve `www.example.com`. Assuming no cached entries and three hierarchical levels (Root → TLD → Authoritative), determine the **number of DNS messages exchanged** and the **total query time** if each RTT = 20 ms. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the inverted tree structure with **Root (.)** at the top, branching to TLDs (`.com`, `.org`, `.in`, etc.), then SLDs (`example`, `ktu`, `iitb`), then subdomains, then hostnames. Mark the `www.example.com` path. Label root, TLD, SLD, and authoritative level.

- **Four categories of DNS servers (4 marks):**
  1. **Root DNS servers (13 logical):** Return address of TLD server. Do not resolve names themselves. Operated via anycast.
  2. **TLD servers:** Maintain records for all domains under a TLD (e.g., all `.com` domains). Return the authoritative server for the requested domain.
  3. **Authoritative servers:** Hold the actual DNS records (A, MX, NS, etc.) for an organization. Provide the *final* answer.
  4. **Local / Caching resolvers (ISP-level):** Perform recursion on behalf of clients and cache responses per TTL.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Iterative Query (3.5 marks):**
- Client receives a *referral* at each step.
- Each step costs **1 query + 1 response = 2 messages**.
- Sequence: `H → LDNS → Root → TLD → Auth → LDNS → H` involves **2 (host↔LDNS) + 2×3 (LDNS↔servers) = 8 messages** in worst case (recursive ask + 3 iterative round trips + final answer).

**Recursive Query (3.5 marks):**
- Client sends **1 query**, gets **1 final answer**. The contacted server does all the work recursively.
- Number of messages from *client's perspective* = **2**. Total messages in the system (including internal recursion) = 2 × (# of DNS levels touched) = 2 × 3 = 6 internally.

**Numerical Calculation:**
- Round-trip time per hop = **20 ms**.
- For a hybrid model (host→LDNS recursive + LDNS→external iterative), total latency:

$$T_{\text{total}} = RTT_{H \to \text{LDNS}} + 3 \times RTT_{\text{LDNS} \to \text{server}} = 20 + 3 \times 20 = 80 \text{ ms}$$

- **Total DNS messages** (counting both queries and responses on each segment):
  - Host ↔ LDNS: 2
  - LDNS ↔ Root: 2
  - LDNS ↔ TLD: 2
  - LDNS ↔ Authoritative: 2
  - **Total = 8 messages.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — E-Mail Focus

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **SMTP protocol** in detail. With a sample dialogue, show how a client sends an e-mail to a server. What are the **limitations of SMTP**, and how does **MIME** address them? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **POP3 and IMAP** with reference to protocol mechanics, port numbers, message handling, and suitable use-cases. Explain the **three phases of a POP3 session** with appropriate commands. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**SMTP Details (3.5 marks):**
- SMTP is a **text-based, push protocol** running over **TCP port 25** (MTA-to-MTA), **587** (MSA submission, RFC 6409), or **465** (SMTPS).
- Uses **request-response commands** ending in `\r\n`; responses use 3-digit codes (2xx = success, 3xx = intermediate, 4xx = transient error, 5xx = permanent).
- Key commands: `HELO/EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `RSET`, `NOOP`, `QUIT`, `VRFY`, `EXPN`, `STARTTLS`, `AUTH`.
- Connection: persistent (one TCP connection can handle multiple messages via pipelining).

**Sample Dialogue (2 marks):**

```
S: 220 mail.example.com ESMTP Postfix
C: EHLO client.example.org
S: 250-mail.example.com Hello
S: 250-SIZE 10240000
S: 250-STARTTLS
S: 250 AUTH PLAIN LOGIN
C: MAIL FROM:<alice@example.org>
S: 250 OK
C: RCPT TO:<bob@example.com>
S: 250 OK
C: DATA
S: 354 End data with <CRLF>.<CRLF>
C: From: alice@example.org
C: To: bob@example.com
C: Subject: Test
C: [Message body]
C: .
S: 250 OK queued as 12345
C: QUIT
S: 221 Bye
```

**Limitations of SMTP + MIME Solution (1.5 marks):**
- Limitation 1: SMTP supports only **7-bit ASCII** → MIME adds **Content-Transfer-Encoding** (base64, quoted-printable) to carry binary.
- Limitation 2: SMTP does not support **attachments or multimedia** → MIME defines **Content-Type** (e.g., `multipart/mixed`, `image/jpeg`, `application/pdf`).
- Limitation 3: No character set support → MIME provides **charset** parameter (e.g., `text/plain; charset=UTF-8`).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | POP3 | IMAP |
|---|---|---|
| Port | 110 / 995 (TLS) | 143 / 993 (TLS) |
| Mode | Download-and-delete (default) | Online sync, server-side state |
| Server folders | Single INBOX | Full folder hierarchy |
| Multi-device | Poor | Excellent |
| Bandwidth | Higher (full download) | Lower (headers first) |
| Search | Client-side only | Server-side supported |
| State storage | Local | Server (flags, UID, mod-sequence) |

**Three Phases of POP3 (4 marks):**

1. **Authorization Phase:** Client authenticates.
   - `USER bob` → `+OK`
   - `PASS secret` → `+OK` (maildrop lock acquired)

2. **Transaction Phase:** Client retrieves/manages mail.
   - `STAT` → returns message count and total size
   - `LIST` → lists messages and sizes
   - `RETR n` → retrieves message n
   - `DELE n` → marks message n for deletion
   - `TOP n k` → first k lines of message n (header)
   - `UIDL` → unique-ID listing
   - `NOOP` → keep-alive
   - `RSET` → undo deletions

3. **Update Phase:** Server commits deletions and closes.
   - `QUIT` → enters UPDATE state, releases lock, deletes marked messages, removes maildrop, sends `+OK`.

**Suitable Use-Cases (included in marks):**
- **POP3:** Single-device, low-bandwidth, offline access preferred.
- **IMAP:** Multi-device, server-side search, organizational folder structures (corporate mail).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Port 25 vs 587:** Many students write that SMTP uses port 110 or 143. **Port 25** is MTA-to-MTA; **587** is submission (MSA). POP3 is **110**, IMAP is **143**. One mark lost per wrong port.
> 2. **Confusing iterative vs recursive:** Students often swap the *agent that does the work*. Remember: **recursive = server does the work; iterative = client does the work step-by-step**.
> 3. **Forgetting TTL in caching answer:** A DNS cache answer without a TTL is incomplete — you must state the TTL governs expiry.
> 4. **SMTP dialogue missing EHLO/MAIL FROM/RCPT TO/DATA order:** Always write the **exact sequence** — marks are awarded per command present.
> 5. **Confusing CNAME with A record:** CNAME is an *alias*; A is a *direct IPv4 mapping*. They are not interchangeable.
> 6. **MIME without base64 explanation:** If you mention MIME, you must explain *how* it extends SMTP (encodings + types). Generic "MIME adds multimedia" is half-credit at best.
> 7. **Forgetting NXDOMAIN / negative caching:** A complete DNS answer should mention **negative caching** per RFC 2308.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **DNS** = distributed, hierarchical, mostly UDP/53, port for TCP is also 53.
- [ ] **Root servers** = **13 logical, ~1,500+ physical** (anycast).
- [ ] **Domain hierarchy:** Root → TLD → SLD → Subdomain → Host.
- [ ] **Resource Record Types:** **A** (IPv4), **AAAA** (IPv6), **MX** (mail), **NS** (name server), **CNAME** (alias), **PTR** (reverse), **SOA** (zone), **TXT** (text), **SRV** (service).
- [ ] **Iterative query:** server returns best referral; client queries next server.
- [ ] **Recursive query:** server takes full responsibility; client gets one final answer.
- [ ] **Hybrid model:** host→LDNS = recursive; LDNS→external = iterative.
- [ ] **TTL** governs caching; **negative caching** (RFC 2308) prevents repeated bad queries.
- [ ] **EDNS(0)** extends UDP payload from **512 → 4,096 bytes**.
- [ ] **E-mail uses 4 agents:** MUA, MSA, MTA, MDA (+ MRA for retrieval).
- [ ] **SMTP** = TCP/25 (MTA), 587 (submission), 465 (SMTPS); push protocol; text-based.
- [ ] **SMTP commands in order:** EHLO → MAIL FROM → RCPT TO → DATA → . → QUIT.
- [ ] **POP3** = TCP/110 (995 for TLS); download-and-delete; 3 phases: Authorization, Transaction, Update.
- [ ] **IMAP** = TCP/143 (993 for TLS); server-side state; multi-device sync; partial fetch.
- [ ] **MIME** = RFC 2045–2049; provides Content-Type, Content-Transfer-Encoding (base64 / quoted-printable), MIME-Version.
- [ ] **Base64 efficiency = 75%**; expands 3 input bytes → 4 output bytes.
- [ ] **DNS message format:** Header (12 B) + Question + Answer + Authority + Additional.
- [ ] **Header flags:** QR, Opcode, AA, TC, RD, RA, Z, RCODE.
- [ ] **DNSSEC (RFC 4033–4035)** provides authentication; **DoT (RFC 7858)** port 853, **DoH (RFC 8484)** uses HTTPS/443.
- [ ] **Engineering utilities:** load balancing (round-robin DNS), CDN routing, anti-spam (SPF/DKIM/DMARC via TXT), service discovery (SRV).
- [ ] **SMTP max line** = 998 octets; **recommended** = 78 octets.
- [ ] **MTA-to-MTA** uses port 25; modern best practice is to use **port 587 for submission** to allow authenticated relay (RFC 6409).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Wide Web - Architectural Overview, HTTP - The Hypertext Transfer Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITT402 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Wide Web - Architectural Overview, HTTP - The Hypertext Transfer Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The World Wide Web (WWW)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **World Wide Web (WWW or W3)** is a distributed, hypermedia-based information system that operates as an *application-layer* service on top of the Internet. It enables the retrieval and display of interconnected **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** documents (alongside images, videos, scripts, and other resources) using a uniform addressing scheme known as the **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** and a client-server request-response protocol called the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested)**: The Internet is the *global network infrastructure* (physical/logical layer of routers, links, and protocols like TCP/IP). The World Wide Web is an *application service* that *runs on top of* the Internet. Other applications on the Internet include Email (SMTP), File Transfer (FTP), and DNS.

### Historical Origin
* Invented by **Sir Tim Berners-Lee** in **1989** at **CERN** (European Organization for Nuclear Research, Geneva).
* First prototype browser and server were deployed on a **NeXT computer**.
* Three foundational pillars proposed by Berners-Lee:
    1. **HTML** — the document markup language
    2. **URI/URL** — the addressing scheme
    3. **HTTP** — the transport protocol

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Library Analogy**: Imagine the Internet is a giant **library building** with infinite hallways (network of networks). The **World Wide Web** is one specific **reading room** inside that library. Inside this reading room:
> - Each **book** is a *webpage* (HTML document).
> - Each book's **call number** is its *URL* (address).
> - The **librarian** is the *web server* (responds to your requests).
> - **You** (with your reading slip) are the *web client/browser*.
> - The **language** you speak to the librarian is *HTTP*.
> - **Cross-references** at the bottom of pages are *hyperlinks*.

The WWW is, therefore, simply a *standardized way to ask for, retrieve, and link digital documents across the Internet*.

---

## 1.2 Architectural Overview of the WWW

The WWW follows a classic **Client–Server Architecture**, but with additional intermediary components such as **proxies, gateways, and caches**.

### Primary Architectural Components

| Component | Role | Standard/Identifier |
|-----------|------|---------------------|
| **Web Browser (Client)** | Issues HTTP requests, renders HTML/CSS/JS | Chrome, Firefox, Edge |
| **Web Server** | Hosts resources, responds with HTTP responses | Apache, Nginx, IIS |
| **Proxy Server** | Intermediate relay for caching, filtering, security | Squid, HAProxy |
| **Cache** | Stores recently fetched objects closer to client | Browser cache, CDN |
| **Origin Server** | The definitive source of a resource | The "real" web server |
| **Tunnel** | Passively relays HTTP traffic (e.g., SSL via CONNECT) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The WWW architecture is **resource-oriented** (not connection-oriented). Every interaction begins with a request for a uniquely identified *resource*, identified by a **URI**.

### WWW Architectural Layers (Conceptual Stack)

| Layer | Protocol/Standard | Function |
|-------|-------------------|----------|
| **Content Layer** | HTML, XML, CSS, JSON, JavaScript | Document structure & behavior |
| **Application Layer** | HTTP, HTTPS | Request/Response protocol |
| **Transport Layer** | TCP (port 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS) | Reliable byte stream |
| **Network Layer** | IP (IPv4/IPv6) | Routing & addressing |
| **Link/Physical Layer** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Fiber | Physical transmission |

---

## 1.3 The HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)

### Formal Academic Definition
**HTTP** is a **stateless, request-response, application-layer protocol** standardized in **RFC 9110 (2022)** that defines how clients and servers exchange *representations of resources* (HTML pages, images, JSON data, etc.) over the Internet. By default, HTTP uses **TCP port 80** for communication and is built atop a reliable, ordered byte-stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Stateless"** means each HTTP request-response pair is independent of previous interactions. The server retains *no memory* of prior client requests. State, when needed, is maintained using mechanisms like **Cookies**, **Sessions**, or **Tokens**.

### Conceptual Analogy for HTTP
> [!TIP]
> **Restaurant Analogy**: HTTP is like ordering food at a restaurant:
> - **You (Client)** read the menu and tell the waiter what you want → this is an **HTTP Request**.
> - **The Waiter** writes your order on a slip (standard format) → this is the **HTTP Message Format**.
> - **The Kitchen (Server)** prepares the dish and the waiter returns with it → this is the **HTTP Response**.
> - Once you finish your meal and leave, the waiter *forgets* you → this is **Statelessness**.
> - If you come back tomorrow and the waiter remembers your preference via a "loyalty card" → that's a **Cookie**.

### HTTP Properties (Syllabus Highlights)

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Application Layer** | Operates above TCP/IP |
| **Stateless** | No client state retained between requests |
| **Connectionless (in classic sense)** | Each request may open a new TCP connection |
| **Media Independent** | Can transmit any content type via MIME |
| **Request-Response** | Client always initiates; server never sends unsolicited data |
| **Default Port** | TCP **80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS) |
| **Transport** | Relies on TCP for reliability (HTTP/3 uses QUIC/UDP) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Uniform Resource Locator (URL)

The URL is the addressing mechanism of the WWW. A standard URL conforms to **RFC 3986** and has the following structure:

```
scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment
```

| Component | Example | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **scheme** | `http`, `https`, `ftp` | Protocol to use |
| **host** | `www.example.com` | Domain name of server |
| **port** | `80`, `8080` | TCP port (often omitted for defaults) |
| **path** | `/index.html` | Resource location on server |
| **query** | `?id=42&sort=asc` | Parameters passed to resource |
| **fragment** | `#section2` | Client-side sub-resource reference |

> [!NOTE]
> The **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** is the more general superset of **URL (Locator)** and **URN (Name)**. A URL is technically a URI that provides *both* a name *and* a means of locating the resource via a network access protocol.

---

## 2.2 HTTP Transaction — The Core Workflow

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **DNS Resolution**: Browser resolves the hostname (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) to an IP address via DNS.
2. **TCP Connection Establishment**: A **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) is performed to establish a TCP connection on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).
3. **HTTP Request Transmission**: The client sends an **HTTP request message** to the server, identifying the resource via a method + URL.
4. **Server Processing**: The web server processes the request, possibly invoking application logic, querying a database, or retrieving a file.
5. **HTTP Response Transmission**: The server returns an **HTTP response message** containing a status code, headers, and (typically) a body with the requested resource.
6. **Connection Management**: Depending on HTTP version and headers, the connection is either closed or kept alive for further requests.
7. **Rendering**: The browser parses HTML, fetches embedded objects (CSS, JS, images), and renders the page.

### Why "Stateless" Matters
Because HTTP is stateless, every request must be **self-contained** (carrying all needed context: cookies, auth tokens, session IDs). This simplifies server design (load balancing is easier) but pushes state-management responsibility to the application.

---

## 2.3 HTTP Request Message Format

An HTTP message is **plain ASCII text** (in HTTP/1.1). It consists of:
- A **start line** (request line for requests, status line for responses)
- **Header fields** (`Header-Name: Value`)
- A **blank line** (CRLF) separating headers from body
- An **optional message body**

### Generic HTTP Request Structure

```
Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF
Header1: Value1 CRLF
Header2: Value2 CRLF
... CRLF (blank line)
[Optional Body]
```

### Concrete Example (HTTP/1.1 GET Request)

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml
Accept-Language: en-US
Connection: keep-alive
```

### Generic HTTP Response Structure

```
HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase CRLF
Header1: Value1 CRLF
Header2: Value2 CRLF
... CRLF (blank line)
[Optional Body]
```

### Concrete Example (HTTP/1.1 Response)

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 15 Sep 2025 12:00:00 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4.52 (Ubuntu)
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 1024
Last-Modified: Sun, 14 Sep 2025 18:30:00 GMT

<html>... HTML content ...</html>
```

---

## 2.4 HTTP Methods (Verbs)

The method token indicates the operation to be performed on the resource. Defined primarily in **RFC 9110**.

| Method | Safe? | Idempotent? | Body? | Purpose |
|--------|-------|-------------|-------|---------|
| **GET** | ✅ | ✅ | No (recommended) | Retrieve a representation of a resource |
| **HEAD** | ✅ | ✅ | No | Same as GET, but only headers returned |
| **POST** | ❌ | ❌ | Yes | Submit data to be processed (e.g., form) |
| **PUT** | ❌ | ✅ | Yes | Replace the resource at the URI with the request payload |
| **DELETE** | ❌ | ✅ | No (recommended) | Remove the resource |
| **OPTIONS** | ✅ | ✅ | No | Describe communication options for the resource |
| **PATCH** | ❌ | ❌ | Yes | Apply partial modifications to a resource |
| **TRACE** | ✅ | ✅ | No | Echo back the received request (diagnostic) |
| **CONNECT** | ❌ | ❌ | No | Establish a tunnel to the origin server (used by HTTPS proxies) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe** = method *should not* alter server state (side-effect free).
> **Idempotent** = multiple identical requests yield the *same result* as a single request.
> *GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST is NOT.*

---

## 2.5 HTTP Status Codes (RFC 9110)

Status codes are **3-digit integers** grouped into five classes:

| Class | Range | Meaning | Example Codes |
|-------|-------|---------|---------------|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continuing process | 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request successfully received/understood/accepted | **200 OK**, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed to complete request | **301 Moved Permanently**, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | **400 Bad Request**, **401 Unauthorized**, **403 Forbidden**, **404 Not Found** |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill a valid request | **500 Internal Server Error**, **502 Bad Gateway**, **503 Service Unavailable** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these for exams**: 200, 301, 302, 304, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500, 502, 503.

---

## 2.6 HTTP Header Fields

Headers carry **metadata** about the request, response, or the message body. Standardized in **RFC 9110**, with many extensions.

### Common Request Headers

| Header | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `Host` | Required in HTTP/1.1; specifies virtual host |
| `User-Agent` | Identifies the client software |
| `Accept` | Media types the client can process |
| `Accept-Language` | Preferred natural languages |
| `Accept-Encoding` | Supported compression algorithms (gzip, deflate, br) |
| `Cookie` | Sends stored cookies back to server |
| `Authorization` | Credentials for authentication |
| `If-Modified-Since` | Conditional GET — fetch only if changed |
| `If-None-Match` | Conditional GET — uses ETag |

### Common Response Headers

| Header | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `Server` | Software running on the origin server |
| `Date` | Time the response was generated |
| `Content-Type` | MIME type of the body (e.g., `text/html`) |
| `Content-Length` | Size of the body in bytes |
| `Content-Encoding` | Encoding applied (e.g., gzip) |
| `Last-Modified` | Last modification time of resource |
| `ETag` | Unique identifier for a version of the resource |
| `Set-Cookie` | Stores a cookie in the client |
| `Location` | Used in 3xx redirects to specify new URI |
| `Cache-Control` | Caching directives |

---

## 2.7 Non-Persistent vs. Persistent (Keep-Alive) Connections

This is a **critical topic** in KTU numerical problems.

### Non-Persistent Connection (HTTP/1.0 default)
* Each TCP connection handles **exactly one** request-response pair.
* After the response, TCP is closed.
* Requires a fresh **3-way handshake** for every new object.

### Persistent Connection (HTTP/1.1 default)
* A single TCP connection handles **multiple** request-response pairs.
* Two variants: 
    * **Without pipelining**: client waits for each response before sending the next request.
    * **With pipelining** (HTTP/1.1): client sends multiple requests back-to-back without waiting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Content-Length** header is *mandatory* in HTTP/1.1 persistent connections so the client knows where one response ends and the next begins.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Total RTT for one object (non-persistent) | $T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | RTT (one-way ping) | $RTT = 2 \cdot t_{prop}$ where $t_{prop}$ is one-way propagation delay | ms |
| 3 | File transfer time (parallel) | $T = \max\left(\dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{BW}},\ RTT_{TCP} + \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{BW}}\right)$ | seconds |
| 4 | HTML page load time — non-persistent | $T = (N+1) \cdot (2RTT + t_{setup}) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{O_i}{BW}$ | seconds |
| 5 | HTML page load time — persistent, no pipelining | $T = 2RTT + t_{setup} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(RTT + \dfrac{O_i}{BW}\right)$ | seconds |
| 6 | HTML page load time — persistent, pipelining | $T = 2RTT + t_{setup} + \max_{i}\left(RTT + \dfrac{O_i}{BW}\right) + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\dfrac{O_i}{BW}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = BW \cdot RTT$ | bits |
| 8 | Throughput over TCP | $\le \dfrac{\text{Window Size}}{RTT}$ | bits/s |
| 9 | HTTP message size | request $\le 1\text{ KB}$, response $= \text{headers} + \text{body}$ | bytes |
| 10 | Object count for inline embedding | All small objects inlined in HTML = 1 file | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables**: 
> - $N$ = number of referenced objects on a page (CSS, JS, images)
> - $O_i$ = size of the $i^{th}$ object in bits
> - $RTT$ = round-trip time in seconds
> - $BW$ = bottleneck link bandwidth in bits/second
> - $t_{setup}$ = TCP handshake/setup overhead (often $\approx RTT$)

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Web Development**: HTTP request-response understanding underpins REST API design (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE).
* **Network Engineering**: Status codes and headers are inspected by monitoring tools (Wireshark, Postman, curl).
* **Cybersecurity**: Understanding HTTP headers (Cookies, Authorization) is essential for penetration testing, XSS, CSRF analysis.
* **Performance Optimization**: Persistent connections, pipelining, multiplexing (HTTP/2), and caching headers (ETag, Cache-Control) directly impact page load performance — a critical metric in SEO and UX.
* **Load Balancing**: Statelessness enables horizontal scaling — any request can land on any server, with shared session stores (Redis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why "2 × RTT" Appears in HTTP Timing

### The Problem
Many students struggle with the standard formula for HTTP latency: $T = 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{L}{R}$ (where $L$ = file size, $R$ = transmission rate).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a client downloading a file from a server. The steps are:

**Step 1 — TCP Connection Establishment**:
A 3-way handshake consumes one full RTT.
- $t = 0$: Client sends SYN → server.
- $t = \dfrac{RTT}{2}$: Server receives SYN, sends SYN-ACK.
- $t = RTT$: Client receives SYN-ACK, sends ACK.
- Connection is now established.
- **Time consumed** = $1 \cdot RTT$

**Step 2 — HTTP Request Transmission**:
The client sends the GET request. For very small requests, this is approximated as negligible (or $\dfrac{L_{req}}{R}$, which is tiny). Often approximated as part of the setup.

**Step 3 — Server Processing + First-Byte Flight**:
The server processes the request and sends the first byte of the response. The first byte reaches the client after one more RTT.
- **Time consumed** = $1 \cdot RTT$

**Step 4 — File Transmission**:
The server transmits the entire file (size $L$ bits) at rate $R$ bits/sec.
- Transmission time = $\dfrac{L}{R}$ seconds.
- The last byte arrives after this transmission time.

**Total time to fetch the file**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= t_{handshake} + t_{firstbyte} + t_{transmission} \\[4pt]
&= RTT + RTT + \dfrac{L}{R} \\[4pt]
T_{total} &= 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{L}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical HTTP latency formula**. The $2 \cdot RTT$ accounts for: 1 RTT to *establish* the TCP connection, and 1 RTT to *deliver* the request and receive the *first byte* of the response.

---

## 3.2 Derivations: Page Load Times — The Three Variants

### Problem Setup (Standard KTU Question Type)
* A webpage consists of a base **HTML file** plus $N$ referenced objects (images, CSS, JS).
* Base HTML file size: $H$ bits.
* Each of the $N$ objects has size $O$ bits (simplified assumption).
* Round-trip time: $RTT$ seconds.
* Bandwidth: $R$ bits/sec.
* All objects are on the **same origin server**.
* TCP handshake consumes $\approx 1 \cdot RTT$.

---

### Case A — Non-Persistent Connections (HTTP/1.0 style)

Each object (including the base HTML) requires **a fresh TCP connection** (2 RTTs per object).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{non-persistent} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N+1} \left( 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{O_i}{R} \right) \\[4pt]
&= (N+1) \cdot (2 \cdot RTT) + \dfrac{H}{R} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot (N+1) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum_{i=1}^{N} O_i}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick**: Non-persistent means *every* object costs $2 \cdot RTT$. So total handshake overhead = $2(N+1) \cdot RTT$.

---

### Case B — Persistent Connection, No Pipelining (HTTP/1.1 style)

The base HTML is fetched (1 TCP handshake = $2 \cdot RTT$). Then subsequent objects reuse the same TCP connection sequentially.

**Step 1 — Fetch base HTML**:
Time $= 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H}{R}$

**Step 2 — Fetch each of the $N$ objects sequentially**:
After the HTML is loaded, the client parses it, discovers each object, and sends a GET request. Each request waits for the previous response. Each subsequent object requires:
- $1 \cdot RTT$ for request + first byte arrival
- $\dfrac{O}{R}$ for transmission

So each additional object costs $RTT + \dfrac{O}{R}$.

**Total time**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{persistent, no\ pipeline} &= 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H}{R} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( RTT + \dfrac{O_i}{R} \right) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H}{R} + N \cdot RTT + \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= (N+2) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum_{i=1}^{N} O_i}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Case C — Persistent Connection WITH Pipelining

After the base HTML, **all $N$ requests** are sent back-to-back without waiting for individual responses. The server processes them in order and sends responses.

* Requests flight time = $\dfrac{N \cdot req_{size}}{R} \approx 0$ (requests are tiny).
* Server takes a *worst-case* time to send the largest response, but all responses share the link serially.
* After pipelining requests, the **last byte** of the last response arrives after:
    - $RTT$ (for request-flight + first byte of first response)
    - $+\ \dfrac{\sum O_i}{R}$ (transmission of all bodies sequentially on the bottleneck link)

**Total time**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{persistent, pipelined} &= 2 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H}{R} + RTT + \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= (N_{pipe} = 3) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum_{i=1}^{N} O_i}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transmission delay** for all object bodies is *unavoidable* — pipelining only removes request flight delays, not the actual bytes that must cross the wire.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement
A webpage has a base HTML file of size **50 KB** and references **10 image objects**, each of size **200 KB**. The RTT between client and server is **100 ms**. The bottleneck link bandwidth is **100 Mbps**. The HTML file and objects are all on the same origin server. Calculate the total page load time for:
1. (a) Non-persistent HTTP
2. (b) Persistent HTTP without pipelining
3. (c) Persistent HTTP with pipelining

Ignore the transmission time of HTTP request messages.

### Given Data
* $H = 50 \text{ KB} = 50 \times 8 \times 1024 = 409{,}600 \text{ bits}$
* $N = 10$ objects
* $O_i = 200 \text{ KB} = 200 \times 8 \times 1024 = 1{,}638{,}400 \text{ bits}$ each
* $RTT = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$
* $R = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$

### Common Sub-Expressions

Compute the sum of object sizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{10} O_i &= 10 \times 1{,}638{,}400 = 16{,}384{,}000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total data to transfer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits} &= H + \sum O_i = 409{,}600 + 16{,}384{,}000 = 16{,}793{,}600 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Transmission time for all data:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \dfrac{16{,}793{,}600}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.167936 \text{ s} \approx 167.94 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part (a) — Non-Persistent HTTP

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= 2 \cdot (N+1) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot 11 \cdot 0.1 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
&= 2.2 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
T_a &= 2.367936 \text{ s} \approx \mathbf{2.37 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Persistent, No Pipelining

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= (N + 2) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 12 \cdot 0.1 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
&= 1.2 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
T_b &= 1.367936 \text{ s} \approx \mathbf{1.37 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part (c) — Persistent with Pipelining

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 3 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R} \\[4pt]
&= 3 \cdot 0.1 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
&= 0.3 + 0.167936 \\[4pt]
T_c &= 0.467936 \text{ s} \approx \mathbf{0.47 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Analytical Interpretation Matrix

| Scenario | Page Load Time | RTT Overhead | Transmission Overhead |
|----------|----------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Non-persistent | 2.37 s | 11 × 2 RTT = 2.20 s (≈93%) | 0.17 s (≈7%) |
| Persistent, no pipeline | 1.37 s | 1.20 s (≈88%) | 0.17 s (≈12%) |
| Persistent, pipelined | 0.47 s | 0.30 s (≈64%) | 0.17 s (≈36%) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight**: In high-latency links, the *RTT* dominates (notice 2.2 s out of 2.37 s in non-persistent!). Reducing RTT (via CDNs, HTTP/2 multiplexing, HTTP/3 over QUIC) yields more improvement than increasing bandwidth. This is why **performance engineers focus on latency, not just bandwidth**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: HTTP Versions Comparison

| Feature | HTTP/0.9 | HTTP/1.0 | HTTP/1.1 | HTTP/2 | HTTP/3 |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|--------|--------|
| Year | 1991 | 1996 | 1997 | 2015 | 2022 |
| Methods | GET only | Full set | Full set | Full set | Full set |
| Headers | None | MIME-like | Host required, rich | HPACK-compressed | QPACK-compressed |
| Connection | Non-persistent | Non-persistent | Persistent default | Multiplexed | Multiplexed over QUIC |
| Status Codes | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Pipelining | No | No | Yes (rarely used) | N/A (multiplexed) | N/A (multiplexed) |
| Transport | TCP | TCP | TCP | TCP | **QUIC (UDP)** |
| Encryption | No | No | Optional (TLS) | TLS 1.2+ | TLS 1.3 built-in |
| Binary | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Server Push | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WWW Client-Server Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide["CLIENT SIDE (Web Browser)"]
        UA[User Agent<br/>Chrome Firefox Edge]
        UI[Rendering Engine<br/>HTML CSS JS]
        LS[Local Storage<br/>Cache Cookies]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer["NETWORK LAYERS"]
        DNS[DNS Resolver<br/>UDP Port 53]
        TCP[TCP Stack<br/>3-Way Handshake]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["SERVER SIDE"]
        WS[Web Server<br/>Apache Nginx IIS]
        APPS[Application Logic<br/>PHP Java Python Node]
        DB[(Database<br/>MySQL PostgreSQL)]
        FS[(File System<br/>HTML Images)]
    end

    UA -->|Step 1: URL Parse| DNS
    DNS -->|Step 2: Returns IP| UA
    UA -->|Step 3: HTTP Request| TCP
    TCP -->|Step 4: SYN SYN-ACK ACK| WS
    WS -->|Step 5: Route Resource| APPS
    APPS -->|Step 6: Query| DB
    APPS -->|Step 6: Read File| FS
    APPS -->|Step 7: Build Response| WS
    WS -->|Step 8: HTTP Response + Body| TCP
    TCP -->|Step 9: Receive Bytes| UA
    UA -->|Step 10: Render| UI
    UA -.->|Step 11: Cache| LS
    UI -->|Step 12: Display to User| User((End User))

    style UA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style WS fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#b30000
    style DB fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style DNS fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram**: Start at the top-left (User Agent), follow arrows clockwise. Notice the **request path** goes down (1→5), and the **response path** comes back up (7→10). The cache (LS) is consulted on subsequent requests for the same resource.

---

## 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Transaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant DNS as DNS Server
    participant S as Web Server
    participant FS as File System / DB

    Note over C: User enters URL<br/>e.g. http://example.com/page.html

    C->>DNS: 1. DNS Query (A record)
    DNS-->>C: 2. Returns IP 93.184.216.34
    C->>S: 3. TCP SYN (Port 80)
    S-->>C: 4. TCP SYN-ACK
    C->>S: 5. TCP ACK [Connection Established]
    C->>S: 6. HTTP Request: GET /page.html HTTP/1.1
    Note over S: Parse request,<br/>identify resource
    S->>FS: 7. Read /page.html
    FS-->>S: 8. File contents
    S-->>C: 9. HTTP Response 200 OK + HTML body
    C->>C: 10. Parse HTML, find references
    C->>S: 11. GET /logo.png HTTP/1.1
    S-->>C: 12. HTTP Response 200 OK + image
    Note over C: Render complete page
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this for the exam**: In KTU's question paper, when asked to "explain HTTP transaction", label each step like the numbered comments above. Examiners reward clear, sequential explanations.

---

```mermaid
graph TB

        RL["Start Line<br/>GET /index.html HTTP/1.1<br/>(Method URI Version)"]
        RH1["Header: Host: www.example.com"]
        RH2["Header: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0"]
        RH3["Header: Accept: text/html"]
        RBL["BLANK LINE (CRLF)"]
        RB["[Optional Body<br/>e.g. POST payload]"]
    end

    subgraph ResponseMsg["HTTP RESPONSE MESSAGE"]
        SL["Status Line<br/>HTTP/1.1 200 OK<br/>(Version Code Phrase)"]
        SH1["Header: Content-Type: text/html"]
        SH2["Header: Content-Length: 1024"]
        SH3["Header: Set-Cookie: session=abc123"]
        SBL["BLANK LINE (CRLF)"]
        SB["Body<br/>HTML / JSON / Image bytes"]
    end

    style RL fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style SL fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style RBL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style SBL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

## 4.4 Non-Persistent vs. Persistent Connection Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title HTTP Connection Timeline (3 Objects)
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S

    section Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0)
    TCP Handshake 1  :a1, 00, 100ms
    GET obj1 + Receive :a2, after a1, 100ms
    TCP Tear-down 1     :crit, after a2, 0ms
    TCP Handshake 2  :a3, after a2, 100ms
    GET obj2 + Receive :a4, after a3, 100ms
    TCP Tear-down 2     :crit, after a4, 0ms
    TCP Handshake 3  :a5, after a4, 100ms
    GET obj3 + Receive :a6, after a5, 100ms

    section Persistent (HTTP/1.1)
    TCP Handshake 1  :b1, 00, 100ms
    GET obj1 + Receive :b2, after b1, 100ms
    GET obj2 + Receive :b3, after b2, 100ms
    GET obj3 + Receive :b4, after b3, 100ms
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam takeaway**: In non-persistent, the connection establishment overhead is repeated for *every* object. In persistent, only the **first** object pays this cost. The total time saved is roughly $(N-1) \times RTT$ — substantial for HTTP/1.0.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the three foundational components of the World Wide Web as proposed by Tim Berners-Lee.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Tim Berners-Lee, while working at CERN in 1989, identified three foundational components without which the WWW cannot function:

1. **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** — A markup language used to structure web documents with elements, headings, links, and media tags. It is the *content format* of the Web. (1 mark)
2. **URI/URL (Uniform Resource Identifier / Locator)** — A standardized addressing scheme that uniquely identifies every resource on the Web. It enables documents to reference each other unambiguously. (1 mark)
3. **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** — An application-layer, request-response protocol used by clients and servers to exchange resources over the Internet. (1 mark)

Together, these three form the *triad of the Web*: HTML is the **what**, URL is the **where**, and HTTP is the **how**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | HTTP | HTTPS |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Full Form | HyperText Transfer Protocol | HTTP Secure |
| Port | 80 | 443 |
| Encryption | None (plaintext) | TLS/SSL (encrypted) |
| Certificate | Not required | Requires digital certificate (X.509) |
| URL Prefix | `http://` | `https://` |
| Use Case | Insecure, legacy, internal tools | Secure, public websites, e-commerce |

**Key Points to Remember** (for the 3rd mark):
- HTTPS = HTTP + TLS handshake + encrypted payload. (1 mark)
- HTTPS protects against eavesdropping, tampering, and man-in-the-middle attacks. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**a) Explain the World Wide Web architecture with a neat diagram. List the key components and their roles. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Introduction (1 mark)**:
The World Wide Web (WWW) is a distributed hypermedia information system that operates as an application-layer service on top of the Internet. It uses a **client-server model** in which a *web browser* (client) requests resources from a *web server* using the **HTTP** protocol, identified by **URLs**, and rendered as **HTML** documents.

**Key Components** (4 marks):

1. **Web Client (Browser)**: The user-facing software (Chrome, Firefox, Edge). It issues HTTP requests, processes responses, and renders content. It also caches responses, manages cookies, and executes scripts (JavaScript).

2. **Web Server**: A long-running process (Apache, Nginx, IIS) that listens on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS), accepts incoming TCP connections, parses HTTP requests, and returns responses. It may serve static files (HTML, images) directly or invoke application logic (CGI, PHP, Node.js) for dynamic content.

3. **HTTP Protocol**: The application-layer protocol that defines the format of requests and responses. It is stateless and uses TCP as the transport.

4. **Resources / HTML Documents**: The actual content being served. HTML is the document markup language; CSS adds styling; JavaScript adds interactivity.

5. **URL/URI**: The addressing scheme that uniquely identifies each resource on the Web.

6. **Proxy Servers and Caches**: Intermediaries that improve performance (caching), security (filtering), or anonymity.

7. **DNS (Domain Name System)**: Not strictly part of the WWW, but essential infrastructure. Resolves domain names to IP addresses before HTTP can be initiated.

**Architecture Diagram (1 mark)**:
Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the standard WWW architecture block diagram.

**Working Flow (1 mark)**:
- The user enters a URL in the browser.
- DNS resolves the hostname.
- TCP connection established (3-way handshake).
- HTTP request is sent (GET).
- Server processes and returns HTTP response.
- Browser renders the page, parsing HTML and fetching embedded objects.

---

**b) With a neat diagram, explain the HTTP request and response message formats. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**HTTP Request Message Format** (3.5 marks):

An HTTP request consists of four parts:

1. **Request Line** (1 mark):
   Format: `Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF`
   Example: `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`

2. **Header Lines** (1 mark):
   Provide metadata about the request, client, or message body.
   Example:
   ```
   Host: www.example.com
   User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
   Accept: text/html
   ```

3. **Blank Line (CRLF)** (0.5 marks):
   A single empty line separating headers from the optional body.

4. **Message Body** (1 mark):
   Optional. Used primarily with POST, PUT, PATCH to send data to the server (e.g., form fields, JSON).

**HTTP Response Message Format** (3.5 marks):

An HTTP response consists of four parts:

1. **Status Line** (1 mark):
   Format: `HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase CRLF`
   Example: `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`

2. **Response Headers** (1 mark):
   Provide metadata about the response or server.
   Example:
   ```
   Content-Type: text/html
   Content-Length: 1024
   Server: Apache/2.4
   ```

3. **Blank Line (CRLF)** (0.5 marks):
   Separator between headers and body.

4. **Message Body** (1 mark):
   Contains the requested resource (HTML, JSON, image bytes, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - Students often forget the **blank line (CRLF)** separating headers from the body. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if this is missing from your diagram or explanation.
> - Don't confuse the **request line** with a **status line**. Many students interchange them.
> - The **body is optional** in both request and response — explicitly mention this for full credit.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**a) Explain the HTTP methods GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE with examples. Differentiate between safe and idempotent methods. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**HTTP Methods Overview** (1 mark):
HTTP methods (also called verbs) indicate the action to be performed on the resource identified by the Request-URI. They define the **semantics** of the request.

**GET** (1.5 marks):
- **Purpose**: Retrieve a representation of a resource.
- **Side Effects**: Should not modify server state.
- **Body**: Generally no body.
- **Example**: `GET /users/42 HTTP/1.1` → fetches user with ID 42.
- **Safe**: ✅ Yes
- **Idempotent**: ✅ Yes (multiple identical GETs yield same result)

**POST** (1.5 marks):
- **Purpose**: Submit data to be processed (e.g., create a new resource, submit a form).
- **Side Effects**: Often creates new server-side resources.
- **Body**: Yes (carries the submitted data).
- **Example**: `POST /users HTTP/1.1` with body `{"name":"Alice","email":"a@b.com"}` → creates a new user.
- **Safe**: ❌ No
- **Idempotent**: ❌ No (multiple POSTs may create multiple users)

**PUT** (1.5 marks):
- **Purpose**: Replace the resource at the URI with the request payload, or create it if it doesn't exist.
- **Side Effects**: Replaces server state.
- **Body**: Yes (carries the full new resource representation).
- **Example**: `PUT /users/42 HTTP/1.1` with body `{"name":"Alice","email":"new@b.com"}` → replaces user 42's record.
- **Safe**: ❌ No
- **Idempotent**: ✅ Yes (replacing with the same data yields the same state)

**DELETE** (1.5 marks):
- **Purpose**: Remove the identified resource.
- **Side Effects**: Deletes server state.
- **Body**: Generally no body.
- **Example**: `DELETE /users/42 HTTP/1.1` → deletes user 42.
- **Safe**: ❌ No
- **Idempotent**: ✅ Yes (deleting an already-deleted resource is still "deleted")

**Safe vs. Idempotent** (1 mark bonus for clarity):

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Safe** | The method is intended to be *read-only*; no side effects on server state. |
| **Idempotent** | Multiple identical requests yield the *same final state* as a single request. |

All safe methods are idempotent, but NOT all idempotent methods are safe. Example: `DELETE` is idempotent but NOT safe.

---

**b) A webpage consists of a base HTML file of size 60 KB and 8 referenced image objects, each of size 250 KB. The RTT between the client and server is 80 ms, and the link bandwidth is 50 Mbps. Calculate the total time to load the complete webpage using: (i) Non-persistent HTTP, (ii) Persistent HTTP without pipelining, and (iii) Persistent HTTP with pipelining. Ignore HTTP request transmission times. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Given Data**:
* $H = 60 \text{ KB} = 60 \times 8 \times 1024 = 491{,}520 \text{ bits}$
* $N = 8$ image objects
* $O_i = 250 \text{ KB} = 250 \times 8 \times 1024 = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bits}$ each
* $RTT = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.08 \text{ s}$
* $R = 50 \text{ Mbps} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$

**Total Object Bits**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{8} O_i &= 8 \times 2{,}048{,}000 = 16{,}384{,}000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Data Bits**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= H + \sum O_i = 491{,}520 + 16{,}384{,}000 = 16{,}875{,}520 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transmission Time for All Data**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{tx} &= \dfrac{16{,}875{,}520}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.33751 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (i) — Non-Persistent HTTP** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 2(N+1) \cdot RTT + t_{tx} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.08 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
&= 1.44 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
T_1 &= \mathbf{1.7775 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (ii) — Persistent HTTP, No Pipelining** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= (N+2) \cdot RTT + t_{tx} \\[4pt]
&= 10 \cdot 0.08 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
&= 0.80 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
T_2 &= \mathbf{1.1375 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (iii) — Persistent HTTP WITH Pipelining** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= 3 \cdot RTT + t_{tx} \\[4pt]
&= 3 \cdot 0.08 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
&= 0.24 + 0.33751 \\[4pt]
T_3 &= \mathbf{0.5775 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Comparative Analysis** (1 mark):

| Scenario | Total Time | RTT Overhead | Transmission |
|----------|------------|--------------|--------------|
| Non-persistent | 1.78 s | 1.44 s | 0.34 s |
| Persistent (no pipe) | 1.14 s | 0.80 s | 0.34 s |
| Persistent (pipelined) | 0.58 s | 0.24 s | 0.34 s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Calculation Questions**:
> 1. **Unit conversion is mandatory**: Always convert KB → bits (multiply by $8 \times 1024$); Mbps → bps (multiply by $10^6$); ms → s (divide by 1000). Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing conversions.
> 2. **Don't confuse RTT with one-way delay**: $RTT = 2 \times$ one-way propagation delay. If the question says "propagation delay = 40 ms", then $RTT = 80 \text{ ms}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the base HTML file**: Many students compute time only for the $N$ objects and forget the base HTML. That's a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Wrong formula for pipelining**: A common error is using $(N+2) \cdot RTT$ for pipelining. The correct formula uses only $3 \cdot RTT$ (1 for TCP setup + 1 for base HTML + 1 for the pipelined request/response cycle).
> 5. **Show every formula step**: Don't write only the final answer. Each substitution earns a partial mark if a calculation error occurs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam**:

### Conceptual Distinctions
- [ ] **Internet vs. WWW**: Internet = network infrastructure; WWW = application service on it.
- [ ] **WWW Triad**: HTML (content) + URI/URL (address) + HTTP (transport).
- [ ] **Stateless**: HTTP does not retain client info between requests.
- [ ] **Default Ports**: HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**.
- [ ] **Transport**: HTTP uses TCP (HTTP/3 uses QUIC/UDP).

### HTTP Methods (Memory Mnemonic: "Get Pulled Down, Options Cannot Hide Their Traces")
- [ ] **GET**: Read; safe & idempotent; no body.
- [ ] **POST**: Create/submit; not safe; not idempotent; has body.
- [ ] **PUT**: Replace; not safe; idempotent; has body.
- [ ] **DELETE**: Remove; not safe; idempotent.
- [ ] **HEAD**: GET without body.
- [ ] **OPTIONS**: Ask what methods are allowed.
- [ ] **TRACE**: Echo back the request (diagnostic).

### Status Code Classes (Memory Mnemonic: "1 Inform, 2 Succeed, 3 Redirect, 4 Client Fault, 5 Server Fault")
- [ ] **2xx Success**: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content.
- [ ] **3xx Redirection**: 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified.
- [ ] **4xx Client Error**: 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found.
- [ ] **5xx Server Error**: 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable.

### Numerical Formulas (Must Know)
- [ ] $T_{non-persistent} = 2(N+1) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R}$
- [ ] $T_{persistent, no-pipeline} = (N+2) \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R}$
- [ ] $T_{persistent, pipelined} = 3 \cdot RTT + \dfrac{H + \sum O_i}{R}$
- [ ] Single-object basic latency: $T = 2RTT + \dfrac{L}{R}$
- [ ] Unit conversions: KB → bits ($\times 8 \times 1024$), Mbps → bps ($\times 10^6$).

### Connection Management
- [ ] HTTP/1.0 → **Non-persistent** by default.
- [ ] HTTP/1.1 → **Persistent** by default (`Connection: keep-alive`).
- [ ] Pipelining → send multiple requests without waiting; rare in practice.
- [ ] HTTP/2 → **Multiplexed** (no need for pipelining).
- [ ] HTTP/3 → Over QUIC (UDP-based, built-in TLS 1.3).

### Message Format Essentials
- [ ] **Request**: Request-Line + Headers + Blank Line + Optional Body.
- [ ] **Response**: Status-Line + Headers + Blank Line + Optional Body.
- [ ] `Content-Length` header is **mandatory** in HTTP/1.1 for persistent connections.
- [ ] `Host` header is **mandatory** in HTTP/1.1 (virtual hosting).

### Key Headers to Memorize
- [ ] `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Cookie`, `Authorization`.
- [ ] `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `Last-Modified`, `Set-Cookie`, `Location`.

### Historical Facts (Often Asked as 2-Mark Questions)
- [ ] WWW invented by **Tim Berners-Lee** in **1989** at **CERN**.
- [ ] First proposal of HTTP in **1989**.
- [ ] HTTP/1.1 standardized in **RFC 2068 (1997)**; current: **RFC 9110 (2022)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy**: If you remember only **3 things** from this topic, remember:
> 1. WWW = HTTP + URL + HTML.
> 2. HTTP is **stateless**, **TCP-based**, **port 80** (443 for HTTPS).
> 3. The **3 RTT formulas** for page load time (non-persistent, persistent, persistent+pipelined).

**End of Module 4 Notes — World Wide Web & HTTP**
